# Infinite Loops and Explicit Exits

_1132 messages · 27 participants · 2004-10 → 2005-01_

---

### Infinite Loops and Explicit Exits

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2004-10-29T12:01:03-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<clu41g$1t2b$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`

```
For those who have been following the "perform forever" and "exit perform
[cycle]" threads, I'm just tidying up the first drafts of two proposals that
I hope to submit Real Soon Now to INCITS/J4 for evaluation and consideration
for a future standard, if nothing else so that they can be added to the
"candidates list" for future consideration.

One of these proposals relaxes the current restriction that an EXIT PERFORM
or EXIT PERFORM CYCLE statement may only appear within a PERFORM ...
END-PERFORM range.

The other proposal is for PERFORM ... UNTIL FALSE.   I didn't want to add a
new reserved word and for that reason rejected PERFORM ... FOREVER.  I was
concerned about the potential semantic ambiguities that one might encounter
in sequences like "PERFORM ... PERFORM ... UNTIL EXIT PERFORM ...
END-PERFORM ...", which led me away from PERFORM ... UNTIL EXIT.  I don't
care for PERFORM ... WITH NO TEST because it really doesn't convey the sense
of iteration.  I am not wedded to the syntax I chose, but I think the
technical reasons for avoiding the other three here presented are sound.

Unless WG4 so directs, these capabilities can't be included in the proposed
2008 standard.  But I can envision no technical barrier at all to the second
of these, and the only barriers I can envision to the first are
implementor-specific, WG4 *might* acquiesce so long as the additions do not
cause a delay in the production of that next standard.

At the very least this action on my part will ensure that these topics are
on the standards committees' radar screens, and stimulate the discussion and
clarification as to why EXIT PERFORM (with or without CYCLE) was limited to
inline PERFORMs in the 2002 standard.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Infinite Loops and Explicit Exits

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2004-10-30T14:42:07+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3LNgd.18045$5b1.3902@newssvr17.news.prodigy.com>`
- **References:** `<clu41g$1t2b$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <S7Jgd.3709$K7.2223@news-server.bigpond.net.au>`

```
"Esmond Pitt" <esmond.pitt@not.bigpond.com> wrote in message
news:S7Jgd.3709$K7.2223@news-server.bigpond.net.au...
> Chuck Stevens wrote:
>
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> May I ask why this is needed?

Why what is needed, the CYCLE option within a PERFORM.. END-PERFORM loop?
That's there for the reason the PERFORM.. END-PERFORM exists, to offer
complete loop control.

Why you'd want EXIT PERFORM in a PERFORM'd paragraph?  My best guess is that
you'd want this to replace to total obscurity lost when ALTER was dropped.
Supporting this kind of programming is IMNSHO one of the dopiest suggestions
I've ever heard.

MCM
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Infinite Loops and Explicit Exits

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net>
- **Date:** 2004-10-30T11:57:09-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<10o7eij23spll38@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<clu41g$1t2b$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <S7Jgd.3709$K7.2223@news-server.bigpond.net.au>`

```

"Esmond Pitt" <esmond.pitt@not.bigpond.com> wrote in message
news:S7Jgd.3709$K7.2223@news-server.bigpond.net.au...
> Chuck Stevens wrote:
>
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> May I ask why this is needed?

Standard COBOL provides no means, similar to
EXIT PARAGRAPH and EXIT SECTION, to
"short cycle" PERFORM ... THRU without using
a GO TO statement. EXIT PERFORM CYCLE
eliminates those GO TO statements.

The common exception processing feature, added for
2002, is indirect and complex because it uses directives,
declaratives, and exception objects. EXIT PERFORM
provides a means for implementing simple and direct,
localized common exception processing without the
need for directives and declaratives or, for that matter,
additional testing in PERFORM statements.

Following is a pattern for common exception processing
using EXIT PERFORM.

    PERFORM some-process
            UNTIL some-expected-condition
    IF exception-variable NOT = ZERO
        *> common exception handler
    END-IF
    ...

some-process.
    ....
    PERFORM some-other-process
    IF exception-variable NOT = ZERO
        EXIT PERFORM
    END-IF
    ...

some-other-process.
    ...
    IF some-exception-condition
        MOVE some-exception-value TO exception-variable
        EXIT PERFORM
    END-IF
    ...

I would suggest that EXIT PERFORM [CYCLE] for
out-of-line PERFORM is as needful as EXIT PARAGRAPH,
EXIT SECTION, and the common exception processing
feature.
```

```
> Standard COBOL provides no means, similar to
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Infinite Loops and Explicit Exits

_(reply depth: 6)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2004-11-01T08:46:06-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<cm5p8e$10a2$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<clu41g$1t2b$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <S7Jgd.3709$K7.2223@news-server.bigpond.net.au> <10o7eij23spll38@corp.supernews.com> <217e491a.0410302009.c1781e9@posting.google.com> <10o8vebpi1os15d@corp.supernews.com> <FJedne9zZPLmcBncRVn-rw@giganews.com>`

```

"JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com> wrote in message
news:FJedne9zZPLmcBncRVn-rw@giganews.com...
> Rick Smith wrote:
> > "Richard" <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote in message
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
> Nobody uses SECTIONS and PERFORM THRU... is used only by people with
rabies.
>
> This seems like a solution in search of programmers who have a problem,
> which means the solution wouldn't get used anyway.

Not entirely clear, given that Burroughs Large System COBOL(68) had the
widely-used EXIT HERE as an implementor extension, to allow early
termination of a[n out-of-line] PERFORM statement, and that the absence of
that feature in Burroughs Large System COBOL74 and a number of customer
complaints about compatibility and migration led to the addition of EXIT
PERFORM to provide the same functionality back in '87.   Our COBOL74 and
COBOL85 compilers both allow EXIT PERFORM in out-of-line PERFORM as an
extension; neither happens to allow EXIT PERFORM CYCLE,. but I think if the
standard's going to allow one it should allow the other, and I've already
worked out what the object code needs to look like for the Unisys MCP
environment if management decided to add the CYCLE variant.  Since we've got
customers who have been doing EXIT PERFORM from out-of-line PERFORMs for
over thirty years now, I'm not at all convinced that this feature would
suddenly disappear from use if it were made a part of standard COBOL.  EXIT
PERFORM CYCLE is a slightly different matter, but if both EXIT PERFORM and
EXIT PERFORM CYCLE are allowed in one context (in-line PERFORM), and EXIT
PERFORM is a defensible enhancement to out-of-line PERFORM (as I believe it
is), I don't think the CYCLE variant should be prohibited in that context.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Infinite Loops and Explicit Exits

_(reply depth: 5)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2004-10-31T09:49:31-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0410310949.6a916128@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<clu41g$1t2b$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <S7Jgd.3709$K7.2223@news-server.bigpond.net.au> <10o7eij23spll38@corp.supernews.com> <217e491a.0410302009.c1781e9@posting.google.com> <10o8vebpi1os15d@corp.supernews.com>`

```
"Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net> wrote 

> > There are other ways to eliminate the GO TOs.
> 
> Not with multiple paragraphs or sections in PERFORM ...
> THRU.

The only purpose of having labels within a PERFORM range is to cater
for a GO TO. If it is your intent to eliminate the GO TOs then the
first thing to do is to eliminate the labels within the range (and
dump the THRU).

>> >     PERFORM some-other-process
>> >     IF exception-variable NOT = ZERO
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>> >     END-IF

> It won't work in all but the last paragraph of a
> PERFORM ... THRU  or PERFORM section and will
> not cause the PERFORM to terminate without a
> test for the same condition.

It will work perfectly if your intent is to eliminate the GO TO.  All
labels within the scope of the perform are unneccessary, drop the THRU
(and SECTION) and comment out or delete the labels then there is no
impediment to using simple logic that does not require any EXIT
pragraph/section/perform.

Granted, with pre-85 coding it was difficult to structure code without
repeating tests because scope termination could only be done by using
a full stop, but that was 20 years ago.

You do not need EXIT ~ to eliminate GO TO, you need to get rid of the
paragraph labels and THRU/SECTION so GO TO does not fit anywhere and
then you will find that EXIT isn't needed either.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Infinite Loops and Explicit Exits

_(reply depth: 6)_

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net>
- **Date:** 2004-10-31T13:46:11-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<10oacqs81h6tk62@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<clu41g$1t2b$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <S7Jgd.3709$K7.2223@news-server.bigpond.net.au> <10o7eij23spll38@corp.supernews.com> <217e491a.0410302009.c1781e9@posting.google.com> <10o8vebpi1os15d@corp.supernews.com> <217e491a.0410310949.6a916128@posting.google.com>`

```

"Richard" <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote in message
news:217e491a.0410310949.6a916128@posting.google.com...
> "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net> wrote
>
…[34 more quoted lines elided]…
> then you will find that EXIT isn't needed either.

My response had nothing to do with either *my intent* or
*my need*; it had to do with standard COBOL. Standard
COBOL permits PERFORM ... THRU to have many
paragraphs or sections, permits sections to have many
paragraphs, and permits GO TO statements.

I recognize the truth in what you say; but until WG4 takes
an overt step to deprecate PERFORM ... THRU,
multiple paragraph sections, and GO TO; such as by
make these archaic, then what I have said remains valid
for standard COBOL.

When I discuss standard COBOL, my intent is noble;
that is, I try to rise above the petty; but, in my own use
of COBOL, I embrace the petty. Mr Plinston, you are
preaching to the wrong person.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Infinite Loops and Explicit Exits

_(reply depth: 7)_

- **From:** Peter Lacey <lacey@mb.sympatico.ca>
- **Date:** 2004-10-31T14:47:55-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<41854F7B.D2849CDB@mb.sympatico.ca>`
- **References:** `<clu41g$1t2b$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <S7Jgd.3709$K7.2223@news-server.bigpond.net.au> <10o7eij23spll38@corp.supernews.com> <217e491a.0410302009.c1781e9@posting.google.com> <10o8vebpi1os15d@corp.supernews.com> <217e491a.0410310949.6a916128@posting.google.com> <10oacqs81h6tk62@corp.supernews.com>`

```
Rick Smith wrote:
> 

> 
> I recognize the truth in what you say; but until WG4 takes
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> preaching to the wrong person.

When I see posts like this, it seems to me that the purpose is to force
the elimination a style of programming (which is perfectly valid,
productive, and reliable) in favour of one which the author of the post
prefers.  The ostensible reason is to eliminate errors which can be
caused by the misuse of the statements involved.  Why not then eliminate
every verb which can be misused?  Consider the COMPUTE verb: if somebody
accidentally uses a "+" instead of a "-", or enters 3.15 instead of 3.14
in his computation - dreadful possibly even catastrophic results can
occur.  Get rid of the thing!!!

My example is facetious, of course, but my point remains valid.  There
are very few verbs in the Cobol canon which can't be misused.  Seems to
me that the argument ought to be applied everywhere.

The misuse of GOTO's only applies during the debugging and testing
phase, anyway.  It isn't as if a GOTO will suddenly misbehave after
years of laying in wait.  If a situation hasn't been anticipated by the
programmer after the same years and the program misfunctions as a
result, it's a design fault, not the fault of the verb in question.

I agree: Djikstra and his acolytes have done us a dubious service.

PL
```

```
I'm not convinced it's the job of the COBOL standards committee to eliminate
a style of programming.  While one could argue that that's what they've done
with the archaization of NEXT SENTENCE in the 2002 standard, the point there
is that many users (and a few implementors!) have incorrectly understood
NEXT SENTENCE to mean NEXT STATEMENT (or, if you wish, CONTINUE), and that's
not correct.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

```
"Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com> wrote 

>> I see that it facilitates elimination of a style of programming.  
Big
>> difference.

> I'm not convinced it's the job of the COBOL standards committee to eliminate
> a style of programming. 

Many things, such as scope terminators _facilitated_ the elimination
of a style, this is quite different from _mandating_ a change, such as
eliminating ALTER, or having NEXT SENTENCE illegal when combined with
END-IF.
```

```
.    On  01.11.04
  wrote  riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
     on  /COMP/LANG/COBOL
     in  217e491a.0411012238.79aec2c@posting.google.com
  about  Re: Infinite Loops and Explicit Exits

   anwering Chuck Stevens:

CS>> I'm not convinced it's the job of the COBOL standards committee
CS>> to eliminate a style of programming.

r> Many things, such as scope terminators _facilitated_ the elimination
r> of a style, this is quite different from _mandating_ a change, such as
r> eliminating ALTER, or having NEXT SENTENCE illegal when combined with
r> END-IF.

  For ALTER, there was and is a long transition period.

  As to "NEXT SENTENCE" with in a IF/END-IF structure -- it is simply  
misleading to use it there, since it should transfer control to the  
statement following the next period, really confusing what is meant in  
such a structure.

   Imagine the following piece of code which reads a file and does  
something with the records found, provided the fulfill a certain  
condition:

--------- schnipp -----------------------------------------

   READ input-file
   PERFORM UNTIL NOT file-status-OK in filestatus-input-file
     IF condition-1
       IF condition-2
         NEXT SENTENCE
       ELSE
         PERFORM process-the-record
       END-IF
     ELSE
       NEXT SENTENCE
     END-IF
     READ input-file
   END-PERFORM
   .

------------------ schnapp --------------------------------

   This would stop processing as soon as a record is found which  
should not be processed, since the NEXT SENTENCE causes processing to  
proceed with the next statement after the next period (full stop),  
i.e. it would skip to read the next record, and skip out of the  
PERFORM-loop. Since it is quite sure that this is not meant, NEXT  
SENTENCE _must_ be replaced by CONTINUE in such a situation.

   Imagine the ELSE branch of the "IF contition-1" being eliminated --  
the results would be even more unpredictable.

   END-IF is new, and to include NEXT SENTENCE in such a new structure  
should have been specified as a syntax error right from the beginning.  
That is quite something different from saying that people should give  
up on the old ALTER, as they would give up e.g. on segmentation.


Yours,
Lï¿½ko Willms                                     http://www.willms-edv.de
/--------- L.WILLMS@jpberlin.de -- Alle Rechte vorbehalten --

Er urteilt nach dem jedesmaligen Aggregatzustand seiner Empfindungen. -G.C.Lichtenberg
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Infinite Loops and Explicit Exits

_(reply depth: 12)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2004-11-02T18:53:53+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<5JQhd.3773006$ic1.378769@news.easynews.com>`
- **References:** `<clu41g$1t2b$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <cm5q0b$10mk$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <217e491a.0411012238.79aec2c@posting.google.com> <9K6j-cXPflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de>`

```

"Lueko Willms" <l.willms@jpberlin.de> wrote in message 
news:9K6j-cXPflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de...
>.    On  01.11.04
>  wrote  riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
…[20 more quoted lines elided]…
>

NEXT SENTENCE is only misleading to those who don't uderstand that there is (was 
and probably always will be) a difference in COBOL between a "statement" and a 
"sentence".  NEXT SENTENCE (in a conforming compiler) does EXACTLY what it says 
it will do.  For those implementations which match the (old) JOD (where END-IF 
and scope delimiters were first introduced) and DO allow NEXT SENTENCE with 
END-IF and END-SEARCH, there is no confusion and it is used correctly.

Having said this, the Standard has never allowed NEXT SENTENCE with END-IF and 
END-SEARCH - and it suse in other cases was placed into the ARCHAIC (not 
OBSOLETE) category in the 2002 Standard.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Infinite Loops and Explicit Exits

_(reply depth: 13)_

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2004-11-02T19:25:41+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<cm8mvk$h9v$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<clu41g$1t2b$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <cm5q0b$10mk$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <217e491a.0411012238.79aec2c@posting.google.com> <9K6j-cXPflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de> <5JQhd.3773006$ic1.378769@news.easynews.com>`

```

On  2-Nov-2004, "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> wrote:

> NEXT SENTENCE is only misleading to those who don't uderstand that there is
> (was
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> END-IF and END-SEARCH, there is no confusion and it is used correctly.

Before END-IF and CONTINUE, people coded with NEXT SENTENCE.   Habits die hard.

> Having said this, the Standard has never allowed NEXT SENTENCE with END-IF and
>
> END-SEARCH - and it suse in other cases was placed into the ARCHAIC (not
> OBSOLETE) category in the 2002 Standard.

It still works with my IBM mainframe compiler.

The following does what you would expect it to do:
	IF MY-DATE > END-DATE
	OR MY-DATE < START-DATE
		NEXT SENTENCE
	ELSE
		PERFORM FOUND-ONE
	END-IF.

It's illegal according to the standard, but it works.

I do see why the following style of code is undesirable:
	IF MY-DATE > END-DATE
	OR MY-DATE < START-DATE
		IF  X = Y
			NEXT SENTENCE
		ELSE
			PERFORM NOT-Y
		END-IF
		ADD 1  TO X
	ELSE
		PERFORM FOUND-ONE
	END-IF.

Some programmers would get confused.   But this is a style issue.    The reason
it could be made illegal is that that particular code could not have been
written that way before the END-IF command.    Making it illegal did not break
any existing programs because existing programs did not have END-IF.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Infinite Loops and Explicit Exits

_(reply depth: 14)_

- **From:** "Peter E.C Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2004-11-09T20:46:28+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1099986393.VyGzC2pPvZL1GQp0iCuZWw@teranews>`
- **References:** `<clu41g$1t2b$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <cm5q0b$10mk$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <217e491a.0411012238.79aec2c@posting.google.com> <9K6j-cXPflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de> <5JQhd.3773006$ic1.378769@news.easynews.com> <cm8mvk$h9v$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```

"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote in message
news:cm8mvk$h9v$1@peabody.colorado.edu...
<snip>
> It still works with my IBM mainframe compiler.
>
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> I do see why the following style of code is undesirable:

I don't...(see below.)

> IF MY-DATE > END-DATE
> OR MY-DATE < START-DATE
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> Some programmers would get confused.
Is that a top priority when coding? Many programmers would simply read it as
a correctly nested IF...

>  But this is a style issue.

Agreed.

I'd like to elucidate my position here a little further, Howard. In the
following discussion I am not suggesting that this way is the ONLY way or
that no other style is valid; only that this is the way I do it, having been
taught that by a programmer now tracing hex dumps in heaven (That would be
his idea of heaven <G>).

I was taught that there is a special case for using nested IFs and the way
to remove ambiguity and confusion from them is to only nest into the TRUE
branch. In the old days (before scope delimiters) we did it like this:

IF condition 1
     IF condition 2
          IF condition 3
              action 1
          ELSE
              action 2
     ELSE
         action 3
ELSE NEXT SENTENCE. (or full stop after action 3.)

(Note the similarity to your structure above...)

When scope delimiters arrived the above structure remained intact:

IF condition 1
     IF condition 2
          IF condition 3
              action 1
          ELSE
              action 2
          END-IF
     ELSE
         action 3
     END-IF
END-IF

It represents a decision tree  and can easily be drawn as such. I don't
believe any competent COBOL programmer would find this confusing, but I
don't want to argue style, and we agree that's what it is.

Now let's take a look at your example...

>  The reason
> it could be made illegal is that that particular code could not have been
> written that way before the END-IF command.

I have a major problem here, because I believe it COULD have been.

Consider this...

IF MY-DATE > END-DATE
OR MY-DATE < START-DATE
   IF  X = Y
      NEXT SENTENCE
   ELSE
      PERFORM NOT-Y
   END-IF  *> removing this END-IF has NO IMPACT on the logic!
   ADD 1  TO X *> this statement could only ever be executed if condition 1
is TRUE
                          *> and condition 2 is FALSE. That is the case
whether the END-IF is
                          *> present or not. The implicit branch in the NEXT
SENTENCE makes
                          *> these forms logically equivalent.
ELSE
   PERFORM FOUND-ONE
END-IF.  *> I hope we would agree that this could be replaced by either
                *> NEXT SENTENCE. or simply a full stop.

therefore, pre scope delimiters it would look like this:

IF MY-DATE > END-DATE
OR MY-DATE < START-DATE
   IF  X = Y
      NEXT SENTENCE
   ELSE
      PERFORM NOT-Y
      ADD 1  TO X
ELSE
   PERFORM FOUND-ONE.

I contend that this is exactly logically equivalent to the "post" scope
delimiter structure.

Having said that, there is no way I would go back to coding WITHOUT scope
delimiters <G>

Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Infinite Loops and Explicit Exits

_(reply depth: 15)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2004-11-09T08:10:36+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<0Y_jd.4150405$ic1.403613@news.easynews.com>`
- **References:** `<clu41g$1t2b$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <cm5q0b$10mk$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <217e491a.0411012238.79aec2c@posting.google.com> <9K6j-cXPflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de> <5JQhd.3773006$ic1.378769@news.easynews.com> <cm8mvk$h9v$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <1099986393.VyGzC2pPvZL1GQp0iCuZWw@teranews>`

```
In a compiler that treats the following as "valid" (extension for NEXT SENTENCE 
with END-IF)

IF MY-DATE > END-DATE
OR MY-DATE < START-DATE
   IF  X = Y
      NEXT SENTENCE
   ELSE
      PERFORM NOT-Y
   END-IF  *>   removing this END-IF has NO IMPACT on the logic!
                  *> see below
       ADD 1  TO X
ELSE
     whatever
End-IF

there is BIG difference on whether or not the END-IF is code.

When the END-IF is there, then
    When X=Y, the ADD *is* performed

When the END-IF is NOT there, then
     the ADD is ONLY performed when X *not* = Y

***

Was I missing something in your post Pete?
```

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)

```
"William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> wrote 

> In a compiler that treats the following as "valid" (extension for NEXT SENTENCE 
> with END-IF)
 
> IF MY-DATE > END-DATE
> OR MY-DATE < START-DATE
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
>     When X=Y, the ADD *is* performed

That would be true if NEXT SENTENCE has the same action as CONTINUE,
but it doesn't (though this can depend on compiler and options), it
makes a GO to after the next full stop.  Thus the ADD does not get
executed.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Infinite Loops and Explicit Exits

_(reply depth: 16)_

- **From:** l.willms@jpberlin.de (Lueko Willms)
- **Date:** 2004-11-09T18:36:00+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9KZY-xbuflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de>`
- **References:** `<clu41g$1t2b$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <1099986393.VyGzC2pPvZL1GQp0iCuZWw@teranews> <0Y_jd.4150405$ic1.403613@news.easynews.com>`

```
.     Am  09.11.04
 schrieb  wmklein@nospam.netcom.com (William M. Klein)
     bei  /COMP/LANG/COBOL
      in  0Y_jd.4150405$ic1.403613@news.easynews.com
   ueber  Re: Infinite Loops and Explicit Exits

WMK> In a compiler that treats the following as "valid" (extension for
WMK> NEXT SENTENCE with END-IF)
WMK>
WMK> IF MY-DATE > END-DATE
WMK> OR MY-DATE < START-DATE
WMK>    IF  X = Y
WMK>       NEXT SENTENCE
WMK>    ELSE
WMK>       PERFORM NOT-Y
WMK>    END-IF         *>  removing this END-IF
WMK>                   *>  has NO IMPACT on the logic!
WMK>                   *>  see below
WMK>    ADD 1  TO X
WMK> ELSE
WMK>      whatever
WMK> End-IF

  *> let us add some more code here:

     PERFORM action-2
     .
     PERFORM action-3
     .


WMK>
WMK> there is BIG difference on whether or not the END-IF is code.
WMK>
WMK> When the END-IF is there, then
WMK>     When X=Y, the ADD *is* performed
WMK>
WMK> When the END-IF is NOT there, then
WMK>      the ADD is ONLY performed when X *not* = Y

   Besides the error of accepting NEXT SENTENCE where it is illegal,  
that would mean to perform a false action for the NEXT SENTENCE.

   NEXT SENTENCE does transfer the control to the next _sentence,_  
i.e. after the next full stop. So, with or without this first END-IF  
present, the above code would NOT execute ADD 1 TO X if X = Y, since  
it must transfer the control to the sentence following, and this can  
be a long way after the final END-IF.

   I added PERFORM action-2 and PERFORM action-3 above, following the  
final END-IF closing the IF MY-DATE ...

   There is no point (period, full stop) between this END-IF and the  
PEFORM action-2, but here is a point after this and before PERFORM  
action-3.

   So, the DATE-condition is TRUE, and X = Y, neither ADD 1 TO X  
should be performed, nor the PERFORM action-2. The action would have  
to continue with PERFORM action-3, because that is the NEXT SENTENCE  
after that IF/ELSE.

   Good reason why NEXT SENTENCE is illegal between IF and END-IF.

   When really IBM allows that nonsense, they should be castigated for  
confusing their customers.


Yours,
Lï¿½ko Willms                                     http://www.mlwerke.de
/--------- L.WILLMS@jpberlin.de -- Alle Rechte vorbehalten --

  "Nach meiner Ansicht besitzt die Presse _das_ _Recht_,
Schriftsteller, Politiker, Komï¿½dianten und andere ï¿½ffentliche
Charaktere zu _beleidigen_. Achtete ich [so einen Angriff gegen mich]
einer Notiz wert, so galt mir in solchen Fï¿½llen der Wahlspruch: ï¿½
corsaire, corsaire et demi [auf einen Schelmen anderthalben]."
                  - Karl Marx   17.11.1860  (Herr Vogt, Kapitel XI)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Infinite Loops and Explicit Exits

_(reply depth: 16)_

- **From:** "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<2ve66uF2lfahsU1@uni-berlin.de>`
- **References:** `<clu41g$1t2b$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <cm5q0b$10mk$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <217e491a.0411012238.79aec2c@posting.google.com> <9K6j-cXPflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de> <5JQhd.3773006$ic1.378769@news.easynews.com> <cm8mvk$h9v$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <1099986393.VyGzC2pPvZL1GQp0iCuZWw@teranews> <0Y_jd.4150405$ic1.403613@news.easynews.com>`

```
 Hi Bill,

thanks for being polite in what may well have been my error <G>.

I understood that NEXT SENTENCE (a construct I have used in the old days
EXTREMELY rarely - I used to use it with SEARCH and nowhere else. Since
scope delimiters, I can honestly say I have NEVER used it, so my real
experience with it is limited...) did what it says, and transferred control
to the next sentence. You seem to believe it transfers it to the NEXT
STATEMENT and that is not the same thing at all. However, I know you are
seldom wrong in these matters so it is far more likely I got it wrong. If it
worked by transferring control to the next SENTENCE (i.e. after the full
stop) then my post is correct. If it doesn't do that, then I am in error and
I apologise abjectly to the group at large.

A quick comment on the code is below.

Cheers,

Pete.


"William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> wrote in message
news:0Y_jd.4150405$ic1.403613@news.easynews.com...
> In a compiler that treats the following as "valid" (extension for NEXT
SENTENCE
> with END-IF)
>
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
>     When X=Y, the ADD *is* performed

Only if control transfers to the NEXT STATEMENT... If it transfers to the
statement following the full stop (as I believed) then it certainly makes no
difference whether the END-IF is there or not.
>
> When the END-IF is NOT there, then
…[45 more quoted lines elided]…
> > Is that a top priority when coding? Many programmers would simply read
it as
> > a correctly nested IF...
> >
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> > following discussion I am not suggesting that this way is the ONLY way
or
> > that no other style is valid; only that this is the way I do it, having
been
> > taught that by a programmer now tracing hex dumps in heaven (That would
be
> > his idea of heaven <G>).
> >
> > I was taught that there is a special case for using nested IFs and the
way
> > to remove ambiguity and confusion from them is to only nest into the
TRUE
> > branch. In the old days (before scope delimiters) we did it like this:
> >
…[33 more quoted lines elided]…
> >> it could be made illegal is that that particular code could not have
been
> >> written that way before the END-IF command.
> >
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> >   ADD 1  TO X *> this statement could only ever be executed if condition
1
> > is TRUE
> >                          *> and condition 2 is FALSE. That is the case
> > whether the END-IF is
> >                          *> present or not. The implicit branch in the
NEXT
> > SENTENCE makes
> >                          *> these forms logically equivalent.
…[20 more quoted lines elided]…
> > Having said that, there is no way I would go back to coding WITHOUT
scope
> > delimiters <G>
> >
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Infinite Loops and Explicit Exits

_(reply depth: 17)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2004-11-10T08:33:12-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<cmtfsc$20p0$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<clu41g$1t2b$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <cm5q0b$10mk$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <217e491a.0411012238.79aec2c@posting.google.com> <9K6j-cXPflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de> <5JQhd.3773006$ic1.378769@news.easynews.com> <cm8mvk$h9v$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <1099986393.VyGzC2pPvZL1GQp0iCuZWw@teranews> <0Y_jd.4150405$ic1.403613@news.easynews.com> <2ve66uF2lfahsU1@uni-berlin.de>`

```

"Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote in message
news:2ve66uF2lfahsU1@uni-berlin.de...

> I understood that NEXT SENTENCE (a construct I have used in the old days
> EXTREMELY rarely - I used to use it with SEARCH and nowhere else. Since
> scope delimiters, I can honestly say I have NEVER used it, so my real
> experience with it is limited...) did what it says, and transferred
control
> to the next sentence. You seem to believe it transfers it to the NEXT
> STATEMENT and that is not the same thing at all.

I think he's already straightened out his example elsewhere, and I''d be
VERY surprised if Bill were unaware of this text, but for those who might
still be confused, I still think it's important to point this out:

One of the reasons NEXT SENTENCE is deprecated as archaic in 2002 COBOL is
that too many users (and at least one implementor!) ERRONEOUSLY assumed that
it did indeed transfer control to the next STATEMENT ("It is a common belief
among users that control is transferred to a position after the scope
delimiter rather than to a separator period that follows it somewhere" -- 
ISO/IEC 1989:2002 page 833, G.1, Archaic language elements, item 3, NEXT
SENTENCE phrase in the IF and SEARCH statements).

I can think of no case in which a combination of CONTINUE and
scope-delimiters in 2002 COBOL cannot be used to accomplish the same
functionality with greater clarity.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Infinite Loops and Explicit Exits

_(reply depth: 18)_

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2004-11-10T17:05:14+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<cmthoa$3rs$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<clu41g$1t2b$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <cm5q0b$10mk$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <217e491a.0411012238.79aec2c@posting.google.com> <9K6j-cXPflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de> <5JQhd.3773006$ic1.378769@news.easynews.com> <cm8mvk$h9v$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <1099986393.VyGzC2pPvZL1GQp0iCuZWw@teranews> <0Y_jd.4150405$ic1.403613@news.easynews.com> <2ve66uF2lfahsU1@uni-berlin.de> <cmtfsc$20p0$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`

```

On 10-Nov-2004, "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com> wrote:

> I can think of no case in which a combination of CONTINUE and
> scope-delimiters in 2002 COBOL cannot be used to accomplish the same
> functionality with greater clarity.

However there are people who have invented a function of NEXT SENTENCE.  
Because scope-delimiters have eliminated the requirement to use a period for
that task, they only use periods at the end of a paragraph, allowing them to use
NEXT SENTENCE to mean NEXT PARAGRAPH.

I dislike that style very much, and haven't seen it in places where I work.  
But it does exist.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Infinite Loops and Explicit Exits

_(reply depth: 19)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2004-11-10T09:39:38-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<cmtjor$23dn$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<clu41g$1t2b$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <cm5q0b$10mk$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <217e491a.0411012238.79aec2c@posting.google.com> <9K6j-cXPflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de> <5JQhd.3773006$ic1.378769@news.easynews.com> <cm8mvk$h9v$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <1099986393.VyGzC2pPvZL1GQp0iCuZWw@teranews> <0Y_jd.4150405$ic1.403613@news.easynews.com> <2ve66uF2lfahsU1@uni-berlin.de> <cmtfsc$20p0$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <cmthoa$3rs$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```

"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote in message
news:cmthoa$3rs$1@peabody.colorado.edu...
>
> On 10-Nov-2004, "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com> wrote:
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> Because scope-delimiters have eliminated the requirement to use a period
for
> that task, they only use periods at the end of a paragraph, allowing them
to use
> NEXT SENTENCE to mean NEXT PARAGRAPH.
>
> I dislike that style very much, and haven't seen it in places where I
work.
> But it does exist.

A pity, I'd say.

But this illustrates a good reason why the standard says "Archaic elements
are intended to remain in the next revision of standard COBOL.  There is no
plan for the removal of archaic elements; however, should their use in
program inventories become insignificant, they can be considered for
designation as obsolete by future architects of standard COBOL."   The
sooner people realize that NEXT SENTENCE is a "delusion, a mockery and a
snare" and the standards folks have basically put that in writing, the
quicker it'll disappear from the standard; the more people discover New and
Cleverer ways to make their code obscure using the construct, the longer
it'll take to get rid of it!

"Doctor!  Doctor!  It HURTS when I do this!"

    -Chuck Stevens
```

- **In-Reply-To:** `<cmthoa$3rs$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<clu41g$1t2b$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <cm5q0b$10mk$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <217e491a.0411012238.79aec2c@posting.google.com> <9K6j-cXPflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de> <5JQhd.3773006$ic1.378769@news.easynews.com> <cm8mvk$h9v$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <1099986393.VyGzC2pPvZL1GQp0iCuZWw@teranews> <0Y_jd.4150405$ic1.403613@news.easynews.com> <2ve66uF2lfahsU1@uni-berlin.de> <cmtfsc$20p0$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <cmthoa$3rs$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
Howard Brazee wrote:
> On 10-Nov-2004, "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com> wrote:
> 
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> But it does exist.

No, in fact it means EXIT PARAGRAPH in that case. It has nothing to do 
with a NEXT paragraph, and would work the same in the last paragraph of 
the code. In fact, if the paragraph is being performed multiply times, 
it will mean EXIT CYCLE.

The period has been, and remains the most important scope delimiter in 
the language, however you look at it. I find it amusing that in the 
great rush to politically correct code, the exit from that particular 
scope of statements is being marked archaic (which is why I suggested an 
EXIT SENTENCE that means exactly what NEXT SENTENCE was always suppose 
to mean).

I am currently maintaining a mess of about 2 million lines of code with 
about 10 conflicting styles in it. The single biggest problem with it is 
huge run-on sentences, loaded with inline performs, scope delimiters by 
the dozens, that go on for 15 pages. Stuff like
   IF CUSTOMER-five
	300 lines of copied code
   end-if
   if customer-USA
	2000 lines of code
   end-if
   if BRITAIN
	900 lines of code
   ELSE
	PERFORM VERSION-ONE-CODE.
   .


Frankly, I will take a program organized into meaningfull paragraphs and 
meaningfull sentences that do one thing at a time any day of the week. 
Done right, it does not *NEED* all those scope delimiters.

Unfortunately, TOPDOWN has come to mean "GO-TO-LESS". It originally 
meant start with a summation, and break it down into smaller and smaller 
parts until each part is simple.

STRUCTURED means something a lot closer to "go-to-less", but *Top-down 
Structure* started out as a method of FORCING a programmer to write 
top-down code. It has degenerated to the point where horrific code that 
must be cut and pasted has neccessitated extra grammar so programmers 
can follow the stated rules while enjoying the benefit of being able to 
write anything in one huge monolithic statment with the only structure 
at all being historical precident and programmer's name. (Damn, it's 
even starting to creep into my posts.)

Donald (my rant for the day)
;<)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Infinite Loops and Explicit Exits

_(reply depth: 16)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2004-11-09T11:14:19-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0411091114.67413cf5@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<clu41g$1t2b$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <cm5q0b$10mk$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <217e491a.0411012238.79aec2c@posting.google.com> <9K6j-cXPflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de> <5JQhd.3773006$ic1.378769@news.easynews.com> <cm8mvk$h9v$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <1099986393.VyGzC2pPvZL1GQp0iCuZWw@teranews> <cmqm8r$520$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote 

> > IF MY-DATE > END-DATE
> > OR MY-DATE < START-DATE
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
> >                 *> NEXT SENTENCE. or simply a full stop.
 
> It makes a significant difference whether that END-IF is there.   Without the
> END-IF, the addition is only performed when X=Y.
  
     I do hope that you meant to write X NOT = Y, I will assume that.

I think that you have justified the standard in making this illegal. 
PECD is correct for what his compiler usually does with NEXT SENTENCE.
 However it is possible to get the behaviour you describe (ie there is
a difference) in MF with OLDNEXTSENTENCE (ie pre'85 JOD behaviour).

In those IFs with the END-IFs, there is also a difference between NEXT
SENTENCE and CONTINUE, and the effects will vary depending on whether
the full stop is after the last END-IF or at some other place.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ OLDNEXTSENTENCE (was: Infinite Loops and Explicit Exits

_(reply depth: 17)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2004-11-09T20:26:58+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<mK9kd.2670829$yk.421861@news.easynews.com>`
- **References:** `<clu41g$1t2b$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <cm5q0b$10mk$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <217e491a.0411012238.79aec2c@posting.google.com> <9K6j-cXPflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de> <5JQhd.3773006$ic1.378769@news.easynews.com> <cm8mvk$h9v$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <1099986393.VyGzC2pPvZL1GQp0iCuZWw@teranews> <cmqm8r$520$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <217e491a.0411091114.67413cf5@posting.google.com>`

```
Concerning the statement:

"I think that you have justified the standard in making this illegal.
PECD is correct for what his compiler usually does with NEXT SENTENCE.
 However it is possible to get the behaviour you describe (ie there is
a difference) in MF with OLDNEXTSENTENCE (ie pre'85 JOD behaviour)."

Having been the J4 representative for Micro Focus when the "OLDNEXTSENTENCE" 
directive was added, let me tell you simply that the ORIGINAL behavior was 
simply a mistake.  (And the developer admitted it).  Unfortunately, it had gone 
out to users with this (incorrect) behavior.

I believe that NOoldnextsentence is the default and has been for over a decade 
in every MF product.

However, you are correct that this "mistake" (and Micro Focus was not the only 
vendor to make it) was why the ANSI Standard never allowed the syntax.

Finally, the JOD *never* specified that "NEXT SENTENCE" would work like a 
CONTINUE.  Only vendors making an implementation error and programmers who don't 
understand the difference between a COBOL sentence and a COBOL statement have 
made that mistake.
```

```
> 
…[26 more quoted lines elided]…
> END-IF, the addition is only performed when X=Y.

Congratulations - you (and Bill before you) just showed why this was 
prohibited.  Two bright COBOL professionals - misled by combining "Next 
Sentence" with "End-If"!  The "next sentence" isn't at the End-If, it's 
all the way at the bottom of the example.  :)
```

```
If condition-is-true
     If condition-is-true
         next sentence
     else
         blah blah
else
     blah blah
end-if

That will compile cleanly (and, as an aside, actually proceed to the 
"next sentence", wherever that may be - but, it's *way* too easily 
broken in maintenance to think about putting that sort of structure into 
production code....).
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Infinite Loops and Explicit Exits

_(reply depth: 21)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2004-11-15T20:45:56+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8A8md.4533314$ic1.432104@news.easynews.com>`
- **References:** `<clu41g$1t2b$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <cm5q0b$10mk$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <217e491a.0411012238.79aec2c@posting.google.com> <9K6j-cXPflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de> <5JQhd.3773006$ic1.378769@news.easynews.com> <cm8mvk$h9v$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <1099986393.VyGzC2pPvZL1GQp0iCuZWw@teranews> <cmqm8r$520$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3scld.894$aW.757@fe40.usenetserver.com> <cnag2q$er7$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <O27md.2115$aW.904@fe40.usenetserver.com> <1v7md.4530469$ic1.431925@news.easynews.com> <cnb13n$qo9$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
Howard,
  You are correct.  The "inner" IF (with the NEXT SENTENCE) must be terminated 
by the ELSE of an outside IF - and not by an END-IF (hence, the rule that the 
NEXT SENTENCE phrase and END-IF phrase are not in the SAME *if* statement).  The 
inner IF, (usually)  uses a CONTINUE for the other (never reached) branch.  The 
END-IF is a phrase of the outermost IF (without any NEXT SENTENCE) and is, 
therefore, conforming (but "ugly" <G>)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Infinite Loops and Explicit Exits

_(reply depth: 14)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2004-11-09T10:19:33-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0411091019.6b451868@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<clu41g$1t2b$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <cm5q0b$10mk$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <217e491a.0411012238.79aec2c@posting.google.com> <9K6j-cXPflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de> <5JQhd.3773006$ic1.378769@news.easynews.com> <cm8mvk$h9v$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote 

> It still works with my IBM mainframe compiler.
 
> The following does what you would expect it to do:
> 	IF MY-DATE > END-DATE
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> 	END-IF.

That only works 'as expected' because there is a full stop after the
END-IF. The point about END-IF is that it removes the need to end the
scope with a full stop, this caters for having IF ... END-IF as an
imperitive statement rather than having IF .. ELSE .. as a conditional
statement with a dangling scope.

Imperitive statements can be used anywhere a simple statemnet can be. 
In many cases these cannot be terminated by a full stop.

> It's illegal according to the standard, but it works.
> 
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> 	END-IF.
 
> Some programmers would get confused.

The IF X = Y ... END-IF is an imperitive statement.  Thus one would
expect the next imperitive statement (ADD 1) to be executed next
regardless of the condition.  Without the END-IF it would be a
conditional statement terminated by scope with the next ELSE.

> But this is a style issue.    

No.  It is not just style, it is a fundemental issue of structure. 
Where you are using it is just in nested IFs where the scope is
terminated by counting ELSEs and the full stop.  As long as the full
stop remains then the action is merely confusing.

But the full stop is not otherwise needed, it doesn't end the scope. 
The IF MY-Date .. END-IF is an imperitive statement and may be put
anywhere that any simple statement may be, but _without_ the full
stop.

       PERFORM
           UNTIL something
 
           <get my date>
           IF MY-Date ...
               IF X = Y
                   NEXT SENTENCE
               ELSE
               ...
               END-IF
               ...
            END-IF
            <do something else>
       END-IF
 
Now this is not just 'style' it is _wrong_.  For IF ... END-IF to
operate correctly as an imperitive the NEXT SENTENCE must not exist
anywhere within that  scope.

And, of course, merely removing the full stop from your example
changes the action of the code.

> The reason
> it could be made illegal is that that particular code could not have been
> written that way before the END-IF command.    Making it illegal did not break
> any existing programs because existing programs did not have END-IF.

That is true.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Infinite Loops and Explicit Exits

_(reply depth: 15)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2004-11-03T22:34:25+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<R1did.2333765$yk.377523@news.easynews.com>`
- **References:** `<clu41g$1t2b$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <cm5q0b$10mk$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <217e491a.0411012238.79aec2c@posting.google.com> <9K6j-cXPflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de> <217e491a.0411022209.45e15981@posting.google.com> <Kdbid.4425209$6p.728283@news.easynews.com> <cmbfme$1rmn$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`

```
An INVALID "extension" is one that VIOLATES the rules of Standard COBOL.

For example,

If an '85 Standard (*NOT* '02 Standard) compiler produces code that fails to
continue after a divide-by-zero when no ON SIZE ERROR is coded, that is an
INVALID extension (compare the interpretation request on this - and the
substantive change in the '02 Standard).

I use this example, as a no of one (or more) compilers that CLAIMED to be
conforming that had this (sometimes documented) extension.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Infinite Loops and Explicit Exits

_(reply depth: 12)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2004-11-03T09:04:13-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<cmb32d$1j1b$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<clu41g$1t2b$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <cm5q0b$10mk$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <217e491a.0411012238.79aec2c@posting.google.com> <9K6j-cXPflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de>`

```

"Lueko Willms" <l.willms@jpberlin.de> wrote in message
news:9K6j-cXPflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de...
> .    On  01.11.04
>   wrote  riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
>   For ALTER, there was and is a long transition period.

I'm not sure about "is" as far as the standard goes; it's not in ISO/IEC
1989:2002, but I agree that it did take a while.  Part of the reason it was
deprecated was that it was a "widget" on GO TO to begin with.

NEXT SENTENCE has always been illegal in a scope-terminated IF (ANSI
X3.23-1985 page VI-90, 6.16.3, The IF Statement, syntax rule 3.)

NEXT SENTENCE is only defined in standard COBOL in the contexts of IF and
SEARCH to begin with.  Its deprecation in those contexts (which is
essentially equivalent to its deprecation everywhere) is the result of the
MISconception, for example, that NEXT SENTENCE within a SEARCH that is in
turn within a scope-terminated IF will proceed to the next STATEMENT within
the IF rather than to the first SENTENCE after the next period.  That
reasoning is explicitly spelled out in ISO/IEC 1989:2002 page 833, Annex G,
Section G.1, Archaic language elements, item 3 (q.v.).

For something to be archaized, it looks to me like two factors must be
present:  (1) the construct represents a problem in context; and (2) a
construct exists that provides the desired functionality, with fewer or no
ambiguities, in the same context.  While the stipulation of the second
factor for ALTER in ANSI X3.23-1985 strikes me as weak, that for NEXT
SENTENCE in the 2002 standard does not.  CONTINUE works like NEXT SENTENCE
is MISunderstood to work, and the full complement of scope delimiters can be
used to cover the cases in which it does what the standard says it's
supposed to do.   And in any case "there is no plan for the removal of
archaic elements ... "

> As to "NEXT SENTENCE" with in a IF/END-IF structure -- it is simply
> misleading to use it there,  ...

No, it's a *syntax error* to use it there, unless it's actually in something
like a SEARCH that's embedded inside the IF/END-IF.


>    END-IF is new, and to include NEXT SENTENCE in such a new structure
> should have been specified as a syntax error right from the beginning.

Just out of curiosity, in what way do ANSI X3.23-1985 page VI-60 paragraph
6.16.3 Syntax Rule 3, as well as the distinction between the syntax diagrams
for Format 1 (delimited) and Format 2 (historic) IF statements in ISO/IEC
1989:2002 page 455, 14.8.18, IF statement, fail to accomplish that?

    -Chuck Stevens
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Infinite Loops and Explicit Exits

_(reply depth: 9)_

- **From:** LX-i <lxi0007@netscape.net>
- **Date:** 2004-11-03T17:27:17-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<SPdid.2477$9W1.1855@fe40.usenetserver.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<cm5hhd$8pa$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<clu41g$1t2b$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <S7Jgd.3709$K7.2223@news-server.bigpond.net.au> <10o7eij23spll38@corp.supernews.com> <217e491a.0410302009.c1781e9@posting.google.com> <10o8vebpi1os15d@corp.supernews.com> <217e491a.0410310949.6a916128@posting.google.com> <10oacqs81h6tk62@corp.supernews.com> <41854F7B.D2849CDB@mb.sympatico.ca> <cm5hhd$8pa$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
Howard Brazee wrote:
> On 31-Oct-2004, Peter Lacey <lacey@mb.sympatico.ca> wrote:
> 
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> difference.

While Mr. Lacey and I have had differences of opinion here before, I 
have to agree with him here.  I've created 5 "endless-loop" programs for 
this project, and a "Go to" with a well-named paragraph is completely 
clear, and unlikely to be broken during maintenance.

theBeginning.
     [one-time stuff like version date moves]
     .
theTop.
     [driver for doing stuff]
[assorted other paragraphs omitted]
     .
theEnd.
     [reinit statements]
     go to theTop
     .

Whenever the program is ready to exit, it can "Go to theEnd".  When the 
end is reached, instead of a "stop run" it goes back to the top.  The 
equivalent would be, as discussed, a switch that indicates when the loop 
should end.

01  pic X value "N".
     88  keepGoing  value "N".
     88  timeToGo   value "Y".

perform until timeToGo
     [statements]
     if exit-condition-met
          set timeToGo to true
     end-if
end-perform

If the exit condition is never met, the second loop is an infinite loop. 
  There are times when "Go to" is completely acceptable because it most 
accurately reflects what the program is doing (especially in the case of 
an endless loop).
```

```
> On  3-Nov-2004, LX-i <lxi0007@netscape.net> wrote:

I didn't address that part of his argument.

> If this extension is
> added, your code will still work perfectly well - and nobody's proposing to make
> it illegal.

Right.  What I was more-or-less defending was "Go to" as a legitimate 
structuring tool.  Yes, Perform Thru bugs me, and I still think that 
exit paragraphs serve to make maintenance more complicated.  But, I was 
showing that "Go to" can be used, and be completely understood, in cases 
where a "Perform Forever" type structure is needed.

It's been demonstrated here how several constructs can be misused - Go 
to is but one of them.  :)
```

```
PL> I agree: Djikstra and his acolytes have done us a dubious service.

   I guess you mean Dijkstra -- he was Dutch (or Frisian by his name),  
not Yugoslav.

   But he was right to wage a campaign against GOTO (btw, the title  
"GOTO statement considered harmful" is not his formulation, but of  
Niklaus Wirth, who happened to be the editor of the ACM Communications  
at that time), and I hope he is not right with his other statement:

be regarded as a criminal offence" which he wrote in "How do we tell  
truths that might hurt?" (
  http://www.cs.utexas.edu/users/EWD/transcriptions/EWD04xx/ 
EWD498.html
  )

   That was written in 1975, when it was not possible to write a  
proper loop in COBOL.

   BTW, Dijkstra reported quite positively about a presentation of  
Jackson's Structured Programming - you remember, the man who made  
proposals on how to do structured programming with COBOL-74.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Infinite Loops and Explicit Exits

_(reply depth: 11)_

- **From:** Jeff York <ralf4@btinternet.com>
- **Date:** 2004-11-02T22:53:58+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<j93go01u10i1e5hprm4fominlg6qgvli2l@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<clu41g$1t2b$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <9K6g4pOuflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de> <1qffo0h3kmfntothb7662fv6hfqu7oet0c@4ax.com> <9K6kt7VeflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de>`

```
l.willms@jpberlin.de (Lueko Willms) wrote:

>.     Am  02.11.04
> schrieb  ralf4@btinternet.com (Jeff York)
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>Sandra is her first name.

Olde Englishe epithet..  :-)   Derived, I believe, from the diminutive
suffix "ock" - eg, hillock, small hill, appended to the old word
"boll" which was "ball"..  Result "small balls" - I leave it to your
imagination to decide which balls were those in question. :-)

However, I've never been one to worship at the alter of Dijkstra -
I've always considered him not much more than a second-rate academic
who found that being controversial was a good carreer move.

>JY> Bad structure is caused by bad structuring -
>JY> not the language it's written in or the verbs used.
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>   That's how I see the situation...

This is where we disagree.  It appears that "structured programming"
equates to "go to-less" programming, which, I feel is a step toward
Messianic fervour that I'm not willing to take.  Over the 35 or so
years that I've been programming I've seen work that uses GO TO and is
the most apalling spaghetti, but equally I've seen code that would be
"defined" as "structured" - not a GO TO or PERFORM THRU in sight that
is utterly impenetrable and a nightmare to maintain. On the obverse,
I've seen beautifully crafted code in "structured" form, but just as
much in "non-structured".

Removing a couple of verbs, and adding a few in-line operations
doesn't make a pigs ear into a silk purse.  Good, clear, working code
can be written in different styles, none of which is, IMHO, either the
"right" or "wrong" way to do it.

But I'm old..  And still write quite a lot of assembler..  So what do
I know..  :-)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Infinite Loops and Explicit Exits

_(reply depth: 12)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2004-11-02T15:22:05-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<cm94qt$7qd$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<clu41g$1t2b$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <9K6g4pOuflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de> <1qffo0h3kmfntothb7662fv6hfqu7oet0c@4ax.com> <9K6kt7VeflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de> <j93go01u10i1e5hprm4fominlg6qgvli2l@4ax.com>`

```
"Jeff York" <ralf4@btinternet.com> wrote in message
news:j93go01u10i1e5hprm4fominlg6qgvli2l@4ax.com...
> l.willms@jpberlin.de (Lueko Willms) wrote:
>
…[41 more quoted lines elided]…
> much in "non-structured".

The most "purely Dijkstra" language I've run into was the Burroughs B1000
system software development language called SDL, and its user-available
variant UPL.  The language itself didn't have a GO TO at all, and only had
one "loop control" verb -- DO FOREVER -- with only one mechanism for exiting
it -- UNDO.  As I understand matters, it was developed shortly before
Dijkstra's tenure as a "Burroughs Fellow" (1973 - 1984), and I know of no
other language that comes anything like as close to the principles he
espoused.

That having been said, the language had "labelled DO" -- as in "DO SOMETHING
FOREVER", and the ability to "IF <condition> THEN UNDO SOMETHING",
regardless of whether SOMETHING was the "innermost" active DO FOREVER at the
time.

Many academicians and Dijkstra devotees pointed out "What do you mean SDL
doesn't have a GO TO!  What the heck do you think UNDO <DO-label> is?!?!?"
at the time.

I don't recall having heard that Dijkstra stomped out of the Halls of
Burroughs and resigned his fellowship over the issue of allowing UNDO
<label> in a language that purported to conform to his principles and his
wishes.

In fact, given that the first rumblings of No Further Development of the
Burroughs B1000 -- the only system that supported this language -- were
starting to circulate right around the time he left Burroughs, there's some
circumstantial evidence to the contrary, I'd say!   It's unfortunate he's
not around to ask about this!

    -Chuck Stevens
```

```
statement following the end of the outermost active DO in the procedure you
were in ...
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Infinite Loops and Explicit Exits

_(reply depth: 9)_

- **From:** Peter Lacey <lacey@mb.sympatico.ca>
- **Date:** 2004-11-03T12:26:35-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<418922DB.B84C389F@mb.sympatico.ca>`
- **References:** `<clu41g$1t2b$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <10oacqs81h6tk62@corp.supernews.com> <41854F7B.D2849CDB@mb.sympatico.ca> <9K6g4pOuflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de>`

```
Lueko Willms wrote:

> 
> PL> The misuse of GOTO's only applies during the debugging and testing
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>    That starts with coding it in the first place.

Absolute hogwash and closed-mind nonsense.

Have you written programs using both styles for any significant amount
of time?
> 
> PL> I agree: Djikstra and his acolytes have done us a dubious service.
> 
>    I guess you mean Dijkstra -- he was Dutch (or Frisian by his name),
> not Yugoslav.

I have spelled his name wrong.  Doesn't alter the sentiment.


> 
>    But he was right to wage a campaign against GOTO (btw, the title
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> truths that might hurt?"

Dijkstra said this?  Then why are you writing in COBOL?  Be consistent.

Whether or not you like the idea, it's a fact that programs using GOTO's
are in every way as "good" as those written without them as long as they
BOTH are properly written.  (I said this once before and somebody more
or less stated that "properly written" is an undefinable concept and
that therefore I didn't have a case).  People who don't like GOTO's cite
all sorts of horrendous possibilities as proof of their case.  However,
if a program is PROPERLY written these never turn up.  As somebody else
said recently, programs using GOTO's have been functioning for decades
with no untoward effects.

You might consider this:  all the contructs you use to avoid GOTO's are
goto's in disguise.  The EXIT verbs that have been discussed in this
thread are GOTO's, no more, no less.  In-line PERFORMs are GOTO's with a
condition, at the top or the bottom of the loop.  Out-of-line PERFORMS
are GOTO's with a save-next-address function going out, and a GOTO the
"saved-next-address" coming back.  It is not possible to write a
non-trivial program without using transfers of control.  Every one of
these can be implemented with a GOTO.  In fact, it's only because the
PERFORM can be logically "dconstructed" that Dijkstra's fiat works at
all.

Having read these messages over and over, it still seems to me that the
GOTO-challenged see the situation as personal, and want to change the
world to suit themselves.  So far as I'm concerned, it's a non-issue. 
Either style works, very well.  Nothing more needs to be said.  (And as
I've said before, I've used both styles).

PL
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Infinite Loops and Explicit Exits

_(reply depth: 9)_

- **From:** Peter Lacey <lacey@mb.sympatico.ca>
- **Date:** 2004-11-03T13:53:19-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<4189372F.364D73DE@mb.sympatico.ca>`
- **References:** `<clu41g$1t2b$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <10oacqs81h6tk62@corp.supernews.com> <41854F7B.D2849CDB@mb.sympatico.ca> <9K6g4pOuflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de>`

```
Lueko Willms wrote:
> 
> .    On  31.10.04
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> 


Your attitude annoys me enough to challenge you.

I offer as examples two systems I've written.  Both use GOTO's and I'm
not ashamed of the fact!

1: a time-and-attendance reporting system for a warehousing company:
interfacing with swipe-barcode clocks.  45 programs, ~65k lines.  Not
especially large.  the system has now been running since 1996 without
problems.  It ran through Y2K with NO changes needed.  The only
modifications required have been spec changes: a new data format from
the clocks, and a requirement for running the system at another
location; a couple of other things.

2: a toolcrib system for the car shop at the local Canadian National
Railways yard.  About the same size.  Implemented in 1995; maintenance
taken over by somebody there who wasn't even a programmer.  The system
was superseded by an SAP implementation.

I don't know how you expect programs with GOTO's in them to behave in
production.  Note that in both these cases the problems I was called in
about were always data-related: usually because the clocks
malfunctioned; I never had to change the programs as a result.  Note
that once debugged the programs ran perfectly.  Note that the fellow
that took over the toolcrib system never needed to call me to explain
the code.  And I never had any trouble figuring out how the programs
worked when I had to do mods.

Lueko: how could this possibly be improved on?  The programs have worked
properly all the time.  The only conundrums I had during development
were due to failures to communicate, not failures to design.  The only
really difficult program was the edit, and that was only because the
clock-data format was so peculiar and convoluted.  While I might be
expected to understand my own programs easily for maintenance purposes,
obviously the guy at CN had no problems with them.  So: how would it
have benefited me to use a goto-less style?

I date say I'm not unique in this.  PROPERLY-written programs are always
excellent, regardless of the style.

PL
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Infinite Loops and Explicit Exits

_(reply depth: 10)_

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2004-11-03T20:12:18+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<cmbe32$squ$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<clu41g$1t2b$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <10oacqs81h6tk62@corp.supernews.com> <41854F7B.D2849CDB@mb.sympatico.ca> <9K6g4pOuflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de> <4189372F.364D73DE@mb.sympatico.ca>`

```

On  3-Nov-2004, Peter Lacey <lacey@mb.sympatico.ca> wrote:

> I date say I'm not unique in this.  PROPERLY-written programs are always
> excellent, regardless of the style.

It is easier to get new programmers to produce PROPERLY-written code when they
use structured programming than otherwise.  It is easier to find bad examples of
code from people who don't use structured code than otherwise.

It's possible that this may be because those who pick a particular style of code
(especially one touted by "experts"), is more likely to put thought into his
work and do a good job.

But standards aren't selected for people who always write PROPERLY-written
programs.   A particular programming style is designed to raise the lowest
common denominator of programming.   I'd rather maintain a poorly written
structured program than a poorly written spaghetti program.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Infinite Loops and Explicit Exits

_(reply depth: 10)_

- **From:** l.willms@jpberlin.de (Lueko Willms)
- **Date:** 2004-11-06T23:06:00+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9KR5JP99flB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de>`
- **References:** `<clu41g$1t2b$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <9K6g4pOuflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de> <4189372F.364D73DE@mb.sympatico.ca>`

```
.    On  03.11.04
  wrote  lacey@mb.sympatico.ca (Peter Lacey)
     on  /COMP/LANG/COBOL
     in  4189372F.364D73DE@mb.sympatico.ca
  about  Re: Infinite Loops and Explicit Exits


PL> I offer as examples two systems I've written.  Both use GOTO's and
PL> I'm not ashamed of the fact!

  I have even programmed in assembler (BAL, i.e. byte machine  
assembler) and script languages which could transfer control only via  
GOTO, and my first programming experience has been on a Wang 700B  
which was like a programmable calculator by todays standards, but big  
like an office typewriter.

  The programming language of the database system I mainly work with  
today does not have a GOTO, and nobody misses it. The same is true for  
languages like SYMSTREAM (SSG, Skeleton) or WFL, and others.

  GOTO is something for machine code which the compiler produces, but  
not for specifying a transformation algorithm (transforming one set of  
symbolic representations into another set).


PL> 1: a time-and-attendance reporting system for a warehousing company:
PL> interfacing with swipe-barcode clocks.  45 programs, ~65k lines.


PL> 2: a toolcrib system for the car shop at the local Canadian National
PL> Railways yard.  About the same size.  Implemented in 1995;
PL> maintenance taken over by somebody there who wasn't even a
PL> programmer.  The system was superseded by an SAP implementation.


PL> Lueko: how could this possibly be improved on?

   The size of the source code could have been reduced to at most 33  
thousand lines, I venture to say, and the time you spent for building  
those systems reduced by a similar dimension, doubling your confidence  
in the code.

   Why? Because it is easier and safer to draw a map instead of  
describing trails (when you are here, you go right or left, and then  
.... ).

   That's why we don't program in assembler, but in a so-called higher  
level language, to free our mind from those details.

PL> The programs have worked properly all the time.

   I don't doubt it.

PL> I date say I'm not unique in this.  PROPERLY-written programs are
PL> always excellent, regardless of the style.

   Sure, but modern COBOL, i.e. one which implements the 1985  
standard, makes properly written programs easy, and I can't understand  
why anybody rejects that. For programs written in the 1990es, as the  
two examples above, there was not the slightest need to do awkward  
turns in a COBOL program.

   But this is one of the effects of using COBOL which Edsger Dijkstra  
pointed to in his 1975 provocative statement; and remember, I noted  
explicitly that Dijkstra wrote that in 1975, the year after the 1975  
COBOL standard failed to include statements for proper structuring of  
program flow in the standard, I might dare to say. This has changed  
with the 1985 standard, which should have been greeted as a liberation  
from the former straitjacket. I find it just amazing that the  
assembler straitjacket as turned into a second nature for so many  
programmers.

   And why I do use COBOL? Well, rather I _did_ use COBOL, and mostly  
with compilers conforming to the 1974 standard, and I did it for a  
living. There was not alternative to it. In 1994 I did one more year  
in COBOL, but this with a COBOL-85 conforming compiler, and embedded  
SQL, i.e. an RDMS instead of a CODASYL database as before.

   Currently I am exploring my old acquaintance COBOL, and evaluate it  
to see if I could add some COBOL programs -- especially using the  
REPORT WRITER -- to my database applications.

   What I like about COBOL is the strict separation of data  
declarations and procedural instructions, the means to put a very  
large part of the program specification into the data declaration  
(instead of putting formatting parameters into a "printf" function  
call in C), which is topped by the REPORT WRITER as a complete program  
generator.


Yours,
Lï¿½ko Willms                                     http://www.willms-edv.de
/--------- L.WILLMS@jpberlin.de -- Alle Rechte vorbehalten --

Als er am Kirchhofe vorbei ging, sagte er: Die da kï¿½nnen nun sicher sein, daï¿½ sie nicht mehr gehï¿½ngt werden, das kï¿½nnen wir nicht. -G.C.Lichtenberg
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Infinite Loops and Explicit Exits

_(reply depth: 7)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2004-10-31T17:52:43-08:00
