- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0410311752.141b49be@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<clu41g$1t2b$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <S7Jgd.3709$K7.2223@news-server.bigpond.net.au> <10o7eij23spll38@corp.supernews.com> <217e491a.0410302009.c1781e9@posting.google.com> <10o8vebpi1os15d@corp.supernews.com> <217e491a.0410310949.6a916128@posting.google.com> <10oacqs81h6tk62@corp.supernews.com>`

```
"Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net> wrote 

> My response had nothing to do with either *my intent* or
> *my need*; 

It did appear that your argument in support of EXIT PERFORM CYCLE was
intended to eliminate GO TO when you posted:

RS>>> EXIT PERFORM CYCLE eliminates those GO TO statements.

> it had to do with standard COBOL. Standard
> COBOL permits PERFORM ... THRU to have many
> paragraphs or sections, permits sections to have many
> paragraphs, and permits GO TO statements.

Cobol also supports the use of completely spagetti code entirely based
on GO TO, this does not mean that enhancements should be added to the
language to promote this use.

My argument against the introduction of out-of-line EXIT PERFORM CYCLE
is that it is not required.
 
> I recognize the truth in what you say; but until WG4 takes
> an overt step to deprecate PERFORM ... THRU,
> multiple paragraph sections, and GO TO; such as by
> make these archaic, then what I have said remains valid
> for standard COBOL.

RS>>> EXIT PERFORM CYCLE eliminates those GO TO statements.

RS>> It won't work in all but the last paragraph of a
RS>> PERFORM ... THRU  or PERFORM section and will
RS>> not cause the PERFORM to terminate without a
RS>> test for the same condition.

What I see here is a conflict in your argument between wanting a
mechanism to "eliminate the GO TOs" and yet retain the structure that
is there merely to support their use.
 
> When I discuss standard COBOL, my intent is noble;
> that is, I try to rise above the petty; but, in my own use
> of COBOL, I embrace the petty. Mr Plinston, you are
> preaching to the wrong person.

I am not 'preaching' at all, simply providing counter arguments to
your assertions.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Infinite Loops and Explicit Exits

_(reply depth: 8)_

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net>
- **Date:** 2004-11-01T01:08:05-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<10obkpnmsnk32e8@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<clu41g$1t2b$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <S7Jgd.3709$K7.2223@news-server.bigpond.net.au> <10o7eij23spll38@corp.supernews.com> <217e491a.0410302009.c1781e9@posting.google.com> <10o8vebpi1os15d@corp.supernews.com> <217e491a.0410310949.6a916128@posting.google.com> <10oacqs81h6tk62@corp.supernews.com> <217e491a.0410311752.141b49be@posting.google.com>`

```

"Richard" <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote in message
news:217e491a.0410311752.141b49be@posting.google.com...
> "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net> wrote
>
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> RS>>> EXIT PERFORM CYCLE eliminates those GO TO statements.

Not to eliminate GO TO; but only *those* GO TO statements
that transfer control to the last paragraph (of the last section) of
a PERFORM ... THRU range.

> > it had to do with standard COBOL. Standard
> > COBOL permits PERFORM ... THRU to have many
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> language to promote this use.

Every use I can think of would replace a GO TO statement
with EXIT PERFORM CYCLE; thus it does not enhance
the use of GO TO nor does it have any effect on what some
consider to be "spaghetti" code.

> My argument against the introduction of out-of-line EXIT PERFORM CYCLE
> is that it is not required.

I understood that but the solution you propose is for all
programmers to perform single paragraphs only, indent
code under a negated condition, etc.; in effect, to change
the way they have been programming for years.

Out-of-line EXIT PERFORM CYCLE is useful only for
those who use PERFORM ... THRU; since you do not
use this style it has no effect on how you program.

> > I recognize the truth in what you say; but until WG4 takes
> > an overt step to deprecate PERFORM ... THRU,
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
> is there merely to support their use.

Again, not to eliminate GO TO; but only *those* GO TO
statements that transfer control to the last paragraph (of the
last section) of a PERFORM ... THRU range.

It is possible, as I have mentioned before with US tax forms,
that the structure of a COBOL procedure may support the
simple and direct implementation of a written procedure. It
just happens to be that such an application would use
GO TO statements. You argument fails to take that into
account. It is not there merely to support the use of GO TO
statements but to support a specific type of application.

> > When I discuss standard COBOL, my intent is noble;
> > that is, I try to rise above the petty; but, in my own use
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> your assertions.

How is your opposition to be measured, if not in the terms
of your counter arguments. It has no obvious effect on how
you program and your insistence that it is not needed is
based on how others could (re)write their programs. How
is it not preaching, "to advocate earnestly" (www.m-w.com)
a particular style of programming, especially one in
opposition to a style that would benefit from the change.
Mr Plinston, you are not countering my assertions; but
objecting to the use of PERFORM ... THRU as a style of
programming.

Appropriate contexts for opposition may be technical
infeasibility or a survey showing that so few would benefit
from the change that imposing the cost on implementors is
an unreasonable burden. But, these are issues the
implementors may raise after reviewing the proposal.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Infinite Loops and Explicit Exits

_(reply depth: 9)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2004-11-01T09:13:20-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<cm5qrh$11f1$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<clu41g$1t2b$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <S7Jgd.3709$K7.2223@news-server.bigpond.net.au> <10o7eij23spll38@corp.supernews.com> <217e491a.0410302009.c1781e9@posting.google.com> <10o8vebpi1os15d@corp.supernews.com> <217e491a.0410310949.6a916128@posting.google.com> <10oacqs81h6tk62@corp.supernews.com> <217e491a.0410311752.141b49be@posting.google.com> <10obkpnmsnk32e8@corp.supernews.com>`

```
"Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net> wrote in message
news:10obkpnmsnk32e8@corp.supernews.com...

> Out-of-line EXIT PERFORM CYCLE is useful only for
> those who use PERFORM ... THRU; since you do not
> use this style it has no effect on how you program.

Also PERFORM section-name.  If the goal is to get out of the current PERFORM
cycle, I'm not convinced having to determine how control got into that cycle
in the first place is obligatory.   That a paragraph is within a section
does not necessarily imply that the code was entered by a PERFORM
paragraph-name or a PERFORM section-name.

Personally, I don't think *either* the standard *or* the implementor should
care whether a programmer decided to divide his Procedure Division up into
SECTIONs, or which sort of out-of-line PERFORM (sections or paragraphs, with
or without THROUGH).  These capabilities have been in COBOL for four decades
and more, as I see it, and I don't think having the standards suggest or
require the rewrite of COBOL code will stimulate any wholesale change except
the further abandonment of COBOL altogether.

One of COBOL's *strengths* is that code that works continues to work.   In a
PERFORM THRU environment, the purpose of EXIT PERFORM is *clearer* than GO
TO <perform-range-exit>, and the purpose of EXIT PERFORM CYCLE is *clearer*
than GO TO <perform-range-entry>, whether the PERFORM is in-line or
out-of-line, and changing such a GO TO in the scope of an out-of-line
PERFORM would, I believe, be an improvement in clarity and maintainability.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Infinite Loops and Explicit Exits

_(reply depth: 10)_

- **From:** Robert Wagner <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net>
- **Date:** 2004-11-01T23:04:27+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<r88do0hgoqarvib383eb5mr9i6dciheatv@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<clu41g$1t2b$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <S7Jgd.3709$K7.2223@news-server.bigpond.net.au> <10o7eij23spll38@corp.supernews.com> <217e491a.0410302009.c1781e9@posting.google.com> <10o8vebpi1os15d@corp.supernews.com> <217e491a.0410310949.6a916128@posting.google.com> <10oacqs81h6tk62@corp.supernews.com> <217e491a.0410311752.141b49be@posting.google.com> <10obkpnmsnk32e8@corp.supernews.com> <cm5qrh$11f1$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`

```
On Mon, 1 Nov 2004 09:13:20 -0800, "Chuck Stevens"
<charles.stevens@unisys.com> wrote:


>One of COBOL's *strengths* is that code that works continues to work.   In a
>PERFORM THRU environment, the purpose of EXIT PERFORM is *clearer* than GO
>TO <perform-range-exit>, 

They are not equivalent. Assuming iteration control is in PERFORM ..
THRU,  GO TO <perform-range-exit> = EXIT PERFORM CYCLE = EXIT
PARAGRAPH; EXIT PERFORM stops iterating and has no equivalent except
setting an indicator or a spaghetti GO TO.

>and the purpose of EXIT PERFORM CYCLE is *clearer*
>than GO TO <perform-range-entry>,

You mean GO TO <perform-range-exit>.

> whether the PERFORM is in-line or
>out-of-line, and changing such a GO TO in the scope of an out-of-line
>PERFORM would, I believe, be an improvement in clarity and maintainability.

It would also remove any defense of a '70s shop standard requiring
that every paragraph to have an exit paragraph .. because it might
want to exit prematurely. The standard is more common than mainframers
will admit. It requires every paragraph follow this form:

    PERFORM 1000-COPY-A-TO-B THRU 1000-COPY-A-TO-B-EXIT

1000-COPY-A-TO-B.
     MOVE A TO B.
1000-COPY-A-TO-B-EXIT.
     EXIT.
```

```
Mr Wagner, you seem to be constitutionally incapable of allowing for human

'Sorry, Prod Control won't allow this code... shop standards say that 
every PERFORM must be THRU an -EXIT; go back and re-write it.'

'Don't you realise that the constructs which have been introduced since 
1985 render this obsolete?'

'I realise that shop standards are shop standards and code that doesn't 
meet them won't get moved into Prod; go back and re-write it.'

'Maybe it is time for the standards to change.'

'Only people who can change the shop standards are the Standards 
Committee; now go back and re-write it.'

'I'd like to present a case for this... when's the next time the meets?'

'There isn't a Standards Committee, it was disbanded when we were bought 
out in '87... or maybe it was the '92 buyout, the guy who told me wasn't 
too sure; now go back and re-write it.'

>The standard is more common than mainframers
>will admit.

Mr Wagner, nobody I know who has ever spent much time in a mainframe COBOL 
shop is unaware of this practise or loth to admit that it exists... we 
just must know different folks, I guess.

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Infinite Loops and Explicit Exits

_(reply depth: 14)_

- **From:** Robert Wagner <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net>
- **Date:** 2004-11-04T01:23:58+00:00

- **References:** `<clu41g$1t2b$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <r88do0hgoqarvib383eb5mr9i6dciheatv@4ax.com> <cm6gtv$olo$1@panix5.panix.com> <me5go0972uedud8erm7b167s0uathbevbk@4ax.com> <cm9g1d$52k$1@panix5.panix.com>`

```
On 2 Nov 2004 21:33:17 -0500, docdwarf@panix.com wrote:

>In article <me5go0972uedud8erm7b167s0uathbevbk@4ax.com>,
>Robert Wagner  <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net> wrote:

>>Those standards go out the window when code is re-platformed to Unix.
>
>Then why bother to mention that 'It would also remove any defense of a 
>'70s shop standard requiring that every paragraph to have an exit 
>paragraph .. because it might want to exit prematurely.'?

Just in case someone advocates keeping old standards, the availability
of EXIT PERFORM discounts his argument.

>>Good programming is no longer prohibited for maintenance and new code.
>
>It seems that it will be prohibited as long as it ever has been, Mr 
>Wagner.

Nineteen years? No way.

>>Like big lizards, mainframe culture cannot survive environmental
>>change.
>
>Might you have something to support this assertion, Mr Wagner, or might it 
>be discarded along with assertions about 'good programming'?

It is supported by my experience in mainframe shops and five former
mainframe shops ported to Unix. 

>>>>The standard is more common than mainframers will admit.
>>>
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>'admissions', not defenses or mentions.

The first step to rehabilitation is acknowledging the problem.
Mainframers are still in denial.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Infinite Loops and Explicit Exits

_(reply depth: 17)_

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com
- **Date:** 2004-11-04T07:52:54-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<cmd8n6$f01$1@panix5.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<clu41g$1t2b$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <eclio0lsn0n3onoal1grjnk5kkp5p5abbv@4ax.com> <cmc4c6$dgf$1@panix5.panix.com> <cp5ko0h5befh6q3bu9cit1u3i9gnuqm0f3@4ax.com>`

```
In article <cp5ko0h5befh6q3bu9cit1u3i9gnuqm0f3@4ax.com>,
Robert Wagner  <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net> wrote:
>On 3 Nov 2004 21:32:38 -0500, docdwarf@panix.com wrote:
>
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>conformance where one exception disproves the rule.

Mr Wagner, I quote your words and respond; if you wish to have your words 
reflect generalities then you might try to craft them as such.

>
>>>It is supported by my experience in mainframe shops and five former
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>five exceptions to an implied generality to the contrary. 

When you make a statement without qualifying it in any way, Mr Wagner, I 
respond to it as though it were an unqualified statement.  If you have 
dificulties with the way you express things there are few folks other than 
you who can address it.

>
>It's not surprising that logical fallacies are used to defend a
>programming style characterized by weak logic. Most readers of this
>forum will recognize them as fallacies. 

I cannot speak for 'most reader of this forum', Mr Wagner... but when a 
statement is made that 'People of a certain background respond in a 
certain way' I - rightly or wrongly - respond to what has been written; it 
is my assumption - with all the baggage that such an action entails - that 
the author is capable of differentiating between 'People of a certain 
background' and 'My experiences with people of a certain background'.

>
>>>Mainframers are still in denial. 
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>It's also supported by postings to this forum. 

Mr Wagner, I do not recall reading anything which would support this 
conclusion; please be so kind as to document your assertion.

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Infinite Loops and Explicit Exits

_(reply depth: 21)_

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com
- **Date:** 2004-11-06T10:15:10-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<cmippu$s40$1@panix5.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<clu41g$1t2b$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <9milo0h9pdak4gju9ue396ndaq0re5hlku@4ax.com> <cmfjo5$qvq$1@panix5.panix.com> <a3coo0hdsv50hqrs2afs7jndmq40me091o@4ax.com>`

```
In article <a3coo0hdsv50hqrs2afs7jndmq40me091o@4ax.com>,
Robert Wagner  <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net> wrote:
>On 5 Nov 2004 05:13:25 -0500, docdwarf@panix.com wrote:
>
…[32 more quoted lines elided]…
>though other styles don't work. You deny the demo is Bad Code. 

First, Mr Wagner, to say that one thing works is to say... that one thing 
works; any implication that it is saying something else does not work is 
in the mind of the beholder.

Second, Mr Wagner... my posting states my willingness to wager that a 
reader 'who looks at that and cannot almost viscerally grasp what is going 
on has fewer than two year's worth of experience'; it says nothing about 
good, bad, Good or Bad... as I am not in the habit of applying such terms 
to code.  I leave such things for Tender, Delicate Aesthetes; my approach 
is more along the lines of

IF PROGRAM-RUNS
    PERFORM NEXT-ASSIGNMENT
ELSE
    PERFORM CODE-LIKE-HELL UNTIL DAMNED-THING-WORKS.

My apologies if this pragmatic approach gives someone the Fainting 
Vapors.

>
>If it had been more complete, I would have rewritten it in Good Code.

One does what one is able to, Mr Wagner... and you did not re-write; see 
how easy it is to draw conclusions other than what is stated?

DD
```

- **References:** `<clu41g$1t2b$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <r88do0hgoqarvib383eb5mr9i6dciheatv@4ax.com> <cm6gtv$olo$1@panix5.panix.com> <me5go0972uedud8erm7b167s0uathbevbk@4ax.com> <cm9g1d$52k$1@panix5.panix.com> <eclio0lsn0n3onoal1grjnk5kkp5p5abbv@4ax.com> <5vhid.2347998$yk.379385@news.easynews.com> <sb8ko01iblpvmm2gu5p0oqch1jhngr4trh@4ax.com> <V%pid.2373306$yk.382542@news.easynews.com> <fiklo0l4ovkccg41rh3i1p3i33b11gao72@4ax.com> <3QEid.2402288$yk.386023@news.easynews.com>`

```
On Fri, 05 Nov 2004 06:11:12 GMT, "William M. Klein"
<wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> wrote:

>So you think that the coding STYLES are different on (IBM) mainframe shops 
>depending upon whether they used VSE or MVS?  If not, then in what way did they 
>NOT select you for the assignments based on your "familiarity" with that 
>("old-style") programming style?

People making hiring decisions don't know or care about style. Some
select by matching keywords between requirement and resume, some
assess 'soft skills' during a phone interview and some appear to
simply take the first names on the candidate list. 

I had difficulty finding the first project. Three unproductive weeks
passed at the height of the Y2K hiring frenzy, when others were
finding projects within a week. So I added one line to my online
resume: "Weakness: no experience with MVS." Within a week I got three
offers to work on MVS. It seems keyword scanners don't understand
semantics. :) 

>
>-- 
…[27 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

```
…[27 more quoted lines elided]…
>other than you?

If I took your wisecrack seriously, I'd turn down every project on the
grounds I didn't want to work for a company that hired people like me.

On larger teams about 10% were unreformed practitioners of Old Style
Cobol. The other 90% considered them the weakest on the team and
wouldn't refer recruiters to them after the project ended. In my
experience, most contractors really are way above average. It's not
evident from talking to them, but leaps out in their code.
```

```

On  4-Nov-2004, Robert Wagner <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net> wrote:

> I worked in many mainframe shops prior to 1985. When I returned to the
> mainframe world for two years in 1998-2000, I saw nothing had changed
> in applications style or shop standards. Therefore, my older
> experience was still a valid base for comparison.

Well, of course the old code that needed Y2K remediation was written with old
standards.     Unlike most modern languages, CoBOL was designed to stick around
and to continue working without being rewritten.

So shops often have standards for new programs that are inconsistent with what
was written 15 years earlier.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Infinite Loops and Explicit Exits

_(reply depth: 12)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2004-11-02T13:29:24-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<cm8u7k$3ao$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<clu41g$1t2b$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <cm5qrh$11f1$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <r88do0hgoqarvib383eb5mr9i6dciheatv@4ax.com> <9K6g5FseflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de>`

```
"Lueko Willms" <l.willms@jpberlin.de> wrote in message
news:9K6g5FseflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de...
> .    On  01.11.04
>   wrote  spamblocker-robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
…[21 more quoted lines elided]…
>   Looks bad ...

I will certainly assert that I've run into many a shop that had such a
standard in my day, and many a program written accordingly, whether or not
the particular logic of the paragraph required a GO TO the exit point of
that paragraph or not.

Whether one agrees with the *style* is one thing; whether one believes the
standards committees should act to invalidate such code and thereby require
companies to swallow the cost of revising their programs that will no longer
compile quite another!

My personal opinion is that the *worse* the spaghetti code the more *useful*
EXIT PERFORM becomes for maintenance!

    -Chuck Stevens
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Infinite Loops and Explicit Exits

_(reply depth: 14)_

- **From:** Peter Lacey <lacey@mb.sympatico.ca>
- **Date:** 2004-11-04T12:42:13-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<418A7805.D4C9605D@mb.sympatico.ca>`
- **References:** `<clu41g$1t2b$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <cm5qrh$11f1$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <r88do0hgoqarvib383eb5mr9i6dciheatv@4ax.com> <9K6g5FseflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de> <cm8u7k$3ao$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <cmao0d$5pf$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
If I understand the discussion properly, there's a need for a
distinction between not performing one iteration of the loop, and
getting out of it altogether.

EXIT CYCLE resembles EXIT PERFORM in the sense that they both imply
getting the hell out of there; as such, EXIT CYCLE might be better
expressed by SKIP CYCLE.  Only a suggestion.

PL
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Infinite Loops and Explicit Exits

_(reply depth: 15)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2004-11-04T11:33:38-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<cme06i$hdf$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<clu41g$1t2b$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <cm5qrh$11f1$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <r88do0hgoqarvib383eb5mr9i6dciheatv@4ax.com> <9K6g5FseflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de> <cm8u7k$3ao$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <cmao0d$5pf$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <418A7805.D4C9605D@mb.sympatico.ca>`

```
"Peter Lacey" <lacey@mb.sympatico.ca> wrote in message
news:418A7805.D4C9605D@mb.sympatico.ca...
> If I understand the discussion properly, there's a need for a
> distinction between not performing one iteration of the loop, and
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> PL

The 2002 standard added EXIT PERFORM CYCLE (and EXIT PERFORM -- without
CYCLE), not EXIT CYCLE.  CYCLE was added as a *context-sensitive* word in
the 2002 standard (where it is only recognized as "special" in the context
of the EXIT statement).

To provide SKIP CYCLE as new syntax in 2002, it would have been necessary to
add "SKIP" as a reserved word. ; it would have been necessary to add SKIP
asa s reserved word SKIP would have SKIP isn't a reserved word.  J4 is
reluctant to add new reserved words unless there's really no other
reasonable alternative.  That didn't happen, so what we *now have* in 2002
COBOL is "EXIT PERFORM CYCLE".

Or are you suggesting we should have EXIT PERFORM and EXIT PERFORM CYCLE in
the context of in-line PERFORM (as the 2002 standard now specifies), and
EXIT PERFORM and SKIP CYCLE in the context of out-of-line PERFORM?   I don't
think I like that idea all that much ...

    -Chuck Stevens
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Infinite Loops and Explicit Exits

_(reply depth: 15)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2004-11-04T12:24:39-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<cme368$jch$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<clu41g$1t2b$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <cm5qrh$11f1$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <r88do0hgoqarvib383eb5mr9i6dciheatv@4ax.com> <9K6g5FseflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de> <cm8u7k$3ao$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <cmao0d$5pf$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <418A7805.D4C9605D@mb.sympatico.ca>`

```
Sorry, I garbled my response.  Let's try this again:

"Peter Lacey" <lacey@mb.sympatico.ca> wrote in message
news:418A7805.D4C9605D@mb.sympatico.ca...
> If I understand the discussion properly, there's a need for a
> distinction between not performing one iteration of the loop, and
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> PL

The 2002 standard added EXIT PERFORM CYCLE (and EXIT PERFORM -- without
CYCLE), not EXIT CYCLE.  CYCLE was added as a *context-sensitive* word in
the 2002 standard (where it is only recognized as "special" in the context
of the EXIT statement).

To provide SKIP CYCLE as new syntax in 2002, it would have been necessary to
add "SKIP" as a reserved word.  J4 is reluctant to add new reserved words
unless there's really no other reasonable alternative.  That didn't happen,
so what we *now have* in 2002 COBOL is "EXIT PERFORM CYCLE".

Or are you suggesting we should have EXIT PERFORM and EXIT PERFORM CYCLE in
the context of in-line PERFORM (as the 2002 standard now specifies), and
EXIT PERFORM and SKIP CYCLE in the context of out-of-line PERFORM?   I don't
think I like that idea all that much ...

    -Chuck Stevens
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Infinite Loops and Explicit Exits

_(reply depth: 11)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2004-11-02T12:13:22-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<cm8pp2$e3$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<clu41g$1t2b$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <S7Jgd.3709$K7.2223@news-server.bigpond.net.au> <10o7eij23spll38@corp.supernews.com> <217e491a.0410302009.c1781e9@posting.google.com> <10o8vebpi1os15d@corp.supernews.com> <217e491a.0410310949.6a916128@posting.google.com> <10oacqs81h6tk62@corp.supernews.com> <217e491a.0410311752.141b49be@posting.google.com> <10obkpnmsnk32e8@corp.supernews.com> <cm5qrh$11f1$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <r88do0hgoqarvib383eb5mr9i6dciheatv@4ax.com>`

```

"Robert Wagner" <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net> wrote in message
news:r88do0hgoqarvib383eb5mr9i6dciheatv@4ax.com...
> On Mon, 1 Nov 2004 09:13:20 -0800, "Chuck Stevens"
> <charles.stevens@unisys.com> wrote:
>
>
> >One of COBOL's *strengths* is that code that works continues to work.
In a
> >PERFORM THRU environment, the purpose of EXIT PERFORM is *clearer* than
GO
> >TO <perform-range-exit>,
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> setting an indicator or a spaghetti GO TO.

Only partly true.  GO TO <perform-range-exit> is equivalent to EXIT PERFORM
CYCLE only if <perform-range-exit> is a paragraph that contains only the
EXIT statement (not a requirement), and EXIT PERFORM CYCLE is equivalent to
EXIT PARAGRAPH only if both occur in the last or only paragraph in the
PERFORM range.

> >and the purpose of EXIT PERFORM CYCLE is *clearer*
> >than GO TO <perform-range-entry>,
>
> You mean GO TO <perform-range-exit>.

Yes, but again, that only works if <perform-range-exit> is a paragraph
containing only EXIT.  I was thinking of the PERFORM FOREVER case, in which
event I think they're pretty much equivalent.

> > whether the PERFORM is in-line or
> >out-of-line, and changing such a GO TO in the scope of an out-of-line
> >PERFORM would, I believe, be an improvement in clarity and
maintainability.
>
> It would also remove any defense of a '70s shop standard requiring
> that every paragraph to have an exit paragraph .. because it might
> want to exit prematurely.

The defense of an existing '70's shop standard so stating is that it exists
and that most likely the owners of a company have spent a fair amount of
money in programs that conform to it.  Because there may be no particular
technical necessity for a construct in a given context does not mean it is
valueless to those whose programs contain it.  "Because I'm used to it being
there" is not such a terrible thing!

> The standard is more common than mainframers
> will admit.  ...

Yes, I am well aware of the Holy Wars as to whether a GO TO to the single
EXIT point of the paragraph you're in was a violation of Proper Structured
Programming rules.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Infinite Loops and Explicit Exits

_(reply depth: 12)_

- **From:** Robert Wagner <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net>
- **Date:** 2004-11-03T01:20:56+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<qr6go0tqvutkqbtuji7q0919s8j22plnl2@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<S7Jgd.3709$K7.2223@news-server.bigpond.net.au> <10o7eij23spll38@corp.supernews.com> <217e491a.0410302009.c1781e9@posting.google.com> <10o8vebpi1os15d@corp.supernews.com> <217e491a.0410310949.6a916128@posting.google.com> <10oacqs81h6tk62@corp.supernews.com> <217e491a.0410311752.141b49be@posting.google.com> <10obkpnmsnk32e8@corp.supernews.com> <cm5qrh$11f1$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <r88do0hgoqarvib383eb5mr9i6dciheatv@4ax.com> <cm8pp2$e3$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`

```
On Tue, 2 Nov 2004 12:13:22 -0800, "Chuck Stevens"
<charles.stevens@unisys.com> wrote:

>
>"Robert Wagner" <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net> wrote in message
…[20 more quoted lines elided]…
>PERFORM range.

In practice, both are true. Programmers don't perform a thru c, they
perform a thru a-exit.

The significant change is that EXIT PERFORM not only exits but also
stops iteration. There is no equivalent Cobol function to do that
under 2002.

>> >and the purpose of EXIT PERFORM CYCLE is *clearer*
>> >than GO TO <perform-range-entry>,
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>event I think they're pretty much equivalent.

PERFORM FOREVER doesn't exist yet, and do-it-yourself methods are
uncommon. Again, the common case is PERFORM A THRU A-EXIT UNTIL
<an-indicator-is-set>. EXIT PERFORM CYCLE would replace A-EXIT and
THRU A-EXIT. EXIT PERFORM would additionally replace the indicator. 

There is no need for THRU A-EXIT *now*, and the terminating condition
belongs on UNTIL rather than embedded in the procedure. But there are
some who need an 'excuse' to change their obsolete style. 

>The defense of an existing '70's shop standard so stating is that it exists
>and that most likely the owners of a company have spent a fair amount of
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>there" is not such a terrible thing!

It is terrible when it prompts outsiders to call Cobol a dinosaur and
abandon it for 'more modern' languages.

>Yes, I am well aware of the Holy Wars as to whether a GO TO to the single
>EXIT point of the paragraph you're in was a violation of Proper Structured
>Programming rules.

A properly structured program has no need for premature exits (except
to abort). The audience for EXIT PERFORM is programmers who don't know
how to do structured programming. For them, EXIT PERFORM is an
improvement over PERFORM A THRU A-EXIT.
```

- **Date:** 2004-11-03T08:12:27-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<cmb01c$1gm4$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<S7Jgd.3709$K7.2223@news-server.bigpond.net.au> <10o7eij23spll38@corp.supernews.com> <217e491a.0410302009.c1781e9@posting.google.com> <10o8vebpi1os15d@corp.supernews.com> <217e491a.0410310949.6a916128@posting.google.com> <10oacqs81h6tk62@corp.supernews.com> <217e491a.0410311752.141b49be@posting.google.com> <10obkpnmsnk32e8@corp.supernews.com> <cm5qrh$11f1$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <r88do0hgoqarvib383eb5mr9i6dciheatv@4ax.com> <cm8pp2$e3$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <qr6go0tqvutkqbtuji7q0919s8j22plnl2@4ax.com>`

```

"Robert Wagner" <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net> wrote in message
news:qr6go0tqvutkqbtuji7q0919s8j22plnl2@4ax.com...
> On Tue, 2 Nov 2004 12:13:22 -0800, "Chuck Stevens"
> <charles.stevens@unisys.com> wrote:
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> >> >PERFORM THRU environment, the purpose of EXIT PERFORM is *clearer*
than
> >GO
> >> >TO <perform-range-exit>,
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> >Only partly true.  GO TO <perform-range-exit> is equivalent to EXIT
PERFORM
> >CYCLE only if <perform-range-exit> is a paragraph that contains only the
> >EXIT statement (not a requirement), and EXIT PERFORM CYCLE is equivalent
to
> >EXIT PARAGRAPH only if both occur in the last or only paragraph in the
> >PERFORM range.
>
> In practice, both are true. Programmers don't perform a thru c, they
> perform a thru a-exit.

Well, Mr. Wagner, I guess that depends on how you define "programmer".  I
have personally seen production code that does exactly the sort of PERFORM
you claim programmers never do.

> The significant change is that EXIT PERFORM not only exits but also
> stops iteration. There is no equivalent Cobol function to do that
> under 2002.

Unless, of course, what you are PERFORMing is inline code.

> >> >and the purpose of EXIT PERFORM CYCLE is *clearer*
> >> >than GO TO <perform-range-entry>,
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> >containing only EXIT.  I was thinking of the PERFORM FOREVER case, in
which
> >event I think they're pretty much equivalent.
>
> PERFORM FOREVER doesn't exist yet, and do-it-yourself methods are
> uncommon.

The methods are neither common nor uncommon.  What is relevant is whether
they exist today or not, and whether they are straightforward or not, and I
believe the answer to both of those is "yes".  Now, if what you meant by
"uncommon" was "not commonly used", that's a matter of personal style and of
your exposure to it.  I suspect the most straightforward method -- an
88-level item referencing an invariant filler -- isn't that common precisely
because many practicing programmers seem to avoid 88-level items to begin
with (despite the continued enhancement to the feature in the various
standards).

> Again, the common case is PERFORM A THRU A-EXIT UNTIL
> <an-indicator-is-set>. EXIT PERFORM CYCLE would replace A-EXIT and
> THRU A-EXIT.

Could, but needn't.  The PERFORM and the labels could remain if the
programmer so chose.  .

> EXIT PERFORM would additionally replace the indicator.

Only if you had a PERFORM FOREVER or equivalent.

> There is no need for THRU A-EXIT *now*,

Depends on whether The Person Paying Your Salary wants it there -- or wants
it to stay there, even if it's for the simple reason that he wants it there
and he wants it to stay there.

> and the terminating condition
> belongs on UNTIL rather than embedded in the procedure.

So the Dijkstra-inspired "DO FOREVER ... IF <condition> THEN UNDO ...END" is
completely wrong-headed?

I personally think the choice as to whether the termination logic belongs in
the outer loop or in the code within the loop depends on the application and
the circumstances, and I believe the programmer should have the ability to
choose whichever he (or his shop's standards) deem is appropriate for the
context.

> But there are
> some who need an 'excuse' to change their obsolete style.

???

> >The defense of an existing '70's shop standard so stating is that it
exists
> >and that most likely the owners of a company have spent a fair amount of
> >money in programs that conform to it.  Because there may be no particular
> >technical necessity for a construct in a given context does not mean it
is
> >valueless to those whose programs contain it.  "Because I'm used to it
being
> >there" is not such a terrible thing!
>
> It is terrible when it prompts outsiders to call Cobol a dinosaur and
> abandon it for 'more modern' languages.

So the appropriate response of a successful and profitable business, when
some outsider says "your programming standards are hopelessly unfashionable"
is "yessir, yessir, whatever you say sir.  How many years of unprofitability
are we going to have to go through to rectify this heinous crime?" ???

> A properly structured program has no need for premature exits (except
> to abort). The audience for EXIT PERFORM is programmers who don't know
> how to do structured programming. For them, EXIT PERFORM is an
> improvement over PERFORM A THRU A-EXIT.

Again, Mr. Wagner:  we just received a trouble report from a large customer
that turns out to have been a problem with the ALTER verb.  The bug was
preventing this customer from upgrading to new system software because the
program, which has clearly (from internal evidence) been performing to the
customer's satisfaction for something on the order of twenty years or more,
no longer works correctly.

Neither I, nor the customer, nor the applicable standard, nor the compiler
cares whether anybody using ALTER today is a poopie-face or not.  Neither I,
nor my company, nor the standards committee is in a position to go back to
the customer and say "You know, you should NEVER have used ALTER in this
program in the first place, everybody KNOWs anyone who uses ALTER is asking
for trouble."

A compiler that *corrects* this problem with the ALTER verb in this
customer's production program
is now available for their retrieval, with our apologies for having
inadvertently caused this problem during a maintainability rewrite in a
difficult area.  It does not matter that ALTER is marked obsolete in the '85
standard and is absent from the 2002 standard; this is a production COBOL74
program with which the customer was delighted and upon which the customer
depended.

It is not the business of the *standards* committee to decide whether a
program is "properly structured" or not.  Nor is it the business of the
*compiler vendor* to decide that.  It is the *customer's* job to decide
that -- or to care, or not to care.

    -Chuck Stevens.
```

```
On  3-Nov-2004, "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com> wrote:

> > But there are
> > some who need an 'excuse' to change their obsolete style.
>
> ???

The best (only?) excuse is the bottom line.   This isn't always obvious to
determine, especially since short term and long term goals can conflict.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Infinite Loops and Explicit Exits

_(reply depth: 14)_

- **From:** Robert Wagner <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net>
- **Date:** 2004-11-04T01:23:59+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<tcmio011erm6g9nebq1k8vbea4sghsnohm@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<217e491a.0410302009.c1781e9@posting.google.com> <10o8vebpi1os15d@corp.supernews.com> <217e491a.0410310949.6a916128@posting.google.com> <10oacqs81h6tk62@corp.supernews.com> <217e491a.0410311752.141b49be@posting.google.com> <10obkpnmsnk32e8@corp.supernews.com> <cm5qrh$11f1$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <r88do0hgoqarvib383eb5mr9i6dciheatv@4ax.com> <cm8pp2$e3$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <qr6go0tqvutkqbtuji7q0919s8j22plnl2@4ax.com> <cmb01c$1gm4$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`

```
On Wed, 3 Nov 2004 08:12:27 -0800, "Chuck Stevens"
<charles.stevens@unisys.com> wrote:

>
>"Robert Wagner" <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net> wrote in message
>news:qr6go0tqvutkqbtuji7q0919s8j22plnl2@4ax.com...

>> PERFORM FOREVER doesn't exist yet, and do-it-yourself methods are
>> uncommon.
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>standards).

I haven't noticed avoidance of 88s. 

Use of PERFORM .. FOREVER is uncommon because the standard doesn't
provide a mechanism to stop iterations in an out-of-line perform.

>> There is no need for THRU A-EXIT *now*,
>
>Depends on whether The Person Paying Your Salary wants it there -- or wants
>it to stay there, even if it's for the simple reason that he wants it there
>and he wants it to stay there.

Wants and Pays do not speak with a single voice. 

The Person Paying My Salary is an executive who knows nothing about
programming style. If asked, he'd champion the style with lowest
maintenance cost and highest reliability, without regard for shop
traditions. 

The person enforcing shop standards is a supervisor whose motivation
is job security, not money. 

>> and the terminating condition
>> belongs on UNTIL rather than embedded in the procedure.
>
>So the Dijkstra-inspired "DO FOREVER ... IF <condition> THEN UNDO ...END" is
>completely wrong-headed?

It's unfamiliar to Cobol programmers because the equivalent to UNDO --
EXIT PERFORM -- can only be used with in-line PERFORM. We want the
option to convert in-line to out-of-line when it becomes unwieldy. We
avoid EXIT PERFORM because it removes that option.

>So the appropriate response of a successful and profitable business, when
>some outsider says "your programming standards are hopelessly unfashionable"
>is "yessir, yessir, whatever you say sir.  How many years of unprofitability
>are we going to have to go through to rectify this heinous crime?" ???

A straw man argument. You haven't shown there would be years of
unprofitability. I cited one case where savings were realized the
first year. I could have cited at least four others. 

>> A properly structured program has no need for premature exits (except
>> to abort). The audience for EXIT PERFORM is programmers who don't know
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>no longer works correctly.

I say EXIT PERFORM would be an improvement for unstructured programs;
your response talks about seriously unstructured code still in
production. How does the response disprove the assertion? It sounds
like an example supporting the assertion. If the customer upgrades to
2002, it will have to rewrite ALTER one way or another. EXIT PERFORM
might well be the least intrusive translation. 

>It is not the business of the *standards* committee to decide whether a
>program is "properly structured" or not.  Nor is it the business of the
>*compiler vendor* to decide that.  It is the *customer's* job to decide
>that -- or to care, or not to care.

I agree that quality is not the concern of the standards committee nor
compiler vendor, but it is a valid concern of this forum. Posters here
can legitimately criticize ALTER, GO TO and PERFORM THRU. Readers who
care about quality might learn to program better. Reactionaries will
continue writing bad code.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Infinite Loops and Explicit Exits

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

```
On  3-Nov-2004, Robert Wagner <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net> wrote:

> I agree that quality is not the concern of the standards committee nor
> compiler vendor, but it is a valid concern of this forum. Posters here
> can legitimately criticize ALTER, GO TO and PERFORM THRU. Readers who
> care about quality might learn to program better. Reactionaries will

Reactionaries will react.   The people who continue writing bad code are the
opposite of reactionaries.

Of course if the code does what it's supposed to, we need to examine what we
mean by "bad code".
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Infinite Loops and Explicit Exits

_(reply depth: 16)_

- **From:** Robert Wagner <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net>
- **Date:** 2004-11-05T05:22:43+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<igflo0deasceo87ii2ohpkp2nevlrlipo7@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<217e491a.0410310949.6a916128@posting.google.com> <10oacqs81h6tk62@corp.supernews.com> <217e491a.0410311752.141b49be@posting.google.com> <10obkpnmsnk32e8@corp.supernews.com> <cm5qrh$11f1$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <r88do0hgoqarvib383eb5mr9i6dciheatv@4ax.com> <cm8pp2$e3$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <qr6go0tqvutkqbtuji7q0919s8j22plnl2@4ax.com> <cmb01c$1gm4$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <tcmio011erm6g9nebq1k8vbea4sghsnohm@4ax.com> <cmdisg$gc2$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
On Thu, 4 Nov 2004 15:46:24 GMT, "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>
wrote:

>
>On  3-Nov-2004, Robert Wagner <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net> wrote:
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>opposite of reactionaries.

Reaction is defined:
"resistance or opposition to a force, influence, or movement;
especially : tendency toward a former and usually outmoded political
or social order or policy"

By that definition, supporters of Old Style Cobol are reactionaries. 

>Of course if the code does what it's supposed to, we need to examine what we
>mean by "bad code".

Bad code is difficult to understand and to maintain reliably. 

Examples: 

.. Redundant code. Multiple copies of the same or slightly modified
code which must be manually kept in synch. 
Cause: copy-and-paste rather than callable programs, failure to use
copybooks and typedef.

.. Decision made in one place, action based on the decision taken far
away .. in the same or another program.
Cause: indicators, monolithic programs.

.. Code that "does what it's supposed to" when presented with
anticipated cases but produces 'undefined results' for unexpected
inputs.
Cause: unstructured programming.

.. Code that abends without reporting the case (record) or reason. 
Cause: laziness. 

.. Inconsistent naming of variables such that a global search misses
references. 
Cause: lack of naming discipline, variables that are a function of
other variables, weak typing, missing call prototypes. 

.. Flow of control that's difficult to follow.
Cause: unstructured programming, weak logic skills.

.. Awkward conditional logic.
Cause: weak logic skills.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Infinite Loops and Explicit Exits

_(reply depth: 17)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2004-11-05T11:08:22-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0411051108.5b8c4d41@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<217e491a.0410310949.6a916128@posting.google.com> <217e491a.0410311752.141b49be@posting.google.com> <10obkpnmsnk32e8@corp.supernews.com> <cm5qrh$11f1$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <r88do0hgoqarvib383eb5mr9i6dciheatv@4ax.com> <cm8pp2$e3$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <qr6go0tqvutkqbtuji7q0919s8j22plnl2@4ax.com> <cmb01c$1gm4$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <tcmio011erm6g9nebq1k8vbea4sghsnohm@4ax.com> <cmdisg$gc2$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <igflo0deasceo87ii2ohpkp2nevlrlipo7@4ax.com>`

```
Robert Wagner <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net> wrote 

> Bad code is difficult to understand and to maintain reliably. 

How easy code is to 'understand and maintain' is primarily a function
of how familiar the maintainer is with the way it is written.  It is
to do with expectations, being able to rely on assumptions, knowing
where particular code fits and where to find it.

This is why site standards are so important to most companies. If all
programs are written the same way then one can pick up a program that
hasn't been looked at for decades and can understand it immediately.

Of course that does stifle progression, but the company is there to
run the business, not write books on style.

The main problem with adopting the very latest trendy styles (or even
'what C did in the 80s') is that the systems become fractured into
'old unmaintainable' and 'new trendy' with various intermediate and
experimental styles between. So do the programmers.  Individuals
become tied to certain styles because they can easily and reliably
work with programs they understand.

As programmers turnover the old systems become unmaintainable, not
because the style is inherently unmaintainable, but because the
programmers are unused to it, or more likely, unwilling.  This reduces
the value of the system, makes it expensive to maintain (at the charge
rate that Doc would like to ask for), and/or would require
replacement.

The way that companies retain value in their investment is to set
coding standards that keep the programmers producing consistent code
so that even the oldest programs can still be easily maintained by any
of them.

Otherwise they wind up with a bunch of coders rewriting the whole
thing to do exactly the same job in some new-fangled way at huge
expense with no site standards, and then find that commodity
programmers can't reliably maintain it and have to rewrite it all over
again back to follow some standards.


> Cause: failure to use .. typedef.

How can you possibly criticise them for not using a feature that is
not available in their compilers ?

> .. Decision made in one place, action based on the decision taken far
> away .. in the same or another program.

Decisions and actions must always be made in different places, it
can't all be in one line of code.  Tax rates may depend on where the
delivery is made to, you can't decide where the delivery is to be in
the tax calc, nor would you calculate the tax in the address code.

> Cause: indicators, 

One uses an indicator, such as 'zero rated'.

> ..  monolithic programs.

How can 'monolithic programs' be the cause of something 'in another
program'. Monolithic means there are no 'other programs' - mono - get
it ?


> .. Code that "does what it's supposed to" when presented with
> anticipated cases but produces 'undefined results' for unexpected
> inputs.
> Cause: unstructured programming.

It has zero to do with whether the code is 'structured' or not.  The
best structured code can still produce 'undefined results' if the
specification for the program does not specify what results should be
given for those inputs.

> .. Code that abends without reporting the case (record) or reason. 
> Cause: laziness. 

One could argue that a program should never abend.  If the programmer
could predict what would make the program fail and thus surround it
with reporting code, then he could make the changes necessary to stop
it abending, and thus the reporting is superfluous.
 
> .. Inconsistent naming of variables such that a global search misses
> references. 

Consistent naming is usually one of the things that are in the site
standards that you seem to deride.

> Cause: lack of naming discipline, variables that are a function of
> other variables, weak typing, missing call prototypes. 

Oh yes, more things that aren't in their compilers.
 
> .. Flow of control that's difficult to follow.
> Cause: unstructured programming, weak logic skills.

This relates back to the first point. What is 'easy' and what is
'difficult' is entirely dependent on what one is used to and what
one's expectations are.
 
> .. Awkward conditional logic.
> Cause: weak logic skills.

What is 'awkward' to one is 'simplified' to another.  The advantage of
writing in a style that assumes the reader has 'weak logic skills' is
that when the assumption is correct at 2am the job can still be done.


> By that definition, supporters of Old Style Cobol are reactionaries. 

Now you will probably characterise me as a 'supporter of Old Style
Cobol'. In fact others think that I don't - as is obvious from other
posts.  What I do understand is why companies want to protect their
investment in the code that represents and runs their business. They
want code to last as long as their business will and so they want it
done conservatively and they want their programmers to be equally
conservative.

We have all heard stories about how a bunch of maverick programmers
rewrote all Cobol into some style that average coders had difficulty
with.  This forced it to be rewritten again back into a rational style
with site standards but the company was driven into bankruptcy   ;-)

By and large the companies will also be slow to update to the latest
technologies.  They adopted '85 compilers when forced to, they,
generally, did not rush into OO, because there was no final standard.
They will move to '02 compilers some years after they become available
(if they do).

Your strategy of attacking the programmers and calling their code
'bad' and requiring a revolution is flawed. You just annoy them and
make them resolute that they don't want no stinkin' 'real programmers'
here.

This is especially true when you insult them with 'weak logic skills'
and other deficiencies.
```

```
On Fri, 5 Nov 2004 19:26:04 GMT, "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>
wrote:

>
>On  5-Nov-2004, riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard) wrote:
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>gets noticed and corrected.

Thank you for confirming what I said. It reminds me of a recent
conversation during a mainframe-to-Unix conversion:

How do I abend under Unix?
RW: Abends are mainframe culture. Under Unix, you log the error and
keep running. 
Nobody reads logs. We must have abends.
RW: If you insist .. call 'abort'.

How do I read a memory dump under Unix?
RW: You don't. You log enough information to diagnose the error.
I know it creates a 'core' file. I need a way to read it.
RW: If you insist .. compile with -g and run anim. It will filter core
thru source code. 
Source code! Wow.
```

- **References:** `<217e491a.0410310949.6a916128@posting.google.com> <217e491a.0410311752.141b49be@posting.google.com> <10obkpnmsnk32e8@corp.supernews.com> <cm5qrh$11f1$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <r88do0hgoqarvib383eb5mr9i6dciheatv@4ax.com> <cm8pp2$e3$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <qr6go0tqvutkqbtuji7q0919s8j22plnl2@4ax.com> <cmb01c$1gm4$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <tcmio011erm6g9nebq1k8vbea4sghsnohm@4ax.com> <cmdisg$gc2$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <igflo0deasceo87ii2ohpkp2nevlrlipo7@4ax.com> <217e491a.0411051108.5b8c4d41@posting.google.com> <cmgk9e$742$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote in message
news:cmgk9e$742$1@peabody.colorado.edu...

> Of course, what you are talking about as "trendy" in a CoBOL newsgroup is
20
> year old techniques.   Others find structured code and even CoBOL to be
close to
> obsolete they are so non-trendy.

To elaborate a bit on this point, if you manage to convince a Bean Counter
that he *really* needs to spend the money to have his COBOL code rewritten
so that it no longer Looks Yucky, the approach he's *most* likely to take,
from all I hear, is that rather than rewrite it from Yucky COBOL into Trendy
COBOL he'll elect to rewrite it from Yucky COBOL into LdJ (Langue-du-Jour)
even if it costs more.  I've seen such efforts(successful and otherwise) for
several decades now, some successful, some otherwise.  Doesn't seem to
matter much what LdJ is; PL/I, Pascal, ADA, APL, VB, C, C++, Java and C#
have all occupied that position at one time or another, in one context or
another.   As a COBOL programmer, would you rather work on programs written
in Yucky COBOL or LdJ?

    -Chuck Stevens
```

```
<charles.stevens@unisys.com> wrote:

>To elaborate a bit on this point, if you manage to convince a Bean Counter
>that he *really* needs to spend the money to have his COBOL code rewritten
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>in Yucky COBOL or LdJ?

BIFURCATION

Also referred to as the "black and white" fallacy, bifurcation occurs
when one presents a situation as having only two alternatives, where
in fact otheralternatives exist or can exist.
```

- **References:** `<217e491a.0410310949.6a916128@posting.google.com> <217e491a.0410311752.141b49be@posting.google.com> <10obkpnmsnk32e8@corp.supernews.com> <cm5qrh$11f1$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <r88do0hgoqarvib383eb5mr9i6dciheatv@4ax.com> <cm8pp2$e3$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <qr6go0tqvutkqbtuji7q0919s8j22plnl2@4ax.com> <cmb01c$1gm4$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <tcmio011erm6g9nebq1k8vbea4sghsnohm@4ax.com> <cmdisg$gc2$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <igflo0deasceo87ii2ohpkp2nevlrlipo7@4ax.com> <217e491a.0411051108.5b8c4d41@posting.google.com> <cmgk9e$742$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```

"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote in message
news:cmgk9e$742$1@peabody.colorado.edu...
>
> On  5-Nov-2004, riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard) wrote:
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> Of course, what you are talking about as "trendy" in a CoBOL newsgroup is
20
> year old techniques.   Others find structured code and even CoBOL to be
close to
> obsolete they are so non-trendy.
>

I find COBOL to be close to obsolete, not because it is non-trendy (the
particular COBOL I write (Object Oriented) is VERY trendy, and programmers
who don't know COBOL express interest that I am still using it, and like to
look at the code. I have heard the word "quaint" used, and it makes me
smile...), but because there are better ways to do many of the things I used
to do with COBOL.

What did I used to do with COBOL?

Develop online and batch processing systems for the commercial environment.

COBOL is still very useful for batch, but that is because of the innate
procedural nature of batch processing.

I find it (the OO implementation) excellent for building commercial
components also, but I seem to be a majority of 1 where that's concerned...
<G>

The point that's being missed here, Howard, is not whether COBOL is trendy
(it would be very hard to support that argument... <G>), but whether it
STILL represents a good solution to the problem of implementing commercial
computer systems. (If it doesn't then this thread serves little purpose...)

And Richard's point about what is familiar to us is probably the single most
compelling reason this discussion is happening at all.

The fact is that there are completely new approaches, tools, and
philosophies in data processing that have bypassed COBOL.

(I have used the analogy of the barbarians on their swift little ponies
sweeping around the fortress of COBOL, rather than storming it head on in a
confrontation they could never  hope to win...Instead, the fortess is left
isolated, and the riches of the land go to the barbarians... Of course, they
then settle on the land, and stop being barbarians...they reshape the
culture, and the civilian population see new (and often, better...) ways of
doing things, and so they adopt them. Eventually, a new population emerges,
and the old Knights, besieged in their fortress are forgotten. One day they
realise the siege has been lifted. They venture out, only to find that their
world has changed and they are now objects of curiosity, rather than
veneration and awe, as was once the case.  The secret knowledge which they
cherished and protected for centuries is now in the hands of everyone, and
it is obsolete. The war is over; the world has moved on...)

My point is that the whole concept of infinite loops and explicit exits
relates ONLY to procedural programming, and procedural programming may have
a limited life span.

The main reason it is being discussed at all, is because careless
implementation of these facilities can lead to maintenance nightmares and we
all know how awful THAT can be... (For me, that was in a past life, but I
accept that for many here, it is still pertinent...)

If you are not maintaining source code, it has no relevance at all.

Many modern solutions to commercial computer system implementation do not
require the maintenance of source code. (modules of packages, components,
generators, etc.).

Other solutions are event driven and do not normally require loops and
exits. (Database triggered procedures, for example.)

So, it is really only the Knights of COBOL  who would post several hundred
entries to a thread about it.<G>

We could argue at great length about the factors leading to the demise of
COBOL, but we can no longer argue about its demise.

In practical terms, the only employment hope for most people reading this is
to expand their skill set. That means expanding your mind set. And that is
unlikely be achieved by fixation on minutae and the exchanges of abuse which
we see exhibited here.

Actually, I guess that is one justification for the OT posts that happen
here. They are usually more stimulating than the COBOL discussion... wonder
why that is?

(OK...OK... I'm leaving... <sheesh>)

Pete.
```

```
PECD> I find COBOL to be close to obsolete, not because it is non-trendy
PECD> (the particular COBOL I write (Object Oriented) is VERY trendy, and
PECD> programmers who don't know COBOL express interest that I am still
PECD> using it, and like to look at the code. I have heard the word
PECD> "quaint" used, and it makes me smile...), but because there are
PECD> better ways to do many of the things I used to do with COBOL.


   "Quaint" is probably the nicest thing programmers of the current
generation can say about COBOL.

   I recently did a search at the site of the German labour office for
job offers using the name of a programming language. The numbers I will
give are those that the search engine indicated at the top with a 100%
match.

   I found 3 for COBOL, 21 for C++, and 71 fï¿½r Java.

   I can't of course be sure, but I very much doubt that COBOL is still
used for and large scale development of new applications. Maybe that  
management lets the old COBOL systems rot and wait for their knights  
to die out, since these stick to their old habits, but the new  
recruits coming from the universities probably don't know anything  
about COBOL, except that COBOL is a programming language.

   I'm currently trying to find out in how far there are still COBOL  
courses being offered a) in the adademic courses for informatics (here  
in Germany), and b) in private course business. Re the latter I found  
that the current CDI (which has no longer anything to do with Control  
Data) does apparently not offer COBOL anymore, but Integrata, which is  
a company with good contacts in the mainframe business, still offers  
quite a number of courses. I doubt that any of the students of  
informatics nowadays learn anything about COBOL except that it is  
"quaint". But I'll see and will forward my findings to this newsgroup.



Yours,
```

```

"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote in message
news:cmo66b$oc1$1@peabody.colorado.edu...
>
> On  6-Nov-2004, "Peter E.C Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote:
>
> > We could argue at great length about the factors leading to the demise
of
> > COBOL, but we can no longer argue about its demise.
>
> Of course some people assume that if they switch to another tool, that
tool will
> last them until retirement.   CoBOL will die - but it may out-live many of
the
> current "popular" languages.
>
I don't disagree , Howard.

The point I have been trying to make is that it is no longer a contest
between languages. It is a contest between design and build philosphies. The
days of writing thousands of lines of procedural code in order to implement
a commercial computer system are definitely numbered. It is simply too
expensive, too error prone, and too time consuming. A luxury that industry
can no longer afford. As long as there was no alternative (most of the
latter half of the twentieth century) there was no choice and they HAD to do
it. Now they don't. MANY companies don't WANT to be in IT. They were in it
because they had no choice. Now it can be safely outsourced and there are
better and quicker approaches than trying to build and maintain systems
in-house.

Sadly, they have woken up to what many of us found out pretty early in our
careers (about the time you found yourself writing the same application in
three different companies...): "Although I may tell myself my business needs
are unique, they are not. My systems have to achieve exactly what my
competitors systems have to achieve. Modern packages can deliver a better
than 90% solution to my core admin. processes. Why would I maintain a costly
IT department for 10% of my needs?"

Add to this the influx of "smarter" business users, many of whom have grown
up with personal computers, and the outlook for procedural computer
programming approaches looks much less attractive.

Don't take my word for it (it's Ok...I know you don't <G>)... just look
around. How many instances of outsourcing, package implementation, and
user-developed spreadsheet/database applications to do the rest, have you
seen personally in the last year? How many major new developments undertaken
in COBOL? (I am talking commercial enterprises here, not institutes of
learning where they probably still teach Latin out of academic interest.)

The computer programming boom is over; the computer usage boom is poised to
start.

I foresee people taking their computers everywhere (just like their
wristwatches), software becoming so user friendly it may be unrecognisable
as software (smart jewellery, for instance... earrings or glasses that
whisper people's names and salient details to you as the person approaches
at a party...).

In the workplace we can look for higher interactivity with software to solve
business problems. Skills in demand will not be programming, rather people
who know the business and its processes, intimately.They will simply
interact iteratively with a computer until it "understands" what they
require and produces it instantly. From then on, the only interaction
required would be when a Business process changes. At last, the dog wags the
tail...

I guess, if I was being unkind, I'd call it "the revenge of the users". For
decades they were put down by supercilious techno freaks, who showed little
patience or understanding, saw users as PITAs who were never happy and were
always requiring changes, and thinly veiled their contempt for them. Now its
payback time... They don't need experts any more. The whole industry has
been (and is continuing to be) de-skilled.

Eventually, ( I calculate around 75 years and am really sorry I won't be
here to see it...) the ultimate goal of software developers will be
attained, the goal that made a group of commercial competitors sit around a
table in 1959 and put aside their petty differences in a desire to build
something that would benefit EVERYBODY in commerce, and bring the dream of
computer resource availability for every single person on the planet a step
nearer. That CODASYL group caught a glimpse of something fine and gave us
COBOL. Eventually, through our own smugness and inertia, we lost it.  But
the dream they glimpsed goes on...

"One day, EVERY single human being will have access to, and be able to
communicate easily with, a computer that has the power to help him/her solve
problems and improve their life. The world will be changed for the better by
cyber technology."

We are seeing the beginning of it. We ain't seen nothin' yet...

Pete.
```

```
On Tue, 9 Nov 2004 16:14:06 +1300, "Pete Dashwood"
<dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote:

>Sadly, they have woken up to what many of us found out pretty early in our
>careers (about the time you found yourself writing the same application in
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>IT department for 10% of my needs?"

That last 10% DOUBLES the cost. Several of my recent projects followed
this pattern:

1. In-house system.
2. Management decision to replace in-house system with SAP.
3. Recognition that SAP R/3 cannot handle or is poor at --
rebates/discounts, supply chain optimization (which is why they offer
the APO system, also lacking), environmental regulation, R&D
management, the list goes on and on. 
4. Decision to retain in-house system for those functions.
5. Former development programmers now  writing interface programs that
feed data from SAP to Legacy and from Legacy to SAP, often
cross-platform which requires new skills (contractors).
    Former legacy maintenance programmers still maintaining.

The result is a costly IT department PLUS millions spent on SAP PLUS
interfaces.
```

```
news:kis7p09gorss1t3a7j8nqeh6godujnlm2s@4ax.com...
> On Wed, 10 Nov 2004 22:57:11 +1300, "Pete Dashwood"

>
> >OK, Robert. So who do you blame for this (if we were going to blame
anyone,
> >and we all know that assigning blame is a pretty pointless exercise
anyway)?
>
> In politics, assigning blame is everything.

That's one reason why many democracies are not happy with their politicians.
The avoidance of blame also promotes the avoidance of responsibility.

We have an interesting situation on tonight's news here in NZ. The Prime
Minister was driven at high speed by a number of senior and well qualified
police officers, in order to attend a Rugby match. (You have to understand
that Rugby in New Zealand is not a sport, it is a Religion, (like many
religions, a Big Business...) and the whole country goes into mourning when
the All Blacks lose... It is therefore pretty important to the PM to be
there for a major match).

Anyway, half a dozen senior policemen are now charged with dangerous driving
(anything over 120 KPH is considered "dangerous"; these cars averaged 140KPH
for around 90 minutes). They were in the South Island so the risk was pretty
low (You can drive on some South Island roads and not see another car for
over an hour...) but they each stand to lose their licences for a minum 6
months, plus whatever other disciplinary measures their Bosses and/or the
Courts see fit to impose.

Wouldn't you think the PM might say something on their behalf? Maybe: "My
fault, I asked them to hurry..." or some such? Not a bit of it. She is
keeping a very low profile and refusing reporters with: "It's sub judice, I
can't comment."

Her spin doctors are putting as much distance between her and the escort as
possible, saying she was working in the back seat of the limousine and had
no idea how fast it was travelling...

Gives you a real warm feeling to see a female politician who is so caring...
<G>. I'm glad our country is in good hands.

(I guess a lot of my American friends are feeling that way since recent
events there. There were several thousand applications by Americans for
emigration to NZ when the results were announced. Particularly from Gays.
Gay marriage is legal here and socially acceptable. The PM is generally
accepted to be a Lesbian (she neither confirms nor denies, but does admit
that she only got married for the sake of her political career). Apparently,
we are portrayed  in the US as an unspoilt natural paradise where Gays can
cavort with Hobbits and Elves, and everything is pink or lavender.

Not where I live. I have one friend who is Gay and he has never come on to
me in any way, so we have stayed friends for years.)

> >If managers buy a package from the vendor who bought them the best lunch
> ><G>, instead of properly evaluating the business requirements against the
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> to middle-managers produces the expected result -- business as before.

Only if those middle managers are not properly managed... <G>

> Well-managed companies tolerate the legacy system near-term, until the
> phase-out plan can be executed .. typically in 1-2 years.
>

I thought about this, Robert, and I'm inclined to agree with you. It is a
good (if expensive) strategy. A major corporation I worked with in the U.K.
did exactly that. I was responsible for managing their legacy systems (a
team of around 15) WHILE they implemented the Siebel replacement (a team of
around 40). It took around 18 months. We were not allowed to enhance the
existing systems, but we sure had them running smoothly by the time the
Siebel solution struggled to its feet.  On reflection, I think it was a
pretty good management strategy, but they could afford to pay �5 million a
year to keep things running, as well as �24 million for Siebel... Many
(especially smaller) companies couldn't do that.

Packages often fail when they are thrust into companies overnight, without
proper regard for the Business, and are under the control of middle managers
who are resisting change anyway. I think that was your point?

Pete.
```

```
> That above sentiment is what irks me about some folks - all we hear

…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>
The democracy they so cherish - that's why the Daily Mirror (in the UK) 
,  queried, "Why can some 59 million people be so wrong ?" ( It's a UK 
daily tabloid  and definitely a Labour/Socialist newspaper, cleverly 
crafted over the past century keeping to small words to convey their 
message to blue-collar workers. Currently its circulation is seeing hard 
times, so provocative headlines are "in").

I take their point; while it is a majority it is nevertheless almost a 
50-50 split.

Your blog indicates you are both right-wing and an 'evangelical' - 
nothing wrong with that - but you have to consider the middle ground.

You and I both *volunteered* for the military putting in roughly the 
same number of years I guess. In both cases, a USAF programmer and RAF 
secretarial/shorthand-typing - we  were, because of our training,  not 
*assets* to be wasted lightly by being pushed into the front-line at 
Fallujah..

If someone does volunteer, then they have to take it as it comes. 
(Subject to military rules you may be able to buy your way out of your 
'contract' - but I doubt that's possible during a war situation). Now 
what I do strongly disagree with is two US volunteers, who concluded 
they didn't want fun and games in Iraq and opted to slip across the 
border into Canada - seeking immunity.

However, if the situation is such that 'conscription' is introduced, 
then in all conscience, young men have the right to consider their 
country's  political situation - and if they don't agree,  then, as 
during WWII,  they can become conscientious objectors - with the 
penalties that can entail. Given your current situation, in good 
conscience, they have the right to opt out. In practical terms that may 
mean making a physical break with the US, and perhaps more 
significantly, their family members left behind. (In practical terms, I 
doubt the actual exodus from the US will be any great numbers).

Saved this for you - because in a previous message you tried to 
segregate 'Islam' from 'terrorists' in your mind. I'm paraphrasing to 
keep it short :-

Sunday Nov 7, 2004 - Hussein Dakroub AP, Beirut :

SAUDI SCHOLARS SUPPORT HOLY WAR :

"Some 26 prominent Saudi religious scholars have called on Iraqis to 
support insurgents waging holy-war against the US-led coalition saying 
fighting the occupation was a duty and a right. (In a letter posted on 
the Internet on Saturday). It was "legitimate" resistance. They issued a 
fatwa, prohibiting Iraqis from offering support for US-coalition against 
rebel strongholds.

"Fighting the occupiers is a duty for all those who are able. It is a 
jihad to push back the assailants... Resistance is a legitimate right. A 
Muslim must  not inflict harm on any resistance man or inform about 
them. Instead they should be supported and protected".

**** Remember I previously mentioned Israel. Put a rag head on American 
TV and ask him about a problem like increasing the productivity of 
growing dates - sure as eggs is eggs - although not relevant to the 
topic - the word Jew will spew out of his mouth !
**** Now I continue quote :

"The scholars said inter-Iraqi fighting would cause 'great damage to the 
Iraqis and give a free service to the Jews who are infiltrating into 
Iraq and to the coalition forces which exploit differences to 
consolidate their domination".
******

I would suggest Ruben Goldberg has got to be out of his tiny mind if 
he's currently thinking of setting up shop in Iraq - oil or no bloody 
oil ! I'd love to see some Muslim give me a set of quotes from the 
Koran, where it authorizes killing anybody. And what religion in the 
name of Allah or  nationalism can send a 16-year old boy/girl on a 
suicide mission of self-destruction. (The boy is supposed to get some 72 
virgins when he makes it up stairs. Not confirmed, but I suppose the 
girl gets 72 studs ?)

I'm afraid the words 'Muslim' and 'Arab' mix just like vinegar and olive 
oil.

Jimmy
```

```
Robert Jones wrote:

>"James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca> wrote in message news:<pvgld.210666$%k.192508@pd7tw2no>...
>
…[27 more quoted lines elided]…

A good reasoned response Robert. I wouldn't disagree with you one bit 
about the Caucasian and Christian connection - historically there has 
probably been more 'evil'  generated by Christians than any other 
religious group, Crusades, Reformation (both sides), Pogroms in Imperial 
Russia, anti-Semitism, (No - not *just* the Nazis),  etc.

 Just like you in UK where you now have a pretty polyglot population, 
here in Canada, although still predominantly 'Christian' when pollsters 
take figures, we have our  mix of Arab and Asian immigrants and agreed 
the majority  just want to be good 'Canucks'.  They came here to get 
away from their homeland poverty or 'troubles'.

I recall from my days in Egypt in the RAF (1951 - '54) - the average 
fellaheen (rural farmer), just wants to keep his head above water and 
nurture his own family group. (Other than knowing that they had got rid 
of their bloated King, subsequently replaced by Gamel Abdel Nasser, that 
was probably the extent of his politics - what actually went on in Cairo 
had no direct impact on him in his daily living).

The fanaticism comes from urban dwellers, (just like the IRA problem is 
centred around Belfast, or that jerk in the mosque in Finsbury, N. 
London.). Urban dwellers, be it Amman or Baghdad,  they are the ones 
'aware' of politics - and if you arrive at a particular point of view, 
you have a captive urban audience to address your ideas to.

It's sad when you think the three monotheistic religions all have their 
routes in the cradle of civilization. I once wrote an article around 
Christmas time for a charity, expressing this very idea, quoting St. 
Paul, "Faith, Hope and Charity. And the greatest of these is 
Charity(Love)". I contacted a rabbi and a Muslim and got parallel quotes 
for the Jewish Bible and the Koran - wish I had those references handy. 
If only all three groups could get back to the basics, their original 
creed and ignore the writings of subsequent writers who have just 
expanded on themes, sometimes distorting their respective sacred books.

Step One in the Middle East - a compromise to achieve *peaceful*  Israel 
and Palestinian states. What else goes on in the Middle East is just 
fluff - the current Iraq problem is just a side issue. I have perhaps 
reached the stage of cynicism on this one, but don't forget I've lived 
over 50 years as an adult - watching what is happening out there. Sadly, 
I don't see it being resolved any time soon.

PS: In case anyone is trying to guess - I'm a catholic but haven't been 
near a catholic church in decades, (well, baptisms, marriages and 
funerals). I have fostered an intense dislike of *organized* religion.

Jimmy
```

```
the world, one bomb at a time.

> Sunday Nov 7, 2004 - Hussein Dakroub AP, Beirut :
> 
> SAUDI SCHOLARS SUPPORT HOLY WAR :

[snip]

> I would suggest Ruben Goldberg has got to be out of his tiny mind if 
> he's currently thinking of setting up shop in Iraq - oil or no bloody 
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> oil.

Arafat is dead.  I heard some folks talk about who might replace him, 
and if that person would be any more amenable to peace than Arafat was. 
  One of the commentators used the phrase "the well has been poisoned" - 
they've been taught hate for so long that any prospective leader would 
have been taught from day 1 to hate Israel.

And people act like folks here are bigots if they're uncomfortable 
around someone who's not like them.  I guess everything's relative...
```
