"LX-i" <lxi0007@netscape.net> wrote in message
news:CVdld.899$aW.179@fe40.usenetserver.com...
> Pete Dashwood wrote:
> >
> > (I guess a lot of my American friends are feeling that way since recent
> > events there. There were several thousand applications by Americans for
> > emigration to NZ when the results were announced. Particularly from
Gays.
> > Gay marriage is legal here and socially acceptable. The PM is generally
> > accepted to be a Lesbian (she neither confirms nor denies, but does
admit
> > that she only got married for the sake of her political career).
Apparently,
> > we are portrayed  in the US as an unspoilt natural paradise where Gays
can
> > cavort with Hobbits and Elves, and everything is pink or lavender.
> >
> > Not where I live. I have one friend who is Gay and he has never come on
to
> > me in any way, so we have stayed friends for years.)
>
> Are the Hobbits still there?  ;)

Absolutely. "Hobbiton" (where the Shire was filmed) is around 30 minutes
drive from where I'm writing this.(It is called the Waikato Valley, the
township was "Matamata" (although I believe there are moves afoot to
officially rename it "Hobbiton"). You can go and see Bag End, and it is well
worth a visit.
>
> That above sentiment is what irks me about some folks - all we hear
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Hey hold on a minute!!!! What about OUR republic?!!!<G>

There was an interview on TV here with a Gay American who emigrated two
years ago (well before the Bush -Kerry contest) with his partner, because
same-sex marriage was illegal in the state they lived in. They got "married"
in Wellington.

I couldn't help feeling some sympathy for their point of view. Why we can't
we just live and let live? (Well, here we do... but it would be really cool
if the world could do it too...). That is not to say we are paradise or have
no problems. But we are working on them and we try to do it fairly.

Actually, Daniel, I completely agree with your sentiment regarding
democracy.

There was a time when "democracy" meant the majority ruled. (With due regard
and respect for the minority). If you lost the election (and felt passionate
about losing) you set about changing minds by debate and reasoned, unheated,
persuasion so that you  (hopefully) didn't lose next time. If you eventually
came to the conclusion that the population at large were NEVER going to
agree with your viewpoint, you had to consider either changing it or finding
somewhere a bit more liberal to live (I think that's what the Gays are
doing...)

This concept seems to have been lost on many people today. If they lose,
they decide (as a minority) to bomb and terrorise the majority into
submission. The idea is to destroy democracy because it didn't go their way,
and they have more chance of achieving their goals if there is anarchy.
Leaving the country, when viewed against this alternative, is a much more
civilized course of action.

(I just would hate to think of everyone here wearing pink and lavender...<G>
It is a very green place...)

Pete.
```

they who are urinating on the institution of marriage, it's the 50% 
divorce rate, and celebrities (Britney Spears or Nicky Hilton, anyone?). 
  They also say they can't will things to their "partner", which is 
absolute bunk - I could will my effects to a stranger, make another one 
the beneficiary of my insurance policy...

Although marriage is, in some ways, a civil ceremony, it was also 
started way back when by you-know-Who - and He created Adam and Eve, not 
Adam and Steve.  :)

> Actually, Daniel, I completely agree with your sentiment regarding

…[4 more quoted lines elided]…

The first part is what I try to do with my blog (which, I guess, at 
least one person here reads occasionally...  :>  ) - educate.  And that, 
I think, is the biggest failure of Bush's first term.  Never in my 
memory has there been such a stark difference between candidates, and it 
was still close!  Had the President used his position to educate the 
American people on exactly why it is he does some of the things he does, 
I think the election would have been a lot less close.

> This concept seems to have been lost on many people today. If they lose,
> they decide (as a minority) to bomb and terrorise the majority into
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> civilized course of action.

> It is a very green place...)

Maybe they just want to travel there to be "happy".  ;)

- **Date:** 2004-11-14T10:06:41-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0411141006.1ed06ea3@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<cmb01c$1gm4$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <cmgk9e$742$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <1099788832.A+t5mc/MBSk6YJDwEDwkpg@teranews> <cmo66b$oc1$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <2var03F2j6ia2U1@uni-berlin.de> <d903p0t5ivups1c5vk21utbcllj5vf7h4r@4ax.com> <2ve6vsF2jo78oU1@uni-berlin.de> <kis7p09gorss1t3a7j8nqeh6godujnlm2s@4ax.com> <2vjbvtF2j3ndaU1@uni-berlin.de> <CVdld.899$aW.179@fe40.usenetserver.com> <2vm7fuF2jh99hU1@uni-berlin.de> <W1Cld.1224$aW.357@fe40.usenetserver.com>`

```
LX-i <lxi0007@netscape.net> wrote 

>   They also say they can't will things to their "partner", which is 
> absolute bunk - I could will my effects to a stranger, make another one 
> the beneficiary of my insurance policy...

You could do, yes, but your relatives would probably contest the will
and would probably win in court.  The courts would be less likely to
overturn a will to a wife. Having a legal partnership giving it the
status equivqlent of marriage would  make the will less able to be
challenged.

Actually in NZ there are 'standards' of entitlement which can be used
by courts in the event of no will or when a will is challenged.  For
example you cannot disinheret your wife and children.

In NZ we also have the 'Matrimonial Property Act' (acually it's called
something else now) which basically says that all property owned by a
married couple is owned 50-50 by each partner. For example if a house
is just in one person's name the other still owns half of it. And they
don't even have to be married if they are living as if they were
married, even if they are same-sex.
```

On 14 Nov 2004 10:06:41 -0800, riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard) wrote:

>LX-i <lxi0007@netscape.net> wrote 
>
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>married, even if they are same-sex.

In the US, marriage is regulated by States, not the federal
government. Fifteen US States and Canada recognize 'common law
marriage'. Canada allows same-sex; the US doesn't (many have tried and
failed). About half of US States have 'community property laws', which
split assets AND LIABILITIES (including those hidden from the
partner) 50-50.
```

- **References:** `<cmgk9e$742$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <1099788832.A+t5mc/MBSk6YJDwEDwkpg@teranews> <cmo66b$oc1$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <2var03F2j6ia2U1@uni-berlin.de> <d903p0t5ivups1c5vk21utbcllj5vf7h4r@4ax.com> <2ve6vsF2jo78oU1@uni-berlin.de> <kis7p09gorss1t3a7j8nqeh6godujnlm2s@4ax.com> <2vjbvtF2j3ndaU1@uni-berlin.de> <CVdld.899$aW.179@fe40.usenetserver.com> <2vm7fuF2jh99hU1@uni-berlin.de> <W1Cld.1224$aW.357@fe40.usenetserver.com> <217e491a.0411141006.1ed06ea3@posting.google.com> <okigp0pjkg6qka012vtqjste7lah4c08c0@4ax.com>`

```
Robert Wagner wrote:

>On 14 Nov 2004 10:06:41 -0800, riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard) wrote:
>
…[21 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Only 'partially' correct with respect to Canada - and not a topic I 
follow assiduously. Any Canucks care to confirm/disagree with what I'm 
about to write ?

A few provinces have 'allowed' same-sex marriage within their 
jurisdictions, but I believe marriage is a federal 'perogative' - it all 
gets pushed by our bloody Charter of Rights - when we quite happily 
operated on basic British Common Law since Confederation in 1837 (?). 
You can safely assume it isn't going to be 'authorized' any time soon 
here in Alberta which is 'Texas North' with 1 in 10 being a US citizen, 
(some 80,000) - and to my surprise some of those have dual-citizenship 
and they all appear to have got actively involved in your current 
presidential election.

I've said before, to my own knowledge we have had catholic prime 
ministers since 1975 - Trudeau, (Quebec), Clarke (Alberta), Turner 
(Ontario), Mulroney(Quebec), ******, Chretien(Quebec) and currently Paul 
Martin(Quebec). The "****" was female Kim Campbell's "six-month summer 
job" as Chretien put it - she was the only non-catholic.

So just like John Kerry and the homosexual issue - what was their 
solution. (1) Don't mention your Catholicism or the topic, until (2) 
along comes Jean Chretien and as it is a political hot potato, submit to 
our Supreme Court, asking them "Does this draft legislation look OK to 
you, before we try to ram it through parliament with a Liberal majority 
?". The justices are still deliberating, meanwhile new Prime Minister 
Martin finishes up with a minority government - so he's also hoping the 
judges will get him out of a hole. (Meanwhile some of his Liberal MPs 
are not happy campers about the topic). It's funny that the press refers 
to Martin as being a 'devout catholic' -  even on his campaign swings, 
always ensuring he attends Sunday mass.

It's difficult to gauge how the country feels as a whole. Certainly 
Christian groups, "evangelicals', Mormons, Hutterites, Mennonites, 
'conservative' catholics, Bhuddists, Hindus, Islamists, Sikhs etc.,  
feel affronted by use of the word 'marriage' applied to same sex unions. 
I can only guess that the aforementioned groups might go with 'same sex 
civil unions', officiated over by a registrar, and the legal rights 
giving a 'partner' access to property etc.

So your political structure in US gives a different slant from the 
Canadian perceived solution. But I for one am totally pissed off with 
judicial activism - something as emotive as this should be rejected for 
judgement and bounced back to parliament - either promoted as a Bill or 
go for a national referendum.

Now here's a interesting twist on using judges as the supreme arbiters. 
If you are a retired (pensionable) US citizen living in the UK or vice 
versa, both countries allow a cost of living index on your pension. No 
so for 'foreign' countries like Canada, Australia, New Zealand and South 
Africa. Your pension is based on the year you left the UK. The only 
champions for ex-pat pensioners were the Daily Telegraph and young 
Winston Churchill while an MP, (i.e., the old man's grandson). Got 
nowhere during the Maggie Thatcher years and bearing in mind Tony Blair 
is a SOCIALIST - it hasn't gone anywhere during his time either.

Some few years back a UK female retiree settled in S. Africa, 
(presumably family there), and found she got clobbered on the 
cost-of-living aspect. She tediously followed it through the UK legal 
system over quite a number of years. I think it's about two years back 
she got to make her case to the Court of Appeal. (Canadian government 
representatives, and possibly Aussies and New Zealanders too, were 
present to 'lend' moral support - plus of course they have a vested 
interest; and in Canada they make up some of the UK shortfall by 
increasing Canadian pensions).

So what did the British judge(s) conclude ? This was not something that 
could be determined by the judiciary; it was a political matter. So 
bounce it back to Parliament ! Ain't life great !

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT: demise of COBOL WAS: Infinite Loops and Explicit Exits

- **From:** "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2004-11-20T13:13:43+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<307ghuF2quo69U1@uni-berlin.de>`
- **References:** `<cmgk9e$742$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <r0Vmd.502$3K3.65@fe40.usenetserver.com> <cnhsn2$phh$1@panix5.panix.com> <7sgnd.782$3K3.459@fe40.usenetserver.com> <cnkhcs$br9$1@panix5.panix.com>`

```

<docdwarf@panix.com> wrote in message news:cnkhcs$br9$1@panix5.panix.com...
<snip>
>
> Well, one has to be careful with colors... after all, white is the color
> of the flag of surrender and folks might object to being associated with
> that, as well.
>
Reading this reminded me of how powerfully these symbols are embedded in our
collective subconscious.

One of the national symbols for NZ is the native "silver fern".  The All
Blacks (our National Rugby team, for those of you who live off world...)
wear this symbol proudly embroidered in white on their black jerseys.

For a long time, the Royal New Zealand Air Force also used the silver fern
as a symbol, and displayed it inside the red of the red, white, and blue
roundels on our aircraft.

However, a symbol of cowardice in our culture is a white feather (it comes
from the practice of cockfighting in Mediaeval England, where a white
feather in the tail of a cock was taken as a sign of degenerate breeding,
and not a pure bred gamecock), and the silver fern can look like one of
these...

RNZAF people stationed in Malaya were constantly in fist fights with the
Australians also stationed there because of reference to the "white feather"
shown on our aircraft. Today, our aircraft (what is left of them; the
current PM has worked very hard to de-motivate and disband our air force)
carry a small white kiwi inside the red roundel...

Ironic that an almost blind, flightless, bird has become the symbol of an
air force...Ironic, but appropriate.

Pete.


> DD
>
>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT: demise of COBOL WAS: Infinite Loops and Explicit Exits

_(reply depth: 40)_

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com
- **Date:** 2004-11-19T19:44:58-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<cnm42a$ivt$1@panix5.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<cmgk9e$742$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <7sgnd.782$3K3.459@fe40.usenetserver.com> <cnkhcs$br9$1@panix5.panix.com> <307ghuF2quo69U1@uni-berlin.de>`

```
In article <307ghuF2quo69U1@uni-berlin.de>,
Pete Dashwood <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote:
>
><docdwarf@panix.com> wrote in message news:cnkhcs$br9$1@panix5.panix.com...
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>One of the national symbols for NZ is the native "silver fern".

[snip]

>However, a symbol of cowardice in our culture is a white feather (it comes
>from the practice of cockfighting in Mediaeval England, where a white
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>shown on our aircraft.

... and these were two groups (RNZAF and Australians) who are of similar 
(Brit-based) background... imagine the confusion encountered by, say, a 
native of Bhutan trying to figure out how yellow can be the color for both 
cowardice and caution.

This is something that hits me when I watch Japanese films... I *know* 
there are references that are just tossed off and understood by members of 
this ancient, arrogant, ethnocentric culture... but which of the various 
symbols *are* they?  In 'Merry Christmas, Mr Laurence' (a British/Japanese 
collaboration) there is a scene of David Bowie, buried up to his neck in 
the earth... and a white moth lands on his forehead.  Now I know that 
white is the traditional funeral-color in Japan... but a white moth, what 
is that extra bit about?  A raven (black bird) is common in my society to 
signify death and foreboding but a black cat is mere bad luck (while in 
other parts of the world a black cat is good luck... but never mind 
that)... the color denotes something negative, then, but changing the 
animal changes the association.  What about the white moth, then?

The other one that comes to mind is from the film 'The Narayama Trilogy'; 
during a rainstorm there are shots of buildings, plants and puddles in the 
rain... and there is one shot, very brief, of two frogs copulating under a 
leaf.  Is there a phrase in Japanese about this - 'as happy as frogs 
copulating under a leaf in the rain', similar to 'as snug as a bug in a 
rug' - or is it something else?

These are confusions caused by members of my own species... I'll leave 
extraterrestrials for others, for now.

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ OT: Purple country

_(reply depth: 39)_

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2004-11-19T15:53:23+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<cnl4tk$19l$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<cmgk9e$742$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <USzmd.242$3K3.32@fe40.usenetserver.com> <cnf8ap$rpa$1@panix5.panix.com> <r0Vmd.502$3K3.65@fe40.usenetserver.com> <cnhsn2$phh$1@panix5.panix.com> <7sgnd.782$3K3.459@fe40.usenetserver.com> <joe_zitzelberger-F36CE8.09335019112004@knology.usenetserver.com>`

```
The following map mixes red and blue by population.

http://www.boingboing.net/images/Purple-USA.jpg


The following is in shades by county:

http://www.princeton.edu/~rvdb/JAVA/election2004/

The following has a bunch of maps that have had the states adjusted so that the
most populous states are largest:

http://www-personal.umich.edu/~mejn/election/


What is interesting to note is that the divide wasn't between red states and
blue states, but it was between urban voters and rural voters.

IMHO, a candidate who wants the rural votes needs to talk slowly, with a
friendly smile.   While city folk think country folk are foolish bumpkins,
country folk believe city folk are fast talking used car salesmen.     Invite a
country person to a New York Sunday dinner, and after the city people will
winder why the country person was so stand-offish and unfriendly.   The country
cousin wonders why the city people were so rude that they never allowed him to
talk - they were too busy talking and interrupting each other to allow a polite
person a word in edgewise.

The last fast-talking president to be elected was Kennedy, who was also the last
Democrat from a non-confederate state to be elected.

Only one person born in New England has ever been re-elected President of the
U.S., which happened this month.

To a country person - someone who is slow talking is friendly and listens, and
is often wise.   To a city person - someone who is slow talking is slow.
```

On 15-Nov-2004, docdwarf@panix.com wrote:

> >I'd love to see it go
> >back there, especially in an era when palimony and child support are not
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> Brazee, then what constitutes 'spousedom' is a matter of law.

If it is a law that says I can not choose to have my wife as my medical
insurance partner instead of my mother - that law needs to be changed.    I
already mentioned that marriage is no longer a requirement for palimony and
child support.    I don't agree in giving tax breaks depending upon marital
status.

So which laws that confer particular privileges upon a spouse should be kept?

I'm in favor of separation between church and state.

but until such time as they are changed they are still in effect.

On 16-Nov-2004, docdwarf@panix.com wrote:

> Mr Zitzelberger, you seem to be ignoring just a few minor laws which
> confer upon a spouse a direct legal status different than any other
> person... have you ever heard of, say, 'spousal privelege' applied to
> abstaining from offering legal testimony?

Is there a similar privilege protecting a mother from testifying against her
child?   Or is this a left-over from the era when spouses were considered
"owned"?    Can this privilege be modified to include people who "live in sin"?

I know of a pair of retired people who "live in sin" because the government
would cut back their retirement benefits if they were to be married.   I suppose
that means one could be sent to jail for failing to testify against the other.

I suppose the state could declare them common-law married and force them to take
a benefit cut.   But I don't see that happening.
```

In article <joe_zitzelberger-FAF17E.18560916112004@knology.usenetserver.com>,
Joe Zitzelberger  <joe_zitzelberger@nospam.com> wrote:
>In article <cncaen$kv6$1@panix5.panix.com>, docdwarf@panix.com wrote:
>
…[51 more quoted lines elided]…
>such things, and states which do not have such things.

Not that the existence of such a thing will, in all probability, change 
your view a whit, Mr Zitzelberger, but yes, there seems to be such a 
thing.  From:

http://www.huskylaw.net/StudyGuides/hardisty_familylaw_2000.doc

--begin quoted text:

D. Evidentiary Consequences of Marriage
1.  Privilege as to Testimony by Spouses

Historically the common law rule in every state rendered spouses 
incompetent to testify for or against each other in both criminal and 
civil proceedings.  Some states have abolished the spousal privilege 
altogether in civil and criminal cases, while a few states still maintain 
a complete ban on testimony.  In Federal court, the privilege against 
testimony may be asserted only by the witness-spouse.

--end quoted text

>
>
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>Indeed I have -- it is a bright and happy red color.

Mr Zitzelberger, how daring of you... but I am sure the honor to memory 
you show is pleasing to others who also think fondly of Chairman Mao.

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT: demise of COBOL WAS: Infinite Loops and Explicit Exits

_(reply depth: 39)_

- **From:** Joe Zitzelberger <joe_zitzelberger@nospam.com>
- **Date:** 2004-11-18T02:19:13-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<joe_zitzelberger-A483DC.02191318112004@knology.usenetserver.com>`
- **References:** `<cmgk9e$742$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <joe_zitzelberger-FAF17E.18560916112004@knology.usenetserver.com> <cnebpg$jqd$1@panix5.panix.com> <joe_zitzelberger-33A68C.08595017112004@knology.usenetserver.com> <cnfnnf$o2q$1@panix5.panix.com>`

```
In article <cnfnnf$o2q$1@panix5.panix.com>, docdwarf@panix.com wrote:

> In article <joe_zitzelberger-33A68C.08595017112004@knology.usenetserver.com>,
> Joe Zitzelberger  <joe_zitzelberger@nospam.com> wrote:
…[42 more quoted lines elided]…
> whit...'

I do not mean 'care' as in 'be concerned with', but 'care' as in 'ever 
confront such a situtation'.

From the various crime statistics (see doj.gov), we know that most crime 
is comitted by late-teen, early-20-something males.  As they age, they 
are more likely to be married (a function of consent laws) and less 
likely to commit crimes.

We also know that most people are basically law abiding, somewhere in 
the high 90 percential. (excluding minor traffic violations and the 
like)  

We also know that most crimes are state crimes, not federal.

So it is quite easy to conclude that only a very small minority of 
married people will have spouses commit a federal crime.

An even smaller subgroup will be caught, charged and enter a not guilty 
plea.

Of that tiny subgroup, the spouse must have some knowledge of the crime 
in question for this right of spousal non-testimony to become relevent.

So perhaps I should have phrased 'hardly something most people care 
about' as 'hardly something most people will ever encounter'.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT: demise of COBOL WAS: Infinite Loops and Explicit Exits

_(reply depth: 40)_

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com
- **Date:** 2004-11-18T05:25:23-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<cnhtaj$nsk$1@panix5.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<cmgk9e$742$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <joe_zitzelberger-33A68C.08595017112004@knology.usenetserver.com> <cnfnnf$o2q$1@panix5.panix.com> <joe_zitzelberger-A483DC.02191318112004@knology.usenetserver.com>`

```
In article <joe_zitzelberger-A483DC.02191318112004@knology.usenetserver.com>,
Joe Zitzelberger  <joe_zitzelberger@nospam.com> wrote:
>In article <cnfnnf$o2q$1@panix5.panix.com>, docdwarf@panix.com wrote:
>
…[47 more quoted lines elided]…
>confront such a situtation'.

Oh, *that* definition of 'care'... I should have realised you were using 
it as it is such a common one...

... and I am the King of England, of course.

[snip]

>So perhaps I should have phrased 'hardly something most people care 
>about' as 'hardly something most people will ever encounter'.

If the situation is so rare as to be dismissible then what might your 
reason have been for asking into it?  

A spouse has certain legal rights which no other person has, one of these 
is spousal immunity.  You asked if spousal immunity exists on the Federal 
level and I responded 'According to (citation) it does... (n)ot that the 
existence of such a thing will, in all probability, change your view a 
whit...'.

You then shift from addressing the documented fact that a spouse has 
certain legal rights which no other person has to 'Oh, the situation I 
asked about is so rare it doesn't matter'... indicating that the existence 
of such a thing has not changed your view a whit.  

Do tell, Mr Zitzelberger... if a spouse does not have legal rights that 
you believe are 'hardly something most people care about' then what legal 
basis is there for caring about who, among the population that is 
considered capable of giving informed consent, assumes the status?

DD
```

In article <cnhtaj$nsk$1@panix5.panix.com>, docdwarf@panix.com wrote:

…[58 more quoted lines elided]…
> ... and I am the King of England, of course.

Have you never thought about the origins of the phrase 'why should I 
care?' or its gen-x equilivent 'I care.'?

An SUV driver might care about the melting polar ice cap in an abstract, 
hand-wringing, ohh-thats-terrible way.  But the same persone will _CARE_ 
when their living room is filled with water.

Care is a good generic word that runs the gamut from 'mildly disinterest 
in' to 'deeply invested in'.


> [snip]
> 
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
> of such a thing has not changed your view a whit.  

You brought this one up -- not I.  I only asserted that there were few 
places of federal recognition of marriage.  And the two most notable  
spousal rights on the list were of the nature of 'benefit 
qualifications'.  

State law is the vast majority of marital related law in the U.S.  Even 
the above two recognitions I cited and the testamoney exemption require 
a state to actually certify your marriage.  

There may well be other federal benefits to being married, but I suspect 
the are mostly of the obscure, minor variety.  Hardly reasons for two 
people to rush out and get married.


> 
> Do tell, Mr Zitzelberger... if a spouse does not have legal rights that 
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

'Informed consent' is a function of state law and not always related to 
marriage.  Various family members are often automatically granted the 
implicit power of attorney to decide matters of informed concent.  And 
in all cases, a person can execute an explicit power of attorney for 
medical decisions.

Are not the spouses of diplomats also issued special credentials to keep 
them out of trouble in the host country?  Is this a federal benefit of 
marriage that most people care about?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT: demise of COBOL WAS: Infinite Loops and Explicit Exits

_(reply depth: 46)_

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com
- **Date:** 2004-11-19T14:16:11-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<cnlgpr$870$1@panix5.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<cmgk9e$742$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <joe_zitzelberger-5FBD61.09580019112004@knology.usenetserver.com> <cnl2pl$b52$1@panix5.panix.com> <joe_zitzelberger-0F9B46.13394719112004@knology.usenetserver.com>`

```
In article <joe_zitzelberger-0F9B46.13394719112004@knology.usenetserver.com>,
Joe Zitzelberger  <joe_zitzelberger@nospam.com> wrote:
>In article <cnl2pl$b52$1@panix5.panix.com>, docdwarf@panix.com wrote:
>
…[47 more quoted lines elided]…
>What does the 'status of spouse' have to do with informed consent?

At present those incapable of giving informed consent are hindered from 
taking the status of a spouse (see the phrase 'age of consent'); this also 
removes the difficulties some see possible with minors as spouses or 
interspecies relationships as neither minors nor other species are capable 
(by present legal standards) of giving informed consent.

>My 
>example showed pretty clearly that at least one large state does not 
>confer any special status upon the spouse as opposed to any other of the 
>group able to provide informed consent.

That is why I rephrased the question.  Infomed consent is something that 
one has to be able to give *before* one becomes a spouse, it is not 
something a person gives for another.

>
>The question does not seem to be making much sense to me as there seems 
>to be no legal need for someone to assume spousal status.

In order to obtain the legal rights of a spouse one must assume the status 
of a spouse.

>
>However, assuming I understand what you are asking, the legal basis for 
>most things that are not specifically codified into law by a legislature 
>exists in the 8 centuries of English common law that we inherited from 
>them.

Mr Zitzelberger, I will try one last time:

A person is hindered from becoming a spouse unless that person is capable 
of giving informed consent.  (eg, minors, certain mentally handicapped 
persons and animals cannot give informed consent and therefore cannot 
become spouses)

(damnation... do you know how hard it is for me not to type the plural of 
'spouse' as 'spice'?)

A spouse is a person who, as a spouse, has certain legal rights ('spousal 
rights') which a person who is not a spouse does not have; the only way to 
obtain these rights (at present) is to assume the status of a spouse.

You have asserted that spousal rights are 'hardly something most people 
care about'.

If spousal rights are 'hardly something most people care about' then what 
legal basis is there for caring about who, among the population that is 
considered capable of giving informed consent, assumes the status of a 
spouse?

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT: demise of COBOL WAS: Infinite Loops and Explicit Exits

_(reply depth: 47)_

- **From:** Joe Zitzelberger <joe_zitzelberger@nospam.com>
- **Date:** 2004-11-20T15:07:06-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<joe_zitzelberger-371215.15070620112004@knology.usenetserver.com>`
- **References:** `<cmgk9e$742$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <joe_zitzelberger-5FBD61.09580019112004@knology.usenetserver.com> <cnl2pl$b52$1@panix5.panix.com> <joe_zitzelberger-0F9B46.13394719112004@knology.usenetserver.com> <cnlgpr$870$1@panix5.panix.com>`

```
In article <cnlgpr$870$1@panix5.panix.com>, docdwarf@panix.com wrote:

<snip>
> At present those incapable of giving informed consent are hindered from 
> taking the status of a spouse (see the phrase 'age of consent'); this also 
> removes the difficulties some see possible with minors as spouses or 
> interspecies relationships as neither minors nor other species are capable 
> (by present legal standards) of giving informed consent.

<and>
> In order to obtain the legal rights of a spouse one must assume the status 
> of a spouse.

I am almost certain we are speaking of two different concepts of 
'informed consent'.

My mostly medical family usually makes me think in medical terms.    
Informed consent, in that context is making decisions for ones self or 
other persons for whom you have responsibility after receiving the 
various information on risks and benefits or the proposed treatment.

This pretty much lines up with what www.m-w.com claims:

   Main Entry:  informed consent
   Function:  noun
   : consent to surgery by a patient or to participation
   in a medical experiment by a subject after achieving 
   an understanding of what is involved

Minor spouses do not enter into the discussion.  Either they have 
parents who still are responsible for medical decisions or the state in 
which the reside has an 'emancipated minor' approach (like Texas) in 
which the spouse of any age is granted that right.


<snip>
> That is why I rephrased the question.  Infomed consent is something that 
> one has to be able to give *before* one becomes a spouse, it is not 
> something a person gives for another.

It is frequently given for others.  Parents exercise 'informed consent' 
for their minor children.  Adult children exercise if for incapacitated 
parents.  In emergencies where no next-of-kin is available and a patient 
is incapacitated, judges are sometimes asked by medical personnel to 
make choices.

Becoming a spouse requires no informed consent -- this is why I do not 
grasp what you are asking.

(Unless some of your inter-species marriages require medical procedures 
... but I really don't understand that.)


> Mr Zitzelberger, I will try one last time:
> 
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> become spouses)

One often has others provide consent for them.  I hear tale from various 
talking heads that many in the rural red-state require parental 
signatures before joining their first cousins in matrimony.

If those tales are true, then it would seem that parents can provide 
consent for their minor children.


> (damnation... do you know how hard it is for me not to type the plural of 
> 'spouse' as 'spice'?)

Isn't it?

> A spouse is a person who, as a spouse, has certain legal rights ('spousal 
> rights') which a person who is not a spouse does not have; the only way to 
> obtain these rights (at present) is to assume the status of a spouse.

There are very few that can be assumed only by spouses.

The top three commonly cited by gay-marriage advocates -- hospital 
visitation, inheritance and mortgage lending -- do not require spousal 
rights.

Hospital visitation is easily managed with a simple power of attorney.  
Inheritance is easily managed with a will.  And getting a mortgage is 
frequently done with non-spousal people -- business partners, parental 
cosigners, domestic partners, etc.

The two 'spousal rights' that exist in federal law that are spouse 
specific are married filing jointly tax returns and social security 
survivors benefits.  You did bring up a third -- that of non-testimony 
against ones spouse.  These three require spouses, but I'm hard pressed 
to think of more.


> You have asserted that spousal rights are 'hardly something most people 
> care about'.

I asserted that some spousal rights -- like non-testimony and diplomatic 
passports -- are 'hardly something most people care about'.  I also 
asserted that tax breaks and benefits are spousal rights that most 
people DO care about.

In fact, my original argument was that the push for non-traditional 
marriage is not about hospital visitation or such non-sensical tripe, it 
is about getting a slice of the benefit pie from big-brotherment.


> If spousal rights are 'hardly something most people care about' then what 
> legal basis is there for caring about who, among the population that is 
> considered capable of giving informed consent, assumes the status of a 
> spouse?

You are twisting what I have said and misusing the term informed consent.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT: demise of COBOL WAS: Infinite Loops and Explicit Exits

_(reply depth: 48)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2004-11-29T10:46:37-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<cofqqe$2hj4$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<cmgk9e$742$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <joe_zitzelberger-5FBD61.09580019112004@knology.usenetserver.com> <cnl2pl$b52$1@panix5.panix.com> <joe_zitzelberger-0F9B46.13394719112004@knology.usenetserver.com> <cnlgpr$870$1@panix5.panix.com> <joe_zitzelberger-371215.15070620112004@knology.usenetserver.com>`

```

"Joe Zitzelberger" <joe_zitzelberger@nospam.com> wrote in message
news:joe_zitzelberger-371215.15070620112004@knology.usenetserver.com...

> The top three commonly cited by gay-marriage advocates -- hospital
> visitation, inheritance and mortgage lending -- do not require spousal
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> cosigners, domestic partners, etc.

Why should two men or two women be required by the state, or by the federal
government, to address these individually?   For that matter, why should a
man and a woman *not* be required to address these individually, rather than
being accorded them automatically?

My personal view is that from the government's standpoint -- local, state
and federal -- marriage is a *civil contract* between two people who are
otherwise legally able to enter into binding contracts, and any effort to
restrict the physical characteristics of the signators to that contract to
complementary genitalia is way outside the bounds of what the government
needs to care about.

In one of the presidential debates, I recall a candidate saying something
about the government preserving the "sanctity of marriage" -- a phrase I
have heard often in discussions on this topic -- and that points to the
heart of the problem in my view:  Given the dictionary (e.g., Eighth New
Collegiate) definition for "sanctity", having the government decide what is
*legal* based on what the government, or even the popular vote, determines
is *holy* is a really bad idea.     I think federal and/or state laws
restricting marriage to a subset of the population fall in the same category
as laws that would seek to ban the eating of pork or shellfish on the
grounds that doing so needs to be treated as fundamentally abominable by the
government.

Some examples of formal denominations that I believe are friendlier to
same-sex marriages than this candidate seems to have been are:  Unitarian
Universalist, Religious Science, Divine Science, some Methodists, some
member churches of the Anglican Communion, at least the Open and Affirming
congregations of the United Church of Christ (the direct descendant of the
Pilgrims!) -- to say nothing of pretty much the entire population of the
Universal Fellowship of Metropolitan Community Churches.

I don't believe that the government, or the people at large, ought to be
deciding whether the doctrines and the ceremonies of some denominations -- 
Christian or otherwise -- are holier than those of others.

      -Chuck Stevens
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT: demise of COBOL WAS: Infinite Loops and Explicit Exits

_(reply depth: 34)_

- **From:** Robert Wagner <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net>
- **Date:** 2004-11-17T06:18:58+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8mblp0p2hb2b35s4dij3kgvh3l825i38jr@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<cmgk9e$742$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <W1Cld.1224$aW.357@fe40.usenetserver.com> <217e491a.0411141006.1ed06ea3@posting.google.com> <okigp0pjkg6qka012vtqjste7lah4c08c0@4ax.com> <cnb04h$prs$1@panix5.panix.com> <cnb1bs$quj$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <joe_zitzelberger-C64B47.02200616112004@knology.usenetserver.com>`

```
On Tue, 16 Nov 2004 02:20:06 -0500, Joe Zitzelberger
<joe_zitzelberger@nospam.com> wrote:

>Still the only two things keep marriage anywhere in the eyes of 
>government --
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>to a personal and spiritual thing...

The ones you cited (plus immigration) are the primary Federal
benefits, and the reason the US Supreme Court has standing to override
State definitions of marriage, but there are many State-level benefits
such as:

.. Family insurance -- health, auto
.. Medical decision making
.. Inheritance
.. Divorce. More a license for lawyers to gouge than a protection.
.. Adoption
.. Domestic violence protection
.. Crime victim recovery 
.. Bankruptcy (a Federal action that follows State rules)

I don't think government cares one way or the other who's married.
Like abortion, marriage is an emotional issue in which government
tries to gauge what the majority wants, then jumps in front to provide
'moral leadership', whichwas said to be the factor that pushed GWB
over the top in this month's election. It's a side-show that distracts
us from legitimite questions such as fighting in the Middle East.

My proposed solution to same-sex marriage moves it from an issue of
Law to an issue of Equity. Equity (also known as quasi-contracts, not
to be confused with home equity) is a poorly-understoon field of
jurisprudence that deals with contract-type situations in which the
remedy at Law is inadequate. In particular, it deals with cases where
the petitioning party has no Legal remedy because no contract exists.
Same-sex unions fall into that category. Marriage is a contract.

Equity provides a money judgment. Its objective is different from the
Law of Contracts in that Equity tries to do what's 'fair', which
usually makes the plaintiff (the injured party) 'even'. Contract Law
tries to provide the same outcome that would have occurred had the
contract been performed. Under Equity, the plaintiff does not get the
profit he or she would have gotten under Contract -- half the increase
in community property -- nor be liable for half the loss, if any.

Since Equity deals with cases lacking a Legal Contract, it seems to me
an Equitable 'divorce' should be possible today. A quick Web search
failed to find any such cases.

Suing government under Equity is more problematical. For instance, to
recover excess income tax.  Since the Feds are letting States decide
who's married, they can say your complaint should be against the
State. Citizens are not allowed to sue States under Law or Equity, per
the 11th Amendment to the US Constitution.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT: demise of COBOL WAS: Infinite Loops and Explicit Exits

_(reply depth: 34)_

- **From:** Robert Wagner <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net>
- **Date:** 2004-11-18T06:23:28+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<hg5op05grm7dm2bca77msfp51kqnlsa2n5@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<cmgk9e$742$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <W1Cld.1224$aW.357@fe40.usenetserver.com> <217e491a.0411141006.1ed06ea3@posting.google.com> <okigp0pjkg6qka012vtqjste7lah4c08c0@4ax.com> <cnb04h$prs$1@panix5.panix.com> <cnb1bs$quj$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <joe_zitzelberger-C64B47.02200616112004@knology.usenetserver.com>`

```
On Tue, 16 Nov 2004 02:20:06 -0500, Joe Zitzelberger
<joe_zitzelberger@nospam.com> wrote:


>Still the only two things keep marriage anywhere in the eyes of 
>government --
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>to a personal and spiritual thing...

I'm surprised no one has mentioned the most important Federal
legistlation on same-sex marriage: the Defense of Marriage Act (DOMA)
passed in 1996. It says:

1. The Federal government does not recognize same-sex marriage for any
Federal purpose. That includes Social Security, Income Tax and
immigration.

2.  States are not required to recognize same-sex marriages approved
by other States.

Text of the act is here:

http://www.lectlaw.com/files/leg23.htm

Out of fear the DOMA would be judged unconstitutional, the Marriage
Protection Act (MPA) was passed by the House in July, 2004. It is not
expected to pass the Senate. In a nutshell, it says the DOMA cannot be
reviewed/overturned by the Supreme Court. An analysis is here:

http://writ.news.findlaw.com/grossman/20040727.html

Same-sex couples can be legally married today in Massachusetts and
some Provinces of Canada, with no wait nor residency nor medical test
in Canada.  Undoing the marriage (or Vermont's quasi-marriage) may be
more than they bargained for. They cannot marry someone else in any
State without first getting divorced, but they can only get divorced
in the place where they were married .. after moving and living there
for one year to meet residency requirements.
```

Truly, this battle began being lost when the divorce rate skyrocketed, 
shacking up became the "in" thing to do.  Argue for stronger families, 
and the homo lobby says "We're not mocking marriage, it's the 50% 
divorce rate, Britney Spears, and Nicky Hilton who are doing that!"

While this may be true to some extent, the government does have a vested 
interest in maintaining strong families.  They ensure more stable, 
successful children, and a vibrant society.

(And, the cool part about this argument is, I've got roughly 60% of the 
country behind me on this one!)

wives, and children killing their parents. One would not like it, but

society.

It's nowhere close.  According to the US Department of Justice, the

My statement was based on the (ballpark) average margin of victory among 
the eleven states that had defense of marriage/anti-gay-marriage 
initiatives on the ballot.  In fact, voting in favor of a ban...

Arkansas     - 75% / 25%
Georgia      - 76% / 24%
Kentucky     - 75% / 25%
Michigan     - 59% / 41%
Mississippi  - 86% / 14%
Montana      - 67% / 33%
North Dakota - 73% / 27%
Ohio         - 62% / 38%
Oklahoma     - 76% / 24%
Oregon       - 57% / 43%
Utah         - 66% / 34%

That means that, among the 11 states, the average "for" vote was 70.2%. 
  So - thanks for correcting me.  *70%* of the country is behind me on 
this.  :)

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT: Marriage and the family

- **From:** l.willms@jpberlin.de (Lueko Willms)

- **References:** `<cmgk9e$742$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <9L0V9sHuflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de> <PkVmd.503$3K3.407@fe40.usenetserver.com>`

```
.    On  17.11.04
  wrote  lxi0007@netscape.net (LX-i)
     on  /COMP/LANG/COBOL
     in  PkVmd.503$3K3.407@fe40.usenetserver.com
  about  Re: OT: Marriage and the family


>> l>           the most basic build block of society, the family.
>>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>> beings.

l> We've got records from 2,000 B. C. or so, that indicate families.

   Sure, family is not _so_ recent a development. But beware that  
family is not and has not been the same in different social formations  
and for different classes.

   The word originated from Latin, and it included all people living  
under the jurisdiction of one man, including his slaves, not only a  
wife and offspring.

   Marriage always had different meaning for propertied classes as a  
means to acquire property, and for the feudal aristocracy as a means  
to distribute ruling posts. Love did not play a role in such a union,  
only the power and property which could be acquired by it.

   On the other hand, a family had virtually no meaning for e.g. a  
slave in the US-american plantation system. A human being was owned  
just as horses, mules, or hogs, and were treated as such.

   The North American Indians of the time of the beginning of North  
America's colonization, as described by Lewis H. Morgan in his famous  
book about "Ancient Society, or Researches in the Lines of Human  
Progress from Savagery, through Barbarism to Civilization" (London  
1877), did know a pairing family, or rather that couples lived  
together in a easily severable bond, but the woman would call all  
children of her sisters, actually feminine menbers of her clan, as  
their own children, but would call children of her brothers as  
nephews; the man would call not only his own, but also the children of  
his brothers as sons and doughters, but not those of his sisters.

   So this is quite a different concept of family from what is known  
in todays's capitalist society as family nucleus.

   Other "primitive" people had similar systems of relationships, and  
in part not even a pairing family.

   So, when one starts to look around, one cannot but recognize that  
"family" does mean quite different things for different people, or  
rather for different societies.

   The classic Greek tragedies reflect and discuss the transition from  
a matriarchal, or rather more egalitarian society to a patriarchal  
society; the Christian Old Testament is the manifest of the triumph of  
patriarchy, with the Ten Commandments as the basic law of patriarchal  
family, private property, and the state as the garantor of these  
relationhips of power, listing women as part of "thy neighbour's"  
property which should be sacrosanct.


l> As I believe evolution is complete bunk, and that the earth was
l> created some time around 4,000 B. C.,

   is that serious? Are you one of those "creationists"? I heard that  
such beliefs exist in the USA, but I had not imagined that I would  
meet one of those in person.

   So you would maintain that all matter, organic life, plus human  
society was created in one big bang 6 millenia ago? Whow.

l> I wouldn't add the "current" qualifier at all.  :)

   Actually, 6000 years is quite a short time in the evolution of  
humankind.

   Just today, I read a news item according to which scientists in the  
USA had found proof that humans had already settled in the Americas  
50'000 years ago, as opposed to the 13'000 years ago, which was up to  
now the common knowledge.

   Besides human fossiles in Africa, where humanity had its deepest  
roots, being even much older, it must be odd to know that human being  
have lived and procreated even in North America, many millenia before  
the world as such existed.


>> l> While this may be true to some extent, the government does have a
>> l> vested interest in maintaining strong families.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>> slave states did before the US Civil War did away with that?

l> No - but the laws should encourage the ideal.

   Well, different people have always had different ideals of how they  
would relate to other human beings, and even sexual and sensual  
relations between human beings have found very different forms in the  
long history of humankind.

   You would not go and try to force _your_ ideal on other people,  
making those unhappy? You know, the pursuit of happiness is a very  
basic ideal to the people of the USA.

>>   So, family is a mixed blessing. It provides protection, but also
>> chains and danger.

l> It's nowhere close.  According to the US Department of Justice, the
l> percentage is around 11%.  I looked to see if I could find concrete
l> numbers, and this is the page from which I got the 11% number.
l>
l> http://www.ojp.usdoj.gov/bjs/homicide/intimates.htm

   I will check that out later.


l> And, just because "there are" doesn't mean we should discard the
l> entire structure.

   Sure, I just mentioned it to illustrate the reality of family  
instead of an imagined ideal.


l> Research shows, however, that even homosexuals who
l> claim to be in a "committed" relationship appear to have a "nuanced"
l> definition of what "committed" means.

   Sure, this is completely independent from the sexual orientation of  
humands.


l> (This link has a lot of text -look at the chart "Percent Reporting
l> Sexual Fidelity" about 15% of the way down the page, and the text
l> surrounding it.)
l>
l> http://www.frc.org/get.cfm?i=IS04C02

>> l> (And, the cool part about this argument is, I've got roughly 60% of
>> l> the country behind me on this one!)
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>> wrong.

l> My statement was based on the (ballpark) average margin of victory
l> among the eleven states that had defense of marriage/anti-gay-marriage
l> initiatives on the ballot.  In fact, voting in favor of a ban...

   Ah, excuse me for my misunderstanding.

   But even in those votes -- in 11 federal states -- not all those  
eligible to vote have participated, so still the claim that 70% "of  
the country" shares your views is not correct. 70% of those in those  
11 federal states who took it upon them to cast a vote.

   And I think, these votes are a positive sign -- not necessarily  
their outcome, but the fact that the issue _is_ being debated.

   It is not the _end,_ but the _beginning_ of a big debate within the  
people of the USA, and I am confident that in the end, not only a big  
majority and not only of a relatively small part of voters will come  
to the conclusion that marriage and the family, and the sexual  
orientation is a private matter, and that nobody has the right to  
prevent a couple to marry just based on the sexual orientation of that  
couple.

   Especially when you are in favor of marriage as an  
institutionalized relationship, you should be in _favor_ of the right  
of _any_ couple to marry formally instead of just cohabitating "in  
sin", and thus to _strengthen_ this institution.

   Many of those male members of the propertied and ruling classes, as  
mentioned above, did marry a women, just for the economic advantages,  
and because it "had to be so", but actually had never had any sexual  
relationships with her spouse, but only with other men.

   What to you think does strengthen or weaken the institution of  
marriage?


Yours,
Lï¿½ko Willms                                     http://www.willms-edv.de
/--------- L.WILLMS@jpberlin.de -- Alle Rechte vorbehalten --

Die Superklugheit ist eine der verï¿½chtlichsten Arten von Unklugheit. -G.C.Lichtenberg
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT: Marriage and the family

_(reply depth: 41)_

- **From:** LX-i <lxi0007@netscape.net>
- **Date:** 2004-12-02T13:24:37-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<0_Jrd.4009$A26.3199@fe40.usenetserver.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<joe_zitzelberger-AD51AE.21525724112004@knology.usenetserver.com>`
- **References:** `<cmgk9e$742$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <9L0V9sHuflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de> <PkVmd.503$3K3.407@fe40.usenetserver.com> <9L4hLiyPflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de> <ULTod.1917$_6.1545@fe40.usenetserver.com> <co268i$jaj$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <joe_zitzelberger-AD51AE.21525724112004@knology.usenetserver.com>`

```
Joe Zitzelberger wrote:
> In article <co268i$jaj$1@peabody.colorado.edu>,
>  "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote:
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
> omnipotent being create people to watch them suffer?

That wasn't how He created them.  Check out Isaiah 14 for how Lucifer 
fell and became the driving force for evil, and the first few chapters 
of Genesis to see how he interfered with the then-perfect Creation. 
It's also important to remember that while suffering is bad, good can 
come out of bad things.  It's not guaranteed, but it happens quite a bit.

A recent example from my experience - in traveling to Greenville, SC 
from Montgomery the day before Thanksgiving, I stopped in Newnan, GA to 
get some coffee.  The Dunkin Donuts where I stopped had a line back to 
the door, and I was quite frustrated that I'd have to wait so long. 
(I'd already spent 15 minutes in a drive-thru in Auburn, AL, and I was 
only the 3rd in line...)

When I get almost to my father-in-laws house in Greenville, there has 
been a 3-car wreck that happened at that intersection just a few minutes 
prior.  If there hadn't been such a line when I stopped, would mine have 
been one of those three cars?  The slow drive-thru and long line 
suddenly didn't bother me - I was just grateful to have made it there 
safely.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT: Marriage and the family

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>

On  2-Dec-2004, LX-i <lxi0007@netscape.net> wrote:

> > I tend to be of the opinion that even if God exists, he isn't very nice.
> >
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> come out of bad things.  It's not guaranteed, but it happens quite a bit.

Our experience on Earth shows that good things can come from bad.  Strength can
come from suffering.  Enlightenment can come from suffering.  I don't *want*
perfection.    I don't want to play heavenly golf and score 18 every time I
play.   Please let me die instead.    But even worse than the Heaven we see many
worshipers wanting for themselves, is the Hell many worshipers want for the rest
of us.   They are looking forward to watching us being tortured beyond all
understanding forever and ever without hope of parole - for being fooled into
not believing.  And eternity is a long, long, time.

Of course they worship a being who they believe can stop this.    How can people
mix up love and hate so greatly?
```

Neither I nor anyone with whom I regularly associate myself at my church

nowhere in the _Baptist Faith and Message_, which is the Southern 
Baptist Convention's published statement of faith).

It saddens me that there are folks who feel this way.

- **References:** `<cmgk9e$742$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <9L0V9sHuflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de> <PkVmd.503$3K3.407@fe40.usenetserver.com> <9L4hLiyPflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de> <ULTod.1917$_6.1545@fe40.usenetserver.com> <orfaq0t6ad0ofa4nut5j5u4e6hpdlkgfh0@4ax.com> <30m372F2up125U1@uni-berlin.de>`

```
On Fri, 26 Nov 2004 01:58:02 +1300, "Pete Dashwood"
<dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote:

>"Robert Wagner" <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net> wrote in message
>news:orfaq0t6ad0ofa4nut5j5u4e6hpdlkgfh0@4ax.com...
…[25 more quoted lines elided]…
>books.

I linked to the wrong page, should have been:
http://www.geraldschroeder.com/tuning.html

>How does this line up with:"Many/most physicists don't think the universe
>happened by chance."?

The page says "the scientific establishment's most prestigious
journals, and its most famous physicists and cosmologists, have all
gone on record as recognizing the objective truth of the fine-tuning."
This is suspect because journals don't have opinions nor authority.
But the page quotes Hawking, Weinberg, Turner, Penrose and Wheeler
commenting on fine-tuning.

Recognizing fine-tuning is not the same as embracing creationism. 

Hawking and Weinberg (at least) have since had second thoughts. 

"Recent publications (2004) by Stephen Hawking suggest that our
universe is much less 'special' than the proponents of the anthropic
principle claim it is. According to Hawking, there is a 98% chance
that a universe of a type as ours will come from a Big Bang. Further,
using the basic wavefunction of the universe as basis, Hawking's
equations indicate that such a universe can come into existence
without relation to anything prior to it, meaning that it could come
out of nothing. As of 2004, however, these publications and the
theories in them are still subject to scientific debate."

Near the bottom of this page:
http://easylink.playstream.com/cwru/CERCA_Conference/michelson_morley_award.wvx

>I'm sure his books are excellent (although even the reviews are mixed
>regarding his style), but that isn't why I visited the site... Do you have
>any hard evidence to support your claim, or was that it?

A search for "anthropic" yields many hits. A review is here:

http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Anthropic_principle

My rational take: since we don't know how the cosmos DOES work, we're
in no position to speculate about how it MIGHT HAVE worked. In
particular, we don't know how gravity works and it's unlikely we'll
ever be able to experiment in Planck scale. The highest energy we can
hope to produce on Earth is in the TeV range = 10^12 eV. Planck scale
requires 10^19 eV to unify gravity with other forces. An accelerator
to produce that would have a diameter half that of the solar system.

My irrational take: the universe looks like an emryo, a form of
reproduction. That doesn't imply the creator is omniscient, or
superior, or even sentient. As they say, a chicken is the egg's method
of reproduction. The creator could be a burned-out universe, which is
where this one seems headed.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT: Marriage and the family

```
Thanks for the follow-up information, Robert.

I have never ascribed to the Anthropic principle, although it is a powerful
argument.

I had not come across the fine-tuning before, but everything else I
recognised and am familiar with.

The jury's still out as far as I'm concerned, but the fact is that the
Universe as we understand it does not NEED a Creator to exist.

We should not give up on the Universe... data collection is proceeding...
:-)

Pete.

On Fri, 26 Nov 2004 12:45:02 +1300, "Pete Dashwood"
<dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote:

>The jury's still out as far as I'm concerned, but the fact is that the
>Universe as we understand it does not NEED a Creator to exist.

I feel no personal need for a creator, if that's what you meant.
Science is about cause and effect. It seems unscientific to see the
biggest effect of all and be indifferent to its cause.

Before this discussion, I had been unaware of the vast number of
crackpot 'physicists'. I knew there were a few from a friend at
FermiLab, whose job was to read and debunk manuscripts that came over
the transom. He was getting 2-3 per day. Now I see that was a drop in
the bucket. There are tens to hundreds of thousands who think they
have a unification theory. 

For laughs, see:

http://www.sciforums.com/showthread.php?t=11473

To be saddened: 

http://www.lhup.edu/~dsimanek/fe-scidi.htm
```

"Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote:

>Thanks for the follow-up information, Robert.
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>recognised and am familiar with.

I think that fine-tuning and the "Lesser Anthropic Priciple (LAP)" are
essentially two sides of the same coin.  If you assume that the LAP
states effectively that "we are only here because the universe evolved
in such a way that it became possible for us to exist", then
fine-tuning is simply part-and-parcel of the evolution of said
universe.

>The jury's still out as far as I'm concerned, but the fact is that the
>Universe as we understand it does not NEED a Creator to exist.

True..  The "Greater AP" strikes me as "a load of dingo's kidneys" if
one may be permitted to quote the late Mr Adams.

>We should not give up on the Universe... data collection is proceeding...
>:-)

Is this like "The beatings will continue until morale improves."?  :-)
```

- **From:** island5@earthlink.net (island)

- **References:** `<cmgk9e$742$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <9L0V9sHuflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de> <PkVmd.503$3K3.407@fe40.usenetserver.com> <9L4hLiyPflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de> <ULTod.1917$_6.1545@fe40.usenetserver.com> <orfaq0t6ad0ofa4nut5j5u4e6hpdlkgfh0@4ax.com> <30m372F2up125U1@uni-berlin.de> <58ubq051bddiuo6olinm59qne35k11c93t@4ax.com> <30n945F32dfreU1@uni-berlin.de> <bc1eq0tb0c9g79mvmmd7h1omg78ssdpp3p@4ax.com>`

```
Jeff York <ralf4@btinternet.com> wrote in message news:<bc1eq0tb0c9g79mvmmd7h1omg78ssdpp3p@4ax.com>...
> "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote:
 
> >Thanks for the follow-up information, Robert.

> >I have never ascribed to the Anthropic principle, although it is a powerful
> >argument.

> >I had not come across the fine-tuning before, but everything else I
> >recognised and am familiar with.

> I think that fine-tuning and the "Lesser Anthropic Priciple (LAP)" are
> essentially two sides of the same coin.  If you assume that the LAP
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> universe.

> >The jury's still out as far as I'm concerned, but the fact is that the
> >Universe as we understand it does not NEED a Creator to exist.

> True..  The "Greater AP" strikes me as "a load of dingo's kidneys"

Not that I mean to support the idea of a "creator", but the problem
with this line of thinking is that it doesn't consider the fact that
the anthropic principle is incomplete, becasue the cosmological model
that Robert Dicke got the hard physics data point from was flawed.

Too much emphasis gets put on the face value of the existing anthropic
principle as some kind of a final best statement on the subject,
which, in its current form, is no more than a truism, or more
popularly, a "tautology".

Very sound logic says that repairing the flawed cosmological model
will clarify and complete the principle, which would then make for a
true "Strong" scientific statement, but never until that happens can
such weight be place on it, and then laughed off, because that isn't
science, its just lame unsubstatiated rationale.
```

```
In article <SPzmd.241$3K3.127@fe40.usenetserver.com>,
LX-i  <lxi0007@netscape.net> wrote:
>docdwarf@panix.com wrote:
>> In article <IDvmd.211$3K3.9@fe40.usenetserver.com>,
…[25 more quoted lines elided]…
>recognized as well.

Mr Summers, are we reading the same words?  What the Court stated was 
'The freedom to marry has long been recognized...'... the *freedom*, not 
the 'attributes'.  If you insist on making up words that the Court has not 
applied you can generate a variety of conclusions.

>One can't take part of the picture, claim 
>"tradition" validates it, and ignore the other attributes (which 
>"tradition" would validate just as much), and expect the argument to 
>stand, based on "tradition".

Mr Summers, the argument is not based on 'tradition', it is based on 
'vital personal rights'.  This can be seen as being similar to the 
tradition of 'Life, Liberty and the Persuit of Happiness', 'rights' which 
many say are not granted by any government at all.

What might be seen as a 'tradition' above is the *recognition* of marriage 
as a 'vital personal right', as this what is called 'long been' done... 
but the right is not referred to as a 'tradition' at all.

>
>>>2.  "Fundamental to our very existence and survival" is procreation - 
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>The only quote I have is the one you provided.

You are more than welcome to find the rest of the decision, it is readily 
available on the web... and I don't believe procreation is mentioned 
therein at all.

>Still, their argument 
>that it was *fundamental* to "our very existence and survival" was the 
>wrong argument.

'Right' argument or 'wrong', Mr Summers, it is the decision of the Supreme 
Court of the United States of America... what do they know about law, eh?

>(Seeing your further comments below, it clicked on what 
>"Loving v. Virginia" was - that was interracial marriage, right?)

As with many decisions there might be ramifications beyond the case 
presented; it might not be the first time such a thing happened.

>
>
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
>That's faulty logic.

It was not presented as logic, it was presented as a trial judge's 
statement which had a particular 'odor'... 

>In the Bible, the prohibition to intermarry with 
>other races was given only to the Jews.  There were lots of other rules 
>given to the Jews as well - are we going to codify those into our body 
>of law?

I would say no more than we have liberty to codify the rules given to the 
Buddhists... or the Zoroastrians... or the Christians, no; Amendment I and 
all that history brought along with it seems a right good idea.

>
>> Quite the ''close-minded' person calling it what it is', no doubt... and 
>> no doubt overturned by the Supreme Court of the United States of America.
>
>He was calling it incorrectly.  :)

He issued a judicial ruling that was upheld on appeal and had to be 
overturned by the Supreme Court of the United States of America... pretty 
good 'sticking power' for something so 'wrong'.

>
>> If you want Congress to start bringing a religion into things, Mr Summers, 
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>the state of the most basic build block of society, the family.

Ummmmm... you do realise that what was addressed in Loving vs Virginia was 
the condition of marriage, not family, right?

>
>Truly, this battle began being lost when the divorce rate skyrocketed, 
>shacking up became the "in" thing to do.

Naw... 'this battle began being lost' when nutrition and sanitation 
changed life-expectancies to the point where 'until death us do part' 
could, for a majority, include a substantial amount of time beyond one's 
thirty-fifth birthday.

>Argue for stronger families, 
>and the homo lobby says "We're not mocking marriage, it's the 50% 
>divorce rate, Britney Spears, and Nicky Hilton who are doing that!"

When this 'homo lobby' makes arguments here you might want to address 
those; what I have presented is a decision of the Supreme Court of the 
United States of America that, clearly and unambiguously, labels marriage 
- without any qualification about the chromosomal make-up of the 
participants - as 'one of the basic civil rights'.

DD
```

- **References:** `<cmgk9e$742$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <IDvmd.211$3K3.9@fe40.usenetserver.com> <cne3li$r3j$1@panix5.panix.com> <SPzmd.241$3K3.127@fe40.usenetserver.com> <cnfgq0$81i$1@panix5.panix.com>`

docdwarf@panix.com wrote:
[huge snip]
> 
> When this 'homo lobby' makes arguments here you might want to address 
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> participants - as 'one of the basic civil rights'.

I'll only respond to this with a dictionary link - that seems to be the 
only satisfactory way out of one of these discussions...

http://www.askoxford.com/concise_oed/marriage?view=get

And, for the lazy, what that says is...

=== paste ====

marriage

   ï¿½ noun 1 the formal union of a man and a woman, by which they become 
husband and wife. 2 a combination of two or more elements.

   ï¿½ PHRASES marriage of convenience a marriage concluded primarily to 
achieve a practical purpose.

   ï¿½ ORIGIN Old French mariage, from marier ï¿½marryï¿½.

=== end paste ===

Loving v. Virginia was about marriage, as defined by the OED.  The 
current day issue is not - it's about a redefinition of marriage. 
That's the difference, and why I think the original findings in Loving 
v. Virginia was wrong, but the 11 states who stood up and stated what 
they wanted on November 2nd were right.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT: demise of COBOL WAS: Infinite Loops and Explicit Exits

_(reply depth: 37)_

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com
- **Date:** 2004-11-18T07:42:40-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<cni5c0$h2t$1@panix5.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<cmgk9e$742$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <SPzmd.241$3K3.127@fe40.usenetserver.com> <cnfgq0$81i$1@panix5.panix.com> <QwVmd.508$3K3.341@fe40.usenetserver.com>`

```
In article <QwVmd.508$3K3.341@fe40.usenetserver.com>,
LX-i  <lxi0007@netscape.net> wrote:
>docdwarf@panix.com wrote:
>[huge snip]
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>http://www.askoxford.com/concise_oed/marriage?view=get

[snip]

>Loving v. Virginia was about marriage, as defined by the OED.

Leaving aside the fact the decision is about a civil right and its denial 
in accordance with Amendment XIV... your assertion does not seem to be 
supported by anything in the text; the word 'woman' is used once 
(describing the person who married Mr Loving) and 'man' is used four 
times, once to describe the person who married Ms Jeter, twice in 
quotations from statues from the State of Virginia - neither of which 
labels the participants by sex and one referring only to 'cohabiting as 
man and wife', again gender-neutral as far as the participants are 
concerned - and once in the phrase 'the basic civil rights of man'.

>The 
>current day issue is not - it's about a redefinition of marriage. 

If this is true then the matter appears to be inconsequential.  Marriage, 
in a country where church and state are held separate, is a matter of 
legal definition and legal definitions change over time... there was a 
Constitutionally mandated '3/5ths rule' that was tossed aside nigh a 
century back.

>That's the difference, and why I think the original findings in Loving 
>v. Virginia was wrong, but the 11 states who stood up and stated what 
>they wanted on November 2nd were right.

Those eleven states, according to Loving vs Virginia, wish to allow a 
civil right to be applicable solely on the basis of the sex of the people 
exercising it; such a discrimination, it seems, has already been declared 
to be unConstitutional... which is which I said, a while back, that it 
would seem to make for an interesting challenge.

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT: demise of COBOL WAS: Infinite Loops and Explicit Exits

…[6 more quoted lines elided]…

What was the civil right in question?  You like to keep deflection the 
issue from marriage to civil rights - but when the sentence starts our 
"marriage is a basic civil right", you're not talking about "civil 
rights", you're talking about a particular civil right.  How that civil 
right is defined is important.

> your assertion does not seem to be 
> supported by anything in the text; the word 'woman' is used once 
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> concerned - and once in the phrase 'the basic civil rights of man'.

Only in todays "what does 'is' mean" world is "man and wife" a 
gender-neutral term.  (I maintain that it isn't.)

>>The 
>>current day issue is not - it's about a redefinition of marriage. 
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> century back.

Yes, and one day it may come about.  Doesn't mean I would believe it to 
be any more right than I do today...

>>That's the difference, and why I think the original findings in Loving 
>>v. Virginia was wrong, but the 11 states who stood up and stated what 
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> would seem to make for an interesting challenge.

And this type of parsing of words reflects what's wrong with things 
these days.  Marriage *is* allowed to people of both sexes - you've just 
gotta have them in matching pairs.  It *will* be an interesting 
challenge - I agree with you in that regard.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT: demise of COBOL WAS: Infinite Loops and Explicit Exits

_(reply depth: 39)_

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com
- **Date:** 2004-11-19T05:38:18-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<cnkieq$h52$1@panix5.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<cmgk9e$742$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <QwVmd.508$3K3.341@fe40.usenetserver.com> <cni5c0$h2t$1@panix5.panix.com> <sMgnd.784$3K3.447@fe40.usenetserver.com>`

```
In article <sMgnd.784$3K3.447@fe40.usenetserver.com>,
LX-i  <lxi0007@netscape.net> wrote:
>docdwarf@panix.com wrote:
>> In article <QwVmd.508$3K3.341@fe40.usenetserver.com>,
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
>right is defined is important.

The civil right in question is, as you stated above, marriage.  Since 
marriage is (according to the decision) 'a civil right' it is, I believe, 
to be treated as a member of the set of 'civil rights'.  'Civil rights', 
as a group, are subject to certain kinds of treatment, one of which is 
that they are not to be readily abridged on the of race, creed or gender.

What in the above appears to be unclear?

>
>> your assertion does not seem to be 
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>gender-neutral term.  (I maintain that it isn't.)

'Man and wife' might not be a gender-neutral term, sure... but the text 
reads '*as* man and wife'.  Do you deny the possibility that two people of 
the same sex can live together in that manner?

>
>>>The 
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>be any more right than I do today...

You are welcome, of course, to believe as you see fit... but 'belief', by 
definition, might not be the same thing as reasoned argument or 
law-of-the-land.  In the case of law-of-the-land... which would you rather 
see used as a primary component of this, someone's belief or a reasoned 
argument?

(Note that 'someone' just might possibly have the Right Beliefs that 
others do.)

>
>>>That's the difference, and why I think the original findings in Loving 
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>these days.

The ability to link together conditions in an apparently logical manner is 
'what's wrong with things these days'?  Take the terms one by one and 
demonstrate the flaw in them:

Marriage, according to Loving vs Virginia, is a civil right.

The eleven states, according to loving vs Virginia, wish to allow a civil 
right to be appliclable solely on the basis of the sex of the people 
exercising it.

Allowing a civil right to be applied on the basis of sex seems already to 
have been determined to be unConstitutional.

Now... where is this insidious 'parsing' that is 'what's wrong with things 
these days' in all that?

>Marriage *is* allowed to people of both sexes - you've just 
>gotta have them in matching pairs.

'Matching' according to whose definitions, please?  

>It *will* be an interesting 
>challenge - I agree with you in that regard.

Ahhhhhh... *now* you may begin to see.  Remember, after all, that it has 
been said that an 'ancient Chinese curse' was 'May you live in interesting 
times.'

DD
```

.    On  19.11.04

>> Marriage *is* allowed to people of both sexes - you've just
>> gotta have them in matching pairs.

d> 'Matching' according to whose definitions, please?

   Leaving homosexuality beside, the concept of "both sexes" has to be  
reexamined in light of scientific research of which I learned  
recently.

   "Male" and "female" are not so clear-cut opposed as a bit which can  
only have the values 1 or 0.

   There are transitional forms between the sexes, what has been known  
in ancient times as "hermaphrodites", there is _transsexuality,_ which  
could also be understood as "the right self in the wrong body".


Yours,
Lï¿½ko Willms                                     http://www.willms-edv.de

news:cnlie5$a7l$1@peabody.colorado.edu...
>
> On 19-Nov-2004, Warren Simmons <wsimmons5@optonline.net> wrote:
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> A lot of CoBOL programs will need to be updated to accept new letters
besides M
> and F.    Give me a head start by telling me the other sexes initials,
will you?
>
M = Male
F =  Female
C = Corporate
G = Group (non-profit)
P = Group or Organization (non-commercial)
H = Hermaphrodite
T = Transsexual
Q = Gay Male
D = Gay female
Z =  Chromosome abnormality
X = won't disclose
I  =  Indeterminate
O = None of the above

See... that wasn't so hard now, was it? <G>  (Races off to quickly write
data entry component that accepts all of them...)

Pete.
```
