_(reply depth: 45)_

- **From:** "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2004-11-21T15:25:29+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<30acl1F2ssqp6U1@uni-berlin.de>`
- **References:** `<cmgk9e$742$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <SPzmd.241$3K3.127@fe40.usenetserver.com> <cnfgq0$81i$1@panix5.panix.com> <QwVmd.508$3K3.341@fe40.usenetserver.com> <cni5c0$h2t$1@panix5.panix.com> <sMgnd.784$3K3.447@fe40.usenetserver.com> <419E3AD6.1030908@optonline.net> <cnlie5$a7l$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <2hznd.282297$%k.167740@pd7tw2no> <pu3vp0d7lmaa7h4ec45r0h07k5bt2tu49g@4ax.com> <VvOnd.290341$%k.181695@pd7tw2no> <i5jvp0hhtb3j340saq94adu703ltu81g6a@4ax.com>`

```

"Robert Wagner" <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net> wrote in message
news:i5jvp0hhtb3j340saq94adu703ltu81g6a@4ax.com...
<snip>> >
> >As the saying goes, "Tell it to the Marines...".
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Watch the generalizations, Robert. They consistenly undermine some otherwise
valuable posts. (NOT all marines are into whoring and drinking.)

As we are seeing a lot of this "Tell it to the Marines"  nonsense, I thought
it might be pertinent at this point to remind all concerned that  the
Marines being spoken about, were NOT that admirable company of "Semper Fi"
warriors who do all the grunt work for the US Government.

This expression (although it WAS used by a US general during the Second
World War, in connection with aforementioned Semper Fi warriors) came into
use LONG before that.

Samuel Pepys, the famous 17th Century diarist, was re-telling some stories
he had gathered from the Navy, to Charles the Second.

One of these involved flying fish.

The assembled courtiers were sceptical, but an officer of the Maritime
Regiment of Foot (The forerunner of the Royal Marines) confirmed that he too
had seen such.

The King accepted this evidence and is quoted as saying:

"From the very nature of their calling no class of our subjects can have so
wide a knowledge of seas and lands as the officers and men of Our Loyal
Maritime Regiment. Henceforward, ere ever we cast doubts upon a tale that
lacks likelihood, we will first 'Tell it to the Marines'."

This expression should be used  only where there is scepticism (if it was
good enough for King Charles, it should be good enough for us... <G>), not
as a dismissal, or acknowledgement of fait accompli.

Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT: demise of COBOL WAS: Infinite Loops and Explicit Exits

_(reply depth: 46)_

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com
- **Date:** 2004-11-21T04:13:56-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<cnpm8k$565$1@panix5.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<cmgk9e$742$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <VvOnd.290341$%k.181695@pd7tw2no> <i5jvp0hhtb3j340saq94adu703ltu81g6a@4ax.com> <30acl1F2ssqp6U1@uni-berlin.de>`

```
In article <30acl1F2ssqp6U1@uni-berlin.de>,
Pete Dashwood <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote:

[snip]

>As we are seeing a lot of this "Tell it to the Marines"  nonsense, I thought
>it might be pertinent at this point to remind all concerned that  the
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>One of these involved flying fish.

Gotta love this Internet.  Would the word of the National Maritime Museum 
in Greenwich have any weight here?

From:

<http://www.nmm.ac.uk/site/request/setTemplate:singlecontent/contentTypeA/conFaq/contentId/64/navId/005002006001002>

--begin quoted text:

The version of the origin of the phrase Tell it to the Marines, approved 
by the Royal Marines tells, of a typically wise and experienced officer of 
the Maritime Regiment (the forerunner of today’s Royal Marines) 
verifying a yarn about flying fish for the benefit of King Charles II in 
the 1660s. Even then, Marines had been everywhere, done everything, and 
knew everything worth knowing..... Unfortunately this version was actually 
invented by the novelist W. P. Drury (a retired Lieutenant Colonel of the 
Royal Marines) in the 1900s

An earlier reference, more in keeping with the contemporary meaning of the 
phrase, is found in an anonymous work of naval fiction, The Post Captain; 
or, The Wooden Walls well manned: comprehending a view of naval society 
and manners (London: 1806). In this, Captain Brilliant, of HMS Desdemona, 
when a tale started to grow too tall for his taste, was given to saying, 
'You may tell that to the Marines, but I'll be d----d if the Sailors will 
believe it!'.

--end quoted text

Cpt. Brilliant's use is echoed by Sir Walter Scott in Redgauntlet (1824) 
(found at http://www.gutenberg.org/dirs/etext01/redg10.txt ); the text 
reads 'Tell that to the marines--the sailors won't believe it.'

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT: demise of COBOL WAS: Infinite Loops and Explicit Exits

_(reply depth: 47)_

- **From:** "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2004-11-22T04:28:35+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<30bqh6F2ugelsU1@uni-berlin.de>`
- **References:** `<cmgk9e$742$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <VvOnd.290341$%k.181695@pd7tw2no> <i5jvp0hhtb3j340saq94adu703ltu81g6a@4ax.com> <30acl1F2ssqp6U1@uni-berlin.de> <cnpm8k$565$1@panix5.panix.com>`

```

<docdwarf@panix.com> wrote in message news:cnpm8k$565$1@panix5.panix.com...
> In article <30acl1F2ssqp6U1@uni-berlin.de>,
> Pete Dashwood <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote:
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> >As we are seeing a lot of this "Tell it to the Marines"  nonsense, I
thought
> >it might be pertinent at this point to remind all concerned that  the
> >Marines being spoken about, were NOT that admirable company of "Semper
Fi"
> >warriors who do all the grunt work for the US Government.
> >
> >This expression (although it WAS used by a US general during the Second
> >World War, in connection with aforementioned Semper Fi warriors) came
into
> >use LONG before that.
> >
> >Samuel Pepys, the famous 17th Century diarist, was re-telling some
stories
> >he had gathered from the Navy, to Charles the Second.
> >
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> in Greenwich have any weight here?

While I have the utmost respect for this establishment, and have spent many
happy hours there looking at (among other things) the uniform Horatio Neslon
was wearing when he was shot (complete with bullet hole), on this occasion I
am not so sure.

I note that the person responding to the query does not sign his name. I
also know that there is inter-service rivalry in Britain and it would be
just like the Navy to try and discredit the Marines... <G>.(Note the
throwaway disparaging asides in the NMM account...)

Furthermore, I remember my father telling me this story when I was quite
young and he made me check it in "Brewer's Dictionary of Phrase and Fable"
(first published in 1870 and an accepted authority on folk lore). As my
father was God like in his infallibility I tend to think he got it right,
but I admit to bias on this account <G>.

And then there is the actual quote (which I reproduced from the current
edition of Brewer's...) and this purports to be from Pepys, who is a pretty
reputable chronicler... To my ear, it has the ring of Royalty about it...

I would agree there may be cause for doubt, but I am not convinced by the
J.P Drury story. I could find no reference to him or his alleged book on the
Internet, but that doesn't mean anything, except I could find no reference.
Even if Drury DID publish this account in a work of fiction, how do we know
he hadn't got it from some other source (like Pepys, maybe...)

>
> From:
>
>
<http://www.nmm.ac.uk/site/request/setTemplate:singlecontent/contentTypeA/co
nFaq/contentId/64/navId/005002006001002>
>
> --begin quoted text:
…[22 more quoted lines elided]…
> reads 'Tell that to the marines--the sailors won't believe it.'

We could reasonably expect Scott to be aware of it if Brilliant had been
created in naval fiction in 1806. This was a year after Trafalgar and there
was a wave of feeling in the population for the invincible Royal Navy and
Jolly Jack Tar and Hearts of Oak (and of course, the war with Napoleon was
still proceeding, although Trafalgar removed the immediate threat of
invasion.). Under those circumstances it is likely that a work of naval
fiction would be widely read and Scott, being of a literary persuasion,
might well have come across it. The real question then is whether the
anonymous author of "The Post Captain" simply made it up or whether it was,
indeed, reported by Pepys as a conversation with Charles II.

I believe the jury is still out and I don't find the Maritime Museum
evidence compelling enough to make the case. However, I do admit that there
is reasonable doubt on the case made by Brewer's.

I believe this one is a case of believe what you prefer. I prefer Brewer's
account becauseI have found it to be accurate on other things. When they are
not sure they usually say "possibly" or otherwise qualify their explanation.

Pete.
```

```
at self promotion.  If one were to look at current news reports one 
would never know about the large US Army presence in Fallujah, Iraq, but 
one will find numerous mentions about the Marines.

They are a very small part of the combat power of the US Government, 
though you would never know it to speak to a Marine PAO.  While they are 
capable and competent at what they do, the vast majority of the 'grunt' 
work done for the US Government, is done by the US Army.
```

```
On Wed, 17 Nov 2004 22:27:27 -0600, LX-i <lxi0007@netscape.net> wrote:

>Loving v. Virginia was about marriage, as defined by the OED.  The 
>current day issue is not - it's about a redefinition of marriage. 
>That's the difference, and why I think the original findings in Loving 
>v. Virginia was wrong, but the 11 states who stood up and stated what 
>they wanted on November 2nd were right.

The 11 State laws were moot. States already had the protection they
sought thanks to  DOMA, which was enacted eight years ago. If DOMA is
found unconstitutional, State laws denying comity to other States will
be overturned by the same decision. 

If you want to blame someone for changing marriage, the finger should
be pointed at Federal government, who converted the institution from
spiritual/religious to an economic privilege. Same-sex marriage
advocates are not seeking approval from society or religion. They want
the same health insurance, joint tax returns and domestic violence
protection that straight couples enjoy. 

Religious people are passionate about marriage because sex and guilt
are involved. They seem to view marriage as an official License To
Have Sex rather than a social contract. What an adolescent idea.
Society would be better if religion stopped peddling guilt and started
practicing the tolerance they preach.
```

```
On 16-Nov-2004, LX-i <lxi0007@netscape.net> wrote:

> I'm not even talking about bringing a religion in - I'm talking about
> the state of the most basic build block of society, the family.
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> divorce rate, Britney Spears, and Nicky Hilton who are doing that!"

Yep. Allowing divorce was a shocking change to the status quo of "traditional
family values"

> While this may be true to some extent, the government does have a vested
> interest in maintaining strong families.  They ensure more stable,
> successful children, and a vibrant society.

So what is the relationship between what we allow a homosexual couple to call
themselves and this?    As long as gays live together without a marriage
certificate, my family will continue to be strong, as will the families of my
children?   But if we let gays have that certificate, my grandchildren will find
their families destroyed?   Or are you talking about your family?    Whose
families are we protecting by not allowing gay couples to have this piece of
paper?   What children won't be successful when this is allowed?
```

- **Date:** 2004-11-18T15:02:17+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

- **References:** `<cmgk9e$742$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <okigp0pjkg6qka012vtqjste7lah4c08c0@4ax.com> <cnb04h$prs$1@panix5.panix.com> <IDvmd.211$3K3.9@fe40.usenetserver.com> <cne3li$r3j$1@panix5.panix.com> <SPzmd.241$3K3.127@fe40.usenetserver.com> <cnfp01$679$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <RMVmd.511$3K3.311@fe40.usenetserver.com>`

```
On 17-Nov-2004, LX-i <lxi0007@netscape.net> wrote:

> > So what is the relationship between what we allow a homosexual couple to
> > call
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
> the word).

Our definition of "marriage" changed considerably when divorce was allowed.  
The impact of divorce upon the family and the institution of marriage was
profound.   Our definition of "marriage" that allows divorce is not 5,950 years
old.    We also have had cultures, that have allowed polygamy.    Dictionaries
discuss what is currently being used - not what is right or wrong.  When
Abraham's wives are mentioned in the Bible, do you pull out your dictionary and
then condemn him as weakening the institution of marriage?

You agree that my marriage and your marriage aren't being weakened by allowing a
homosexual couple to have that piece of paper.   I'm not sure what you mean by
"the institution of marriage", so could you explain what it is, and how we
measure its strength?    Would it be stronger if we proscribed divorce?   How
would it be weaker if we allowed a homosexual couple legal recognition of their
status?

Allowing divorce (for good or for bad), weakened lots of marriages.
Allowing homosexual couples to marry won't effect traditional marriages at all.
```

_(reply depth: 39)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2004-11-19T05:25:32+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<gtfnd.266958$%k.156526@pd7tw2no>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<217e491a.0411181757.5858249f@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<cmgk9e$742$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <okigp0pjkg6qka012vtqjste7lah4c08c0@4ax.com> <cnb04h$prs$1@panix5.panix.com> <IDvmd.211$3K3.9@fe40.usenetserver.com> <cne3li$r3j$1@panix5.panix.com> <SPzmd.241$3K3.127@fe40.usenetserver.com> <cnfp01$679$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <RMVmd.511$3K3.311@fe40.usenetserver.com> <cnidhp$7n$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <217e491a.0411181757.5858249f@posting.google.com>`

```
Richard wrote:

>"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote
>
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
>
You are both right. Richard check with your dad next time you chew the 
fat. I think regardless of religion, back in your dad's generation 
marriage whether in a church or in front of a registrar was perceived as 
a lifelong commitment by the two contractual parties. Divorce, although 
available was frowned upon and wasn't easily obtainable. That of course 
led to the insufferable conditions you outline.

It's eased now - negating the effect of some of what you itemize. 
Looking in from the outside at a few couples going through the process, 
it seems to me it's more tied into the Equity in the marriage. I do 
recall one couple who had a very 'civil' divorce. Their only bone of 
contention - who got the hockey seats for the Flames' games, that had 
already been purchased. Solution - 50/50 split until the season 
finished. :-)

The 'allowing' part of Howard's quote is that the ease of divorce 
removed the mental state of going into a marriage as a commitment - vis 
a vis Britney Spears and her 55 hour marriage. (To her it meant no more 
than a session in a Fake n' Bake salon). Certainly divorce figures have 
ZOOMED, not just amongst non-churchgoers. For same-sex couples wishing 
to parent children, the high divorce rate, (on grounds of lack of family 
continuity), has been targeted  to support their case.  (I dunno...?).

Sadly those most deeply affected by divorce are the children, if there 
are any. Some are resilient, others I think retain a mental scar into 
adulthood.

Jimmy
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT: demise of COBOL WAS: Infinite Loops and Explicit Exits

_(reply depth: 40)_

- **From:** Jeff York <ralf4@btinternet.com>
- **Date:** 2004-11-19T10:25:27+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b3irp010si4t90m6fa62fjfk2e197ncqel@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<cmgk9e$742$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <okigp0pjkg6qka012vtqjste7lah4c08c0@4ax.com> <cnb04h$prs$1@panix5.panix.com> <IDvmd.211$3K3.9@fe40.usenetserver.com> <cne3li$r3j$1@panix5.panix.com> <SPzmd.241$3K3.127@fe40.usenetserver.com> <cnfp01$679$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <RMVmd.511$3K3.311@fe40.usenetserver.com> <cnidhp$7n$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <217e491a.0411181757.5858249f@posting.google.com> <gtfnd.266958$%k.156526@pd7tw2no>`

```
"James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca> wrote:

>Richard wrote:
>
…[20 more quoted lines elided]…
>led to the insufferable conditions you outline.

I was listening to a Church of England Bishop on BBC Radio 4 a couple
of years ago and he commented that the church may have to re-examine
the concept of "until death us do part" as a result of the effects of
modern medecine.  Until 60 to 70 years ago, "until death.." meant
essentially, until the wife died in her mid-30s giving birth to number
11..  Marriages of 25+ years were the exception rather than the rule
in Victorian and pre-victorian times, so widowers, and to a much
lesser extent widows, would be free to remarry. The Bishop's views
suggested that 15 to 20 years is plenty long enough for two people to
get thoroughly fed-up with each other and that divorce is a benign
replacement for death in childbirth.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT: demise of COBOL WAS: Infinite Loops and Explicit Exits

_(reply depth: 39)_

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2004-11-19T14:32:08+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<cnl058$rmj$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<cmgk9e$742$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <okigp0pjkg6qka012vtqjste7lah4c08c0@4ax.com> <cnb04h$prs$1@panix5.panix.com> <IDvmd.211$3K3.9@fe40.usenetserver.com> <cne3li$r3j$1@panix5.panix.com> <SPzmd.241$3K3.127@fe40.usenetserver.com> <cnfp01$679$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <RMVmd.511$3K3.311@fe40.usenetserver.com> <cnidhp$7n$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <217e491a.0411181757.5858249f@posting.google.com>`

```

On 18-Nov-2004, riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard) wrote:

> > Allowing divorce (for good or for bad), weakened lots of marriages.
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> disertion).

Which, while true doesn't change that lots of marriages have been weakened, and
even killed via divorce.   I'm not saying this is good or bad, but just
comparing how divorce changed the "institution of marriage", with how people
fear homosexual marriages will change it.

I'm saying divorce changed it considerably, and some existing marriages were
weakened and even killed because of divorce.   I don't see that recognizing
homosexual marriages will weaken any existing marriages, and that the
"institution of marriage" won't be nearly as effected as it was by divorce.

I mean - if Sue and Mary who live together down the street get a paper that says
they're married - how does that effect *my* marriage and *my* family?

The biggest effect of Joe and Henry having a commitment to each other is that
they will promise to not be promiscuous.  Maybe that will keep my medical
insurance bills down.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT: demise of COBOL WAS: Infinite Loops and Explicit Exits

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com

```
Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net> wrote:

[snip]

>Which, while true doesn't change that lots of marriages have been weakened, and
>even killed via divorce.

[snip]

>I'm saying divorce changed it considerably, and some existing marriages were
>weakened and even killed because of divorce.

Mr Brazee, this assertion confuses me.  Divorce, as I understand it, is 
the process by which the condition of marriage is terminated; that a 
process exists by which the condition can be ended does not seem to weaken 
the condition an more than the fact that controlled demolition exists 
weakens buildings.

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT: demise of COBOL WAS: Infinite Loops and Explicit Exits

_(reply depth: 38)_

- **From:** LX-i <lxi0007@netscape.net>
- **Date:** 2004-11-19T01:13:13-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<f2hnd.787$3K3.288@fe40.usenetserver.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<cnidhp$7n$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<cmgk9e$742$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <okigp0pjkg6qka012vtqjste7lah4c08c0@4ax.com> <cnb04h$prs$1@panix5.panix.com> <IDvmd.211$3K3.9@fe40.usenetserver.com> <cne3li$r3j$1@panix5.panix.com> <SPzmd.241$3K3.127@fe40.usenetserver.com> <cnfp01$679$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <RMVmd.511$3K3.311@fe40.usenetserver.com> <cnidhp$7n$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
Howard Brazee wrote:
> On 17-Nov-2004, LX-i <lxi0007@netscape.net> wrote:
> 
…[27 more quoted lines elided]…
> then condemn him as weakening the institution of marriage?

(Our definition of marriage that allows divorce (without a significant 
societal taboo attached) is around 50 years old - assuming an age of the 
earth of 6,000 years, that's where the 5,950 number came from.))

Polygamy is not allowed in this country (although it does occur).  And, 
as far as the past, I can't do anything about it.  Abraham, David, 
Solomon, all men who had many wives (and, in the case of the latter two, 
concubines as well), but all men who were described as great men of God. 
  (I wonder how far an attempt on my part would go to have a 
concubine...  ;>  I'm guessing "not too far"...)

> You agree that my marriage and your marriage aren't being weakened by allowing a
> homosexual couple to have that piece of paper.   I'm not sure what you mean by
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> status?

Marriage means something.  That's why it's so controversial when a few 
people say, "But why can't it mean _this_ too?"  What's to stop NAMBLA 
members from marrying minor children?  What's to stop lonely Minnesotans 
from marrying a moose?  If you allow these gradual changes to occur, 
over time, the original meaning is lost.

Now, I didn't have a real big problem with the Supreme Court striking 
down Texas's prohibition on sodomy.  What someone does in their own time 
is their business, really.  What I object to is calling it "marriage". 
It's like grade inflation - if the class average is a 60, and instead of 
reteaching the material, you give the people above average an "A", does 
that "A" mean as much as it does to someone who works hard, studies the 
material, and earns an "A"?  On paper, it does.  In practice, you end up 
with dumbed-down students.

> Allowing divorce (for good or for bad), weakened lots of marriages.
> Allowing homosexual couples to marry won't effect traditional marriages at all.

If we allow this now, our grandchildren are going to face the exact same 
questions over some other perversion that seeks to have itself 
legitimized by government.  The struggle never ends for those who want 
to tear down any semblance of what our government and society looked 
like when our nation was founded.  As I said in a previous message, it 
may happen anyway.
```

```
> If you allow these gradual changes to occur,
> over time, the original meaning is lost.

You've already stated that its current meaning is 50 years old.

> Now, I didn't have a real big problem with the Supreme Court striking

…[5 more quoted lines elided]…

So how will my marriage be dumbed down?   (What will they be assuming about my
marriage - the way an employer assumes about a grade?)

I suppose it already has.   People don't assume that my wife and I are in our
first marriages, and that our marriages have been made by the appropriate
religious body.

But no matter what happens with homosexual marriages - they *will* assume that
our marriage is heterosexual.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT: demise of COBOL WAS: Infinite Loops and Explicit Exits

_(reply depth: 37)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2004-11-29T11:29:01-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<coft9u$2jf6$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<cmgk9e$742$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <okigp0pjkg6qka012vtqjste7lah4c08c0@4ax.com> <cnb04h$prs$1@panix5.panix.com> <IDvmd.211$3K3.9@fe40.usenetserver.com> <cne3li$r3j$1@panix5.panix.com> <SPzmd.241$3K3.127@fe40.usenetserver.com> <cnfp01$679$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <RMVmd.511$3K3.311@fe40.usenetserver.com>`

```

"LX-i" <lxi0007@netscape.net> wrote in message
news:RMVmd.511$3K3.311@fe40.usenetserver.com...

> Do you really expect me to believe that we had it wrong for 5,950 years,
> and only in the recent "enlightened" 50 years we now need to redefine a
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> the word).

Although I haven't personally read the book, I am told by those who have
that late John Boswell, in "Same-Sex Unions in pre-Modern Europe" (New York,
Villard, 1994), asserts that *liturgical* ceremonies existed for *same*-sex
unions even before they became customary for  *complementary*-sex unions.

His research is hotly debated, of course.   I have read his "Christianity,
Social Tolerance and Homosexuality" (Chicago, University of Chicago Press,
1980) and while I disagree with him on some small details of his scriptural
citations, my subsequent studies on the Old and New Testaments (in the
original and in various translations) lead me to agree with him in large
part on the topic of how, and where, homosexuality is handled in the Bible.
I seem to recall that the Right Reverend John Selby Spong (retired Bishop of
the Episcopal Diocese of Newark) largely agreed with Boswell in his
"Rescuing the Bible from Fundamentalism" (San Francisco, Harper, 1991).

And to tie this into another part of the thread:  Should the government
explicitly reject the theological precepts of Rt. Rev. Spong in favor of the
theological precepts of, say, the Rev. Fred Phelps, pastor of the Woodbury
Baptist Church of Wichita, Kansas (see www.godhatesfags.com)?   Or should it
step out of the discussion altogether?

    -Chuck Stevens
```

```
Clarification please - is that true about 'divorce' or are you alluding

'sinned' in marrying his brother's widow. (Although rare, annulment is 
still obtainable from the Vatican - probably at a price). As for the 
latter bimbo - proclaimed as King but never actually got to wear a 
crown. Insignificant now, but he sure caused a minor storm in a teacup 
when I was a mere 6/7 years of age.

I bet even today a swipe with an axe probably works out cheaper than the 
overall legal costs of a divorce - apart from which the result of the 
first is indisputable.

BTW : Reference brown shirts, somebody has just written fiction  where 
Lindbergh wins the presidency instead of FDR. Anybody yet read it ?  
Don't tend to read much fiction, but found Dan Brown's "The da Vinci 
Code" one rattling good yarn, and literally couldn't put it down until I 
got through to the end. Now I'm looking for a follow-up which will wet 
my appetite.

Jimmy
```

```
should not recognize any marriage as much as it does -- e.g. tax breaks, 
retirement perks, etc.)

marry whomever they choose, whenever and wherever they choose, 
sterility, age and orientation will not change my view.

How much is a whit?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT: demise of COBOL WAS: Infinite Loops and Explicit Exits

_(reply depth: 38)_

- **From:** LX-i <lxi0007@netscape.net>
- **Date:** 2004-11-19T01:17:47-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<x6hnd.788$3K3.79@fe40.usenetserver.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<cnidpc$cc$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<cmgk9e$742$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <IDvmd.211$3K3.9@fe40.usenetserver.com> <cne3li$r3j$1@panix5.panix.com> <joe_zitzelberger-972C5B.09071317112004@knology.usenetserver.com> <cnfntc$ji6$1@panix5.panix.com> <joe_zitzelberger-3D09E3.02081218112004@knology.usenetserver.com> <cnidpc$cc$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
Howard Brazee wrote:
> 
> Get the state out of the business of recognizing marriage for anything at all,
> this should be the domain of the churches.

(This isn't necessarily directed at you - just the message I picked to 
reply to...  :>  )

You know, there's something that's been bugging me...  I've been reading 
up on that couple in Massachusetts who brought the lawsuit that 
eventually ended up in their Supreme Court.  What started it was when 
one of the ladies gave birth, the other couldn't go in and see the baby 
because the hospital's policy only allows for "immediate family members."

Why did they turn to the government for a remedy for this, instead of 
asking the hospital to alter its policy?  That would have raised much 
less of a stink, and it wouldn't have been asking government to redefine 
a word using a definition it's *never* had before.  "Gay marriage" 
legislation hasn't stopped companies from offering "domestic partner" 
insurance options - why was the private sector not addressed first?  Why 
use the force of the government?
```

```
.    On  19.11.04
  wrote  lxi0007@netscape.net (LX-i)
     on  /COMP/LANG/COBOL
     in  x6hnd.788$3K3.79@fe40.usenetserver.com
  about  Re: OT: demise of COBOL  WAS: Infinite Loops and Explicit Exits


l> You know, there's something that's been bugging me...  I've been
l> reading up on that couple in Massachusetts who brought the lawsuit
l> that eventually ended up in their Supreme Court.

   As a case "couple-of-massachusetts" vs. whom?

l> What started it was when one of the ladies gave birth, the other
l> couldn't go in and see the baby because the hospital's policy only
l> allows for "immediate family members."
l>
l> Why did they turn to the government for a remedy for this, instead of
l> asking the hospital to alter its policy?

   I never heard of the case, but I think it would be wrong to assume  
that they did not try to redress the situation with the hospital, and  
the hospital adminstration.

   When that refuses, what kind of remedy does one have? One goes to  
the court to force the other side to concede that right. But it would  
not be a case against the state, its legislature or government, but  
against that hospital.

   Do you have any information that this was handled differently?

   Then please share it.


Yours,
Lï¿½ko Willms                                     http://www.willms-edv.de
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT: demise of COBOL WAS: Infinite Loops and Explicit Exits

_(reply depth: 39)_

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com
- **Date:** 2004-11-19T07:58:46-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<cnkqm6$9hi$1@panix5.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<cmgk9e$742$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <joe_zitzelberger-3D09E3.02081218112004@knology.usenetserver.com> <cnidpc$cc$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <x6hnd.788$3K3.79@fe40.usenetserver.com>`

```
In article <x6hnd.788$3K3.79@fe40.usenetserver.com>,
LX-i  <lxi0007@netscape.net> wrote:
>Howard Brazee wrote:
>> 
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
>asking the hospital to alter its policy? 

I'm not sure what sense this question makes.  Are you saying that if they 
went to the hospital and were told 'Sorry, this is our Policy, we can set 
them as we want, if you have a problem with that then fine, sue us' then 
turning to the government is appropriate?

>That would have raised much 
>less of a stink, and it wouldn't have been asking government to redefine 
>a word using a definition it's *never* had before.

It is my experience that organisations, when confronted with questions of 
their behavior, frequently respond with 'What we do is within the law, if 
you don't like it then change the law'.

>"Gay marriage" 
>legislation hasn't stopped companies from offering "domestic partner" 
>insurance options - why was the private sector not addressed first?  Why 
>use the force of the government?

Again, it is conjecture on your part that non-governmental remedies were 
not first sought and then rebuffed, leaving legal recourse as a final 
resort.

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT: demise of COBOL WAS: Infinite Loops and Explicit Exits

_(reply depth: 41)_

- **From:** Joe Zitzelberger <joe_zitzelberger@nospam.com>
- **Date:** 2004-11-30T09:05:53-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<joe_zitzelberger-788233.09055330112004@knology.usenetserver.com>`
- **References:** `<cmgk9e$742$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <IDvmd.211$3K3.9@fe40.usenetserver.com> <cne3li$r3j$1@panix5.panix.com> <joe_zitzelberger-972C5B.09071317112004@knology.usenetserver.com> <cnfntc$ji6$1@panix5.panix.com> <joe_zitzelberger-3D09E3.02081218112004@knology.usenetserver.com> <cnidpc$cc$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <x6hnd.788$3K3.79@fe40.usenetserver.com> <joe_zitzelberger-3D1013.09575819112004@knology.usenetserver.com> <coftso$2jrl$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`

```
In article <coftso$2jrl$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>,
 "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com> wrote:

> "Joe Zitzelberger" <joe_zitzelberger@nospam.com> wrote in message
> news:joe_zitzelberger-3D1013.09575819112004@knology.usenetserver.com...
…[36 more quoted lines elided]…
>     -Chuck Stevens

That hoop isn't much -- it took me about twenty minutes to get a LPA to 
manage my brothers household goods when he was en route to Afganistan.  

By contrast, my marriage license took about three hours at the Muscogee 
County marriage and gun license bureau (yes, they really are together).

The why?  Well, 800 years of english common law has failed to offer 
legal recognition of same sex unions.  That is the source of such 
automatic rights for complementary sex unions, but hardly the only 
source of those rights.

As DD asked earlier in this thread, why limit it to a single species?  
Why not have automatic interspecies inheritance rights?  Somewhere a 
line must exist or everyone would have automatic rights to everyone 
else.  Tradition and common law drew that line a a complementary sex 
pair.  I'm not suggesting that is right or wrong, just what has existed 
for hundreds of years.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT: demise of COBOL WAS: Infinite Loops and Explicit Exits

_(reply depth: 42)_

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com
- **Date:** 2004-11-30T09:24:09-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<cohvq9$ba5$1@panix5.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<cmgk9e$742$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <joe_zitzelberger-3D1013.09575819112004@knology.usenetserver.com> <coftso$2jrl$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <joe_zitzelberger-788233.09055330112004@knology.usenetserver.com>`

```
In article <joe_zitzelberger-788233.09055330112004@knology.usenetserver.com>,
Joe Zitzelberger  <joe_zitzelberger@nospam.com> wrote:

[snip]

>As DD asked earlier in this thread, why limit it to a single species?  
>Why not have automatic interspecies inheritance rights?

Mr Zitzelberger, I do not recall suggesting that species be ignored; what 
I asked was why limit it to a single sex as long as the participants are 
capable of giving legal consent (which I mislabelled as 'informed 
consent').  To the best of my knowledge only human beings of a certain age 
and who are not of diminished capacities are capable of giving this 
consent; other species cannot sign contracts.

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT: demise of COBOL WAS: Infinite Loops and Explicit Exits

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2004-11-30T08:58:17-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<coi8ra$1533$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<cmgk9e$742$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <IDvmd.211$3K3.9@fe40.usenetserver.com> <cne3li$r3j$1@panix5.panix.com> <joe_zitzelberger-972C5B.09071317112004@knology.usenetserver.com> <cnfntc$ji6$1@panix5.panix.com> <joe_zitzelberger-3D09E3.02081218112004@knology.usenetserver.com> <cnidpc$cc$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <x6hnd.788$3K3.79@fe40.usenetserver.com> <joe_zitzelberger-3D1013.09575819112004@knology.usenetserver.com> <coftso$2jrl$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <joe_zitzelberger-788233.09055330112004@knology.usenetserver.com>`

```
"Joe Zitzelberger" <joe_zitzelberger@nospam.com> wrote in message
news:joe_zitzelberger-788233.09055330112004@knology.usenetserver.com...
> That hoop isn't much -- it took me about twenty minutes to get a LPA to
> manage my brothers household goods when he was en route to Afganistan.
>
> By contrast, my marriage license took about three hours at the Muscogee
> County marriage and gun license bureau (yes, they really are together).

How many trips to how many government agencies, and how many hours of time
spent with a lawyer, would it take for a Lesbian couple to obtain every
single right that a heterosexual married couple have by virtue of a single
certificate?

> The why?  Well, 800 years of english common law has failed to offer
> legal recognition of same sex unions.  That is the source of such
> automatic rights for complementary sex unions, but hardly the only
> source of those rights.

And some 700 years of English common law continued the idea that females of
the human species were insufficiently equipped to make important decisions,
like voting.  Unless your argument is that English (or American) law (common
or otherwise) has not evolved *at all* since the Magna Carta -- an argument
I can only look at with amazement -- I don't know what it is!   Something
like "My great-great-grandpa held this position, and that's good enough for
me!"?
.
> As DD asked earlier in this thread, why limit it to a single species?
> Why not have automatic interspecies inheritance rights?

As DD has answered in this thread, precisely because the marriage CONTRACT
is a CONTRACT and those of other species are not eligible to enter into
CONTRACTS.

> Somewhere a
> line must exist or everyone would have automatic rights to everyone
> else.

I am not talking about having the "rights to" anyone -- that's a rather
interesting view of the marriage contract, I'd say.  I am talking about any
two people who already have the right to enter into contracts being allowed
to sign this one.

> Tradition and common law drew that line a a complementary sex
> pair.  I'm not suggesting that is right or wrong, just what has existed
> for hundreds of years.

Irrelevant.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

```
"Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote in message
news:314fu2F3472ddU1@individual.net...

> Don't you think this thread is getting, like, surreal...?

Perhaps; I can see how you would think so.  But some parts of the US are
*extremely* conservative, and Orange County, California is one of them.
Maybe you don't run into folks like this:

I've personally run across the folks from Westboro Baptist Church and their
ilk, I've seen what they trumpet as Christianity firsthand.

One gay couple I knew shared a life together for something approaching
thirty years.  They owned a home together, with the appropriate paperwork.
One of them died, and his parents came in, stripped the house of all of its
contents, threw the surviver out onto the street, and convinced the probate
judge to declare the will invalid on the grounds that the survivor was a
gold-digger who had corrupted their son.

I knew another gay man who was told to his face by a co-worker that it was
not only his God-given right but his God-given duty to stone him to death in
front of the company offices for his sins, and that he personally resented
the fact that neither the company nor the government would allow him to
exercize freely the tenets of his religion.

I know another gay man who was literally beaten to the ground by a group of
self-proclaimed Christians wielding Bibles as weapons for the crime of
trying to walk away from their proclamations as to what an abomination
before God he was.

I've encountered people who feel that the men who killed Matthew Shepard
made an oopsie for which they're obviously forgiven because they're
obviously Christian, while Matthew Shepard clearly deserved to be murdered
and to burn in hell forever because he was gay.

Would that these events were surreal.  Unfortunately, they aren't.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

```
Chuck Stevens wrote:

>"Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote in message
>news:314fu2F3472ddU1@individual.net...
…[43 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Incredibly tragic what you relate Chuck. Can you appreciate why much of 
the world is currently disturbed by American attitudes, beliefs. Here's 
me thinking that men in white pointed hoods, with holes poked out for 
eyes, and uttering , "We don't need no niggers, Jews or catholics around 
these parts", was just an historical aberration carried over from the 
Civil War. Obviously a lack of tolerance is still a deep festering wound 
in American society.

I've had a very private thought which has formulated over about a 
ten-fifteen year period, and recent events triggered it in my mind. 
Economically and historically events appear to occur in cycles. While 
most members of 'groups' are quiet majorities, there are the vocal 
activists who wish to push the envelope. I'm thinking of say Gloria 
Steinberg (if that's how you spell her name - the one who did a stint at 
a Bunny Club); she and her friends pushed the envelope for feminism - 
not wrong, but to-day I don't think the concept has much female support 
- young women accept as is, not realizing what Steinberg and company 
actually achieved.

The same with homosexuality. I have no idea of numbers, but somehow 
feel, that given decriminalization of the sexual act and equal 
property/equity rights under the law, coupled with some civil 
recognition of same-sex marriage/partnerships, then again a silent 
majority could live with such a status, quietly leading their own 
lifestyle. Unfortunately there are the activists, who again want to push 
the envelope, 'in your face'.

Now here's my thought - religion, and specifically Christianity in the 
Western World, is going to turn around and bite, with "Enough is enough 
!".  Might seem far-fetched, but I see that we could have something as 
horrific as the Reformation, re-cycled, times a factor of  'n'. Then add 
to that the possibility of it  triggering a confrontation between 
Christianity and Islam.

Note, agnostics and atheists - you don't even get consulted - in all 
likelihood, after the homosexuals, you could be the next targets. 
Reflect on Nazism - first target Jews, but then add mentally-disabled, 
gypsies, Slavs,  homosexuals et al......

(Pause at this point and ask yourself where is atheistic China in all 
this confrontation).

God forbid. And I sincerely hope my thoughts are skewed.

Jimmy
```

```
"James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca> wrote in message
news:Dlcrd.399066$%k.100230@pd7tw2no...

> Incredibly tragic what you relate Chuck. Can you appreciate why much of
> the world is currently disturbed by American attitudes, beliefs. Here's
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> in American society.

When Walt Disney decided to build Disneyland in Anaheim in the early 1950's,
he made it a condition of doing so that the City Council could no longer
wear their Klan garb in Public.

I've heard from several relatively reliable sources that as late as the
1980's there was a police code "NIN" in Newport Beach; the last two letters
stood for "In Newport".  I leave it to your imagination what the first "N"
stood for.

I don't disagree with the points at all.  Stonewall was in 1969, and I think
in general the gay community has quieted *way* down since the early 1980's
(after the decimation -- and devastation -- of the AIDS crisis).

As to the point of "in your face":  Why is a family photograph of wife and
kiddies on one's desk appropriate in the workplace, while a family
photograph of oneself with one's lover a political statement?   "Quitely
leading their own lifestyle" tends to evolve very quickly into "unless we
get a hint that your lifestyle might differ from ours".

The concept of privacy as to sexual activity applying only to heterosexual
activity was confirmed by the US Supreme Court in Bowers v. Hardwick back in
1986.  This decision was reversed by Lawrence and Garner  v. Texas just one
year ago.

The police had entered Lawrence and Garner's apartment without permission to
investigate a (false) report of an armed intruder (having broken into the
wrong apartment), found them "engaged in sodomy" and arrested them.
Lawrence and Garner appealed their conviction on the grounds of equal
protection and right to privacy (basically challenging Hardwick) and won
their appeal.  At the time of that decision, Texas, Kansas, Oklahoma and
Missouri banned "sodomy" between same-sex couples but not between
opposite-sex couples; thirteen more banned such acts outright.  These laws
were overturned by that decision.

> Now here's my thought - religion, and specifically Christianity in the
> Western World, is going to turn around and bite, with "Enough is enough
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> Christianity and Islam.

Well, as I said, I see *much* less activism from the gay community now than
I saw in the early 1980's.

> Note, agnostics and atheists - you don't even get consulted - in all
> likelihood, after the homosexuals, you could be the next targets.
> Reflect on Nazism - first target Jews, but then add mentally-disabled,
> gypsies, Slavs,  homosexuals et al......

I don't disagree.

> God forbid. And I sincerely hope my thoughts are skewed.

They may well not be.  But I am not convinced it's the job of the targets
(of any stripe or ilk) to make themselves invisible and accept whatever the
targeters want to dish out!  The Stonewall Riots in 1969 stemmed from the
police repeatedly raiding the gay bars in Greenwich Village.   I do reflect
on Nazism and similar views; as I indicated elsewhere in this thread the
City Council of the town in which I live was appearing as a group in their
Klan robes until Walt Disney convinced them that this might repel the
tourists he was hoping to attract with Disneyland! And this ain't hardly the
deep south.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

```
Pete Dashwood wrote:

>Oddly enough the Dashwood family motto is "Pro Magna Carta". (no, really,
>I'm not joking...)
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Get outta here ! What does that translate to - Roger the Dodger from Ash 
Wood - just shoving a "D'" in front doesn't make it sound very 
Gallic/Normanesque. They were a bunch of Viking-Norman cut-throats and 
robbers who joined Willie the Conker to seek their fortunes. And you 
know bloody well  that by slipping one of the monks at Battle Abbey a 
few bucks, years after the Battle of Hastings, you could be added to the 
roll-call  as though you *actually* had been around in 1066. By the time 
you get to your Hellfire Club relative - some legitimacy given to the 
'claim' - nobody could really check after some 600-700 years.

Now my brother when he first came over here had the four family crests 
put on to plaques, Maternal - Walsh and McDermot, Paternal - Gavan and 
Dunne. There are outfits in N. America, for a buck,  will print you out 
a computerised family tree, even if granddaddy was a chimpanzee. (There 
just aint no such animal as family crests in Ireland, they weren't into 
heraldry). Really impressed his new Canuck friends. When they were gone 
he would grin and slyly tell me, "Well I tell them we are descended from 
the Kings of Ireland". Quite possible, and there were hundreds of 'em - 
chieftains of small nomadic herdsmen clans drifting to and fro across 
Ireland with their goats, sheep and cows.

Not too many years ago I got a blurb from the promoters assuring me they 
could trace my family tree, using a template letter :-

    "John Gavan was the first Gavan to arrive in New York in 1801. *She* 
did this........".

I was intrigued by the bit that you did on checking accuracy for 
duplicate names and addresses, rejecting, what was it, some 12,00 
records.  Those silly buggers that sent the above couldn't even get his 
sex right !

You bet you've got something in your jeans - probably bovine. I believe 
your fairy tale about as much as you believe there's a God :-)
 
BTW - I do *not* store stuff, (i.e. e-mail) - I keep it in my memory 
box, that's why my quotes are only approximate.

Jimmy
```

```
"James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca> wrote in message
news:wxbrd.393951$nl.202429@pd7tw3no...
> Pete Dashwood wrote:
>
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> robbers who joined Willie the Conker to seek their fortunes.

 A "bunch of cutthroats" who carried out the last successful invasion of
England ....

I spent a Summer afternoon on the field in Battle and visualized the whole
thing. The total numbers on both sides were less than 10,000 men and in one
afternoon they changed the course of Western History. When you think that
100,000 men died on the Somme in one day without making the tiniest bit of
difference, it is a sobering thought.

Despite what you might think, my sympathies were with Harold and I think the
whole thing was a tragedy.

But you missed the whole point of my post, Jimmy. It was about how
ridiculous this thread is becoming.

I certainly did not intend to add fuel to the fire.


> And you
> know bloody well  that by slipping one of the monks at Battle Abbey a
> few bucks, years after the Battle of Hastings, you could be added to the
> roll-call  as though you *actually* had been around in 1066.

Well Jimmy, before you dismiss the research of other people, you should do
some of your own. I understand it is DeBrett's who document Roget D'Ashwood
although the connection is, admittedly, tenuous, and it is over 30 years
since I was told about it. As I don't have a copy, I can't check, so you may
be right.

Personally I neither know nor care whether the Dashwoods were at Hastings,
any more than I care about the properly documented history of the family.
You have obviously been to West Wycombe, gone through the caves and decided
that was the start of the family. It wasn't. The baronetcy was granted by
Queen Elizabeth the first, and the Dashwood family were prominent before
that.  Francis and the Hellfire club (the Mad Monks of Medmenham) are
probably the most interesting, but we had a Lord Mayor of London (1702),
several admirals, a Chancellor of the Exchequer, and an explorer in
Victorian times who found a pass through the Southern Alps of New Zealand
which is named after him. We also had a number of rogues and vagabonds, just
like any family.  Then there is me... <G>

I place no store by any of this stuff and mentioned it in passing only
because the family motto IS "Pro Magna Carta" and there IS a long history of
defending civil rights. I might just as well have mentioned that Francis
introduced the Black Mass to England and was the first British Satanist, so
it is hardly any wonder I am an atheist. While all of these things may be
true, I attach no importance to them and I don't believe they were
instrumental in making me what I am today. I hold the views I do because of
my life experience in THIS lifetime, no other, and certainly not because of
what some ancestor did or didn't do. I am what I am and I make no apology
for it.


> By the time
> you get to your Hellfire Club relative - some legitimacy given to the
> 'claim' - nobody could really check after some 600-700 years.
>

The claim was legitimate long before that, see above.

> Now my brother when he first came over here had the four family crests
> put on to plaques, Maternal - Walsh and McDermot, Paternal - Gavan and
…[22 more quoted lines elided]…
>
That is both our prerogatives. I did not post in the hope of persuading
Jimmy Gavan, and disparaging  my personal beliefs to support your argument
could be seen by some as a cheap shot. Have I ever sneered at your religious
beliefs? (or those of anyone else here?)  Please accord the same respect to
mine. I hold my views just as firmly and with the same reverence you do
yours, Jimmy. Although I recognise you meant no harm by it, your analogy is
out of order.
>
I will not be responding to any further posts here regarding the family. It
was wrong of me to mention it and I realise I shouldn't have. It is
personal, and it is off topic.

Pete.
```

```
seriously...
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT: demise of COBOL WAS: Infinite Loops and Explicit Exits

_(reply depth: 45)_

- **From:** Robert Wagner <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net>
- **Date:** 2004-12-04T23:01:47+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<k2b4r0pi1b4khuen1km4vt1802u0ajkt5p@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<cnfntc$ji6$1@panix5.panix.com> <joe_zitzelberger-3D09E3.02081218112004@knology.usenetserver.com> <cnidpc$cc$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <x6hnd.788$3K3.79@fe40.usenetserver.com> <joe_zitzelberger-3D1013.09575819112004@knology.usenetserver.com> <coftso$2jrl$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <joe_zitzelberger-788233.09055330112004@knology.usenetserver.com> <coi8ra$1533$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <314fu2F3472ddU1@individual.net> <tC9sd.2$Yq6.1@fe40.usenetserver.com>`

```
On Fri, 03 Dec 2004 20:50:55 -0600, LX-i <lxi0007@netscape.net> wrote:


>One can make a pretty good argument for that, particularly in this 
>country.  I'm not saying that we should go back to those "good old 
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>might happen, and who will pander to them by promising "more security"...

You can say that again. The American public's response to danger is
ignoble. Three incidents come to mind:

Jacksonville, Florida, hasn't been hit by a hurricane in more than 20
years because it sits in a 'dent' in the Atlantic coast. Hurricanes
slide by, 100 miles out in the ocean. The main part of the city is 15
miles inland and somewhat elevated. In the unlikely event a hurricane
did hit, the only flooding danger was to beach towns such as Ponte
Vedra and Jacksonville Beach, not the city of Jacksonville.

In 1999, Hurricane Floyd was predicted to make landfall at
Jacksonville. Stores were quickly depleted of plywood, milk and bread
(why do they always go for bread?) The night before it was to hit,
half the city's population, 300,000 people, decided on their own to
evacuate, even though there was no official evacuation order. I-10
westbound became a massive traffic jam. It took 8 hours to go 100
miles. 

It was a false alarm. The hurricane slipped by 50 miles out in the
ocean. By 8am it was north of Jacksonville, yet businesses remained
closed all day and PEOPLE CONTINUED BUYING PLYWOOD. Home improvement
stores were selling it as fast as emergency shipments arrived .. AFTER
the threat had passed.
------------------

The Watts riots, in 1965, were confined to the Watts district in
south-central Los Angeles. I watched their week-long progress from my
high-rise office on Wilshire Boulevard, 2-3 miles to the north. Fifty
miles out in the San Fernando Valley, suburbanites were blockading
streets and running militia patrols to repel 'invasion'. Geez, talk
about over-reaction.
-----------------

Security screening of passengers on the tourist boat to the Statue of
Liberty this year was tighter than any airport. People and their shoes
were searched, cameras and cellphones examined, etc. Yet the statue
was not open. Visitors couldn't get closer than 100 meters from its
base. Why were they looking for bombs concealed in shoes and
cellphones? 

So I took an informal poll. I asked a dozen people whether they
thought such security was overkill. They all shrugged and said "It's
for our own protection."

Protection from what? From things that go bump in the night? From
psychological demons? It certainly doesn't protect us from loss of
civil rights nor from government harassment.

When the US Constitution was written in the 18th century, it was based
on the premise that Americans were self-reliant and proud. That
premise is no longer true; people have become sheep and cowards.
They're like profligate children pissing away the family fortune they
inherited. I question whether they're worthy of liberty, leadership
and money. Much of the world seems to feel the same way.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT: demise of COBOL WAS: Infinite Loops and Explicit Exits

_(reply depth: 46)_

- **From:** "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2004-12-05T16:40:48+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<31feaaF3atelrU1@individual.net>`
- **References:** `<cnfntc$ji6$1@panix5.panix.com> <joe_zitzelberger-3D09E3.02081218112004@knology.usenetserver.com> <cnidpc$cc$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <x6hnd.788$3K3.79@fe40.usenetserver.com> <joe_zitzelberger-3D1013.09575819112004@knology.usenetserver.com> <coftso$2jrl$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <joe_zitzelberger-788233.09055330112004@knology.usenetserver.com> <coi8ra$1533$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <314fu2F3472ddU1@individual.net> <tC9sd.2$Yq6.1@fe40.usenetserver.com> <k2b4r0pi1b4khuen1km4vt1802u0ajkt5p@4ax.com>`

```

"Robert Wagner" <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net> wrote in message
news:k2b4r0pi1b4khuen1km4vt1802u0ajkt5p@4ax.com...
> On Fri, 03 Dec 2004 20:50:55 -0600, LX-i <lxi0007@netscape.net> wrote:
>
…[61 more quoted lines elided]…
> and money. Much of the world seems to feel the same way.

Robert, I enjoyed your post and I understand your points.

Except the last two sentences.

ALL human beings, no matter how bad or misguided are entitled to live in
freedom. Even the ones who we put in jail have certain "liberties" and their
rights are respected.

When we start to question this fundamental axiom, because we are frustrated,
disappointed in, or generally pissed off with our fellows, then the entire
structure of a democratic free society is being eroded from within, more
effectively than any terrorist could ever do from without.

BECAUSE you have liberty you can do something about changing the attitudes
you despise.

You despise the timidity and ovinicity of people. But that doesn't mean they
don't deserve to be part of the society.

If everyone was a hero who would tend the wheat?

Since people started forming communities there have always been the timid
relying on the warriors to protect them.

It is a symbiotic arrangement that works. (Ants find it useful, too...)

America is diverse. It is unlikely that every person will "get along" with
every other person.

Settling differences and resolving conflicts with mutual respect and
acceptance of the underpinning ground rule that ALL have value and the right
to a viewpoint, is fundamental to the success of the society. When someone
questions the "worth" of others, or decides that they are "superior" to
others (even though all of us are "superior" - perhaps "more adept" is a
better description - to others in certain areas), when someone questions
whether others DESERVE the same rights as the questioner enjoys, then the
whole history that bought freedom is invalidated. The lives that were lost,
the cruelty and torture that was endured, the blood that was spilt... all
for nothing.

You are heading down Orwell's road of "Some animals are more equal than
others"... It is not a good place to go.

It is a long time since any of us in the western democracies had our freedom
taken from us or our liberty proscribed. It is easy (for us) to forget the
ones who bought it for us.

What we have, cost us dear and it is worth preserving.

Don't give up on your fellow man because he behaves badly sometimes, or he
doesn't meet your expectations, or he isn't as smart as you are.

As for the opinion of the rest of the World....

There are many around the world who don't get to vote in America but must
still "live with" the results of the US elections. That certainly breeds
resentment, and it is very hard to resolve. Sometimes America takes
positions that are suspect to people in other cultures. That also breeds
resentment. Sometimes blatant mistakes are made by the world's most powerful
nation. That pisses everybody off... And they show it.

But despite all of this, America still has many friends around the world
too.

I'm not happy with the idea of one nation having total power, but I wasn't
happy with the Cold War and the threat of nuclear annihilation that I grew
up with, either. If we have to have one nation with unlimited power, I would
rather it was a nation based on democratic ideals and commitment to the
rights of human beings, even if they get it wrong sometimes...

Besides, popularity is a fickle thing and can change very quickly.

Whether we agree with the politics or not, there is no denying that the USA
is a great nation. It will stay that way only so long as the people in it
remain true to the ideals on which it was founded.

Pete.
```

```
Donald Tees wrote:
> Pete Dashwood wrote:
> 
…[33 more quoted lines elided]…
> to Saudia Arabia by american secret police.

Assuming that you are referring to the Mahar Arar (spelling not 
guaranteed) case, it is very troubling.  For the rest of you who haven't 
heard, Mahar is a naturalized Canadian citizen who was born in Syria. 
On the way back from somewhere in the Muslim world, he was transiting 
through New York enroute to Canada (presumably Ottawa or Montreal). 
Customs and Immigration decided he was a threat of some sort of a threat 
possibly based on information from the Royal Canadian Mounted Police 
(equivalent of the FBI for these purposes) and/or the Canadian Security 
and Intelligence Service (CSIS which I believe expands to what I wrote). 
  They decided to ship him to Syria where he was tortured and it took 
the press and work by a number of Members of Parliament to get him 
released.  There have been leaks purporting to show his ties to 
terrorism and there is a special inquiry looking into this.  In my not 
so humble opinion, even assuming he is a terrorist which is vehemently 
denied this practice is disastrous for both Canadian and US citizens 
especially those born in other countries.  It sets the dangerous 
precedent that you can be sent to a third country any time you incur the 
suspicion of an immigration agent and your country's security 
establishment if you are passing through an airport in a country 
friendly to your country's intelligence service.  It is especially 
ironic given the terrorism label given Syria by the US.
> 
> As someone that live 150 miles from the border that concerns me somewhat.
> 
> Donald
>
```

```
On Sun, 5 Dec 2004 16:40:48 +1300, "Pete Dashwood"
<dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote:


Thank you for the sanguine article. Reading it raised my spirit until
the last paragraph recalled a news report I had just read elsewhere. I
copied to rebut "ideals on which it was founded".

One of my brief assignments in the Marine Corps was Forward Observing
for an artillery unit practicing in Japan, using Mount Fuji as an
impact area. In protest, young Japanese would run into the target area
waving flags, knowing we'd stop shooting (part of my job was to spot
them and call cease fire). We responded by sending military police to
drag them away, kicking and shouting. Then we resumed fire. 

Internal reports called them "Communist agitators".  I knew this
wasn't about Communism, it was about protecting a symbol of the Shinto
religion. We could have put the impact area anywhere. Using Mount Fuji
was the height of insensitivity. 

When I walked in nearby rural Japanese villages, people spit at me.
Two deliberately swerved their cars and tried to hit me. When I tried
to enter a Japanese bar/restaurant, the owner saw me coming, came out
waving his hands and yelling (in Japanese) "No Americans. English,
German, anything else ok, but no Americans." His attitude didn't
change when I answered in semi-fluent Japanese that I wasn't a typical
American (they call us gi-jin, which means barbarian). I said I was on
his side and just wanted to enjoy a Japanese meal without causing
trouble. That wasn't good enough. I was still an Ugly American (The
only thing worse is Korean. Japanese totally despise Koreans.) Some of
my colleagues passed themselves off as German, talking in
German-sounding nonsense they knew Japanese wouldn't understand
either.

At the tender age of 17, I knew the Japanese peasants in those small
towns were right and we (the US) were wrong. 

A few years later I saw millions in Southeast Asia  lose a tradition
of quasi-democracy to be brutalized by well-intentioned but misguided
ideaology. 

>Whether we agree with the politics or not, there is no denying that the USA
>is a great nation. It will stay that way only so long as the people in it
>remain true to the ideals on which it was founded.

WASHINGTON - Evidence gained by torture can be used by the U.S.
military in deciding whether to imprison a foreigner indefinitely at
Guantanamo Bay, Cuba, as an enemy combatant, the government concedes.

Statements produced under torture have been inadmissible in U.S.
courts for about 70 years. But the U.S. military panels reviewing the
detention of 550 foreigners as enemy combatants at the U.S. naval base
in Cuba are allowed to use such evidence, Principal Deputy Associate
Attorney General Brian Boyle acknowledged at a U.S. District Court
hearing Thursday.

Some of the prisoners have filed lawsuits challenging their detention
without charges for up to three years so far. At the hearing, Boyle
urged District Judge Richard J. Leon to throw their cases out.

Attorneys for the prisoners argued that some were held solely on
evidence gained by torture, which they said violated fundamental
fairness and U.S. due process standards. But Boyle argued in a similar
hearing Wednesday that the detainees "have no constitutional rights
enforceable in this court."

Leon asked whether a detention based solely on evidence gathered by
torture would be illegal, because "torture is illegal. We all know
that."

Boyle replied that if the military's combatant status review tribunals
"determine that evidence of questionable provenance were reliable,
nothing in the due process clause (of the Constitution) prohibits them
from relying on it."

Leon asked whether there were any restrictions on using
torture-induced evidence.

Boyle replied that the United States never would adopt a policy that
would have barred it from acting on evidence that could have prevented
the Sept. 11, 2001, terrorist attacks even if the data came from
questionable practices like torture by a foreign power.

Several arguments underlie the U.S. court ban on products of torture.

"About 70 years ago, the Supreme Court stopped the use of evidence
produced by third-degree tactics largely on the theory that it was
totally unreliable," Harvard Law Professor Philip B. Heymann, a former
deputy U.S. attorney general, said in an interview. Subsequent high
court rulings were based on revulsion at "the unfairness and brutality
of it and later on the idea that confessions ought to be free and
uncompelled."

Leon asked whether U.S. courts could review detentions based on
evidence from torture conducted by U.S. personnel.

Boyle said torture was against U.S. policy and any allegations of it
would be "forwarded through command channels for military discipline."
He added, "I don't think anything remotely like torture has occurred
at Guantanamo" but noted that some U.S. soldiers there had been
disciplined for misconduct, including a female interrogator who
removed her blouse during questioning.

The International Committee of the Red Cross said Tuesday it has given
the Bush administration a confidential report critical of U.S.
treatment of Guantanamo detainees. The New York Times reported the Red
Cross described the psychological and physical coercion used at
Guantanamo as "tantamount to torture."

The combatant status review tribunals comprise three colonels and
lieutenant colonels. They were set up after the Supreme Court ruled in
June that the detainees could ask U.S. courts to see to it they had a
proceeding in which to challenge their detention. The panels have
reviewed 440 of the prisoners so far but have released only one.

The military also set up an annual administrative review which
considers whether the detainee still presents a danger to the United
States but doesn't review enemy combatant status. Administrative
reviews have been completed for 161.

Boyle argued these procedures are sufficient to satisfy the high
court.

Noting that detainees cannot have lawyers at the combatant status
review proceedings and cannot see any secret evidence against them,
detainee attorney Wes Powell argued "there is no meaningful
opportunity in the (proceedings) to rebut the government's claims."

Leon suggested that if federal judges start reviewing the military's
evidence for holding foreign detainees there could be "practical and
collateral consequences ... at a time of war."

And he suggested an earlier Supreme Court ruling might limit judges to
checking only on whether detention orders were lawfully issued and
review panels were legally established.

Leon and Judge Joyce Hens Green, who held a similar hearing Wednesday,
said they would try to rule soon on whether the 59 detainees may
proceed with their lawsuits.
```

```
"Robert Wagner" <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net> wrote in message
news:iia7r05ancbggahq429pg0dkh9jr4va7uc@4ax.com...
> On Sun, 5 Dec 2004 16:40:48 +1300, "Pete Dashwood"
> <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote:
…[39 more quoted lines elided]…

USA
> >is a great nation. It will stay that way only so long as the people in it
> >remain true to the ideals on which it was founded.
…[95 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Robert, it is quite apparent that you are genuinely concerend about these
issues.

(I would be too, if my government was doing the things you describe. We have
a known terrorist here who entered the country illegally, is wanted by
Interpol and a dozen other states, and has been incarcerated without trial
for just over a year now. He has been given access to lawyers, well wishers
etc and is not confined in chains in a dungeon. There has never been any
question of torture (mental or physical) yet there are demonstrations in
Wellington (our Capital) for his release and the grant of refugee status.
Why? Because it isn't the ""Kiwi" way to lock people up without due process.
We require a "Fair Go" for EVERYONE. I'm sure in my own mind we would be a
lot better off without this guy in our society, and so are many of his
supporters, but it just "isn't a fair go...". Funny little country this. I
can't help but love it, even though I know patriotism can cause wars...)

The behaviours that Don and yourself have alluded to are certainly cause for
concern. When the head of state takes absolute power it is time for the
people to start thinking about changing the head of state. When soldiers
indulge in brutality using the need for information as an excuse, it is time
to look at who is leading the soldiers. (I was a (at first reluctant)
soldier. It is an honourable calling. Soldiers are not murderers or
torturers, like terrorists.) When you can't tell the good guys from the bad
guys any more it is a pretty fair bet you are losing the war.

What to do about it?

I know from my own experience in America that there are people there who
will be as concerned about this as the rest of us. Surely there must be
opposition to these measures. What of the Supreme Court? Is there no
possible calling to answer for these actions? We have seen Congessional
enquiries before. What if millions of people wrote to their Congressmen and
demanded these practices be stopped? There must be right minded people even
in the armed forces who are disgusted by this.

Are there no Senators or Congressmen who would act as a rallying point for
putting in some checks and balances?

The war against Terrorism is something that affects all of us. Strong
measures are necessary.

But if, in combating scum, we become scum, then the scum have won.

Pete.
```

```

In article <31il6sF3b3l5dU1@individual.net>, "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> writes:
> 
> I know from my own experience in America that there are people there who
> will be as concerned about this as the rest of us. Surely there must be
> opposition to these measures.

Indeed.  A great many of us voted, for example.

> What of the Supreme Court?

It takes a while for things to get to the Supreme Court.  The SC doesn't
just issue rulings on any matter that comes to its attention; it's not
the Papacy.  There must be a case appealed through the Federal court
system to the SC, and then the SC must decide to consider it, and then
it must be added to its docket, which of course is scheduled well in
advance.

> Is there no
> possible calling to answer for these actions? We have seen Congessional
> enquiries before. What if millions of people wrote to their Congressmen and
> demanded these practices be stopped? There must be right minded people even
> in the armed forces who are disgusted by this.

Sure.  There isn't currently a sufficient vocal mass to persuade a
majority of Congress that its political future depends on restraining
the Executive Branch.  Members of Congress are probably correct in
that calculation - their jobs are more secure, at this point, in not
challenging the Bush Administration's power grab.

You must remember that most of the US electorate is gruesomely ill-
informed (as poll after poll shows) and easily manipulated.

> The war against Terrorism is something that affects all of us. Strong
> measures are necessary.

The "war against Terrorism" is a hobgoblin.  There is no such thing,
and there can be no such thing; terrorism is a strategy, not a group
of people.

There are a couple of wars against specific groups being carried out
by the US at the moment.  Exactly what measures are necessary for them
are highly debatable.

Similarly, exactly what measures are necessary, or desirable, to
promote the security of the US against terrorist tactics, by whomever
might elect to use them, is highly debatable.
```

```
> The "war against Terrorism" is a hobgoblin.  There is no such thing,

We also are warring against any country that doesn't currently do what we do
from threatening to emulate us.   Such things as having nuclear weapons or
invading countries we don't like.

Samuel Delaney wrote a story "Time Considered as a Helix of Semiprecious
Stones", in which it is recognized that if you get away with a crime for a
while, the future cops were no longer interested.   It is change that they fight
against.

There was a point in Clavel's _Shogun_ where the shogun tells the Dutchman that
the very worst crime is treason.  The Duchman replies "unless you win", and the
Shogun agreed.
```

```
On Tue, 14 Dec 2004 15:20:05 GMT, "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>
wrote:

>
>On 13-Dec-2004, "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com> wrote:
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
>Che Guevara.

All three fought for a National Army, which includes revolutionary
armies. Present-day terrorists don't belong to a nation, they belong
to a movement. They do not attack a nation or coilition but rather
individual  targets of opportunity. Their goal is not to replace one
regime with their own, it is to destabilize many institutions which
they view as a bloc i.e. The First World. 

>But there have been war-like activities between the powerful and the
>close-to-powerless forever.   Those who don't have power have always used
>tactics which could be called terrorism.    Those who have the power say anybody
>who won't stand up and fight mano a mano are cowards.   I suppose Goliath's
>supporters thought David was a coward for using a sling.

The word 'war' means hostile conflict between states or nations.
Phrases like 'war on drugs' , 'war on poverty' and 'class warfare' are
etymological perversions. What terrorists are doing is outside the
definition of war in the Geneva Accord, the UN Charter, any UN
Resolution, or any other public international law.

They're not guerrillas because they're not engaged in 'irregular
warfare'.  They are not insurgents or rebels because they're not
fighting their own governments. They don't have enough popular support
to legitimize the rodomontade that they represent The Nation of Islam.
They are simply anarchists, which is defined as:
1 : one who rebels against any authority, established order, or ruling
power.
2 : one who believes in, advocates, or promotes anarchism or anarchy;
especially : one who uses violent means to overthrow the established
order.

Some will disagree by pointing out that anarchy means the absence of
government. Don't they want a state based on Islamic law? Sure, for
their people. But Muslims are not their primary target. What do they
want for the First World? The answer is disorder and anarchy.
```

```
facilitate President Bush's attack on Iraq which helped bin Laden's cause much
more than anything he could have done directly.
```

```
Many Americans are. They don't know how to stop it.

>(I would be too, if my government was doing the things you describe. We have
>a known terrorist here who entered the country illegally, is wanted by
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>can't help but love it, even though I know patriotism can cause wars...)

The purpose of torture is to get information, not to torment or punish
someone. (Remember, I used to be in the information gathering
business. :) It doesn't sound like this guy has secrets to hide, so
there'd be no reason for torture. Give him a fair hearing, then kick
him out. It should take days, not a year.

>The behaviours that Don and yourself have alluded to are certainly cause for
>concern. When the head of state takes absolute power it is time for the
>people to start thinking about changing the head of state.

They just had a chance, and they re-elected him. Next time: Hillary
vs. ??? Here's a clue:

 12.03.2004
The front page of USAToday's Fri/Sat/Sun issue is "Should the
Constitution be amended for Arnold?"
The resulting avalanche of hits took our website down. Having handled
5 million hits on this website a few weeks ago, we'll be fascinated to
find out how many visitors it took to take us down today. (We won't
know for a few days how many people checked AmendUs.org out.)

> When soldiers
>indulge in brutality using the need for information as an excuse, it is time
>to look at who is leading the soldiers. (I was a (at first reluctant)
>soldier. It is an honourable calling. Soldiers are not murderers or
>torturers, like terrorists.) 

Some are. Our prisonerf interrogator was fired as a drill instructor
for being too sadistic. That's like firing an accountant for being too
conservative.

Actually, the worst torture is self-inflicted ... the fear of what
might happen. He understood that brilliantly. He cracked people in
hours, by detecting their weakness (everyone has at least one) and
issuing threats against it.

>When you can't tell the good guys from the bad
>guys any more it is a pretty fair bet you are losing the war.
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>in the armed forces who are disgusted by this.

Neither the Supreme Court nor Congress has jurisdiction outside the
US. Right minded people are abandoning the administration. Three
high-level officials left last week, including the head of Homeland
Security.

>Are there no Senators or Congressmen who would act as a rallying point for
>putting in some checks and balances?
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>But if, in combating scum, we become scum, then the scum have won.

We're the ones trying to change the other side to be like us.

I'm not convinced Arabs planned the 9/11 attack. I think they were
fall guys, in the same way McVeigh and Oswald were set up as solitary
villians. There are too many inconsistancies in the official story.
For instance, cellphone calls from 30K feet are a physical
impossibility. Steel framed buildings do not collapse from the heat
produced by a kerosene fire .. especially the number seven building,
which wasn't even hit.

Who would plan such an event? Look at who benefitted. Certainly not
Arab countries.
```
