###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Infinite Loops and Explicit Exits

_(reply depth: 32)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2004-11-15T11:15:08-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0411151115.457be80c@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<cm8pp2$e3$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <9Kl5eEXuflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de> <53mdp0tp5n26v81vibqoud7jjabnnkvrhj@4ax.com> <9Kp6-dkuflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de> <419773DB.6060007@optonline.net> <m38gp0tu6mgaikuont72qa9us4imkr09ql@4ax.com>`

```
Robert Wagner <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net> wrote

> The only hardware change was the addition of stack(s). Early
> compilers, not having hardware stacks, created them by chain-linking
> register save areas (for CALL) or putting return addresses into an
> array (for PERFORM). The mechanics have changed but the principle
> hasn't -- programs store a return address SOMEwhere. 

Yes, they do.  And in machines of my experience that was in the EXIT
statement placeholder at the end of the PERFORM range.  Actually the
particular compilers did not require an EXIT as it would always add
nulops at the end of each paragraph and section.  A PERFORM would be
done by 1) setting return address at end of scope, 2) jumping to the
start of scope, 3) clearing the return back to nulops.

If you jumped out of a PERFORM range and then dropped through the
paragraph the residual return would bite.  Overlapping performs did
not work, and were banned anyway.
 
> Cobol was advertised to be machine-independent. It sounds odd to now
> hear 'The hardware made us do it.'

Cobol was/is 'machine independent', but to be that the language had to
cater for how implementations would be done on different types of
hardware.  The EXIT in its own paragraph was part of the language
_because_ that is where the PERFORM return was stored on some
machines.

In fact, even on stack based machines, code at the end of each
paragraph need to check somehow whether it is the end of a perform
scope or is to drop through. It can't just 'return to last item on
stack' because this is where _some_ PERFORM might be done.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Infinite Loops and Explicit Exits

_(reply depth: 33)_

- **From:** l.willms@jpberlin.de (Lueko Willms)
- **Date:** 2004-11-15T20:54:00+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9KtKloA9flB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de>`
- **References:** `<cm8pp2$e3$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <m38gp0tu6mgaikuont72qa9us4imkr09ql@4ax.com> <217e491a.0411151115.457be80c@posting.google.com>`

```
.    On  15.11.04
  wrote  riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
     on  /COMP/LANG/COBOL
     in  217e491a.0411151115.457be80c@posting.google.com
  about  Re: Infinite Loops and Explicit Exits


r> Cobol was/is 'machine independent', but to be that the language had to
r> cater for how implementations would be done on different types of
r> hardware.  The EXIT in its own paragraph was part of the language
r> _because_ that is where the PERFORM return was stored on some
r> machines.

   But which neither did nor does require that this address is  
referenced by a label in COBOL.

   It is only to provide an address to jump to via GOTO that a  
paragrah, i.e. a label, is coded at the end of the PERFORMED sequence  
of statements, and since the paragraph was not allowed to be empty, it  
got the EXIT statement, which is rather a NOP in assembler language,  
or a CONTINUE in the later COBOLese.


Yours,
Lï¿½ko Willms                                     http://www.willms-edv.de
/--------- L.WILLMS@jpberlin.de -- Alle Rechte vorbehalten --

Wenn ein Buch und ein Kopf zusammenstoï¿½en und es klingt hohl, ist das allemal im Buch? -G.C.Lichtenberg
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Infinite Loops and Explicit Exits

_(reply depth: 33)_

- **From:** Robert Wagner <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net>
- **Date:** 2004-11-16T06:05:39+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ogqip0pi067o8pifjagg29gv9ntotlppco@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<cm8pp2$e3$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <9Kl5eEXuflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de> <53mdp0tp5n26v81vibqoud7jjabnnkvrhj@4ax.com> <9Kp6-dkuflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de> <419773DB.6060007@optonline.net> <m38gp0tu6mgaikuont72qa9us4imkr09ql@4ax.com> <217e491a.0411151115.457be80c@posting.google.com>`

```
On 15 Nov 2004 11:15:08 -0800, riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard) wrote:

>Robert Wagner <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net> wrote
>
…[20 more quoted lines elided]…
>machines.

No. There has always been a need to cater to single-paragraph
PERFORMs. Poorly optimized early compilers generated conditional exit
code at the end of EVERY paragraph. Thus, PERFORM A THRU A-EXIT ran
slower than PERFORM A because of extra code at the end of A. 

>In fact, even on stack based machines, code at the end of each
>paragraph need to check somehow whether it is the end of a perform
>scope or is to drop through. It can't just 'return to last item on
>stack' because this is where _some_ PERFORM might be done.

At least one compiler provided an option to do exactly that. By
selecting the option, you promised the compiler there were no
fall-thru paragraphs, such as the one before A-EXIT. I always used
that option on Realia.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Infinite Loops and Explicit Exits

_(reply depth: 30)_

- **From:** Robert Wagner <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net>
- **Date:** 2004-11-15T06:20:03+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<p52gp0teu0t44ddft6rt52uvnr98r6p2v6@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<cm8pp2$e3$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <9Kl5eEXuflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de> <53mdp0tp5n26v81vibqoud7jjabnnkvrhj@4ax.com> <9Kp6-dkuflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de>`

```
On 14 Nov 2004 08:58:00 GMT, l.willms@jpberlin.de (Lueko Willms)
wrote:

>RW> Cobol's structures via group names are better than any other
>RW> language.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>... END RECORD.

No, he cannot. Pascal (C. Java. etc.) records are defined 'inside
out'.  Consider this Cobol example, cleanly defined from beginning to
end:

01  person.
     02  real-name..
           03  given-name       pic x(20).
           03  surname            pic x(20).
     02  date-of-birth.
           03  year        comp  pic  9(04).
           03  month     comp   pic  9(02).
           03  da           comp   pic  9(02).
     02  age               comp   pic  9(02).

In Pascal, it would look like this. First one defines interior
'records', lastly the real record.

type
   name = record
     GivenName, Surname: array [1..20] of char;
   end;

   date = record
     year, month, da: integer;
   end;

   person = record
     RealName : name;
     DateOfBirth : date;
     Age : integer;
   end;

It gets worse when he wants to reference an element. He must give
full-path qualification on every reference, even though the name is
distinct. A Cobol programmer can say 'move 'Wagner' to surname'; a
Pascal (C, Java) programmer must say:

person.name.Surname := 'Wagner';

or 

with person do begin
   name.Surname := 'Wagner';
end;

When the language punishes one for defining structures well, the
result is flat structures no more than one level deep. That's why I
said Cobol's structures are better than any other language. (PL/I is
similar to Cobol; the others inherited their style from Algol).

>>> but left many other areas open to be abused by
>>> programmers clinging to their assembler lessons.
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>features (e.g. the underutilized REPORT WRITER);

It is widely believed that C is 'machine-independent assembly
language' or 'close to the metal'. That's not true. C is no more or
less close to the machine than Cobol or other 3GL, but people believe
it is. That's why so much systems software was written in C rather
than Cobol.

I tend to agree with your point about top-down design, but in the
context of programmers rather than the language itself. C became
available to the public in the early '70s, about the same time that
structured programming was introduced to computer science undergrad
curricula. Both had been born in the late '60s, but were not widely
known then.

As a result, C programmers had known nothing but structured
programming. They did not have to unlearn bad habits developed in the
'60s, nor change an existing body of work. 

I think a more subtle reason lies in PERFORM being the primary
dispatch to out-of-line code, rather than CALL. With CALL, it's
obvious that the procedure/function is a 'program' worthy of formal
parameter checking and its own local variables. That organization
lends itself to hierarchy charts, either formal or mental. A PERFORMed
paragraph is more easily abused, more easily seen as a fist-full of
code thrown out-of-line for convenience. 

Even in large assembly language programs in the '60s, the primary
organization was callable PROCs (CSECTs on IBM), not something
analogous to performed paragraphs. If early Cobol had used CALL
instead of PERFORM, I think its practice would have evolved into a
better style. 

>b) COBOL did not provide proper decision and looping facilities until  
>25 years of its existence, and this miseducated generations of COBOL  
>programmers;

PERFORM .. VARYING .. UNTIL was in the '68 standard, at least. If
memory serves, it was available in '61. 

>  Besides, one can write bad programs in any language; I have seen  
>awful examples in Pascal...

But other Pascal programmers did not apologize for it by saying
'matter of style' or 'personal preference'. They called it Bad Code.

>>>   Having the same name for the same object in any record a) makes
>>> clear what this data is about
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>   ????

I answered above.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Infinite Loops and Explicit Exits

_(reply depth: 31)_

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com
- **Date:** 2004-11-15T05:19:29-05:00

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Infinite Loops and Explicit Exits

_(reply depth: 31)_

- **From:** l.willms@jpberlin.de (Lueko Willms)
- **Date:** 2004-11-15T16:50:00+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9KtGpMyuflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de>`
- **References:** `<cm8pp2$e3$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <9Kp6-dkuflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de> <p52gp0teu0t44ddft6rt52uvnr98r6p2v6@4ax.com>`

```
.    On  15.11.04
  wrote  spamblocker-robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
     on  /COMP/LANG/COBOL
     in  p52gp0teu0t44ddft6rt52uvnr98r6p2v6@4ax.com
  about  Re: Infinite Loops and Explicit Exits


   Declaring records in COBOL and Pascal:

RW> No, he cannot. Pascal (C. Java. etc.) records are defined 'inside
RW> out'.  Consider this Cobol example, cleanly defined from beginning to
RW> end:
RW>
RW> 01  person.
RW>      02  real-name..
RW>            03  given-name       pic x(20).
RW>            03  surname            pic x(20).
RW>      02  date-of-birth.
RW>            03  year        comp  pic  9(04).
RW>            03  month     comp   pic  9(02).
RW>            03  da           comp   pic  9(02).
RW>      02  age               comp   pic  9(02).
RW>
RW> In Pascal, it would look like this. First one defines interior
RW> 'records', lastly the real record.
RW>
RW> type
RW>    name = record
RW>      GivenName, Surname: array [1..20] of char;
RW>    end;
RW>
RW>    date = record
RW>      year, month, da: integer;
RW>    end;
RW>
RW>    person = record
RW>      RealName : name;
RW>      DateOfBirth : date;
RW>      Age : integer;
RW>    end;


   Not necessarily. Records can be nested. One might as well write  
this:

    type

       date = record
         year, month, da: integer;
       end;

       person = record
          RealName :record
                      GivenName, Surname: array [1..20] of char;
                    end;


         DateOfBirth : date;
         Age : integer;
       end;


RW> It gets worse when he wants to reference an element. He must give
RW> full-path qualification on every reference, even though the name is
RW> distinct. A Cobol programmer can say 'move 'Wagner' to surname'; a
RW> Pascal (C, Java) programmer must say:
RW>
RW> person.name.Surname := 'Wagner';


   actually it had to be person.Realname.Surname

   But in that respect, you are right.

   But with proper naming, one will have names which have to be  
qualified, and in that respect the above form requires a little less  
typing than COBOL, which would require

   MOVE 'Wagner' TO Surname of Realname of person
   .


RW> When the language punishes one for defining structures well, the
RW> result is flat structures no more than one level deep. That's why I
RW> said Cobol's structures are better than any other language.

   They could, but many, if not most, COBOL programmers think their  
datanames should be unique within the program, or even the compilation  
unit, or even the whole application! "Just to confuse the Russians".


RW> I think a more subtle reason lies in PERFORM being the primary
RW> dispatch to out-of-line code, rather than CALL. With CALL, it's
RW> obvious that the procedure/function is a 'program' worthy of formal
RW> parameter checking and its own local variables.

   Even CALLing separately compiled programs was not in the first  
standards, I think. The old code charts which I have for COBOL-68  
compilers mention constructs like

   ENTER LINKAGE.
   CALL 'externprog'.
   EXIT LINKAGE.

RW> PERFORM .. VARYING .. UNTIL was in the '68 standard, at least. If
RW> memory serves, it was available in '61.

   Sure, but only as executing a SECTION (or paragraph), i.e. some  
remote piece of code, and without borders for that SECTION, but not  
immediately a sequence of statements as it was introduced finally with  
the 1985 standard.

   This separation of the condition specified as (negative) entry- 
condition for the loop from the specification of the action which  
creates this termination condition makes it difficult to understand  
the program.

   In this simple piece of code:

   READ input-file
   PERFORM UNTIL has-found-EOF
     PERFORM processing-of-record-contents
     READ input-file
   END-PERFORM

   one does immediately see the relationship between condition and how  
the condition is being created.



Yours,
Lï¿½ko Willms                                     http://www.willms-edv.de
/--------- L.WILLMS@jpberlin.de -- Alle Rechte vorbehalten --

Das Buch, das in der Welt am ehesten verboten zu werden verdiente, wï¿½re ein Katalogus von verbotenen Bï¿½chern. -G.C.Lichtenberg
```

```
On 15 Nov 2004 16:50:00 GMT, l.willms@jpberlin.de (Lueko Willms)
wrote:

>.    On  15.11.04
>  wrote  spamblocker-robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
>     on  /COMP/LANG/COBOL

>
>RW> It gets worse when he wants to reference an element. He must give
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>RW> person.RealName.Surname := 'Wagner';

>   in that respect, you are right.
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>   MOVE 'Wagner' TO Surname of Realname of person

Only in the unlikely event Surname appeared elsewhere. Even then, it
would be 'Surname of spouse'.

Why do block languages have so much trouble finding a unique name?

Supporters call Cobol verbose while ignoring unnecessary qualification
in their own languages.

>RW> When the language punishes one for defining structures well, the
>RW> result is flat structures no more than one level deep. That's why I
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>unit, or even the whole application! "Just to confuse the Russians".

Misuse doesn't disprove my assertion that Cobol structures are better.

>RW> I think a more subtle reason lies in PERFORM being the primary
>RW> dispatch to out-of-line code, rather than CALL. With CALL, it's
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>   EXIT LINKAGE.

It is true that CALL first appeared in the '74 standard, but nearly
all '68 compilers had it in the same form: CALL 'prog' USING ...

ENTER was highly non-standard, different for every compiler. Generally
it was not used for CALL but rather to imbed code written in another
language such as assembly. I didn't use ENTER. I compiled the assembly
language program separately and CALLed it.

>RW> PERFORM .. VARYING .. UNTIL was in the '68 standard, at least. If
>RW> memory serves, it was available in '61.
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>the 1985 standard.

There was no requirement for the paragraph to be remote. This is not
hard to understand:

   perform open-file  
   perform read-file
   perform one-record until end-of-file  
   perform close-file
   stop run.
one-record.
   ...
   perform read-file.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Infinite Loops and Explicit Exits

_(reply depth: 33)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2004-11-16T12:00:19-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0411161200.6c69e456@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<cm8pp2$e3$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <9Kp6-dkuflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de> <p52gp0teu0t44ddft6rt52uvnr98r6p2v6@4ax.com> <9KtGpMyuflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de> <2bsip0hp0vq9vrrub7kab15j75a1tqnsac@4ax.com>`

```
Robert Wagner <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net> wrote 

> Only in the unlikely event Surname appeared elsewhere. Even then, it
> would be 'Surname of spouse'.
> 
> Why do block languages have so much trouble finding a unique name?

Perhaps it is not so much the 'problem' with block languages, but more
the problem that you have understanding them.  Namespaces are very
important in modern languages, and in OO. If you had been using
Pascal, C, C++, Java, or following one of the other paths such as Ada,
then you would know why they developed in the way they did, what
problems they overcame, why their solutions work.

Cobol has a different way of solving the problems. This does not make
it better or worse, but just different.

> Supporters call Cobol verbose while ignoring unnecessary qualification
> in their own languages.

They are not 'unnecessary'.  The qualification is only needed when
there is a requirement to have 'records' (structs or even classes).
This may be bacause there will be multiple occurances of the data
items, which in Cobol would also require qualification, or because it
is emulating what Cobol does, eg with ISAM records.

It is a matter of scalability.  Writing trivial C programs may make it
seem that qualification is 'unnecessary' (as you do), having large
applications in C++ or Java makes it obvious that Namespaces are vital
to keeping the code bug free and extensible.

Take for example your enthusiasm for using 'ENTRY' instead of
paragraphs. You didn't see the danger of the resulting namespace
pollution, you don't understand namesapces. In trivial single programs
there was no problem, but the technique, as implemented by MF Cobol,
was not scalable. C programmers would have seen that immediately,
recognising exactly why C++ uses name mangling for the methods, and
why Java uses qualification.

> Misuse doesn't disprove my assertion that Cobol structures are better.

Your assertion about them being 'better' is purely subjective and
totally ignores why other languages are 'better' (mainly because you
don't seem to understand other languages and thus why they are
different).

> >   Even CALLing separately compiled programs was not in the first  
> >standards, I think. The old code charts which I have for COBOL-68  
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> >   EXIT LINKAGE.

In the very earliest compilers that I used CALL was a PLAN (assembler)
instruction and ENTER started alien code segment.  The parameters were
just listed after the CALL (no USING) and the assembler had to pick
them up with 'LDX 2 0(1)' to get the address of the data item.

> It is true that CALL first appeared in the '74 standard, but nearly
> all '68 compilers had it in the same form: CALL 'prog' USING ...

How did you survey _all_ compilers ?  How did XE20 do this ? 

Or did you just mean 'the small subset that I noticed'.
 
> >   Sure, but only as executing a SECTION (or paragraph), i.e. some  
> >remote piece of code, and without borders for that SECTION, but not  
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
>    perform read-file.

That _is_ 'remote', it just happens that there are only a few lines
remoting it from its perform. The others are more remote.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Infinite Loops and Explicit Exits

_(reply depth: 34)_

- **From:** Robert Wagner <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net>
- **Date:** 2004-11-17T06:18:59+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<kjmlp0hq1r84c0ha7097aupnfq4mj04t95@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<cm8pp2$e3$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <9Kp6-dkuflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de> <p52gp0teu0t44ddft6rt52uvnr98r6p2v6@4ax.com> <9KtGpMyuflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de> <2bsip0hp0vq9vrrub7kab15j75a1tqnsac@4ax.com> <217e491a.0411161200.6c69e456@posting.google.com>`

```
On 16 Nov 2004 12:00:19 -0800, riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard) wrote:

>Robert Wagner <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net> wrote 
>
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>problems they overcame, why their solutions work.

I understand that. What I dislike is their refusal to search. We're
all familiar with search engines, grep, spelling checkers, automatic
completion, etc. We know it can be done. Many of us written code to do
it. 

I think they value ideological 'purity' over service to the
programmer.

>> Supporters call Cobol verbose while ignoring unnecessary qualification
>> in their own languages.
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>is emulating what Cobol does, eg with ISAM records.

Structures are needed in non-trivial programs; they are required in
OO. But explicit qualification is often not necessary. In the context
of a compiler (as opposed to interpreter), the compile step will fail
if the name is not unique.

>It is a matter of scalability.  Writing trivial C programs may make it
>seem that qualification is 'unnecessary' (as you do), having large
>applications in C++ or Java makes it obvious that Namespaces are vital
>to keeping the code bug free and extensible.

I thought that's what prototypes and reflection were for.

>Take for example your enthusiasm for using 'ENTRY' instead of
>paragraphs. You didn't see the danger of the resulting namespace
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>why Java uses qualification.

The examples I gave were static calls. Any pollution would have been
reported at compile time.

>> It is true that CALL first appeared in the '74 standard, but nearly
>> all '68 compilers had it in the same form: CALL 'prog' USING ...
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>Or did you just mean 'the small subset that I noticed'.

Quote from Don Nelson, posted to this newsgroup in 1995:

"CALL was in the COBOL 74 standard and 
almost all COBOL 68 implementations had a CALL even though it was not 
part of the standard then (I have no idea why, since everyone knew 
how to make it work)."
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Infinite Loops and Explicit Exits

_(reply depth: 35)_

- **From:** mwojcik@newsguy.com (Michael Wojcik)
- **Date:** 2004-11-17T15:35:57+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<cnfr4t0k8@news3.newsguy.com>`
- **References:** `<cm8pp2$e3$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <9Kp6-dkuflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de> <p52gp0teu0t44ddft6rt52uvnr98r6p2v6@4ax.com> <9KtGpMyuflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de> <2bsip0hp0vq9vrrub7kab15j75a1tqnsac@4ax.com> <217e491a.0411161200.6c69e456@posting.google.com> <kjmlp0hq1r84c0ha7097aupnfq4mj04t95@4ax.com>`

```

In article <kjmlp0hq1r84c0ha7097aupnfq4mj04t95@4ax.com>, Robert Wagner <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net> writes:
> On 16 Nov 2004 12:00:19 -0800, riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard) wrote:
> >Robert Wagner <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net> wrote 
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
> programmer.

No, they value service to the programmer; it's just that the service
they value is not making assumptions that are very likely incorrect
about the programmer's intentions.  And that's the way I, for one,
like it.

When I write code, I want it to specify precisely what I mean it to
do.  A language and implementation which warns me of ambiguity is, in
my book, far preferable to one that does not.

I don't call accomodating lazy programmers much of a service at all,
since it only encourages them.
```

```
On 17 Nov 2004 15:35:57 GMT, mwojcik@newsguy.com (Michael Wojcik)
wrote:

>
>In article <kjmlp0hq1r84c0ha7097aupnfq4mj04t95@4ax.com>, Robert Wagner <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net> writes:
…[20 more quoted lines elided]…
>like it.

If the programmer had spelled out his intentions, there would be no
ambiguity to resolve. 

>When I write code, I want it to specify precisely what I mean it to
>do.  A language and implementation which warns me of ambiguity is, in
>my book, far preferable to one that does not.

When a data name occurs once, there is no ambiguity.

>I don't call accomodating lazy programmers much of a service at all,
>since it only encourages them.

What lazy programmers do is give up on structures. They write Flat
data definitions requiring no qualification. You'll see examples in
most tutorials. We're teaching new programmers to write in the style
of FORTRAN IV.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Infinite Loops and Explicit Exits

_(reply depth: 37)_

- **From:** mwojcik@newsguy.com (Michael Wojcik)
- **Date:** 2004-11-24T17:09:50+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<co2f8u01hiu@news1.newsguy.com>`
- **References:** `<cm8pp2$e3$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <9Kp6-dkuflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de> <p52gp0teu0t44ddft6rt52uvnr98r6p2v6@4ax.com> <9KtGpMyuflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de> <2bsip0hp0vq9vrrub7kab15j75a1tqnsac@4ax.com> <217e491a.0411161200.6c69e456@posting.google.com> <kjmlp0hq1r84c0ha7097aupnfq4mj04t95@4ax.com> <cnfr4t0k8@news3.newsguy.com> <1l65q0hq2o42n27onjdv9863avgnljnhq5@4ax.com>`

```

In article <1l65q0hq2o42n27onjdv9863avgnljnhq5@4ax.com>, Robert Wagner <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net> writes:
> On 17 Nov 2004 15:35:57 GMT, mwojcik@newsguy.com (Michael Wojcik)
> wrote:
…[19 more quoted lines elided]…
> ambiguity to resolve. 

Programmers in block-structured languages do spell out their intentions,
since the language requires them to do so.

> >When I write code, I want it to specify precisely what I mean it to
> >do.  A language and implementation which warns me of ambiguity is, in
> >my book, far preferable to one that does not.
> 
> When a data name occurs once, there is no ambiguity.

Untrue, since there may be typographical errors in the source, for
example.

Further, since most non-trivial source code is not static, but is
maintained and so changes, duplicate field names in new types, or
multiple objects of the same aggregate type, may be introduced at any
time, creating ambiguity.  That would then have to be resolved by the
maintenance programmer, who might misunderstand the intent of the
original code and err.

The point of requiring explicit qualification is that the language is
provided with better resources for identifying and describing
programmer error.  That is a far-better tradeoff than coddling lazy
programmers who don't want to type.

> >I don't call accomodating lazy programmers much of a service at all,
> >since it only encourages them.
> 
> What lazy programmers do is give up on structures. [snip rest of
> non sequitur]

Your unsubstantiated claim about what "lazy programmers do" is
irrelevant.  I say nothing but laziness justifies dropping the
requirement for explicit qualification in languages that require
it.  Show me an argument otherwise.
```

wrote:

>> What lazy programmers do is give up on structures. [snip rest of
>> non sequitur]
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>it.  Show me an argument otherwise.

The Cobol style encourages programmers to describe structural
relationships. Required explicit qualification discourages it.

My claim is substantiated by examining a number of tutorials, samples
and production code, which seldom contain more than one level.

```
Robert Wagner <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net> wrote

> The Cobol style encourages programmers to describe structural
> relationships. Required explicit qualification discourages it.

In other languages, explicit qualification does not 'discourage'
structuring of data.  In Cobol, the lack of requirement to have such
qualification is entirely neutral on whether structure is described or
not.
 
> My claim is substantiated by examining a number of tutorials, samples
> and ... , which seldom contain more than one level.

Tutorials and samples are designed to be simplifications.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Infinite Loops and Explicit Exits

_(reply depth: 42)_

- **From:** Robert Wagner <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net>
- **Date:** 2004-11-25T15:21:39+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<cgtbq0tkdurvcggtelrojt5un03kk6cqs6@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<9KtGpMyuflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de> <2bsip0hp0vq9vrrub7kab15j75a1tqnsac@4ax.com> <217e491a.0411161200.6c69e456@posting.google.com> <kjmlp0hq1r84c0ha7097aupnfq4mj04t95@4ax.com> <cnfr4t0k8@news3.newsguy.com> <1l65q0hq2o42n27onjdv9863avgnljnhq5@4ax.com> <co2f8u01hiu@news1.newsguy.com> <dfi9q05k354q9608hjtl766urppal2a6un@4ax.com> <217e491a.0411241428.2f6d88e4@posting.google.com> <n3gaq0pbj1ckqk10dvhp9pviokn48blsm8@4ax.com> <217e491a.0411242304.57b33c07@posting.google.com>`

```
On 24 Nov 2004 23:04:04 -0800, riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard) wrote:

>Robert Wagner <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net> wrote 
>
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>Perhaps you could write two tutorials here that illustrate this point.

I did .. in my original posting, copied here:

Pascal (C. Java. etc.) records are defined 'inside
out'.  Consider this Cobol example, cleanly defined from beginning to
end:

01  person.
     02  real-name..
           03  given-name       pic x(20).
           03  surname            pic x(20).
     02  date-of-birth.
           03  year        comp  pic  9(04).
           03  month     comp   pic  9(02).
           03  da           comp   pic  9(02).
     02  age               comp   pic  9(02).

In Pascal, it would look like this. First one defines interior
'records', lastly the real record.

type
   name = record
     GivenName, Surname: array [1..20] of char;
   end;

   date = record
     year, month, da: integer;
   end;

   person = record
     RealName : name;
     DateOfBirth : date;
     Age : integer;
   end;

It gets worse when he wants to reference an element. He must give
full-path qualification on every reference, even though the name is
distinct. A Cobol programmer can say 'move 'Wagner' to surname'; a
Pascal (C, Java) programmer must say:

person.RealNameame.Surname := 'Wagner';

or 

with person do begin
   RealName.Surname := 'Wagner';
end;
----------- end quotation ------------------

Yes, a flat structure is more complicated and less flexible. A typical
flat definition would look like this:

person = record
   GivenName: array [1..20] of char;  
   Surname [1..20] of char;  
   BirthYear, BirthMonth, BirthDay: integer;
   Age: integer;
end;

It is less flexible because one cannot operate on RealName nor on
DateOfBirth. To copy DateOfBirth requires three operations rather than
one.
```

   And, let me add this, in a 2002-compliant COBOL, one might have a  
TYPEDEF for NAME and another TYPEDEF for DATE, and declare the record  
PERSON composed of two sub-records of those two types.

   Also, in Pascal, one can "dequalify" the record components using  
the WITH statement, which can reference more than one record name.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Infinite Loops and Explicit Exits

_(reply depth: 44)_

- **From:** Robert Wagner <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net>
- **Date:** 2004-11-25T22:33:31+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<14hcq0ljuohl67dmoemqt91fthglm8d1el@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<9KtGpMyuflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de> <217e491a.0411242304.57b33c07@posting.google.com> <cgtbq0tkdurvcggtelrojt5un03kk6cqs6@4ax.com> <9LXZa9qeflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de>`

```
On 25 Nov 2004 16:57:00 GMT, l.willms@jpberlin.de (Lueko Willms)
wrote:

>.     Am  25.11.04
> schrieb  spamblocker-robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
…[31 more quoted lines elided]…
>one instead of a record of two distinct types.

I did not respond because nested record/struct raises SCOPE issues
that are beyond the Cobol discussion. 

Take the record:

type
person = record
  RealName:  name = record
    GivenName, Surname: array [1..20] of char;
  end;
end;

Now try to use 'name' in another record:

spouse = record
  ReallName: name;
end;

Logic says it should not work because the definition of 'name' is
within the scope of 'person'. But languages are inconsistent. I know
it works in C. So long as 'name' is defined before used, it is
promoted to file scope. I believe the same construction does not work
in Pascal. It works in some, but not all, C++ compilers. 

If 'name' is a pointer (char* name), the rules change again. Some
compilers will accept it as a forward pointer to a yet-undefined type.

I find it difficult to remember these inconsistencies, and suspect
others do as well. To be safe, they define all interior record/strucs
at file scope. 

>   And, let me add this, in a 2002-compliant COBOL, one might have a  
>TYPEDEF for NAME and another TYPEDEF for DATE, and declare the record  
>PERSON composed of two sub-records of those two types.

Yes, that does make Cobol look like block languages. TYPEDEF has been
available on Micro Focus for years. I use it routinely, but only for
universal types such as dates. 

>   Also, in Pascal, one can "dequalify" the record components using  
>the WITH statement, which can reference more than one record name.

I don't think so. I think this will reject:

with person, person.RealName do begin
   Surname := 'Wagner';
end;

It must be written:

with person do begin
  with RealName do begin
     Surname := 'Wagner';
  end;
end;
```

.    On  25.11.04

  about  Re: Infinite Loops and Explicit Exits


>>   where I said that one could as well define the record "person" as
>> one instead of a record of two distinct types.

RW> I did not respond because nested record/struct raises SCOPE issues
RW> that are beyond the Cobol discussion.

   But you made statements about other languages which were and are  
not correct.

RW> Take the record:
RW>
RW> type
RW> person = record
RW>   RealName:  name = record
RW>     GivenName, Surname: array [1..20] of char;
RW>   end;
RW> end;
RW>
RW> Now try to use 'name' in another record:
RW>

RW> end;

RW> within the scope of 'person'.

   Sure, id all depends of what you want. If you want a record-type  
"person" composed of two other record-types "Realname" and "Date",  
which should be used in other contexts too, then these types have to  
be declared as such.

   The same is true for COBOL's TYPEDEF, isn't it?

   OTOH, you might as well define a record-type which is deeper nested  
than just one level, similar to the record definitions of COBOL.

   Just do it the way which is appropriate to the actual real  
structures to be reflected in symbolic data.

On 26 Nov 2004 10:48:00 GMT, l.willms@jpberlin.de (Lueko Willms)
wrote:

>.    On  25.11.04

That's not true in C and C++, where struct can be declared inline and
used in later structures that are outside the scope of the first. 

>   The same is true for COBOL's TYPEDEF, isn't it?

Yes. TYPEDEF must be at the 01 level.

>   OTOH, you might as well define a record-type which is deeper nested  
>than just one level, similar to the record definitions of COBOL.

Your point is well taken -- one CAN write Cobol-style structures with
nested record/struc.

I've never seen it used in real-world C programs.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Infinite Loops and Explicit Exits

_(reply depth: 43)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2004-11-25T12:35:22-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0411251235.5cccefa4@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<9KtGpMyuflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de> <217e491a.0411161200.6c69e456@posting.google.com> <kjmlp0hq1r84c0ha7097aupnfq4mj04t95@4ax.com> <cnfr4t0k8@news3.newsguy.com> <1l65q0hq2o42n27onjdv9863avgnljnhq5@4ax.com> <co2f8u01hiu@news1.newsguy.com> <dfi9q05k354q9608hjtl766urppal2a6un@4ax.com> <217e491a.0411241428.2f6d88e4@posting.google.com> <n3gaq0pbj1ckqk10dvhp9pviokn48blsm8@4ax.com> <217e491a.0411242304.57b33c07@posting.google.com> <cgtbq0tkdurvcggtelrojt5un03kk6cqs6@4ax.com>`

```
Robert Wagner <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net> wrote 

> Pascal (C. Java. etc.) records are defined 'inside
> out'.  Consider this Cobol example, cleanly defined from beginning to
> end:
 
> In Pascal, it would look like this. First one defines interior
> 'records', lastly the real record.
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
>    end;

If you want to you can in C:

     struct {
         struct { char GivenName[20];
                  char SurName[20];
                } RealName;
         struct { int year, month, da;
                } DateofBirth;
         int    Age;
         } person;

but why would you - unless you were a Cobol programmer.

C like languages, and OO, are programmed on the basis of dealing with
_types_. Types are predefined along with the functions and procedures
that deal with them.  These types are reused as building blocks for
the programs.

Having, for example, a 'date' type means that all instances of that
can be used in the same way and the compiler can tell you when it is
misused.

> It gets worse 

Whether it is 'worse' or 'better' is purely a subjective issue. In
your pontifications you frequently assert that your opinions are some
sort of absolute measure.

> when he wants to reference an element. He must give
> full-path qualification on every reference, even though the name is
> distinct. 

In this trivial fragment of code it is distinct. The whole point of
creating a type, or a class, is that it will be reused, in which case
it is not distinct. In Cobol you would wind up having to use
qualification, or more likely you have:

        01  In-Record.
            03  In-Postal.
             05 In-PostalName ..
             05 In-PostalAddress ..
            03  In-Delivery.
             05 In-DeliveryName ..
             05 In-DeliveryAddress ..
             ...
        01  Out-Record.
            03  Out-Postal.
             05 Out-PostalName ..
              ...

or COPY "nameaddress" REPLACING ==(PRE)== BY ==In-==.

> A Cobol programmer can say 'move 'Wagner' to surname'; a
> Pascal (C, Java) programmer must say:
 
> person.RealNameame.Surname := 'Wagner';

I see not a lot of difference between:

            In-DeliveryName
and
            In.Delivery.Name

If one didn't use prefix then you wind up with 

             DeliveryName of In

You have contructed a completely artificial scenario.  Pascal and C
programmers don't write Cobol programs in C and Pascal, they write C
programs and Pascal programs.

Just like 'Real programmers can write FORTRAN programs in _any_
language', you are trying to write 'Cobol programs in Pascal' and then
finding that Pascal is not the same as Cobol and whining about it.

> Yes, a flat structure is more complicated and less flexible. A typical
> flat definition would look like this:
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> one.

But 'copy DateOfBirth' is a Cobolesque thing to do.  Cobol copies
things around because that is how Cobol works.

In C like languages and OO one reuses existing constructs.  In the
case of a date one uses date.h or a Date class and what is inside the
date is of only passing interest.  functions or methods are used to
process, it is simply not necessary to contrive to have 'BirthYear',
'BirthMonth', etc.  That is only done in Cobol because it is bereft of
adequate data types.

In C you have:

include <date.h>
...
       struct company {
            Address        Postal;
            Address        Delivery;
            LastOrder      Date;
            ..
            };

       struct company      Debtor;
       struct company      Supplier;
        

The problem with Cobol is that you cannot construct types in this way,
except by using OO perhaps, and so must resort to defining every field
in every record. In Cobol (pre'02) you cannot say:

       01  Person.
           03  Real        TYPE Name.
           03  Birth       TYPE Date.

You _have_ to lay it all out, and then they have to be given distinct
names using prefixes or use the IN/OF qualifier.

You may feel that Pascal and C make poor COBOLs, but COBOL is a very
poor C like language.  (actually Pascal makes a very poor anything
unless it is Delphi/Kylix IMHO).
 
It also seems that you have only dealt with tiny amounts of C and
Pascal.  Your criticisms may be valid of tiny examples or tutorial
sized programs.  You don't see the point of qualification, and in 20
line programs there is no point to them.  If you had done something
larger than a trivial program you would find that there is a point to
this qualification and you would also develop techniques (or even
style) that is appropriate to the idiom rather than trying to force
your Cobol idiom onto the language.
```

```
On 25 Nov 2004 12:35:22 -0800, riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard) wrote:

>Robert Wagner <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net> wrote 
>
…[33 more quoted lines elided]…
>but why would you - unless you were a Cobol programmer.

Because the input file is in that format.

>C like languages, and OO, are programmed on the basis of dealing with
>_types_. Types are predefined along with the functions and procedures
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>misused.

I concur when it comes to common types like Date. But files typically
contain many custom types. Strings and numbers come in various sizes.

>> It gets worse 
>
>Whether it is 'worse' or 'better' is purely a subjective issue. In
>your pontifications you frequently assert that your opinions are some
>sort of absolute measure.

Here we see a pontification about "the main design issue". You know
the author is a real computer dude because he uses "elliptical" when
he means 'elided'.

"Records are essentially nested data types; conceptually, they vary
little from language to language, but there is a variety of syntaxes. 

   Employee_record:
     record
       Employee_name:
         record
           first: string;
           middle: string;
           last: string;
         end record;
       Hourly_rate: float;
     end record;

The main design issue is one of simplified reference for complex
nestings of records. The fully qualified reference to the 'first'
field above is 

   Employee_record.Employee_name.first

Cobol and PL/1 permit elliptical references to 'first' so long as it
is unambiguous in the referencing environment (i.e.
Employee_record.first). Checking this can require significant compiler
overhead. Pascal provides the 'with' statement: "

http://www.cs.adfa.edu.au/~corstf/lectures/DS2/DS2.htm

Here we see mechanized 'users' having trouble with long path names.
Perhaps the tools are broken because the humans who wrote them are
unaccustomed to dealing with deep structures.

"Common python services such as pickle, deepcopy and comparison tests
either fail entirely or do not scale for highly recursive data
structures. This recipe presents a reversible "flatten" transformation
that allows for such operations."

http://aspn.activestate.com/ASPN/Cookbook/Python/Recipe/302535

"If your data structure is complex, you may be inclined to represent
your data as a set of nested IDL structure variables. While nesting
structure variables can help you create a data structure that emulates
the structure of your XML file, deeply nested structures can make your
code more difficult to create and maintain. Consider storing data in
several arrays of structures rather than a single, deeply-nested
structure. "

http://www.astro.princeton.edu/~esirko/idl_html_help/xml_parser7.html

>> when he wants to reference an element. He must give
>> full-path qualification on every reference, even though the name is
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
>              ...

You've been reading IBM mainframe code, where Cobol qualification is
forbidden. To avoid it, they qualify EVERY BLASTED NAME with the file
or record name. Intermediate qualifiers (Postal, Delivery) are
duplicated in subordinate elements. After three levels they bump into
the 30-byte limit on name size.

Then they complain because cross-reference tools fail to find
references to a data element.

>or COPY "nameaddress" REPLACING ==(PRE)== BY ==In-==.

No, REPLACING is also forbidden. They'd rather cut-and-paste twenty
copies of the same copybook and use a text editor to change the group
name. Maintenance? We'll cross that bridge when we come to it.

I'm not making this up. I just removed REPLACING and created twenty
copybooks.

>> A Cobol programmer can say 'move 'Wagner' to surname'; a
>> Pascal (C, Java) programmer must say:
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>            In.Delivery.Name

There isn't. Block languages force us to program like mainframers. The
only difference the limit on 30-byte names.

>You have contructed a completely artificial scenario.  Pascal and C
>programmers don't write Cobol programs in C and Pascal, they write C
>programs and Pascal programs.

Pascal and C programs read like BAD Cobol.

>Just like 'Real programmers can write FORTRAN programs in _any_
>language', you are trying to write 'Cobol programs in Pascal' and then
>finding that Pascal is not the same as Cobol and whining about it.

No, I avoid using those languages .. and provide an explanation of
why.

>> Yes, a flat structure is more complicated and less flexible. A typical
>> flat definition would look like this:
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
>things around because that is how Cobol works.

Nonsense. C++ took the revolutionary step of letting us copy
structures with an assignment operator (=) rather than memcpy(). How
progressive.

>In C like languages and OO one reuses existing constructs.  In the
>case of a date one uses date.h or a Date class and what is inside the
…[26 more quoted lines elided]…
>           03  Birth       TYPE Date.

Micro Focus has supported TYPEDEF for years. I use it routinely, but
only for universal types like Date. Names and addresses are not
uniformly sized. 

>You may feel that Pascal and C make poor COBOLs, but COBOL is a very
>poor C like language.  (actually Pascal makes a very poor anything
>unless it is Delphi/Kylix IMHO).

Cobol is an upgrade. It can do everything that C++ can, with code
that's more readable and elegant.

>It also seems that you have only dealt with tiny amounts of C and
>Pascal. 

By choice. But I understand them.

> Your criticisms may be valid of tiny examples or tutorial
>sized programs.  You don't see the point of qualification, and in 20
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>your Cobol idiom onto the language.

Here you expect readers to believe I spent a lifetime working only on
trivial programs. Since that's obviously wrong, they might question
other things you say.

Robert Wagner <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net> wrote 

> >If you want to you can in C:
> >
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> Because the input file is in that format.

You missed the point.  You wanted to contruct the whole record in one
piece with having to define the inner bits first.  Here I just did
that.  It can be done, but C programmers know not to do it that way.

> I concur when it comes to common types like Date. 

Well there yer go.

> But files typically
> contain many custom types. Strings and numbers come in various sizes.

The only reason being that Cobol programmers want to save a few bytes
here and there, something that you have been ranting against.

It is true that _Cobol_ files contain certain things, but files used
by C and Pascal programmers tend to be designed to suit what those
programmers want, not what Cobol programmers want.  And C++ use object
storage.

In between there are SQL databases.  Your argument is based on what
Cobol did in the 60s thru 80s and what Pascal did in the 70s. When
both go to SQL there are fewer difference.

> Here we see mechanized 'users' having trouble with long path names.
> Perhaps the tools are broken because the humans who wrote them are
> unaccustomed to dealing with deep structures.

Perhaps if you actually understand what that reference was talking
about you might understand the issues involved.  Your record example
had 2 levels, the python code was testing 4096 levels of data items. 
But then you probably don't know enough about Python to know what it
is doing.

> After three levels they bump into
> the 30-byte limit on name size.

That depends on the size of the prefixes.
 
> Then they complain because cross-reference tools fail to find
> references to a data element.

Do they ? Why would the xref tool fail ?

> No, REPLACING is also forbidden. ...

> I'm not making this up. I just removed REPLACING and created twenty
> copybooks.

Who wrote the REPLACING ? You ?

> >I see not a lot of difference between:
> >
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> only difference the limit on 30-byte names.

The only difference is that you don't like it.  I don't see the dotted
notation as an imposition.  Nor, it seems, do C like or OO
programmers.

> >You have contructed a completely artificial scenario.  Pascal and C
> >programmers don't write Cobol programs in C and Pascal, they write C
> >programs and Pascal programs.
> 
> Pascal and C programs read like BAD Cobol.

That may be your opinion, but it seems based on the idea that you
judge _everything_ by how it would look as a Cobol program, a rather
one dimensional view.


> No, I avoid using those languages .. and provide an explanation of
> why.

And those languages would probably avoid you, too.

> Nonsense. C++ took the revolutionary step of letting us copy
> structures with an assignment operator (=) rather than memcpy(). How
> progressive.

> Cobol is an upgrade. It can do everything that C++ can, with code
> that's more readable and elegant.

That may be _your_ opinion, probably based on not being used to seeing
C++ or Java.  It probably all looks like hen scratching to you.  But
to someone that is used too seeing Java or C++ your best Cobol
probably looks clunky.

It is all what one is used to.  I am used to Cobol and to C and to
Python and I switch to styles suitable to those.  I don't write Cobol
code in Python.
 
> >It also seems that you have only dealt with tiny amounts of C and
> >Pascal. 
> 
> By choice. But I understand them.

>> Your criticisms may be valid of tiny examples or tutorial
>> sized programs. ....

> Here you expect readers to believe I spent a lifetime working only on
> trivial programs.

While you have obviously worked on substancial amounts of Cobol, you
have only worked on trivial C and Pascal, and probably less on C++,
Java or Python, as you agree.

You may note that I was specific that I was referring to 'your
criticisms', you weren't criticising Cobol.

> Since that's obviously wrong, they might question
> other things you say.

They may question any and all of what I say, or even disagree with me
if they wish.  I don't expect my words to be accepted uncritically as
your seem to for yours.

- **References:** `<1l65q0hq2o42n27onjdv9863avgnljnhq5@4ax.com> <co2f8u01hiu@news1.newsguy.com> <dfi9q05k354q9608hjtl766urppal2a6un@4ax.com> <217e491a.0411241428.2f6d88e4@posting.google.com> <n3gaq0pbj1ckqk10dvhp9pviokn48blsm8@4ax.com> <217e491a.0411242304.57b33c07@posting.google.com> <cgtbq0tkdurvcggtelrojt5un03kk6cqs6@4ax.com> <217e491a.0411251235.5cccefa4@posting.google.com> <j0pcq0969r7ug98t9k22o2rnhmo27u9l11@4ax.com> <217e491a.0411252358.71fe4771@posting.google.com>`

```
On 25 Nov 2004 23:58:18 -0800, riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard) wrote:

>Robert Wagner <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net> wrote 
>
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
>that.  It can be done, but C programmers know not to do it that way.

You're correct when you say they don't. I've never seen C written that
way. My point is they should write it that way, because it follows the
structure of the record layout in the spec. If it were an output file,
they could send the above code to the recipient, who would understand
it.

>> I concur when it comes to common types like Date. 
>> But files typically
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>here and there, something that you have been ranting against.

>It is true that _Cobol_ files contain certain things, but files used
>by C and Pascal programmers tend to be designed to suit what those
>programmers want, not what Cobol programmers want. 

A good tool should be able to handle any data structure, not just
those created by people using the same language. 

How does a C programmer store a string, such as Name, in a file? His
null-terminated memory format is unsuited to storage in a file. He
must pad it to fixed length a'la Cobol or change the file format to
CSV. In either case, the field in the record is not a native C type.

What kind of programming language doesn't natively support
fixed-length fields in a file? A badly designed one.

>In between there are SQL databases.  Your argument is based on what
>Cobol did in the 60s thru 80s and what Pascal did in the 70s. When
>both go to SQL there are fewer difference.

None of these languages (except PL/SQL) support SQL types. They
require conversion to and from the language's types. 

When Cobol gets ANY LENGTH, there will be fewer differences. ANY
LENGTH is a Cobolized version of SQL's VARCHAR2.

>> After three levels they bump into
>> the 30-byte limit on name size.
>
>That depends on the size of the prefixes.

The universal length for identifiers, at least file names, is eight
characters. Just in case we have to return to assembly language.

>> No, REPLACING is also forbidden. ...
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>Who wrote the REPLACING ? You ?

Yes. A NEW program used six files with the same record layout. I wrote
one copybook for select, one FD and one record, then copied each six
times with REPLACING. Some mainframer insisted I remove REPLACING and
create eighteen copybooks, plus another twelve for headers and
trailers.

>> >I see not a lot of difference between:
>> >
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>programmers.

The imposition is not dot notation, it is the requirement for
superfluous qualification. Some defend that, then in the next breath
call Cobol 'too verbose'. I'm pointing out there's a contradiction
between the two.

>> No, I avoid using those languages .. and provide an explanation of
>> why.
>
>And those languages would probably avoid you, too.

They're not content with avoiding Cobol, they throw rocks at it with
terms like verbose and dinosaur. When I point out deficiencies in
their language, they brush them aside, saying 'What do you expect from
a Cobol programmer?'

Then they design yet another language, hoping no one will notice they
did it to escape flaws in the old one.

>That may be _your_ opinion, probably based on not being used to seeing
>C++ or Java.  It probably all looks like hen scratching to you. 

I see bad logic cloaked by syntax unreadable by an outsider. If it
were written in Cobol, the bad logic would be more visible. 

Occasionally, I see elegant style and logic. The logic would be
elegant in Cobol as well, but people and shops interested in good code
have largely abandoned Cobol. 

>But to someone that is used too seeing Java or C++ your best Cobol
>probably looks clunky.

They don't say it's clunky; they say 'Wow. I didn't know Cobol could
do that.'  and 'My professor said Cobol couldn't handle that.'

>It is all what one is used to.  I am used to Cobol and to C and to
>Python and I switch to styles suitable to those.  I don't write Cobol
>code in Python.

I do write C code in Cobol. I don't think of it as C; I think of it as
generic language-independent programming.

>> Here you expect readers to believe I spent a lifetime working only on
>> trivial programs.
>
>While you have obviously worked on substancial amounts of Cobol, you
>have only worked on trivial C

No. I've worked on large production systems.

> and Pascal, and probably less on C++, Java or Python, as you agree.

That's true.

>They may question any and all of what I say, or even disagree with me
>if they wish.  I don't expect my words to be accepted uncritically as
>your seem to for yours.

If I said the sky was blue, people would argue that it's black half of
the time. Real programmers are irritated by my defense of Cobol;
mainframers are afraid they'll be exposed.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Infinite Loops and Explicit Exits

_(reply depth: 47)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2004-11-26T15:22:34-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0411261522.18d28572@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<1l65q0hq2o42n27onjdv9863avgnljnhq5@4ax.com> <dfi9q05k354q9608hjtl766urppal2a6un@4ax.com> <217e491a.0411241428.2f6d88e4@posting.google.com> <n3gaq0pbj1ckqk10dvhp9pviokn48blsm8@4ax.com> <217e491a.0411242304.57b33c07@posting.google.com> <cgtbq0tkdurvcggtelrojt5un03kk6cqs6@4ax.com> <217e491a.0411251235.5cccefa4@posting.google.com> <j0pcq0969r7ug98t9k22o2rnhmo27u9l11@4ax.com> <217e491a.0411252358.71fe4771@posting.google.com> <e8leq0hljooe9c9ubsl2pokkf6u4u5nqi9@4ax.com>`

```
Robert Wagner <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net> wrote 

> You're correct when you say they don't. I've never seen C written that
> way. My point is they should write it that way, because it follows the
> structure of the record layout in the spec. 

No. Which 'spec' are you looking at ? a _Cobol_ one no doubt. System
specs are written for the implementation language.  On Cobol systems
the 'spec' defines the records that Cobol would use.

For systems written in C/Java the 'spec' would define the record in
terms of C/Java.  Once again you show your limited scope by thinking
that the world is defined in Cobol terms because that is all you have
ever seen.

> If it were an output file,
> they could send the above code to the recipient,

 who would understand
> it.

Output files sent to 'recipients' are in a form that they can
understand which may be EDIFACT, CSV, XML, or some agreed format.
Sending out file dumps is a crap way of distributing data.

> who would understand it.

What they would understand is a function of what they are used to.
Sending out an FD of a struct as a definition is a crap way of doing
it.  What is required is a properly documented list of fields.

> >It is true that _Cobol_ files contain certain things, but files used
> >by C and Pascal programmers tend to be designed to suit what those
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> those created by people using the same language. 

Which is why a 'good tool' is SQL databases.  Interestingly SQL
doesn't nest fields like you want for Cobol, but does have structured
types such as TIMESTAMP.

> How does a C programmer store a string, such as Name, in a file? His
> null-terminated memory format is unsuited to storage in a file. 

Why do you think that null-terminated is unsuited to files ?  Is it
because you have only ever seen Cobol files ?

> He must pad it to fixed length a'la Cobol

_Must_ he ? Why is that, is it so that it fits into the 'world as
defined by Robert Wagner' ?

> or change the file format to
> CSV. In either case, the field in the record is not a native C type.

So first you claim that he _must_ change it to non-native then you
whine that 'now it isn't native'.  It must be really easy to create
these criticisms, just expose your ignorence.
 
> What kind of programming language doesn't natively support
> fixed-length fields in a file? A badly designed one.

Actually C has no problem at all outputting fixed-length fields really
easily. See fprintf().

Of course your 'badly' is simply your way of saying that it isn't like
Cobol.

> None of these languages (except PL/SQL) support SQL types. They
> require conversion to and from the language's types. 

How wierd then that Cobol often has to use an extension in the form of
COMP-5 in order to emulate a C type for accessing SQL.

> When Cobol gets ANY LENGTH, there will be fewer differences. ANY
> LENGTH is a Cobolized version of SQL's VARCHAR2.

And Cobol is adding more so that it can keep up with C like languages
then.
 
> >That depends on the size of the prefixes.
> 
> The universal length for identifiers, at least file names, is eight
> characters. Just in case we have to return to assembly language.

Only in some tiny corners of the world that you crawl around in.

> Yes. A NEW program used six files with the same record layout. I wrote
> one copybook for select, one FD and one record, then copied each six
> times with REPLACING. Some mainframer insisted I remove REPLACING and
> create eighteen copybooks, plus another twelve for headers and
> trailers.

Probably just to piss you off.
 
> The imposition is not dot notation, it is the requirement for
> superfluous qualification.

It isn't 'superfluous', it isn't an 'imposition'.

> Some defend that, then in the next breath call Cobol 'too verbose'.

They understand their language and don't understand Cobol, while you
are the reverse. People criticise what they fail to understand, are
you so ably demonstrate.

> They're not content with avoiding Cobol, they throw rocks at it with
> terms like verbose and dinosaur. When I point out deficiencies in
> their language, they brush them aside, saying 'What do you expect from
> a Cobol programmer?'

Exactly, and what's more, a Cobol programmer who refuses to understand
their languages.

> Then they design yet another language, hoping no one will notice they
> did it to escape flaws in the old one.

Oh, you mean like: scope terminators were added in '85 and hoped that
no one noticed that was a deficiency in older versions; and 'ANY
LENGTH' will be added in the meantime hope no one notices it is flaw ?

> I see bad logic cloaked by syntax unreadable by an outsider. If it
> were written in Cobol, the bad logic would be more visible. 

The stntax is only 'unreadable' to those who refuse to understand it.
 
> If I said the sky was blue, people would argue that it's black half of
> the time. Real programmers are irritated by my defense of Cobol;
> mainframers are afraid they'll be exposed.

I suspect that you irritate most people you meet because of your style
of argument which is more like evangulising.
```

procedural code instead of being declared in one place, separate from  
the procedural statements.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Infinite Loops and Explicit Exits

_(reply depth: 49)_

- **Date:** 2004-11-27T12:12:01-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<41A8C371.C549CFDC@mb.sympatico.ca>`
- **References:** `<1l65q0hq2o42n27onjdv9863avgnljnhq5@4ax.com> <e8leq0hljooe9c9ubsl2pokkf6u4u5nqi9@4ax.com> <217e491a.0411261522.18d28572@posting.google.com> <9Lek8Zz9flB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de>`

```
Lueko Willms wrote:
> 
 
> r> Actually C has no problem at all outputting fixed-length fields really
> r> easily. See fprintf().
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

That isn't a problem per se, it's just what you're used to.  Assemblers
and RPG's, and PROGRESS among languages that I'm familiar with, allow
definitions in the procedure.  Sometimes it's even better - a w/s field
that is only used in one short procedure, for instance.  If, however, as
is allowed in PROGRESS, the definition can be the result of a
conditional execution (or even, also as in PROGRESS, in part of a
compile-on-the-fly) then you have a point.

PL
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Infinite Loops and Explicit Exits

_(reply depth: 49)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2004-11-27T12:48:50-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0411271248.6c64b089@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<1l65q0hq2o42n27onjdv9863avgnljnhq5@4ax.com> <e8leq0hljooe9c9ubsl2pokkf6u4u5nqi9@4ax.com> <217e491a.0411261522.18d28572@posting.google.com> <9Lek8Zz9flB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de>`

```
l.willms@jpberlin.de (Lueko Willms) wrote 

>   Normally TIMESTAMP is _not_ a structured type, but just a serial day  
> number, or a real number (floating point) with the time of day as a  
> fraction of the full day.

That is an implementation issue as to whether it is held as a number
or a set of fields. With JDBC a Timestamp is represented as a Date
class object and a time in seconds.  As it also holds the TimeZone
information it cannot be a single simple number.
 
>    The problem with that is that the data definition is hidden in the  
> procedural code instead of being declared in one place, separate from  
> the procedural statements.

The data definition of an fprintf is a format.  The format is a text
string that can be held in a configuration file, or defined by a
#define, or created by some code, or several other ways.  It doesn't
have to be a literal in the parameters.

In some of my C conversion programs, from some years ago, I have the
format of several fixed record files each as an array of struct which
is #included (ie its a copybook) with each field given its format
string, a pointer to the data item, or a literal, and a type.  A
function then processes this in a loop to extract upon input and
create upon output.

If the file format changes I adjust the 'copybook'.  These 'data
definitions' are not in the procedural code but are separate.

I agree that nothing forces the C programmer to do it that way, but
that does not mean that they must be hidden.

However, I have moved on from there and now use templates for most
i-o, from printing forms in text or postscript to creating html, csv
and even fixed field files - in Cobol, C and Python.
```

l.willms@jpberlin.de (Lueko Willms) wrote 

> r> The data definition of an fprintf is a format.  The format is a text
> r> string that can be held in a configuration file, or defined by a
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> be declared explicitly.

It can be declared explicitly, or the FD record can be a simple PIC
X(n) and the formatting done in procedure code using, for example,
reference notation or STRING. I have done this (in different ways)
where, for example, the generalised report program used a dictionary
based system and a user selectable list to specify the output fields,
and of course for templated output.

As with any language there is no 'has to be', or at least very few.
```

.    On  30.11.04

     on  /COMP/LANG/COBOL

HB> So?

   So what?

   I'm not sure what your "so?" asks for -- as to reference notation  
(like in variable-x(startpos:length)), which is expressed in most  
other languages by a funktion SUBSTRING(var, start, lenth), I do use  
it the database development system which I do currently use as my main  
tool, VDP. I'n sure if this particular form of SubStr() is implemented  
in other langauges, too (slice of an array).

   String concatenation is also a normal thing in such programming  
languages.

   For UNSTRING, though, there are not so straightforward equivalences  
in other languages, but pattern matching has been around in SNOBOL for  
a long time, and in REXX I have the PARSE statement, and the WORD()  
function. For my VDP applications, I have written my own procedure to  
parse a string into words, with the separators handed over as a  
parameter.


Yours,

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Infinite Loops and Explicit Exits

_(reply depth: 58)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2004-12-01T12:00:49-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<col7th$4h9$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<1l65q0hq2o42n27onjdv9863avgnljnhq5@4ax.com> <9Lr69p99flB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de> <coi19j$4rq$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <9M2F-czeflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de> <col5ao$8jq$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```

"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote in message
news:col5ao$8jq$1@peabody.colorado.edu...
>
> On  1-Dec-2004, l.willms@jpberlin.de (Lueko Willms) wrote:
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> Being late additions, and being not at all distinctive for CoBOL does not
make
> them any less qualities of CoBOL.
>
> (add the COMPUTE statement to this as well)

COMPUTE doesn't seem to have been a LATE addition to COBOL, but rather an
"optional" capability specified in the standard that not all implementors
elected to include; it's Nucleus Level 2 in '74.  It's been in the spec from
COBOL's earliest days; all evidence I have at hand shows it as part of COBOL
'60.

STRING (likewise Nucleus Level 2) was (with UNSTRING) introduced in the '74
standard and was included in the '69 version of the JoD.  That doesn't, in
my view, qualify it as a "late addition".

Even reference modification has been around for nearly twenty years now.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

- **References:** `<dfi9q05k354q9608hjtl766urppal2a6un@4ax.com> <217e491a.0411241428.2f6d88e4@posting.google.com> <n3gaq0pbj1ckqk10dvhp9pviokn48blsm8@4ax.com> <217e491a.0411242304.57b33c07@posting.google.com> <cgtbq0tkdurvcggtelrojt5un03kk6cqs6@4ax.com> <217e491a.0411251235.5cccefa4@posting.google.com> <j0pcq0969r7ug98t9k22o2rnhmo27u9l11@4ax.com> <217e491a.0411252358.71fe4771@posting.google.com> <e8leq0hljooe9c9ubsl2pokkf6u4u5nqi9@4ax.com> <217e491a.0411261522.18d28572@posting.google.com>`

```
On 26 Nov 2004 15:22:34 -0800, riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard) wrote:

>Robert Wagner <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net> wrote 
>
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>ever seen.

A record spec states the card columns and field names, like this:

  1 16  account number
17 37  name 
38 44  amount
45 47  currency

Is there another way to do it?

>> If it were an output file,
>> they could send the above code to the recipient,
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>Sending out file dumps is a crap way of distributing data.

Boss: Acme needs a copy of our product file.
RW:   Do they want it in EDIFACT, CSV or XML? What's their IP address?
Boss: What are you blabbering about? Just copy the file.
RW:   In what format?
Boss: They said ASCII format. I'll take the floppy this afternoon.
 
>What they would understand is a function of what they are used to.
>Sending out an FD of a struct as a definition is a crap way of doing
>it.  What is required is a properly documented list of fields.

Ok, I'll print it on paper.

>> A good tool should be able to handle any data structure, not just
>> those created by people using the same language. 
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>types such as TIMESTAMP.

Yes it does. Subordinate structures are called Tables.

>> What kind of programming language doesn't natively support
>> fixed-length fields in a file? A badly designed one.
>
>Actually C has no problem at all outputting fixed-length fields really
>easily. See fprintf().

C must be a write-only language. Its Standard Library doesn't have
freadf().

>> None of these languages (except PL/SQL) support SQL types. They
>> require conversion to and from the language's types. 
>
>How wierd then that Cobol often has to use an extension in the form of
>COMP-5 in order to emulate a C type for accessing SQL.

SQL happily converts numbers to and from DISPLAY and PACKED.

>> When Cobol gets ANY LENGTH, there will be fewer differences. ANY
>> LENGTH is a Cobolized version of SQL's VARCHAR2.
>
>And Cobol is adding more so that it can keep up with C like languages
>then.

Oracle's Pro*COBOL supports VARCHAR, but not for any length, you have
to tell it the maximum:

"COBOL string datatypes are fixed length. However, Pro*COBOL lets you
declare a variable-length string pseudotype called VARCHAR. 

You define a VARCHAR host variable by adding the keyword VARYING to
its declaration, as shown in the following example: 

              ... 
         01  ENAME  PIC X(15) VARYING. 
         ... 

VARCHAR is an extended Pro*COBOL datatype or pre-declared group item.
For example, Pro*COBOL expands the VARCHAR declaration 
into a group item with length and string fields, as follows: 

         01  ENAME. 
             05  ENAME-LEN  PIC S9(4) COMP.
             05  ENAME-ARR  PIC X(15). 
-- end quotation --

>> Some mainframer insisted I remove REPLACING 

>Probably just to piss you off.

Before I arrived, they wrote hundreds of programs that way. Seems like
a lot of work just to piss me off.

>> Then they design yet another language, hoping no one will notice they
>> did it to escape flaws in the old one.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>LENGTH' will be added in the meantime hope no one notices it is flaw ?

IBM, Micro Focus and Fujitsu already have ANY LENGTH. It is a POINTER
to OCCURS DEPENDING ON. When I recommended it for parsing text, you
didn't like the idea, calling it '70s C style.

Pro*C lets one use pointers to any-length strings as host variables;
Pro*COBOL does not. After 2008, Oracle will have to support it.

>I suspect that you irritate most people you meet because of your style
>of argument which is more like evangulising.

"Men lean on arguments like a drunk leans on a lamppost -- not for
illumination but for support." Giles StAubin
```

> 
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…

the Cobol FD as you claimed.
 
You will also note that this description is not normally structed but
is flattened.

> Boss: Acme needs a copy of our product file.
> RW:   Do they want it in EDIFACT, CSV or XML? What's their IP address?
> Boss: What are you blabbering about? Just copy the file.
> RW:   In what format?
> Boss: They said ASCII format. I'll take the floppy this afternoon.

Exactly.  You can't send them an OSVS VSAM file or a Fujitsu ISAM file
or even an MF ISAM file if it is default format for Windows.  Even a
C-ISAM file may contain junk and won't physically be in sequence.

Sending an ISAM file is a crap way of distributing data.
  
> >  What is required is a properly documented list of fields.

As you have indicated above as a simple list of fields with starting
positions, length and actual descriptions.

> Yes it does. Subordinate structures are called Tables.

For any competent ISAM file system the ISAM files would relate 1:1 to
SQL tables foir the same application.

> C must be a write-only language. Its Standard Library doesn't have
> freadf().

You just haven't found it yet, or perhaps 'C for under 5s' hasn't
advanced you to that point yet.

> >How wierd then that Cobol often has to use an extension in the form of
> >COMP-5 in order to emulate a C type for accessing SQL.
> 
> SQL happily converts numbers to and from DISPLAY and PACKED.

The preprocessor library may well use functions to do that, just as is
done with C.
 
> VARCHAR is an extended Pro*COBOL datatype or pre-declared group item.
> For example, Pro*COBOL expands the VARCHAR declaration 
> into a group item with length and string fields, as follows: 
 
Yes, I'm not sure what your point was there.  It seemed to be 'Cobol
can't do this'.

> IBM, Micro Focus and Fujitsu already have ANY LENGTH. It is a POINTER
> to OCCURS DEPENDING ON. When I recommended it for parsing text, you
> didn't like the idea, calling it '70s C style.

I did say that your explict use of malloc/free was 'what C did in the
70s'. As you said just the other day, programmers shouldn't be trusted
with such things.

I have used 'ANY LENGTH' in Fujitsu.  There is no visible pointer at
all where I used it, and certainly no explicit one.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Infinite Loops and Explicit Exits

_(reply depth: 50)_

- **From:** Robert Wagner <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net>
- **Date:** 2004-11-29T04:32:10+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<crtkq0leuncmja77mss6bjpjnveshrsp04@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<n3gaq0pbj1ckqk10dvhp9pviokn48blsm8@4ax.com> <217e491a.0411242304.57b33c07@posting.google.com> <cgtbq0tkdurvcggtelrojt5un03kk6cqs6@4ax.com> <217e491a.0411251235.5cccefa4@posting.google.com> <j0pcq0969r7ug98t9k22o2rnhmo27u9l11@4ax.com> <217e491a.0411252358.71fe4771@posting.google.com> <e8leq0hljooe9c9ubsl2pokkf6u4u5nqi9@4ax.com> <217e491a.0411261522.18d28572@posting.google.com> <j5hhq0ppoo3btb88sp4gfae7spg5ute193@4ax.com> <217e491a.0411272055.4128cd25@posting.google.com>`

```
On 27 Nov 2004 20:55:40 -0800, riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard) wrote:

>I have used 'ANY LENGTH' in Fujitsu.  There is no visible pointer at
>all where I used it, and certainly no explicit one.

There are two meanings of ANY LENGTH. The one in Fujitsu, Micro Focus
and the 2002 standard are 01 levels in the Linkage Section. The call
linkage passes a length in addition to a pointer. The meaning I was
referring to is a proposal for the 2008 standard called 'any-length
elementary items'. They will be implemented as pointers to malloc-ed
strings. The standard will provide an option to store them as
Pascal-style 'crunched records' but that will be too slow. The
pointers aren't visible but some compilers will let you read them with
ADDRESS OF.
```
