###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Infinite Loops and Explicit Exits

_(reply depth: 47)_

- **From:** mwojcik@newsguy.com (Michael Wojcik)
- **Date:** 2004-12-01T16:33:51+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<cokrpf012dj@news2.newsguy.com>`
- **References:** `<1l65q0hq2o42n27onjdv9863avgnljnhq5@4ax.com> <dfi9q05k354q9608hjtl766urppal2a6un@4ax.com> <217e491a.0411241428.2f6d88e4@posting.google.com> <n3gaq0pbj1ckqk10dvhp9pviokn48blsm8@4ax.com> <217e491a.0411242304.57b33c07@posting.google.com> <cgtbq0tkdurvcggtelrojt5un03kk6cqs6@4ax.com> <217e491a.0411251235.5cccefa4@posting.google.com> <j0pcq0969r7ug98t9k22o2rnhmo27u9l11@4ax.com> <217e491a.0411252358.71fe4771@posting.google.com> <e8leq0hljooe9c9ubsl2pokkf6u4u5nqi9@4ax.com>`

```

In article <e8leq0hljooe9c9ubsl2pokkf6u4u5nqi9@4ax.com>, Robert Wagner <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net> writes:
> On 25 Nov 2004 23:58:18 -0800, riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard) wrote:
> >Robert Wagner <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net> wrote 
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> >> >but why would you - unless you were a Cobol programmer.

If the inner structs aren't used outside the enclosing one, there's
no need to define them separately.  It may still be best to define
them as structs, however, so that the fields defined using them can
be assigned with structure copy.  Using your definition:

   struct person {...} personA, personB;
   ...
   personA.RealName = personB.RealName;

In this circumstance anonymous inner structures are a perfectly
reasonable C idiom.


> >> Because the input file is in that format.
> >
> >You missed the point.  You wanted to contruct the whole record in one
> >piece with having to define the inner bits first.  Here I just did
> >that.  It can be done, but C programmers know not to do it that way.

It may be an uncommon C idiom, but there are certainly C programmers
who know when it might best be done that way.

> You're correct when you say they don't. I've never seen C written that
> way.

Your experience, as usual, is not definitive.

> My point is they should write it that way, because it follows the
> structure of the record layout in the spec.

So would defining the inner structs separately.

> If it were an output file,
> they could send the above code to the recipient, who would understand
> it.

The same would be true were the inner structs defined separately.

> How does a C programmer store a string, such as Name, in a file? His
> null-terminated memory format is unsuited to storage in a file.

Unsuited why?

> He must pad it to fixed length a'la Cobol or change the file format to
> CSV.

Ridiculous.  There are as many other options as the combinatorics
of the storage space allow.

> In either case, the field in the record is not a native C type.

So what?  Most fields in the record aren't native C types.  That's
the whole point of an extensible type system.

> What kind of programming language doesn't natively support
> fixed-length fields in a file? A badly designed one.

Perhaps, but that criterion is not true of C.  C directly supports
fixed-length fields in a file through fread (for field-at-a-time)
or fscanf (for record-at-a-time).  Some people may not be capable of
constructing proper fscanf format strings, but the language certainly
supports them.

> >The only difference is that you don't like it.  I don't see the dotted
> >notation as an imposition.  Nor, it seems, do C like or OO
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> call Cobol 'too verbose'.

"Some" who?

> I'm pointing out there's a contradiction between the two.

Hardly.  COBOL can still be "too verbose" by some measure even if
it offers one particular syntactic shortcut (or indeed any number
of them).  Another language can in general be terse even if it
requires verbosity in one particular aspect.

> They're not content with avoiding Cobol, they throw rocks at it with
> terms like verbose and dinosaur. When I point out deficiencies in
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> mainframers are afraid they'll be exposed. 

We can't fault you for a deficiency of paranoia, anyway.  I
especially like the bit about the personal vendetta being carried out
against you by language designers - those poor deluded, incompetent
souls trying to hide the deficiencies of their work from the actinic
light of your wisdom.  Scurry, little language designers, like the
cockroach, back into your filthy dens, or embrace the purity of COBOL
and implicit qualification and be redeemed!

Certainly there are things wrong with C; certainly there are things
wrong with every programming language.  This, however, is not the
result of either foolishness on the part of language designers, or a
pervasive hatred of COBOL.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Infinite Loops and Explicit Exits

_(reply depth: 48)_

- **From:** Robert Wagner <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net>
- **Date:** 2004-12-03T03:06:51+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<gtbvq09nf3c1cn0b9r0hp7h1dnp99nosdq@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<dfi9q05k354q9608hjtl766urppal2a6un@4ax.com> <217e491a.0411241428.2f6d88e4@posting.google.com> <n3gaq0pbj1ckqk10dvhp9pviokn48blsm8@4ax.com> <217e491a.0411242304.57b33c07@posting.google.com> <cgtbq0tkdurvcggtelrojt5un03kk6cqs6@4ax.com> <217e491a.0411251235.5cccefa4@posting.google.com> <j0pcq0969r7ug98t9k22o2rnhmo27u9l11@4ax.com> <217e491a.0411252358.71fe4771@posting.google.com> <e8leq0hljooe9c9ubsl2pokkf6u4u5nqi9@4ax.com> <cokrpf012dj@news2.newsguy.com>`

```
On 1 Dec 2004 16:33:51 GMT, mwojcik@newsguy.com (Michael Wojcik)
wrote:

>> How does a C programmer store a string, such as Name, in a file? His
>> null-terminated memory format is unsuited to storage in a file.
>
>Unsuited why?

Because traditional  file systems perform poorly, or not at all, with
variable length strings in a record. Databases and CSVs are better,
but the format must be converted from null-terminated to something
else.

>> He must pad it to fixed length a'la Cobol or change the file format to
>> CSV.
>
>Ridiculous.  There are as many other options as the combinatorics
>of the storage space allow.

Name three, using flat files.

>> In either case, the field in the record is not a native C type.
>
>So what?  Most fields in the record aren't native C types.  That's
>the whole point of an extensible type system.

That's the whole point of standards. They let us understand each
others' files. 

>> What kind of programming language doesn't natively support
>> fixed-length fields in a file? A badly designed one.
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>supports them.

You're right on both points. The functions and their strings were an
add-on. Cobol record formats are part of the base language.

>> >The only difference is that you don't like it.  I don't see the dotted
>> >notation as an imposition.  Nor, it seems, do C like or OO
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>"Some" who?

Some programmers .. the kind that throw rocks at Cobol.

>> I'm pointing out there's a contradiction between the two.
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>requires verbosity in one particular aspect.

That's possible. The test would be well-written, realistic  programs
side-by-side. Here are two I wrote years ago. At the time, the Cobol
version ran faster than any C compilation thanks to the search verb,
which has no in-line counterpart in C. Line counts are 16 for C vs. 19
for Cobol. Where's the verbosity?

main() {
for(iter = 1; iter <=1000; iter++) {
    count=0;
    for(i = 0; i<= size; i++)
        flags[i] = true;
    for(i = 0; i <= size; i++) {
        if(flags[i]) {
           prime = i + i + 3;
           for(k=i+prime; k<=size; k+=prime)
               flags[k] = false;
           count++;
        }
    }
}
    printf("\n%d primes.",count);
}

main.
 perform 1000 times
     move zero to count, flags-area
     set x to 1
     perform until x > 8191
         search flags when flags (x) equal to zero
             set i to x
             compute prime = i + i + 1
             perform varying k from (i + prime) by prime
                  until k > 8191
                  move '1' to flags (k)
             end-perform
             set x up by 1
             add 1 to count
         end-search
     end-perform
 end-perform
 display count ' primes'
 stop run.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Infinite Loops and Explicit Exits

_(reply depth: 49)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2004-12-02T23:27:22-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0412022327.46f660cb@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<dfi9q05k354q9608hjtl766urppal2a6un@4ax.com> <n3gaq0pbj1ckqk10dvhp9pviokn48blsm8@4ax.com> <217e491a.0411242304.57b33c07@posting.google.com> <cgtbq0tkdurvcggtelrojt5un03kk6cqs6@4ax.com> <217e491a.0411251235.5cccefa4@posting.google.com> <j0pcq0969r7ug98t9k22o2rnhmo27u9l11@4ax.com> <217e491a.0411252358.71fe4771@posting.google.com> <e8leq0hljooe9c9ubsl2pokkf6u4u5nqi9@4ax.com> <cokrpf012dj@news2.newsguy.com> <gtbvq09nf3c1cn0b9r0hp7h1dnp99nosdq@4ax.com>`

```
Robert Wagner <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net> wrote 

> Because traditional  file systems perform poorly, or not at all, with
> variable length strings in a record. 

Your usual complete nonsense. One can have variable length strings and
fixed length records, so the variable length makes _ZERO_ difference. 
For variable records, which may be the result of packing variable
strings, Fujitsu ISAM for example is one of the fastest 'traditional
file systems'.

> Databases and CSVs are better,
> but the format must be converted from null-terminated to something
> else.

_Must_ it ?  Databases and CSV are perfectly happy with variable
strings and C programs need not convert at all, except for where the
null is exchanged for comma.

> That's the whole point of standards. They let us understand each
> others' files. 

Exactly, such as XML, CSV, EDIFACT, etc.

> You're right on both points. The functions and their strings were an
> add-on.

The standard C library is part of the language.

> Cobol record formats are part of the base language.

No they aren't.  Field formats may be part of the language and the
files may be part of the language, but 'record formats' are whatever
the programmers want to assemble.  If the programmer wants to create
multivalue null terminated variable length records then the language
supports that as much as it does space filled fixed fields.

> That's possible. The test would be well-written, realistic  programs
> side-by-side. Here are two I wrote years ago. At the time, the Cobol
> version ran faster than any C compilation thanks to the search verb,
> which has no in-line counterpart in C. 

> Line counts are 16 for C vs. 19
> for Cobol. Where's the verbosity?

In the bits you left out.
```

- **References:** `<n3gaq0pbj1ckqk10dvhp9pviokn48blsm8@4ax.com> <217e491a.0411242304.57b33c07@posting.google.com> <cgtbq0tkdurvcggtelrojt5un03kk6cqs6@4ax.com> <217e491a.0411251235.5cccefa4@posting.google.com> <j0pcq0969r7ug98t9k22o2rnhmo27u9l11@4ax.com> <217e491a.0411252358.71fe4771@posting.google.com> <e8leq0hljooe9c9ubsl2pokkf6u4u5nqi9@4ax.com> <cokrpf012dj@news2.newsguy.com> <gtbvq09nf3c1cn0b9r0hp7h1dnp99nosdq@4ax.com> <217e491a.0412022327.46f660cb@posting.google.com>`

```
On 2 Dec 2004 23:27:22 -0800, riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard) wrote:

>Robert Wagner <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net> wrote 
>
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>file systems'.

Someone should tell Fujitsu's manual writers. Page 83 of the current
NetCOBOL defines variable length records as:

RECORD integer-4 TO integer-5 or RECORD clause is not specified.

Variable-length in the following cases: In all other cases, the record
format is fixed-length.
- Two or more record description entries of different
lengths have been written. [Two or more record formats in the same
file, nothing to do with variable length fields.]
- An OCCURS DEPENDING ON clause has been written in the record
description entry. [Only the last field in a record can be ODO].
- A CHARACTER TYPE clause has been written in the
record description entry. [Applies to National Character Sets.]

As you can see above, Fujitsu does not accomodate variable length
fields within a record (except the last one). If records are "the
result of packing", that packing was done by the application, not by
any Fujitsu software.

Word play that tries to equivocate variable length FIELDS with
variable length RECORDS might work in some circles, but not here. We
all know the difference between a field and a record.

>> Databases and CSVs are better,
>> but the format must be converted from null-terminated to something
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>null is exchanged for comma.

One cannot create a packed record by converting nulls to commas. He
must construct a packed record by stringing the data and skipping the
junk that follows nulls.

>> That's the whole point of standards. They let us understand each
>> others' files. 
>
>Exactly, such as XML, CSV, EDIFACT, etc.

Right. The Fujitsu file system doesn't support any of those. Neither
does the C Standard Library.

>> You're right on both points. The functions and their strings were an
>> add-on.
>
>The standard C library is part of the language.

Some add-ons enhance the language into a well-oiled machine; others
run like an empty footlocker falling down a flight of stairs.


>> Cobol record formats are part of the base language.
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>supports that as much as it does space filled fixed fields.

At least records with fixed-length fields can be described in the
Cobol base language. C cannot read ANY file format containing strings
without clunky add-ons.

>> That's possible. The test would be well-written, realistic  programs
>> side-by-side. Here are two I wrote years ago. At the time, the Cobol
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>In the bits you left out.

The only bits I left out were data definitions, which required 3-4
lines in both languages. What a lame come-back.
```

- **References:** `<n3gaq0pbj1ckqk10dvhp9pviokn48blsm8@4ax.com> <cgtbq0tkdurvcggtelrojt5un03kk6cqs6@4ax.com> <217e491a.0411251235.5cccefa4@posting.google.com> <j0pcq0969r7ug98t9k22o2rnhmo27u9l11@4ax.com> <217e491a.0411252358.71fe4771@posting.google.com> <e8leq0hljooe9c9ubsl2pokkf6u4u5nqi9@4ax.com> <cokrpf012dj@news2.newsguy.com> <gtbvq09nf3c1cn0b9r0hp7h1dnp99nosdq@4ax.com> <217e491a.0412022327.46f660cb@posting.google.com> <dct1r0l6d6p5t8kpj1qkmbbte5amtui4a6@4ax.com>`

```
Robert Wagner <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net> wrote 

> As you can see above, Fujitsu does not accomodate variable length
> fields within a record (except the last one). If records are "the
> result of packing", that packing was done by the application, not by
> any Fujitsu software.

You are obviously completely clueless as to how Fujitsu ISAM files are
written on the disk. The 'Fujitsu software' packs the records using
run length encoding so _all_ records are variable length being
compressed and expanded by _Fujitsu_software_.

In any case the application that uses variable length fields may well
do their own packing, and what would be wrong with that ?

You claim is that variable is 'unsuited' because of 'performance
issue' yet one of the fastest has very variable length records created
by varying the fields using RLE.

In other words you are spouting your usual nonsense from a position of
ignorance.

> Word play that tries to equivocate variable length FIELDS with
> variable length RECORDS might work in some circles, but not here. We
> all know the difference between a field and a record.

Your nonsense claim was:

"""Because traditional  file systems perform poorly, or not at all,
with
variable length strings in a record."""

Fujitsu ISAM makes fields into 'variable length strings' (using RLE)
and it performs well.  As it happens that makes the records variable
length also.

As usual you may crap claims based on a limited viewpoint and then
attempt quasi-intellectual bullying to try to change what you meant.

> One cannot create a packed record by converting nulls to commas. He
> must construct a packed record by stringing the data and skipping the
> junk that follows nulls.

That is exactly what strcat() does, or didn't you know that.

> >Exactly, such as XML, CSV, EDIFACT, etc.
> 
> Right. The Fujitsu file system doesn't support any of those. Neither
> does the C Standard Library.

It supports them as a _file_system_ exactly as any other language
does. A _file_system_ doesn't care what the record layout or content
is.


> >The standard C library is part of the language.
> 
> Some add-ons enhance the language into a well-oiled machine; others
> run like an empty footlocker falling down a flight of stairs.

No one is forcing you to like it, you can avoid it if you wish.

> At least records with fixed-length fields can be described in the
> Cobol base language. C cannot read ANY file format containing strings
> without clunky add-ons.

Did you start foaming at the mouth when you wrote that ?
```

Robert Wagner <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net> wrote 

> >> As you can see above, Fujitsu does not accomodate variable length
> >> fields within a record (except the last one). If records are "the
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> the records.

You seem to be changing your criteria entirely now.  First it was:
"variable length has performe issues", then: "packing is not done by
Fujitsu software", now: "no other can expand it".

At least you no longer bother to prefix "I meant .." when shown to be
completely wrong about something, just change the subject.

In fact Fujitsu supplies a C library so that other languages can use
the files.


> >In any case the application that uses variable length fields may well
> >do their own packing, and what would be wrong with that ?
> 
> What's wrong is the file format is not supported by the language.

You seem to have this weird idea that the record layout is somehow
part of the 'file format' and that this should be a function of the
language.

Records are the units that the 'file format' are stored in.  The
content of the record is entirely defined by the application.  Any
record layout that can be written by code in a language is obviously
'supported by the language'.

If I do a STRING into a variable record such that an UNSTRING can
recover it (or even specific character by character code) then what
part is 'not supported by the language' ?

> I didn't write the slow software, I'm just repeating what others said.

Oh, so you take no responsibility for the misinformation and factoids
that you spread.  You have used that excuse before. You just make up
stuff to suit your preconceived conclusions and then it's 'what others
have said'.

> >In other words you are spouting your usual nonsense from a position of
> >ignorance.
> 
> Your points would be more credible if you just addressed the issue and
> omitted the ad homina.

The _issue_ is that you are spread ill-informed nonsense and
misinformation. You have now confirmed that 'position of ignorance' by
stating that it was only 'what others said' and was thus mere hearsay
and not facts or evidence.

It ain't 'ad homina' if it is true and fair comment.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Infinite Loops and Explicit Exits

_(reply depth: 55)_

- **From:** "Richard" <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2004-12-07T11:25:22-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1102447522.859669.229430@c13g2000cwb.googlegroups.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<t6s8r0lb92bsh3vi8jiescqmjsq2hkd8oa@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<j0pcq0969r7ug98t9k22o2rnhmo27u9l11@4ax.com> <217e491a.0411252358.71fe4771@posting.google.com> <e8leq0hljooe9c9ubsl2pokkf6u4u5nqi9@4ax.com> <cokrpf012dj@news2.newsguy.com> <gtbvq09nf3c1cn0b9r0hp7h1dnp99nosdq@4ax.com> <217e491a.0412022327.46f660cb@posting.google.com> <dct1r0l6d6p5t8kpj1qkmbbte5amtui4a6@4ax.com> <217e491a.0412041053.11947d2@posting.google.com> <cch4r01e7vpudski45u46mkhiltp4o1hn0@4ax.com> <217e491a.0412051058.66801033@posting.google.com> <t6s8r0lb92bsh3vi8jiescqmjsq2hkd8oa@4ax.com>`

```
> The discussion was about variable length fields in a record. You
> responded by talking about data compression without making it clear
> that the subject had changed.

The subject didn't change, it is just that your perception of variable
length was rather limited. One reason for using variable length fields
is to reduce the size of the records by removing the spurious trailing
spaces and by dropping runs of zeroes.  Fujitsu does that by using RLE
and other.  Applications can also do that themselves.

>> You seem to have this weird idea that the record layout is somehow
>> part of the 'file format' and that this should be a function of the
>> language.

> J4 has the same weird idea when it discusses native support for XML.

XML is not a 'file format' it is a data, or record, foramt.  The
underlying file format is 'line sequential' or even just a string of
bytes.

>> If I do a STRING into a variable record such that an UNSTRING can
>> recover it (or even specific character by character code) then what
>> part is 'not supported by the language' ?

> That's a good example, but a flawed one. UNSTRING cannot recover the
> original if it contained commas (delimiters). Nor can it handle
quoted
> strings.

You should note that I did not suggest that I would use commas. The
flaw is entirely that you made assumptions about how I would do that.
In any case I recall that you said you would modify user's data to
suit, so perhaps you would just drop the commas from the strings.

In fact I had stated that I would use a mechanism _such_that_ UNSTRING
_can_ recover it, retorting that you can think of mechanisms that do
not fill the criteria show a rather severe limitation.

> The records I'm talking about are Pascal's 'crunched' format

They are not 'Pascal's', but a specific implementation's.

> "#1-36. "Crunched" records should be avoided unless the need for
tight
> packing of the fields overrides the performance loss they cause."

"""Pascal/iX supports "crunched record", an extension beyond a simple
"packed record". In a crunched record, there are *no* unused bits. If
you declare a crunched record with a 1 bit field, followed by an 8 bit
field, followed by a 1 bit field, then the entire record will occupy 10
bits, and the 8-bit field will require extra code to load/store."""

You should first note that the report is dated 1992 when 386s were
common machines. It is obvious (when the quote actually includes
relevant information) that 'packed records' (ie simple compression
resulting in variable length) does _not_ produce a performance loss.

It is quite clear that the 'perfomance loss' is entirely related to the
CPU overhead of the 'crunching' and has _nothing_ to do with 'file
system performance'.

>> The _issue_ is that you are spread ill-informed nonsense and
>> misinformation. You have now confirmed that 'position of ignorance'
by
>> stating that it was only 'what others said' and was thus mere
hearsay
>> and not facts or evidence.

> In that case, you should provide evidence to the contrary.
> Name-calling doesn't help readers' understanding.

You are providing sufficient evidence yourself.  This last bit about
'crunching' is ill-informed and misinformation (it is not related to
_file_ performance), it is also 'what others said', while my
observations about the effects of variable fields via RLE on _file_
performance is based on actual tests with MicroFocus and Fujitsu.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Infinite Loops and Explicit Exits

_(reply depth: 49)_

- **From:** mwojcik@newsguy.com (Michael Wojcik)
- **Date:** 2004-12-09T17:06:59+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<cpa0nj02aun@news3.newsguy.com>`
- **References:** `<dfi9q05k354q9608hjtl766urppal2a6un@4ax.com> <n3gaq0pbj1ckqk10dvhp9pviokn48blsm8@4ax.com> <217e491a.0411242304.57b33c07@posting.google.com> <cgtbq0tkdurvcggtelrojt5un03kk6cqs6@4ax.com> <217e491a.0411251235.5cccefa4@posting.google.com> <j0pcq0969r7ug98t9k22o2rnhmo27u9l11@4ax.com> <217e491a.0411252358.71fe4771@posting.google.com> <e8leq0hljooe9c9ubsl2pokkf6u4u5nqi9@4ax.com> <cokrpf012dj@news2.newsguy.com> <gtbvq09nf3c1cn0b9r0hp7h1dnp99nosdq@4ax.com>`

```

In article <gtbvq09nf3c1cn0b9r0hp7h1dnp99nosdq@4ax.com>, Robert Wagner <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net> writes:
> On 1 Dec 2004 16:33:51 GMT, mwojcik@newsguy.com (Michael Wojcik)
> wrote:
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> variable length strings in a record.

This is flatly untrue for a number of "traditional file systems".  It's
certainly untrue for a number of contemporary file systems, particularly
with memory-mapped files and unified VMM.

> Databases and CSVs are better,
> but the format must be converted from null-terminated to something
> else.

There are vast numbers of C programs that store strings in files
without any explicit reformatting, using standard C library
functions such as fprintf, and they get along just fine.

> >> He must pad it to fixed length a'la Cobol or change the file format to
> >> CSV.
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> Name three, using flat files.

1. Nul-terminated strings written directly to the file.  That's
perfectly workable and performs very well on all the POSIX systems
I can think of.
2. Line-delimited fields in plain-text files - very, very common in
Unix.
3. Colon-delimited fields in plain-text files - also common in Unix.
4. XML.
5. Length-prefixed fields.

And so on.

> >> In either case, the field in the record is not a native C type.
> >
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> others' files. 

The C standard does not cover data organization in files.  There are
other standards that do.  This is not a weakness in C.

> >> What kind of programming language doesn't natively support
> >> fixed-length fields in a file? A badly designed one.
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> add-on.

And you're wrong.  fread and fscanf have both been part of C since
the first standard, and well before that.

Not that your argument is relevant anyway.

> >> The imposition is not dot notation, it is the requirement for
> >> superfluous qualification. Some defend that, then in the next breath
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> Some programmers .. the kind that throw rocks at Cobol.

Your army of straw men has yet to crest the hill.

> >Hardly.  COBOL can still be "too verbose" by some measure even if
> >it offers one particular syntactic shortcut (or indeed any number
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> side-by-side.

Since there can be any number of such pairs, it's hardly a meaningful
test.  There are plenty of these language bake-off contests on the
web, and they demonstrate very little.

In any event, this is a non sequitur.  I argued that a language may
have the attribute "verbose" even if there are particular uses of
that language that have the attribute "terse", and vice versa; in
effect, that anecdotal examples of either attribute in a given
language prove nothing about the language in general.  Yet you seek
to persuade with just such anecdotal examples.

> Here are two I wrote years ago. At the time, the Cobol
> version ran faster than any C compilation thanks to the search verb,

You tested *every single C implementation* available at the time?
Remarkable.  How many years ago was this.

> which has no in-line counterpart in C. Line counts are 16 for C vs. 19
> for Cobol. Where's the verbosity?
> 
> main() {

Since this is already not a well-written C program, I believe you've
failed your own criteria.  On a hosted implementation, there are only
two valid forms of main, and you've used neither.

> for(iter = 1; iter <=1000; iter++) {

Implicit int is deprecated.

>     count=0;
>     for(i = 0; i<= size; i++)
>         flags[i] = true;

There's no declaration in scope for size, flags, or true.  That's a
constraint violation; no conforming implementation should accept this
program.  If it did, the result would have undefined behavior.

This is also the wrong idiom for initializing an array in C.

I don't think we need read any further.  Even if this exercise were
meaningful - which it is not - your chosen examples are poor to the
point of absurdity.
```

As are all others familiar with IBM mainframes who post in C.L.C. with the 
VERY notable exception of your (RW's) perception of such shops.

I won't go as far as to say that no one EVER agrees with your perception of 
what "all <IBM> mainframers / mainframe shops / mainframe COBOL programmers" 
do - but I will go as far as to say that it is quite RARE when others agree 
with your reports.

On the other hand, IBM COBOL mainframe usage is still so wide-spread that I 
am perfectly willing to admit that almost everything that you report/claim 
must exist somewhere in some shop or shops, it is simply your 
generalizations that fail (consistently) a "reasonability" test - when 
compared to the experience of all other (currently involved with IBM 
mainframe environments) posters.

Come on, Doc. You, of all people, know that would never pass muster at
a mainframe code review. They'd make you rewrite it:


PERFORM 60000-CODE-LIKE-HELL 
      THRU 60000-CODE-LIKE-HELL-EXIT
      UNTIL PGM-STATUS = lt-00.
PERFORM 20000-NEXT-ASSIGNMENT
       THRU 20000-NEXT-ASSIGNMENT-EXIT.

>... 'good mainframe code' or not... and if either, for what reason?

There are more things in heaven and earth, Horatio,
Than are dreamt of in your philosophy.
Hamlet, 1. 5

In article <cojapg$bj1$1@panix5.panix.com>, docdwarf@panix.com wrote:

> Sequiturs are bourgeois, Mr Wagner... second request, then: is
> 
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> DD


I would chime in that if you fall into the ELSE part, that is NOT 
PROGRAM-RUNS, then CODE-LIKE-HELL is counter productive.

One should 

   PERFORM DISCOVER-ANNOYING-PROBLEMS
   PERFORM DESIGN-CLEAR-SOLUTIONS
   PERFORM ALTER-CODE-TO-FIT-SOLUTION

rather than just:

   PERFORM CODE-LIKE-HELL UNTIL DAMNED-THING-WORKS

Often, just throwing code at the problem will fix it, but in a less than 
elegant manner.
```

In article <joe_zitzelberger-79F636.09425001122004@knology.usenetserver.com>,
Joe Zitzelberger  <joe_zitzelberger@nospam.com> wrote:
>In article <cojapg$bj1$1@panix5.panix.com>, docdwarf@panix.com wrote:
>
…[23 more quoted lines elided]…
>   PERFORM CODE-LIKE-HELL UNTIL DAMNED-THING-WORKS

Nothing prevents such structuring, Mr Zitzelberger, just as nothing 
prevents

IF INVENTORY-ON-HAND
    PERFORM SEND-OUT-ORDER.

... from being structured as

SEND-OUT-ORDER.
    PERFORM BALANCE-INVENTORY.
    PERFORM DETERMINE-CUSTOMER-DISCOUNTS.
    PERFORM FIND-CHEAPEST-SHIPPER.
    PERFORM GENERATE-LABELS.

... or a similar set of steps.

>
>Often, just throwing code at the problem will fix it, but in a less than 
>elegant manner.

By at least one standard of measure - the hoary, old Lines Of Code - 
elegance is counterproductive... funny world, isn't it?

DD
```

.. Upper case.
.. Unclear what happens after damned thing works.
.. Should be GO TO NEXT-ASSIGNMENT. You will not return to here.

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<comqki$k1$1@panix5.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<dfi9q05k354q9608hjtl766urppal2a6un@4ax.com> <bmupq0ljb3u7tv7o4umm79d770j9a7hsrn@4ax.com> <cojapg$bj1$1@panix5.panix.com> <80isq0dgn8nih9e5v13jeebhm4j7lm73f4@4ax.com>`

```
In article <80isq0dgn8nih9e5v13jeebhm4j7lm73f4@4ax.com>,
Robert Wagner  <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net> wrote:
>On 30 Nov 2004 21:37:36 -0500, docdwarf@panix.com wrote:
>
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>.. Not terminated.

Given its context, Mr Wagner - or lack thereof - termination is moot... 
but it is easily remedied:

IF PROGRAM-RUNS
    PERFORM NEXT-ASSIGNMENT
ELSE
    PERFORM CODE-LIKE-HELL UNTIL DAMNED-THING-WORKS.

>.. Upper case.

A matter of presentation alone, Mr Wagner; that you show the case of the 
letters has an effect on your evaluation on the quality of the code speaks 
volumes.

'That was not good English... the volume was too low!'

>.. Unclear what happens after damned thing works.

Variant of 1), a matter of context.

>.. Should be GO TO NEXT-ASSIGNMENT. You will not return to here.

I was taught that 'good mainframe code' should be reuseable, Mr Wagner; 
one never knows what future specs will cause the code to return here.

So... being unaware of the context and using a different case for the text 
makes code 'good' or 'bad'.  Smells like mere aesthetics, Mr Wagner, and 
nothing more.

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Infinite Loops and Explicit Exits

- **From:** l.willms@jpberlin.de (Lueko Willms)
- **Date:** 2004-12-02T12:13:00+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9M6RM4VeflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de>`
- **References:** `<dfi9q05k354q9608hjtl766urppal2a6un@4ax.com> <80isq0dgn8nih9e5v13jeebhm4j7lm73f4@4ax.com> <comqki$k1$1@panix5.panix.com>`

```
.    On  02.12.04
  wrote  docdwarf@panix.com
     on  /COMP/LANG/COBOL
     in  comqki$k1$1@panix5.panix.com
  about  Re: Infinite Loops and Explicit Exits


d> IF PROGRAM-RUNS
d>     PERFORM NEXT-ASSIGNMENT
d> ELSE
d>     PERFORM CODE-LIKE-HELL UNTIL DAMNED-THING-WORKS.

>> .. Unclear what happens after damned thing works.

d> Variant of 1), a matter of context.

>> .. Should be GO TO NEXT-ASSIGNMENT. You will not return to here.

d> I was taught that 'good mainframe code' should be reuseable, Mr
d> Wagner; one never knows what future specs will cause the code to
d> return here.

    It is anyway describing the cycle of the contracted programmer [no  
pun intended], and not the site looking for his or her services. The  
full loop would look like this:


PERFORM UNTIL Retirement OR Disability OR Death
   ACCEPT Job-Offer FROM anywhere
   PERFORM WITH TEST AFTER
        UNTIL Dammend-Thing-works
     PERFORM Analze-Problem
     PERFORM Code-like-Hell
     PERFORM Test-the-monster
   END-PERFORM
   PERFORM Wait-for-next-job
END-PERFORM



Yours,
Lï¿½ko Willms                                     http://www.willms-edv.de
/--------- L.WILLMS@jpberlin.de -- Alle Rechte vorbehalten --

Man stattete ihm sehr heiï¿½en, schon etwas verbrannten Dank ab. -G.C.Lichtenberg
```

On  1-Dec-2004, Robert Wagner <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net> wrote:

> >IF PROGRAM-RUNS
> >    PERFORM NEXT-ASSIGNMENT
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…

At least not terminated within the piece of code that was quoted.   But that
obviously wasn't the whole program.  (A program ends with a full stop period)

> .. Upper case.

Pray tell me why Upper case is "Bad".

> .. Unclear what happens after damned thing works.

Not without seeing the whole program.   It was a program fragment.

> .. Should be GO TO NEXT-ASSIGNMENT. You will not return to here.

How do you know?   This is a program fragment.   You don't know how we got here
nor whether this is part of a loop.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Infinite Loops and Explicit Exits

_(reply depth: 53)_

- **From:** "Richard" <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2004-12-02T10:08:30-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1102010910.190424.256090@f14g2000cwb.googlegroups.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<80isq0dgn8nih9e5v13jeebhm4j7lm73f4@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<dfi9q05k354q9608hjtl766urppal2a6un@4ax.com> <bicoq0t5cphm7ib6gv8glpcjpmlj7qtgvl@4ax.com> <cohhcm$dnk$1@panix5.panix.com> <bmupq0ljb3u7tv7o4umm79d770j9a7hsrn@4ax.com> <cojapg$bj1$1@panix5.panix.com> <80isq0dgn8nih9e5v13jeebhm4j7lm73f4@4ax.com>`

```
> Bad, for four reasons:
>
> .. Not terminated.

It is not terminated yet.

> .. Upper case.

What is easy to read is in the eye of the beholder.  You may prefer to
look at ersatz C, others may not.

> .. Unclear what happens after damned thing works.

That depends on the context of where this code is placed.  As with all
code it should stand alone to be reusable whereever it is needed.

> .. Should be GO TO NEXT-ASSIGNMENT. You will not return to here.

That may be the fate of the itinerant contractor, such as yourself, and
especially where they are so opinionated and derisive, but 'returning
to here for another assignment' is why they are called 'permanent
staff'.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Infinite Loops and Explicit Exits

_(reply depth: 53)_

- **From:** Joe Zitzelberger <joe_zitzelberger@nospam.com>
- **Date:** 2004-12-05T20:53:56-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<joe_zitzelberger-FF06AD.20535605122004@knology.usenetserver.com>`
- **References:** `<dfi9q05k354q9608hjtl766urppal2a6un@4ax.com> <bicoq0t5cphm7ib6gv8glpcjpmlj7qtgvl@4ax.com> <cohhcm$dnk$1@panix5.panix.com> <bmupq0ljb3u7tv7o4umm79d770j9a7hsrn@4ax.com> <cojapg$bj1$1@panix5.panix.com> <80isq0dgn8nih9e5v13jeebhm4j7lm73f4@4ax.com>`

```
In article <80isq0dgn8nih9e5v13jeebhm4j7lm73f4@4ax.com>,
 Robert Wagner <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net> wrote:

> On 30 Nov 2004 21:37:36 -0500, docdwarf@panix.com wrote:
> 
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
> .. Should be GO TO NEXT-ASSIGNMENT. You will not return to here.


Far cleaner to code:

   PERFORM CODE-LIKE-HELL UNTIL DAMN-THING-WORKS
   PERFORM NEXT-ASSIGNMENT

The moment it works it will fall into the next assignment.  The removed 
IF statement increases simplicity...
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Infinite Loops and Explicit Exits

_(reply depth: 55)_

- **From:** Joe Zitzelberger <joe_zitzelberger@nospam.com>
- **Date:** 2004-12-06T09:43:44-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<joe_zitzelberger-0026BA.09434406122004@knology.usenetserver.com>`
- **References:** `<dfi9q05k354q9608hjtl766urppal2a6un@4ax.com> <cojapg$bj1$1@panix5.panix.com> <80isq0dgn8nih9e5v13jeebhm4j7lm73f4@4ax.com> <joe_zitzelberger-FF06AD.20535605122004@knology.usenetserver.com> <cp0f7s$4c0$1@panix5.panix.com>`

```
In article <cp0f7s$4c0$1@panix5.panix.com>, docdwarf@panix.com wrote:

> In article <joe_zitzelberger-FF06AD.20535605122004@knology.usenetserver.com>,
> Joe Zitzelberger  <joe_zitzelberger@nospam.com> wrote:
…[33 more quoted lines elided]…
> DD

Many programs can run, but I expect that the client will want the damn 
thing to work the way they have requested as a condition of satisfactory 
completion of employement.

If DAMN-THING-WORKS then it works...if "working" involves running a 
program than the PROGRAM-RUNS condition must be a subset of working.  
All working programs run, some non-working run, therefore, PROGRAM-RUNS 
doesn't tell us much.

We are in fact closing a subtle bug -- if the program was coded in such 
a way that the very first execution would actually satisfy PROGRAM-RUNS, 
then the iterative debugging cycle of CODE-LIKE-HELL UNTIL 
DAMN-THING-WORKS would never be entered.

Of course, that assumes that the condition names meaningfully represent 
what the conditions themselves represent.  But nobody would ever misname 
a condition...
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Infinite Loops and Explicit Exits

_(reply depth: 52)_

- **From:** Robert Wagner <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net>
- **Date:** 2004-12-02T05:36:55+00:00

The question was "required by a walk-thru" - not that some code uses it (and 
maintains it).  In another response, Arnold indicates that his (VERY MAJOR) shop 
*does* require numbered paragraphs.  I'll take his response as evidence that 
some of the shops (not the ones I am more familiar with) do have this as a shop 
standard.

I certainly don't see this as "bad programming" - but it also isn't something 
that I (personally) would encourage as a REQUIRED shop standard.
```

On  2-Dec-2004, "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> wrote:

> The question was "required by a walk-thru" - not that some code uses it (and
> maintains it).  In another response, Arnold indicates that his (VERY MAJOR)
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> that I (personally) would encourage as a REQUIRED shop standard.

The disadvantage to this is when a shop purchases code, especially copy code
that doesn't follow the standard.   It is kind of nice if your editor can easily
find non-comment paragraph names with a numeric digit in column 8.   But if we
can't count on it, we are subject to wrong results.

That's the only thing I see "wrong" with such a standard.

Of course if mainframers do it, or once did it a couple of decades ago, then by
definition it must be bad.
```

HB> It is kind of nice if your editor
HB> can easily find non-comment paragraph names with a numeric digit in
HB> column 8.

   That can also be a data description.

   I would simply search for the keyword SECTION, or the paragraph  
name ANFANG (i.e. BEGIN) in my programs.


HB> But if we can't count on it, we are subject to wrong results.

   You loose all that anyway with free format.

   Also, I don't see much sense in searching for ANY section or  
paragraph name, only for those which I am interesting in. Starting  
with the main section, and from there I find my way, and search for  
the sections PERFORMed from there.

   So I type in the name of the section I search for, or pick it up  
via the clipboard, and off it goes.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Infinite Loops and Explicit Exits

_(reply depth: 59)_

- **From:** Robert Wagner <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net>
- **Date:** 2004-12-03T23:08:20+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<fkr1r0pr9mv7h056bqsc6pjsfpfvtv4jv4@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<dfi9q05k354q9608hjtl766urppal2a6un@4ax.com> <DmErd.3969427$yk.605187@news.easynews.com> <conc41$k6t$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <9MA$9ahuflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de> <coqmtd$ig3$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
On Fri, 3 Dec 2004 21:47:05 GMT, "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>
wrote:

>> HB> It is kind of nice if your editor
>> HB> can easily find non-comment paragraph names with a numeric digit in
>> HB> column 8.

I'm told enlightened mainframers stopped numbering paragraphs 20 years
ago. 

>I worked in a DEC shop once that didn't have columns 1-6, but never at a place
>that used free format.   I imagine there's some advantage in free-format, but it
>isn't obvious what that advantage is.

What's the advantage of fixed-format?

Some advantages of free-format:

.. Better use of scarce screen real estate. I prefer seeing a whole
function on one screen.

.. Long literals. Suppose you're composing a letter coded with VALUEs.
With free-form, the source code depicts the letter.

.. Ignored characters in columns 73-80 can cause hard-to-see logic
problems, especially when the character is a period. Or when
truncating one or two characters forms a valid but wrong data name.

.. Not having to split a line into two just because you're over the
(arbitrary) limit by a few characters. I do not advocate code wider
than a screen, with rare exceptions, but 14 extra characters per line
can make the difference between clean and choppy code.

.. The feeling that you're using a modern programming language, rather
than following punched-card standards.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Infinite Loops and Explicit Exits

_(reply depth: 60)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2004-12-03T20:14:35-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0412032014.58a62ce6@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<dfi9q05k354q9608hjtl766urppal2a6un@4ax.com> <DmErd.3969427$yk.605187@news.easynews.com> <conc41$k6t$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <9MA$9ahuflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de> <coqmtd$ig3$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <fkr1r0pr9mv7h056bqsc6pjsfpfvtv4jv4@4ax.com>`

```
Robert Wagner <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net> wrote

> .. Long literals. Suppose you're composing a letter coded with VALUEs.
> With free-form, the source code depicts the letter.

That would be a very poor mechanism to compose a letter.  It is very
difficult to manipulate a series of VALUES, adding words and
rewrapping is difficult and error-prone. It also fixes the text into
the program meaning that any change has to be done by the programmer,
and it is only in one language, which may be typical for Americans,
but Europeans would demand more flexability.

Long literals should _not_ be coded into the program, but should be
external in a 'resource file' or a template that should be accessed by
name that caters for changing language.
```

I'm saying programming style shouldn't be based on text editor
limitations. If your editor can't handle the task, get a better one,
write your own or write a macro. The macro language of mine is Cobol
or any other language that compiles to an executable.

…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>design?

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Infinite Loops and Explicit Exits

_(reply depth: 64)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2004-12-04T14:28:41-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0412041428.73c58537@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<dfi9q05k354q9608hjtl766urppal2a6un@4ax.com> <fkr1r0pr9mv7h056bqsc6pjsfpfvtv4jv4@4ax.com> <217e491a.0412032014.58a62ce6@posting.google.com> <eqr2r0hcd1o93bdoorri8dqrn0u7gedsst@4ax.com> <coscfo$5d1$1@panix5.panix.com> <vao3r0pg6pg5k2ejip70pjsmg6ehgvhc23@4ax.com>`

```
Robert Wagner <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net> wrote 

> >I'm not sure what you are saying here, Mr Wagner... is it a variant of 
> >'All everyone else has to do is what I do and everything will be simple 
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> or any other language that compiles to an executable. 

Given that I consider putting long text values in VALUE statements,
especially when parts may be needed to move from one line to another,
is very poor style, then I wouldn't see an editor that did this easily
to necessarily be an advantage.

I prefer to use editors which do things that _I_ see as advantageous.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Infinite Loops and Explicit Exits

_(reply depth: 65)_

- **From:** Robert Wagner <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net>
- **Date:** 2004-12-04T23:52:26+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<j1j4r0lqn1bv0g7u7009pgaqhjbpsnitak@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<dfi9q05k354q9608hjtl766urppal2a6un@4ax.com> <fkr1r0pr9mv7h056bqsc6pjsfpfvtv4jv4@4ax.com> <217e491a.0412032014.58a62ce6@posting.google.com> <eqr2r0hcd1o93bdoorri8dqrn0u7gedsst@4ax.com> <coscfo$5d1$1@panix5.panix.com> <vao3r0pg6pg5k2ejip70pjsmg6ehgvhc23@4ax.com> <217e491a.0412041428.73c58537@posting.google.com>`

```
On 4 Dec 2004 14:28:41 -0800, riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard) wrote:

>Robert Wagner <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net> wrote 
>
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
>I prefer to use editors which do things that _I_ see as advantageous.

It's simple. Trailing quotes line up at the right margin. I mark a
block around the text enclosed in quotes and hit Ctrl-F (Format).
My editor formats everything within the marked block.

If I line-mark the whole document with Ctrl-A, then hit Ctrl-F, it
reformats everything.

If nothing is marked when I hit Ctrl-F, it reformats the paragraph the
cursor is in.
```

> >> HB> It is kind of nice if your editor
> >> HB> can easily find non-comment paragraph names with a numeric digit in
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> ago.

Who told you this?

What difference does it make whether paragraphs are numbered or not?   How does
that effect whether the code is good or not?

Someone can leave numbers out to demonstrate that they are trendy, or keep
putting them in to show that they are not - but what has that got to do with
whether the code is good or bad?

_(reply depth: 62)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2004-12-06T22:53:41+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Vp5td.6309273$6p.998869@news.easynews.com>`
- **References:** `<dfi9q05k354q9608hjtl766urppal2a6un@4ax.com> <DmErd.3969427$yk.605187@news.easynews.com> <conc41$k6t$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <9MA$9ahuflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de> <coqmtd$ig3$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <fkr1r0pr9mv7h056bqsc6pjsfpfvtv4jv4@4ax.com> <cp1t55$acv$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <mjl9r0l6vsckep9q7akqvq3hnu3gv33q1v@4ax.com>`

```
Robert,
   What (at least I) said was that I didn't know of any shops that would still 
REQUIRE numbered paragraphs or PERFORM THRU during a walk-thru.

I have since heard (in this forum) of one shop that still requires numbered 
paragraphs and one that required both UNTIL RECENTLY.

At no time did I say this hasn't happened within 20 years.

Furthermore, I think that there are still MANY, many, applications with such 
code - and that when MAINTENANCE is done on such code, that "good" programmers 
maintain the style for the existing code - and not "re-write" the entire 
application SIMPLY for "style reasons".
```

```

On  3-Dec-2004, Robert Wagner <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net> wrote:

> >I worked in a DEC shop once that didn't have columns 1-6, but never at a
> >place
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> What's the advantage of fixed-format?

Main thing is that I can configure my editor so that I always see what matters.

> Some advantages of free-format:
>
> .. Better use of scarce screen real estate. I prefer seeing a whole
> function on one screen.

Unless it is too wide to fit.

> .. Long literals. Suppose you're composing a letter coded with VALUEs.
> With free-form, the source code depicts the letter.

I don't know what you mean here.

> .. Ignored characters in columns 73-80 can cause hard-to-see logic
> problems, especially when the character is a period. Or when
> truncating one or two characters forms a valid but wrong data name.

I configure my editor to only see what matters.

> .. Not having to split a line into two just because you're over the
> (arbitrary) limit by a few characters. I do not advocate code wider
> than a screen, with rare exceptions, but 14 extra characters per line
> can make the difference between clean and choppy code.

Why 14?   Why not 100?    One limit is as good as another.

> .. The feeling that you're using a modern programming language, rather
> than following punched-card standards.

I suspect this is your *real* concern.    I can't say that I share it.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Free format (was: Infinite Loops and Explicit Exits

_(reply depth: 60)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2004-12-04T18:49:20+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<QEnsd.4091961$yk.619971@news.easynews.com>`
- **References:** `<dfi9q05k354q9608hjtl766urppal2a6un@4ax.com> <9MA$9ahuflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de> <coqmtd$ig3$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <9MEishc9flB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de>`

```
"Lueko Willms" <l.willms@jpberlin.de> wrote in message 
news:9MEishc9flB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de...
>.    On  03.12.04
>  wrote  howard@brazee.net (Howard Brazee)
>     on  /COMP/LANG/COBOL
>     in  coqmtd$ig3$1@peabody.colorado.edu
>  about  Re: Infinite Loops and Explicit Exits
<snip>
> HB> I worked in a DEC shop once that didn't have columns 1-6, but never
> HB> at a place that used free format.   I imagine there's some advantage
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>

What you describe is how some (many?) implementations did their extension to the 
'85 Standard.

"Free form reference format" in the '02 Standard is slightly different.

Column 1 has no special meaning - rather new "indicators" are introduced (along 
with some compiler directives)

 * in colun 7 (fixed format) is replaced by
   *>   (can occur after or before text on a line - but all data following is a 
comment

/ in column 7 is replaced by
   >>Page directive

D in column 7 is replaced by
   >>D   (and even this is designated OBSOLETE)

- in column 7 is replaced (for literals) with (example)
     "this is line 1"-
     " and this is line two"

NOTE:
    "This is part 1" & " and this is part 2" is NOT quite the same feature

*ALL* of those indicators are also valid in '02 Standard "fixed form reference 
format".

I particularly LIKE the new style continuation of alphanumeric (and national and 
boolean) literals - as you don't have to worry about "counting" where the 
R-margin is (or where trailing spaces are).
```

> D in column 7 is replaced by
>    >>D   (and even this is designated OBSOLETE)

I like the "D" and wish it wasn't designated OBSOLETE.

> - in column 7 is replaced (for literals) with (example)

>      " and this is line two"

I don't like the continuation symbol and haven't used it in years.   It's safer
to use FILLERs.

I don't worry about them - I don't use the continuation.

_(reply depth: 61)_

- **From:** l.willms@jpberlin.de (Lueko Willms)

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

- **References:** `<dfi9q05k354q9608hjtl766urppal2a6un@4ax.com> <9MEishc9flB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de> <cp1tn7$an8$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
.    On  06.12.04
  wrote  howard@brazee.net (Howard Brazee)
     on  /COMP/LANG/COBOL
     in  cp1tn7$an8$1@peabody.colorado.edu
  about  Re: Infinite Loops and Explicit Exits


>>    The advantage is to have those first 7 characters of each line for
>> the program text, which expands the visible length of the line.

HB> My eyes aren't what they used to be, and my appreciation on having
HB> long lines visible isn't what a young person might have.

   I don't know what I should think of your age if you refer to me 58- 
year-old as a young person.

   However, my eyes are'nt very good either, so I like to use a larger  
font which makes the lines longer.

   Actually, I have not yet used free format, and it is only a  
relatively short time ago that I took up COBOL again. I got this  
Fujitsu 3.0 "student edition", and I am playing around with it a  
little bit, and use its editor in extended format, i.e. with line  
numbers in columns 1 to 6, and indicator area, area A, and B.

   These column numbers made a lot of sense in those old days when the  
source program resided on cards (I have begun like that in 1978/79),  
and could easily be brought back into order, should a card deck fall  
on the floor.

   Today it is rather a nuisance. This Fujitsu editor wants to  
renumber the col 1 to 6 everytime I save the program.

   Since when I want to compare two versions of a program with "diff"  
or a similar tool, the differences in the line numbering make  
otherwise identical lines different.

   I should dare to switch to free format!


Yours,
Lï¿½ko Willms                                     http://www.willms-edv.de
/--------- L.WILLMS@jpberlin.de -- Alle Rechte vorbehalten --

Belehrung findet man ï¿½fter in der Welt als Trost.  -G.C.Lichtenberg
```

 "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote:

> > HB> But if we can't count on it, we are subject to wrong results.

…[5 more quoted lines elided]…

When generating Cobol code, the hardest thing to resolve was getting the 
column 72 to column 7 continuation right.  Things were constantly 
getting out of wack, we spent more time on the reformatting than we did 
on the actual generating.

If I could have written it out to a single string, well, that would have 
been a big plus...
```

If it was that big of a problem, why use it?    With the use of FILLER, you
don't need super long strings.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Infinite Loops and Explicit Exits

_(reply depth: 56)_

- **From:** l.willms@jpberlin.de (Lueko Willms)
- **Date:** 2004-12-02T20:13:00+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9M6VlVb9flB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de>`
- **References:** `<dfi9q05k354q9608hjtl766urppal2a6un@4ax.com> <9M6PEK9uflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de> <DmErd.3969427$yk.605187@news.easynews.com>`

```
.    On  02.12.04
  wrote  wmklein@nospam.netcom.com (William M. Klein)
     on  /COMP/LANG/COBOL
     in  DmErd.3969427$yk.605187@news.easynews.com
  about  Re: Infinite Loops and Explicit Exits

  on numbered SECTIONs and Paragraphs:

WMK> I certainly don't see this as "bad programming" - but it also isn't
WMK> something that I (personally) would encourage as a REQUIRED shop
WMK> standard.

  I don't see it neither as what I call "bad programming", but rather  
an odd style, and one which can really lead to important programming  
errors.

  It turns the programmer's attention away from the actual purpose of  
a procedure, which would be expressed in a _name_ and nurtures  
attitudes which don't see the wood for the trees.

  I already mentioned this example of a colleague of mine back in the  
early 1980ies (with COBOL-74) who did prefix the section names  
generated by my program generator with a number, and so he had a twin  
main-processing section with the same name, but a slightly different  
number.

  Any way, not only section names, but paragraph names should be less  
and less important in a COBOL program, since there is no need for  
paragraph names as labels for a GOTO, and less need for SECTIONs to be  
PERFORMed because of the "inline PERFORM" which organizes the loop  
locally instead of remotely.

   BTW, in a German language Web forum on COBOL programming, I have  
seen a program example where each and every SECTION was terminated by  
an EXIT SECTION, sticking out to the left. A cute way, I think, to  
indicate: there is no fall through!


Yours,
Lï¿½ko Willms                                     http://www.willms-edv.de
/--------- L.WILLMS@jpberlin.de -- Alle Rechte vorbehalten --

Ein Buch ist ein Spiegel, wenn ein Affe hineinsieht, so kann kein Apostel herausgucken. -G.C.Lichtenberg
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Infinite Loops and Explicit Exits

_(reply depth: 54)_

- **From:** Arnold Trembley <arnold.trembley@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 2004-12-02T08:25:32+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<0kArd.1015155$Gx4.974278@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<UIyrd.113543$SM5.5589@news.easynews.com>`
- **References:** `<dfi9q05k354q9608hjtl766urppal2a6un@4ax.com> <lhjnq0prhoaks59quhori3r3sk8eej17l6@4ax.com> <ErTqd.3838773$yk.586908@news.easynews.com> <bicoq0t5cphm7ib6gv8glpcjpmlj7qtgvl@4ax.com> <cohhcm$dnk$1@panix5.panix.com> <bmupq0ljb3u7tv7o4umm79d770j9a7hsrn@4ax.com> <217e491a.0411302311.2c5af71a@posting.google.com> <ip8tq0h2kih2jnt1q0pahjmltri31mbapv@4ax.com> <UIyrd.113543$SM5.5589@news.easynews.com>`

```


William M. Klein wrote:

> How about ONE out of those with which any of us currently working with IBM 
> mainframe sites (that continue to develop and enhance their mainframe COBOL 
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

We do code walkthrough's and numbered paragraphs (and datanames) are 
required by our shop standard naming conventions.

All the code I see is still upper-case, but that appears to be simply 
an old habit.  There is absolutely no requirement in our style 
standards for upper case code, and the compiler doesn't care one way 
or the other.  I am seeing mixed case text in reports and DISPLAY'ed 
messages.

Our shop standards forbid PERFORM THRU and GO TO, but we still see it, 
generally in very old programs, almost never in newer programs.  I was 
researching a program within the last month that had a 1974 date in 
the comments.  Perhaps that's what Mr. Wagner had in mind.

What I don't see in my mainframe shop is DECLARATIVES, or nested 
programs, SECTION, or any use of floating point data.  Even the most 
hidebound old coders here seem to have gotten comfortable with 
EVALUATE and scope terminators.  And since I've only worked in two 
mainframe shops in 25 years, I have no idea what is typical for other 
IBM mainframe shops.

With kindest regards,
```

- **References:** `<dfi9q05k354q9608hjtl766urppal2a6un@4ax.com> <lhjnq0prhoaks59quhori3r3sk8eej17l6@4ax.com> <ErTqd.3838773$yk.586908@news.easynews.com> <bicoq0t5cphm7ib6gv8glpcjpmlj7qtgvl@4ax.com> <cohhcm$dnk$1@panix5.panix.com> <bmupq0ljb3u7tv7o4umm79d770j9a7hsrn@4ax.com> <217e491a.0411302311.2c5af71a@posting.google.com> <ip8tq0h2kih2jnt1q0pahjmltri31mbapv@4ax.com> <UIyrd.113543$SM5.5589@news.easynews.com> <0kArd.1015155$Gx4.974278@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`

```
On Thu, 02 Dec 2004 08:25:32 GMT, Arnold Trembley
<arnold.trembley@worldnet.att.net> wrote:

>Our shop standards forbid PERFORM THRU and GO TO, but we still see it, 
>generally in very old programs, almost never in newer programs.  I was 
>researching a program within the last month that had a 1974 date in 
>the comments.  Perhaps that's what Mr. Wagner had in mind.

I was referring to new programs, written from scratch.

>What I don't see in my mainframe shop is DECLARATIVES, or nested 
>programs, SECTION, or any use of floating point data. 

Nor do I, but I don't fault them for those things. Sections are just
disguised perform .. thru. Floating point is seldom needed for
business work, unless doing 'mathematical' stuff such as regression. I
don't like Declaratives. Nested programs are a good thing, but offer
only one non-technical advantage over external called programs -- no
need to add another program name to the inventory. The scoping rules
re End Program confuse people and discourage use.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Infinite Loops and Explicit Exits

_(reply depth: 56)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2004-12-03T09:22:17-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<coq7c9$fm5$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<dfi9q05k354q9608hjtl766urppal2a6un@4ax.com> <lhjnq0prhoaks59quhori3r3sk8eej17l6@4ax.com> <ErTqd.3838773$yk.586908@news.easynews.com> <bicoq0t5cphm7ib6gv8glpcjpmlj7qtgvl@4ax.com> <cohhcm$dnk$1@panix5.panix.com> <bmupq0ljb3u7tv7o4umm79d770j9a7hsrn@4ax.com> <217e491a.0411302311.2c5af71a@posting.google.com> <ip8tq0h2kih2jnt1q0pahjmltri31mbapv@4ax.com> <UIyrd.113543$SM5.5589@news.easynews.com> <0kArd.1015155$Gx4.974278@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <76lvq01o6oigfkarge9mde4v3ik5nt2mnp@4ax.com>`

```

"Robert Wagner" <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net> wrote in message
news:76lvq01o6oigfkarge9mde4v3ik5nt2mnp@4ax.com...
> Sections are just
> disguised perform .. thru.

Not necessarily.

> Floating point is seldom needed for
> business work, unless doing 'mathematical' stuff such as regression.

However, it is clear that COBOL as a language is moving toward a
floating-point representation as its basic standard intermediate data item
format, whether or not a corresponding USAGE exists in the language.  This
is evidenced by the requirements of Standard Arithmetic in 2002 COBOL, and
by proposals under development to provide direct support for 128-bit binary
and decimal floating-point formats -- both in USAGE and for intermediate
results -- as described in the revision of IEEE 754 currently under
development.  A decimal floating-point representation of integer or
fixed-point values need not result in approximations unless the requirements
of the particular calculation exceed the 32-digit capacity mandated by the
standard.  And on machines in which integers form a subset of the
floating-point format, the arithmetic operators themselves may not
distinguish between integer and floating-point arguments or results; in such
environments, floating-point arithmetic is used for *all* business work.

> Nested programs are a good thing, but offer
> only one non-technical advantage over external called programs -- no
> need to add another program name to the inventory.

I'm not sure I understand the distinction about "non-technical advantage".
To the person who is used to (or has even had significant exposure to)
block-structured languages like ALGOL and Pascal, there are significant
*stylistic* (and thus non-technical) advantages to nested programs that are
satisfied neither by paragraphs nor by external called programs.

> The scoping rules re End Program confuse people and discourage use.

Ummm ... which "people" is that, Robert?

The END PROGRAM marker contains a program-name, and that program-name
matches the program-name in a preceding PROGRAM-ID.  If no such match
occurs, it's a syntax error, and if it does, the scope of that program is
from the IDENTIFICATION DIVISION that contains the matching PROGRAM-ID to
the END PROGRAM marker.  That should be sufficiently clear.  And if that
program has programs nested within it, those programs each have PROGRAM-ID
paragraphs *and* matching End Program markers (see ISO/IEC 1989:2002 page
174).  What's so tough about that?

    -Chuck Stevens
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Infinite Loops and Explicit Exits

_(reply depth: 57)_

- **From:** Robert Wagner <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net>
- **Date:** 2004-12-04T07:55:09+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<kd82r0lsho4r3uf6pka207dbpq99o6138q@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<ErTqd.3838773$yk.586908@news.easynews.com> <bicoq0t5cphm7ib6gv8glpcjpmlj7qtgvl@4ax.com> <cohhcm$dnk$1@panix5.panix.com> <bmupq0ljb3u7tv7o4umm79d770j9a7hsrn@4ax.com> <217e491a.0411302311.2c5af71a@posting.google.com> <ip8tq0h2kih2jnt1q0pahjmltri31mbapv@4ax.com> <UIyrd.113543$SM5.5589@news.easynews.com> <0kArd.1015155$Gx4.974278@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <76lvq01o6oigfkarge9mde4v3ik5nt2mnp@4ax.com> <coq7c9$fm5$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`

```
On Fri, 3 Dec 2004 09:22:17 -0800, "Chuck Stevens"
<charles.stevens@unisys.com> wrote:

>"Robert Wagner" <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net> wrote in message
>news:76lvq01o6oigfkarge9mde4v3ik5nt2mnp@4ax.com...
>> Sections are just disguised perform .. thru.
>
>Not necessarily.

When are they not?

>> Floating point is seldom needed for
>> business work, unless doing 'mathematical' stuff such as regression.
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
>environments, floating-point arithmetic is used for *all* business work.

I have no quarrel with this development. It will be a good thing when
supported by hardware.

>> Nested programs are a good thing, but offer
>> only one non-technical advantage over external called programs -- no
>> need to add another program name to the inventory.
>
>I'm not sure I understand the distinction about "non-technical advantage".

The non-technical aspect is maintenance of a list of program names ..
keeping an inventory. There is overhead associated with each
'program'. Nested programs do not incur that overhead.

>To the person who is used to (or has even had significant exposure to)
>block-structured languages like ALGOL and Pascal, there are significant
>*stylistic* (and thus non-technical) advantages to nested programs that are
>satisfied neither by paragraphs nor by external called programs.

I concur so much that I'd like to see perform REPLACED by an internal
call. I know that's a radical idea in Cobol-land. I toyed with using
ENTER to play the role but nobody here liked it. ENTER is as
economical as a paragraph. Nested programs, by contrast, are a bit
bulky.

>> The scoping rules re End Program confuse people and discourage use.
>
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>174).  What's so tough about that?

I posted a Demo (in Best Practices) that nested programs two deep. I
don't find it tough, as you imply, but others do. The concept would be
more acceptable if programs were simply packaged in a single source
file without the requirement for nesting.

Now: 
program-id. A.
    call 'B'
program-id. B.
end program B.
end program A.

Simplified:
program-id. A.
    call 'B'
end program A.
program-id. B.
end program B.

What's the point of nesting? One would think working-storage would be
within the SCOPE of nested programs, but it isn't.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Infinite Loops and Explicit Exits

_(reply depth: 58)_

- **From:** LX-i <lxi0007@netscape.net>
- **Date:** 2004-12-04T08:47:12-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<%5ksd.657$Yq6.224@fe40.usenetserver.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<kd82r0lsho4r3uf6pka207dbpq99o6138q@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<ErTqd.3838773$yk.586908@news.easynews.com> <bicoq0t5cphm7ib6gv8glpcjpmlj7qtgvl@4ax.com> <cohhcm$dnk$1@panix5.panix.com> <bmupq0ljb3u7tv7o4umm79d770j9a7hsrn@4ax.com> <217e491a.0411302311.2c5af71a@posting.google.com> <ip8tq0h2kih2jnt1q0pahjmltri31mbapv@4ax.com> <UIyrd.113543$SM5.5589@news.easynews.com> <0kArd.1015155$Gx4.974278@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <76lvq01o6oigfkarge9mde4v3ik5nt2mnp@4ax.com> <coq7c9$fm5$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <kd82r0lsho4r3uf6pka207dbpq99o6138q@4ax.com>`

```
Robert Wagner wrote:
> On Fri, 3 Dec 2004 09:22:17 -0800, "Chuck Stevens"
> <charles.stevens@unisys.com> wrote:
…[41 more quoted lines elided]…
> within the SCOPE of nested programs, but it isn't.

The point of nesting is that it allows you to delimit scope while still 
containing the subprogram within the larger scope of the program unit. 
The "Simplified" structure makes them be two independent structures. 
And, program B could also no longer access variables marked "Global" in 
program A.

IMO, it's only confusing the first time you see it.  When you have 
mammoth copy procedures with internal variables, and you decide to 
change them (because nobody should be using them anyway), and then you 
break 13 programs because they were using variables that they really 
shouldn't have been able to see in the first place....

I really like nested subprograms.  In fact, I wonder if putting one in a 
copy proc would pass muster in my shop.  :)  It would certainly keep 
some of that from happening...
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Infinite Loops and Explicit Exits

_(reply depth: 59)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2004-12-04T10:56:29-08:00

True - and which, if shop standards do not explicitly prohibit it, just 
the fact of something being "new" isn't a good argument against using 
it.  (Again, IMO...)  The first time someone saw "Evaluate True" in my 
code, they came and asked me about it.  Now, I see it regularly in 
peer-reviews from other folks - at this point, I think everyone in the 
shop has coded that at least once.

If a construct is valuable, and fits the need of the program, why not?  :)

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Infinite Loops and Explicit Exits

_(reply depth: 59)_

- **From:** Robert Wagner <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net>
- **Date:** 2004-12-05T21:37:46+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ktv6r0d6k72inqp9t4pkabc38rhu2kpqsq@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<cohhcm$dnk$1@panix5.panix.com> <bmupq0ljb3u7tv7o4umm79d770j9a7hsrn@4ax.com> <217e491a.0411302311.2c5af71a@posting.google.com> <ip8tq0h2kih2jnt1q0pahjmltri31mbapv@4ax.com> <UIyrd.113543$SM5.5589@news.easynews.com> <0kArd.1015155$Gx4.974278@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <76lvq01o6oigfkarge9mde4v3ik5nt2mnp@4ax.com> <coq7c9$fm5$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <kd82r0lsho4r3uf6pka207dbpq99o6138q@4ax.com> <%5ksd.657$Yq6.224@fe40.usenetserver.com>`

```
On Sat, 04 Dec 2004 08:47:12 -0600, LX-i <lxi0007@netscape.net> wrote:

>The point of nesting is that it allows you to delimit scope while still 
>containing the subprogram within the larger scope of the program unit. 
>The "Simplified" structure makes them be two independent structures. 
>And, program B could also no longer access variables marked "Global" in 
>program A.

Hidden sharing mechanisms like GLOBAL and EXTERNAL are a reinvention
of Fortran's Blank Common. Shared variables should all be passed
explicitly as parameters. 

>IMO, it's only confusing the first time you see it.  When you have 
>mammoth copy procedures with internal variables, and you decide to 
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>some of that from happening...

If the procedure is used by more than one caller, it should be an
external called program. Procedure division copybooks shouldn't be
used, IMO.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Infinite Loops and Explicit Exits

_(reply depth: 58)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2004-12-04T10:21:00-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0412041021.2b2a36be@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<ErTqd.3838773$yk.586908@news.easynews.com> <cohhcm$dnk$1@panix5.panix.com> <bmupq0ljb3u7tv7o4umm79d770j9a7hsrn@4ax.com> <217e491a.0411302311.2c5af71a@posting.google.com> <ip8tq0h2kih2jnt1q0pahjmltri31mbapv@4ax.com> <UIyrd.113543$SM5.5589@news.easynews.com> <0kArd.1015155$Gx4.974278@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <76lvq01o6oigfkarge9mde4v3ik5nt2mnp@4ax.com> <coq7c9$fm5$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <kd82r0lsho4r3uf6pka207dbpq99o6138q@4ax.com>`

```
Robert Wagner <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net> wrote 

> The non-technical aspect is maintenance of a list of program names ..
> keeping an inventory. There is overhead associated with each
> 'program'. Nested programs do not incur that overhead.

In what way is that 'non-technical' ?  Is it 'literate' ?  Why are the
other advantages, and disadvantages 'technical', rather than, say,
stylistic ?

The main disadvantage of nested programs is that they are not reusable
outside of the program they are nested in.  This is a technical
disadvantage.
 
> I concur so much that I'd like to see perform REPLACED by an internal
> call. I know that's a radical idea in Cobol-land. I toyed with using
> ENTER to play the role but nobody here liked it. ENTER is as
> economical as a paragraph. Nested programs, by contrast, are a bit
> bulky.

Internals CALLs are not radical at all, nested programs do that. What
is 'radical' is that you want to prevent PERFORM being used.  To
Replace would be to tear out PERFORM and _force_ people to use CALL.

If you don't like Cobol then I suggest that you attempt to learn
another language that already does not have PERFORM, perhaps the Perl
programmers would welcome your ideas.

> I posted a Demo (in Best Practices) that nested programs two deep. I
> don't find it tough, as you imply, but others do. 

_Which_ others ?

> The concept would be
> more acceptable if programs were simply packaged in a single source
> file without the requirement for nesting.

You _can_ do that. For most compilers a compile unit can include
several non-nested programs.  It will usually compile them to separate
object files.
 
> Now: 
> program-id. A.
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> end program B.

Perfectly acceptable and valid, but not nested.  B is not contained
within A.  They are separate programs and this can be done now.

> What's the point of nesting? One would think working-storage would be
> within the SCOPE of nested programs, but it isn't.

Just above you claim that you 'don't find it tough' then you show that
you completely fail to understand nesting.
```

It's administrative.

That's true of paragraphs, as well. 

>> I concur so much that I'd like to see perform REPLACED by an internal
>> call. I know that's a radical idea in Cobol-land. I toyed with using
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>Replace would be to tear out PERFORM and _force_ people to use CALL.

Of major languages, only BASIC had the equivalent of PERFORM. It's
gone now.

"By Visual Basic version 4 (1995), which brought the glimmerings of
true object-oriented programming concepts into the Basic language,
Microsoft was attempting to get rid of the old-style GOSUB/RETURN
commands. While VB4 still supported the feature, it was documented as
being inefficient (read badly implemented). Microsoft recommended
writing separate procedures and calling them instead. Yet, they
continued to support GOSUB/RETURN all the way through the next two
versions of the language for six years before finally doing away with
it altogether in VB.NET, the latest version."

http://www.devx.com/dotnet/Article/16323/1763/page/2

>> The concept would be
>> more acceptable if programs were simply packaged in a single source
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>object files.

I didn't know that.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Infinite Loops and Explicit Exits

_(reply depth: 60)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2004-12-05T19:09:50-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0412051909.c80bbf5@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<cohhcm$dnk$1@panix5.panix.com> <217e491a.0411302311.2c5af71a@posting.google.com> <ip8tq0h2kih2jnt1q0pahjmltri31mbapv@4ax.com> <UIyrd.113543$SM5.5589@news.easynews.com> <0kArd.1015155$Gx4.974278@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <76lvq01o6oigfkarge9mde4v3ik5nt2mnp@4ax.com> <coq7c9$fm5$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <kd82r0lsho4r3uf6pka207dbpq99o6138q@4ax.com> <217e491a.0412041021.2b2a36be@posting.google.com> <cgv6r01vlrfhad10sbs7t2lspfhisdf7o4@4ax.com>`

```
Robert Wagner <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net> wrote

> >The main disadvantage of nested programs is that they are not reusable
> >outside of the program they are nested in.  This is a technical
> >disadvantage.
> 
> That's true of paragraphs, as well. 

But external programs _are_ reusable from many others.

> >Internals CALLs are not radical at all, nested programs do that. What
> >is 'radical' is that you want to prevent PERFORM being used.  To
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> gone now.
 
I am not convinced that doing 'what VB did in the 90s' is a
particularly good model to follow.  Much VB had to be rewritten
several times as new versions were released.  The advantage of Cobol
is that code written decades ago can still be used without having to
reimplement it.

You seem to want to destroy that advantage.  The only result will be
that companies will simply not upgrade to a version that does not
support their existing code.
```

>I am not convinced that doing 'what VB did in the 90s' is a
>particularly good model to follow.  Much VB had to be rewritten
>several times as new versions were released.  The advantage of Cobol
>is that code written decades ago can still be used without having to
>reimplement it.

The disadvantage of Cobol is that code can be written in a decades-old
style. In practice, that style is being mandated by shop standards.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Infinite Loops and Explicit Exits

_(reply depth: 63)_

- **From:** Robert Wagner <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net>
- **Date:** 2004-12-07T00:36:53+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<odu9r0hoqaigbfjq6ci9ndor9mg2fgi4ni@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<UIyrd.113543$SM5.5589@news.easynews.com> <0kArd.1015155$Gx4.974278@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <76lvq01o6oigfkarge9mde4v3ik5nt2mnp@4ax.com> <coq7c9$fm5$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <kd82r0lsho4r3uf6pka207dbpq99o6138q@4ax.com> <217e491a.0412041021.2b2a36be@posting.google.com> <cgv6r01vlrfhad10sbs7t2lspfhisdf7o4@4ax.com> <217e491a.0412051909.c80bbf5@posting.google.com> <fru8r05veslqbomedj27a5ebuo3p6nf1ns@4ax.com> <cp2kkk$qf4$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
On Mon, 6 Dec 2004 21:57:15 GMT, "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>
wrote:

>On  6-Dec-2004, Robert Wagner <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net> wrote:

>> In practice, that style is being mandated by shop standards.
>
>So which mandated standards hurt the bottom line?

Ones that make enhancements slow and expensive. For instance, file I/O
in a program containing business logic. When the record layout
changes, you have to re-test many programs. I believe files should be
encapsulated in their own callable program; database tables should be
accessed through logical views.
```

> >So which mandated standards hurt the bottom line?
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> accessed through logical views.

Most mainframe CoBOL shops would first have to accept the lowered standards for
testing that OO shops have.    Right now, if I change a record layout in a
called program, I have to re-test all the programs that call that program.

The reason that people don't do this testing for widely used objects and called
programs is because it costs too much.

I have lived with a system that used a program to handle I/O with a KSDS VSAM
file - that had a bug in it (It passed a "7" back to the called program whenever
the file status started with a zero - including "97".    We lived with this bug
because testing all of the programs that called it was too expensive.    If we
had used the "obsolete" standard that each program did its own I/O, they would
have been fixed long ago.
```
