###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Mainframers ... according to RW (was: Infinite Loops and Explicit Exits

_(reply depth: 30)_

- **From:** Robert Wagner <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net>
- **Date:** 2004-11-16T01:30:39+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<hulip0logb9765jkq22pc5b0g8jrjj8frd@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<cm8pp2$e3$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <q3scp0la8almpn7j4kvlu2d7dnqov8qn7l@4ax.com> <cn61pq$hcs$1@panix5.panix.com> <cnajck$hjq$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <cnal2p$6v0$1@panix5.panix.com>`

```
On 15 Nov 2004 11:21:45 -0500, docdwarf@panix.com wrote:

>In article <cnajck$hjq$1@peabody.colorado.edu>,
>Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net> wrote:
…[19 more quoted lines elided]…
>this fraction.

It depends on the deviation in the population. The smaller the
deviation, the more likely a sample represents the population (plus or
minus the confidence interval, CI). Suppose, hypothetically, we were
sampling a batch of data files (somehow) known to be identical. In
that case a sample size of one would tell us everything about the
batch.

The intelligent questions are:
.. How much deviation was seen between samples?
.. Were the samples biased? In other words, were they all taken from a
specialized subset. 
.. Was the finding positive (it was in this case) or negative? A
negative finding requires many more samples. 
.. Might there be a third factor causing both variables? In this case,
self-selection and employer-selection would be a likely suspects.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Mainframers ... according to RW (was: Infinite Loops and Explicit Exits

_(reply depth: 31)_

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Mainframers ... according to RW (was: Infinite Loops and Explicit Exits

_(reply depth: 31)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2004-11-16T10:03:59-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0411161003.3a2f1e5f@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<cm8pp2$e3$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <q3scp0la8almpn7j4kvlu2d7dnqov8qn7l@4ax.com> <cn61pq$hcs$1@panix5.panix.com> <cnajck$hjq$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <cnal2p$6v0$1@panix5.panix.com> <hulip0logb9765jkq22pc5b0g8jrjj8frd@4ax.com>`

```
Robert Wagner <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net> wrote 

> >Other folks do other things, of course... but please pardon my imprecison 
> >in what you've quoted; of course Mr Wagner is familiar with 'what a 
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> batch.

This is exactly why your opinion of yourself differs from what others
may think of you.  It is quite true that that if there is a small
deviation then a small sample represents the population.  The problem
is that you _start_ with your preconceived _conclusion_ that 'all
mainframe programmers are the same' and then use that to justify the
vast generalisations that you make from inadequate sampling.

> The intelligent questions are:
> .. How much deviation was seen between samples?

Given that the samples you report and samples reported by others, and
by my direct observation, then they show a large deviation.

> .. Were the samples biased? In other words, were they all taken from a
> specialized subset. 

The samples may also be biased by, for example, you being selective
about which programmers you include as 'mainframe'.  If they don't fit
your preconceived notion about how you want to characterise them then
you may put them in the 'PC' categorey but working on mainframes 'by
mistake'.

> .. Was the finding positive (it was in this case) or negative? A
> negative finding requires many more samples. 

There yer go then.

> .. Might there be a third factor causing both variables? In this case,
> self-selection and employer-selection would be a likely suspects.

Exactly. Your employers may be selecting the sites that you work in
and thus the samples you are exposed to for much the same reason that
they have the employees that you see. You may be working in decrepet,
old-fashioned, poorly programmed sites, _because_ of your particular
qualities and experience.

Or possibly, not working in modern, progressive sites for those same
reasons.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Mainframers ... according to RW (was: Infinite Loops and Explicit Exits

_(reply depth: 32)_

- **From:** Robert Wagner <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net>
- **Date:** 2004-11-19T06:05:26+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<tboqp09nq9rne2ae6ps0f75cinc5kn7mh1@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<cm8pp2$e3$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <q3scp0la8almpn7j4kvlu2d7dnqov8qn7l@4ax.com> <cn61pq$hcs$1@panix5.panix.com> <cnajck$hjq$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <cnal2p$6v0$1@panix5.panix.com> <hulip0logb9765jkq22pc5b0g8jrjj8frd@4ax.com> <217e491a.0411161003.3a2f1e5f@posting.google.com>`

```
On 16 Nov 2004 10:03:59 -0800, riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard) wrote:

>Robert Wagner <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net> wrote 

>> The intelligent questions are:
>> .. How much deviation was seen between samples?
>
>Given that the samples you report and samples reported by others, and
>by my direct observation, then they show a large deviation.

Not in my experience. I've never seen an IBM mainframe shop (except
the ones I managed) where code quality was above grade C.

When other contractors talk about their experience, they tell horror
stories even worse than I've seen. For instance, places where
copybooks were not allowed, others where OCCURS was forbidden. A few
remember one or two enlightened places, like IBM and Bell Labs, but
those were exceptions in their experience, too.

>> .. Were the samples biased? In other words, were they all taken from a
>> specialized subset. 
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>mistake'.

Not in Cobol. I've seen gifted programmers in other languages working
in mainframe shops. Shop standards for those languages are very
different from the Cobol standard in the same shop. The others try to
teach good programming by example, whereas Cobol standards are long
lists of DON'Ts. 

>> .. Might there be a third factor causing both variables? In this case,
>> self-selection and employer-selection would be a likely suspects.
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>qualities and experience.

Not my personal qualities.  Companies don't care about personality
when hiring contractors; they are hiring a skillset for short-term
use. Contractors are expected to do as they're told and keep opinions
to themselves. East Indians are especially adept at that, which is
_one_ of the reasons they're so successful. On the other extreme,
Russian programmers don't do well because they're so opinionated. Most
of their opinions involve American stupidity. :)

I'm inclined to think companies who hire contractors, instead of using
employees, are sub-par technically. By hiring outsiders, they're not
supporting their staff numerically nor in learning new skills. In
response, staff digs in and becomes averse to error by conservatively
resisting change.

On the above-par side are companies that move staff to the new system
and hire contractors to support the old system until it's retired.
Also, managers who hire contractors solely to be sacrificial goats
when the annual 10% cut comes from above. I worked in both situations.
The former was well above-par, but had no Cobol after the tent was
folded. The latter had typical mainframe code.

So, yeah, the Cobol that contractors see might be worse than average
in quality and creativity.

>Or possibly, not working in modern, progressive sites for those same
>reasons.

I worked in modern, progressive sites for 20 years. They weren't so
when I arrived;  they were when I left. I'd love to breathe life into
another medium-sized site.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Mainframers ... according to RW (was: Infinite Loops and Explicit Exits

_(reply depth: 33)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2004-11-19T06:16:27+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<_cgnd.4743855$ic1.451886@news.easynews.com>`
- **References:** `<cm8pp2$e3$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <q3scp0la8almpn7j4kvlu2d7dnqov8qn7l@4ax.com> <cn61pq$hcs$1@panix5.panix.com> <cnajck$hjq$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <cnal2p$6v0$1@panix5.panix.com> <hulip0logb9765jkq22pc5b0g8jrjj8frd@4ax.com> <217e491a.0411161003.3a2f1e5f@posting.google.com> <tboqp09nq9rne2ae6ps0f75cinc5kn7mh1@4ax.com>`

```
My impression is that shops that are REQUIRED to hire contractors often have 
less experienced and less knowledgeable (and possibly less productive) 
programmers.  This is a "generalization" - but one always ask WHY did a shop 
hire a contractor?  Was it *just* that they were (temporarily?) under-staffed - 
or was there some problem

Shops that use only "in-house employees" may (but I agree don't always) have 
better programmers and programming "styles".

Again, Robert, from your statements, you see (IBM) mainframe COBOL code

 - infrequently (two years in 20+ actually working in mainframe shops)
 - that the shop wants to MOVE off its mainframe environment (for whatever 
reason taking them out of the "ongoing commitment to the IBM mainframe - by 
definition)

***

However,
  the simple fact is that you have seen one type of code and I (and others) have 
seen another.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Mainframers ... according to RW (was: Infinite Loops and Explicit Exits

```
On Fri, 19 Nov 2004 06:16:27 GMT, "William M. Klein"
<wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> wrote:

>My impression is that shops that are REQUIRED to hire contractors often have 
>less experienced and less knowledgeable (and possibly less productive) 
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> - infrequently (two years in 20+ actually working in mainframe shops)

I see IBM mainframe code every day, while actually working in
mainframe shops (soon to be Unix shops). In places where mainframers
are still calling the shots, I'm compelled to write NEW CODE in their
style.

> - that the shop wants to MOVE off its mainframe environment (for whatever 
>reason taking them out of the "ongoing commitment to the IBM mainframe - by 
>definition)

'The Shop' doesn't speak with a single voice. Management wants one
thing; techies want another.

>However,
>  the simple fact is that you have seen one type of code and I (and others) have 
>seen another.

That breaks the hegemony of a single IBM mainframe style. You're
saying some IBM shops get it right and others don't. My complaint is
against the ones who don't. Sounds like we're in agreement!
```

```
"Robert Wagner" <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net> wrote in message
news:af35q0tbg0i6pcal4l6troe95duat0pd8j@4ax.com...

>
…[25 more quoted lines elided]…

company
> >of soldiers do you find they more frequently discuss their hardships or
> >their easy assignments?
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Cool!!!

RULES FOR STEALTH COBOL.(These techniques are Top Secret and Company
Private. When properly applied they will outsmart the enemy, who will never
be able to crack our COBOL applications.)

1. All data and procedure names will be encrypted around your maternal
Grandmother's maiden name.

2. You will not reveal the maiden names of any of your family to anyone, on
pain of  3 weeks "Confined to Cubicle" where you will live on cold do-nuts
and stale machine vended coffee, writing "shouted" batch programs in upper
case with one statement per line and NO scope delimiters. (Full stops will
be used and every one of them on the officially filed listing must have a
square drawn round it in red pen.). If, at the end of 3 weeks you have not
produced 5000 lines of working code, the sentence will repeat, until you do.

3. You will wear a ski mask to work and not reveal your true identity to
anyone, least of all your co workers. Non-compliance will result in
immediate dismissal, after being tortured to reveal your maternal
Grandmother's maiden name and the encryption key you used for your data and
procedure names.

4. You will be provided with a noiseless keyboard so that no-one knows
whether you are working or not. This is part of the "stealth" training.

5. The MOVE verb is swapped with ADD, and EVALUATE is replaced by
"OCCASIONALLY", IF by "SOMETIMES", ELSE by "OTHERWISE " [oops, I think that
is still part of COBOL...<G>. Pete], and READ and WRITE, OPEN and CLOSE are
swapped. The idea is to create maximum confusion just in case the opposition
manage to crack the maternal Grandmother encryption.

6. Cubicles will be maintained in a state of total chaos so enemy spies
cannot tell whether anyone has worked there recently. Anyone found
organising or clearing their desk before going home, will be  punished as
for non-compliance with rule 3 above.

7. There will be no discussion of assignments, easy ones or hard ones. This
is in conformance with the Wagner system of Stealth training (on which these
COBOL standards are modelled, and which we should all be proud to aspire
to.) There can be no discussion anyway, because ALL communication with
co-workers will be by 1024 byte encrypted e-mail. See rule 8).

8. Anyone found speaking will be punished as for non-compliance with rule 3.
Hand written notes are forbidden, unless the recipient agrees to eat them
after reading. To prevent the possibility of ball point impressions being
taken, the whole notepad must be eaten. Co-workers may watch, but this is
the ONLY "entertainment" allowed during working hours.
(The current record is 18 seconds for a complete yellow letter pad, washed
down with water from the bullpen cooler, held posthumously by Zweeble
Fudbucket. Unfortunately, the water was infected with Legionnaire's disease
and Fudbucket subsequently died. He received the Stealth COBOL medal which
was presented to his widow by Robert Wagner in person, although as it was a
"Full Stealth Honour" proceeding, Robert was in dress ski mask, and it might
not have been him...)

9. All movement through the facility will be on tip-toe. Stealth is
everything.

10. Anyone who conveys to any person off-site that this is a Stealth COBOL
site, will be summarily shot, without even the benefit of torture to obtain
maternal Grandmother's maiden name. There will be an immediate cover-up to
frame the facility cat for the shooting, using the stealth "dirty tricks"
you had whispered to you  by the Drill Sergeant on your boot camp course.

Now eat this document.


Pete.
```

```
Robert Wagner <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net> wrote 

> >Given that the samples you report and samples reported by others, and
> >by my direct observation, then they show a large deviation.
> 
> Not in my experience. I've never seen an IBM mainframe shop (except
> the ones I managed) where code quality was above grade C.

That may well be true. Perhaps the Grade A shops wouldn't hire you, or
didn't need contractors - _because_ their employees were motivated and
progressive.

You seem to imply that you personally are the _only_ programmer above
'Grade C', at least in IBM shops.  You have stated that your
statistics are faultless and thus _every_ programmer in IBM shops is
two levels below your standard.

Actually, I believe the real reason is that any shop above Grade C
wouldn't let you touch their code with a barge pole.  That fits your
'evidence' exactly.

> Russian programmers don't do well because they're so opinionated. Most
> of their opinions involve American stupidity. :)

Strange that, they seem so much like you in that respect.
 
> I'm inclined to think companies who hire contractors, instead of using
> employees, are sub-par technically. By hiring outsiders, they're not
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> in quality and creativity.

Thank you for finally realising this.
```

- **References:** `<cm8pp2$e3$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <q3scp0la8almpn7j4kvlu2d7dnqov8qn7l@4ax.com> <cn61pq$hcs$1@panix5.panix.com> <cnajck$hjq$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <cnal2p$6v0$1@panix5.panix.com> <hulip0logb9765jkq22pc5b0g8jrjj8frd@4ax.com> <217e491a.0411161003.3a2f1e5f@posting.google.com> <tboqp09nq9rne2ae6ps0f75cinc5kn7mh1@4ax.com> <217e491a.0411191154.3cd4235c@posting.google.com>`

```
On 19 Nov 2004 11:54:36 -0800, riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard) wrote:

>Robert Wagner <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net> wrote 
>
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
>'evidence' exactly.

You have no evidence for any of these claims. You're making up 'facts'
to support a pre-formed conclusion. 

>> Russian programmers don't do well because they're so opinionated. Most
>> of their opinions involve American stupidity. :)
>
>Strange that, they seem so much like you in that respect.

I don't pre-judge skill based on nationality or other social factors.
Some of the best code I've seen was written by people who seemed
unlikely candidates based on meeting and talking to them. Conversely,
I've met people whose convincing talk was given lie by their crappy
code. 

Truth is in code. All the rest is just rhetoric. I've posted lots of
code here. I wish others would do the same.
```

```
Generally, putting aside tapes, one estimates the deviation in a
population by reading earlier studies. If that's not available, by
measuring the deviation in a small, random sample. For instance, 20
mainframe shops.

>In the case of 'mainframe programmers', it seems that you 'know' they
>are all identical because it suits your political agenda, you 'made it
>up'.

Then I must have imagined millions of lines of crap code. Like the
program I worked on today, with the same code cut-and-pasted 16 times.
Thank you for bursting my solipsistic bubble.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Mainframers ... according to RW

_(reply depth: 27)_

- **From:** l.willms@jpberlin.de (Lueko Willms)
- **Date:** 2004-11-14T06:57:00+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9Kp5xfleflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de>`
- **References:** `<cm8pp2$e3$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <oRgld.4957742$6p.805506@news.easynews.com> <q3scp0la8almpn7j4kvlu2d7dnqov8qn7l@4ax.com>`

```
.    On  13.11.04
  wrote  spamblocker-robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
     on  /COMP/LANG/COBOL
     in  q3scp0la8almpn7j4kvlu2d7dnqov8qn7l@4ax.com
  about  Re: Mainframers ... according to RW (was: Infinite Loops and Explicit


WMK>> Personally, I like using a single paragraph for each READ of a
WMK>> single file  (and performing that paragraph) as it provides a
WMK>> single place for changes (when a file's format chagnes).

   When the file format changes, one changes the FILE definition, in  
the FILE SECTION of the DATA DIVISON -- why should the READ be touched  
by this?

RW> I prefer a CALLable program per file, which allows redesign of the
RW> file system or database without the need to touch calling programs.

   The READ or WRITE statement is actually a call for an external  
routine ...

   And when the data organisation changes, that cannot leave the  
program itself unchanged.

   And when one and the same operation on the basic objects occur  
multiple times, one has to rething the overall organisation of the  
application.

   Of course, when consuming a file in COBOL with sequential access,  
one has to have a READ in the loop, once per each available record,  
and one extra, i.e. twice, but both are very close neighbors.

   And with some database systems one has to apply a special FIRSTREC  
operation anyway, and a NEXTREC for the loop.

   My recommendation: do not blow up your programs with superfluous  
externalisations.


Yours,
Lï¿½ko Willms                                     http://www.willms-edv.de
/--------- L.WILLMS@jpberlin.de -- Alle Rechte vorbehalten --

Er urteilt nach dem jedesmaligen Aggregatzustand seiner Empfindungen. -G.C.Lichtenberg
```

```
wrote:
>.    On  13.11.04
>  wrote  spamblocker-robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)

>RW> I prefer a CALLable program per file, which allows redesign of the
>RW> file system or database without the need to touch calling programs.
>
>   And when the data organisation changes, that cannot leave the  
>program itself unchanged.

It certainly can, so long as the same 'logical view' is presented to
the caller. I've moved data from VSAM to database using this
technique, without changing calling programs at all.
```

```
  about  Re: Mainframers ... according to RW

RW> technique, without changing calling programs at all.

   I see two possibilities:

   a) the change of storage is superflouus since the advantages of a  
database system are not used;

   b) when there is not change in the actual logical structure of the  
data storage, there should be no change in how the data is accessed  
and manipulated.
```

```
facilitated by having the READ in one place, as that would be more than 
a data division change.  :)  (And, one could argue that the change from
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Mainframers ... according to RW

_(reply depth: 30)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2004-11-15T19:36:25+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Zy7md.5121437$6p.828392@news.easynews.com>`
- **References:** `<cm8pp2$e3$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <oRgld.4957742$6p.805506@news.easynews.com> <q3scp0la8almpn7j4kvlu2d7dnqov8qn7l@4ax.com> <9Kp5xfleflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de> <cnaj3k$h8m$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <z57md.2117$aW.780@fe40.usenetserver.com>`

```
In fact, (and this may show that I am "wedded" to old habits), this was one of 
the reasons that I used that technique.  For those familiar with IBM 
(historical) stuff, a VSAM file (IBM's "ISAM implementation" - if you use ISAM 
in a generic sense) could be read as an IMS SHISAM database.  At one time (I 
hope not currently) switching between the two was something that some shops and 
some applications did.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Mainframers ... according to RW

_(reply depth: 31)_

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2004-11-15T19:58:24+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<cnb1p0$r3k$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<cm8pp2$e3$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <oRgld.4957742$6p.805506@news.easynews.com> <q3scp0la8almpn7j4kvlu2d7dnqov8qn7l@4ax.com> <9Kp5xfleflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de> <cnaj3k$h8m$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <z57md.2117$aW.780@fe40.usenetserver.com> <Zy7md.5121437$6p.828392@news.easynews.com>`

```

On 15-Nov-2004, "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> wrote:

> In fact, (and this may show that I am "wedded" to old habits), this was one of
>
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> and some applications did.

I converted a system from Univac 9030 to IBM in 1981, keeping the same
functionality.   All files were converted to VSAM.   But these were ESDS and
RRDS, not KSDS.    ESDS is VSAM's flat file, RRDS is its relative file, and KSDS
is its keyed file.

Apparently few enough shops switched to VSAM for anything other than to replace
IBM's inefficient VSAM file system that people forgot that VSAM was not just
IBM's "ISAM implementation".
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Mainframers ... according to RW

_(reply depth: 32)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2004-11-15T20:51:39+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<vF8md.5124240$6p.828758@news.easynews.com>`
- **References:** `<cm8pp2$e3$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <oRgld.4957742$6p.805506@news.easynews.com> <q3scp0la8almpn7j4kvlu2d7dnqov8qn7l@4ax.com> <9Kp5xfleflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de> <cnaj3k$h8m$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <z57md.2117$aW.780@fe40.usenetserver.com> <Zy7md.5121437$6p.828392@news.easynews.com> <cnb1p0$r3k$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
VSAM is the only way to handle COBOL's "relative file" structure, i.e. RRDS. 
However, for whatever reason, I don't think "relative files" are all that common 
in IBM mainframes.  (I think they USED TO BE for some CICS systems, but even 
that seems less common today - than it used to be.  I could be wrong about this, 
but the last time I asked in the CICS list, this seemed to be true).

The funniest IBM implementation was when the '85 Standard introduced "variable 
length" relative files.  At that time MVS (but not VSE) couldn't handle this 
with RRDS, so they came up with the SIMVRDS run-time option - to create variable 
length KSDS files that were treated as "relative".  Although this is no longer 
required (as VSAM on MVS now supports variable length files), the SIMVRDS option 
still exists.  I have no idea if anyone actually USES it.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Mainframers ... according to RW

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>

```
On 15-Nov-2004, LX-i <lxi0007@netscape.net> wrote:

> > I was wondering the same thing.  I've never changed the READ statement for
> > this
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> the format in which it's stored on the disk!)

I've never changed from a file to a database without a re-write.   And I have
changed a lot of file-access programs to database access programs.   I suppose
when you have a database pretending to be a keyed file this will work.  And
databases are easier to come by on PCs than ISAM systems.   But when I've
switched to databases it has been for other reasons than this.
```

_(reply depth: 29)_

- **From:** Robert Wagner <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net>
- **Date:** 2004-11-16T01:30:40+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<tbiip09g14aghbs0ee6te9bs7mq81m12h0@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<cm8pp2$e3$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <oRgld.4957742$6p.805506@news.easynews.com> <q3scp0la8almpn7j4kvlu2d7dnqov8qn7l@4ax.com> <9Kp5xfleflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de> <cnaj3k$h8m$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
On Mon, 15 Nov 2004 15:48:04 GMT, "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>
wrote:

>
>On 13-Nov-2004, l.willms@jpberlin.de (Lueko Willms) wrote:
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>reason.

Imagine a VSAM record containing product information and 40 price
changes in an array. Picture moving the system to database, where
product information is in one table and each price change is a row in
another table. An IO module could assemble the product information and
40 price changes into a 'logical view' identical to the VSAM record,
for use by old programs.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Infinite Loops and Explicit Exits

_(reply depth: 30)_

- **From:** "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2004-11-14T23:37:24+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<2voqrbF2n8fl0U1@uni-berlin.de>`
- **References:** `<cm8pp2$e3$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <skqcp09qor15e234f17ivl3sh9a8ar0ni3@4ax.com> <cn5qja$ejh$1@panix5.panix.com> <p6mdp0de13ntg44doc8o9kn7377ev76s40@4ax.com> <cn7a9o$rn8$1@panix5.panix.com>`

```

<docdwarf@panix.com> wrote in message news:cn7a9o$rn8$1@panix5.panix.com...
> In article <p6mdp0de13ntg44doc8o9kn7377ev76s40@4ax.com>,
> Robert Wagner  <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net> wrote:
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> >>Well, down from 'mainframers' to 'many mainframers'... tell me, Mr
Wagner,
> >>the TSO/ISPF environment is substantially unchanged from decades ago;
how
> >>is it that the justification valid then has since vanished?
> >
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> >>
I disagree. Quite vehemently, actually. <G>

I have NEVER seen a mainframe shop where the the standards, written or
extant in the '80s, were never reviewed and updated.

I can think immediately of three mainframe sites I know from personal
experience, who made a point of reviewing their COBOL standards after COBOL
'85 was released.

But that isn't the basis of my disagreement with Robert's statement.

COBOL is not called a dinosaur because shop standards  (or even the Language
Standard) were not updated. To suggest such is ludicrous.

(The "standard" has never been THAT important. The real setback the standard
inflicted on COBOL was the loss of credibility, but I have agreed not to go
there, so I won't...)

COBOL is called a dinosaur because it got "out evolved" by other
methodologies and solutions.

When some of these things first appeared they were dreadful (program
generators, f'r'instance... Who could forget "The Last One"...?)  COBOLlers
laughed gleefully (I was one... not proud of it, and I have grown since...)
as they failed, and management had to go back to doing it in COBOL.

But the people of vision who conceived these other solutions simply wouldn't
lie down... Lotus produced their spreadsheet (even when "everybody knew" it
could never work...), the GUI interface (which was guffawed out of town by
COBOLlers) has become the normal interface for around a billion computer
users every day... Before 2010 we will see another technology breakthrough
that will change the whole scene again. And it will be accompanied with
previously failed/marginal technology that will be re-enabled and made
viable by the new platforms.

If you want to see what evolved successfully, just look around the Office...

And what didn't?

What are the dinosaurs, sinking in the tarpit....?

Procedural programming... Fortran, PL/1, and .... COBOL (amongst others...)


> >>Hmmmmm... this sounds like you are holding responsible or placing
> >>responsibility on these 'many mainframers' for that with which you
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Or, they might call it his "opinion", and accord it the consideration it
deserves... <G>

Pete.
```

```
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>Procedural programming... Fortran, PL/1, and .... COBOL (amongst others...)

When I look around the offices, I see core mission-critical systems
that used to be in-house developed replaced by ERPs by SAP, PeopleSoft
and Lawson. That software is written in procedural languages:  C/ABAP,
Cobol and Cobol, respectively. 

Sinking in the tarpit is in-house development, not procedural
programming.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Infinite Loops and Explicit Exits

_(reply depth: 32)_

- **From:** "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2004-11-16T00:11:09+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<2vrh6kF2oq404U1@uni-berlin.de>`
- **References:** `<cm8pp2$e3$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <skqcp09qor15e234f17ivl3sh9a8ar0ni3@4ax.com> <cn5qja$ejh$1@panix5.panix.com> <p6mdp0de13ntg44doc8o9kn7377ev76s40@4ax.com> <cn7a9o$rn8$1@panix5.panix.com> <2voqrbF2n8fl0U1@uni-berlin.de> <n7bgp056fmfgc2bpakrn5dfp13b2d74gq7@4ax.com>`

```

"Robert Wagner" <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net> wrote in message
news:n7bgp056fmfgc2bpakrn5dfp13b2d74gq7@4ax.com...
> On Sun, 14 Nov 2004 23:37:24 +1300, "Pete Dashwood"
> <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote:
>
> >If you want to see what evolved successfully, just look around the
Office...
> >
> >And what didn't?
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> >Procedural programming... Fortran, PL/1, and .... COBOL (amongst
others...)
>
> When I look around the offices, I see core mission-critical systems
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Fair and incisive comment, Robert. I should have prefixed my last sentence
with "In-house", thus:

'In-house Procedural programming... Fortran, PL/1, and .... COBOL (amongst
others...)'

Nevertheless, I still believe that even for software houses developing
solutions, Procedural Programming has a limited life; it is just longer than
for in-house development.

I believe that developments currently in the pipeline will change the
procedural nature of processors. There is then no point in the programming
languages reflecting an architecture that is not in use.

Time will tell.

Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Infinite Loops and Explicit Exits

_(reply depth: 32)_

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2004-11-15T15:43:52+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<cnairn$h7q$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<cm8pp2$e3$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <skqcp09qor15e234f17ivl3sh9a8ar0ni3@4ax.com> <cn5qja$ejh$1@panix5.panix.com> <p6mdp0de13ntg44doc8o9kn7377ev76s40@4ax.com> <cn7a9o$rn8$1@panix5.panix.com> <2voqrbF2n8fl0U1@uni-berlin.de> <n7bgp056fmfgc2bpakrn5dfp13b2d74gq7@4ax.com>`

```

On 14-Nov-2004, Robert Wagner <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net> wrote:

> When I look around the offices, I see core mission-critical systems
> that used to be in-house developed replaced by ERPs by SAP, PeopleSoft
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> programming.

Good point.   There is some exaggeration here, but certainly in-house
development is evolving.

Writing Excel macros is in-house development.    Most MS Access work is
in-house.   I believe most web design is in-house.

But the important stuff that might require a lawyer interpreting new laws that
were passed yesterday are being outsourced to companies such as the above.   
There is no competitive advantage to having a different interpretation of a tax
law.

Still, there's some space between these where business rules can be programmed
into core DP.   Insurance companies are developing huge databases looking for
competitive advantages.   And many companies that have tried out-sourcing are
re-thinking their needs.   The balance is changing and will continue to change -
but it won't capsize.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Infinite Loops and Explicit Exits

- **Message-ID:** `<cnai19$gdc$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```

On 13-Nov-2004, Robert Wagner <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net> wrote:

> Their single standard, written in the '80s, is the reason Cobol is
> called a dinosaur.

*Anything* gets called a dinosaur by people using newer tools.   They don't need
a reason.

Peter Coffee said almost a decade ago that CoBOL isn't a dinosaur, it's a shark.
  Sharks are older than dinosaurs, but they are still around.

Still, someone writing in the language of the day doesn't need to know anything
about CoBOL to call it a dinosaur say it has been dead for eons.   Especially
since he expects to be programming in his tool for decades to come.
```

```
On Mon, 15 Nov 2004 15:01:35 GMT, "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>
wrote:

>
>On 12-Nov-2004, Robert Wagner <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net> wrote:
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>- could you point me to a citation of what we claim?

Peter Lacey:
So far as I'm concerned, there are only two criteria that need to be
applied:
1) does the program work
2) is it easy to follow for TPB  ("The Poor Bastard") who's doing
maintenance.

>> Your chance of making it through is less than the
>> chance of a Pakistani being appointed to the board of my contracting
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>have to follow some standards that you like?

I would require them to follow published standards, but not my style.

Many so-called standards in mainframe shops are not published. They're
determined by the reviewer's mood at the moment.

Moreover, many reviewers don't know how to conduct a walkthru .. don't
understand that the leader's role is to elicit answers, not to provide
them.
```

```
On Tue, 16 Nov 2004 15:16:36 GMT, "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>
wrote:

>
>On 15-Nov-2004, Robert Wagner <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net> wrote:
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>sub-programmers use.   

I said it was a collaborative style developed and agreed to by all. In
some cases, I changed my style to follow the consensus.

My standards were organic, encouraging growth. My standards are
positive, telling people what TO do rather than what NOT to do.

> But anyway - if you require them to follow published
>standards, what exactly is your criticism of mainframers with the same
>requirement?

Most mainframe shop standards stifle progress by mandating Lowest
Common Denominator. They should elevate inexperienced programmers
rather than lowering everyone else to their level.

>> Moreover, many reviewers don't know how to conduct a walkthru .. don't
>> understand that the leader's role is to elicit answers, not to provide
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>conform to requirements.

They're penny wise and pound foolish. They conserve what they have at
the expense of losing what their staff could have accomplished.
```

- **References:** `<cm8pp2$e3$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <de53p0lbkeh4i9fokt7g716ggrppeb1crp@4ax.com> <cmtbs1$89$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <5cs7p0hdhvc4mmq7cpe7f78umc8bd3fkkb@4ax.com> <cn0umd$oiv$1@panix5.panix.com> <ituap09pknk18bks34tjlr21pkklmg3lg1@4ax.com> <cnagcf$fdn$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <adlip0pksj9uruftc2sqrvf8uoa6lor0js@4ax.com> <cnd5kk$c9t$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <5h7lp01uhchj3ceai5knesfa7oj7j6nrl0@4ax.com>`

```
"Robert Wagner" <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net> wrote in message 
news:5h7lp01uhchj3ceai5knesfa7oj7j6nrl0@4ax.com...
> On Tue, 16 Nov 2004 15:16:36 GMT, "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>
> wrote:
>
<snip>
>
> Most mainframe shop standards stifle progress by mandating Lowest
> Common Denominator. They should elevate inexperienced programmers
> rather than lowering everyone else to their level.
>

The "coding standards" that I am aware of at (IBM) mainframe shops that see the 
mainframe as a good place to develop and maintain COBOL code do *not* stifle 
proress and/or mandate the lowest common denominator.

They encourage (rarely require) a common coding style that makes it (relatively) 
easy for an experienced COBOL programmer to "take over" an existing program and 
to quickly understand its function - as well as to avoid some common errors.

Although I am not aware of such shops, I can easily imagine that an IBM 
mainframe shop that is trying to move its COBOL *off* the mainframe might not 
have kept its compiler or its programmers "current" and therefore, may have had 
a stifling style.
```

```
I think someone who is either correcting a problem in an existing program or
making an enhancement to the program within its existing framework is not
necessarily "writing bad code" if they elect to follow the programming
conventions and style *as they exist in that program*.    Sometimes it's
actually *preferable* to live with the warts than it is to go through the
trouble to remove them.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

```
Robert Wagner <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net> wrote 

> A before-and-after test with full production volume will insure new
> code is doing the same as old.

It will only 'insure' that for the particular items that are in that
test. There will be many things that a 'full production volume' may
not test.  Many times I have looked at a 'full production volume' and
seen that the actual data is only a subset of all possible data types
and would fail to excersize all the program.

In any case each individual program will still need testing before
reaching the point that it can be system tested.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Infinite Loops and Explicit Exits

_(reply depth: 15)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2004-11-04T09:59:07-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<cmdqlb$dl4$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<217e491a.0410302009.c1781e9@posting.google.com> <10o8vebpi1os15d@corp.supernews.com> <217e491a.0410310949.6a916128@posting.google.com> <10oacqs81h6tk62@corp.supernews.com> <217e491a.0410311752.141b49be@posting.google.com> <10obkpnmsnk32e8@corp.supernews.com> <cm5qrh$11f1$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <r88do0hgoqarvib383eb5mr9i6dciheatv@4ax.com> <cm8pp2$e3$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <qr6go0tqvutkqbtuji7q0919s8j22plnl2@4ax.com> <cmb01c$1gm4$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <tcmio011erm6g9nebq1k8vbea4sghsnohm@4ax.com>`

```
I had intended to comment on this part, but neglected to do so ...

"Robert Wagner" <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net> wrote in message
news:tcmio011erm6g9nebq1k8vbea4sghsnohm@4ax.com...

> I haven't noticed avoidance of 88s.

I have.  I don't know why.  I have noticed even more avoidance of 66's (I
don't think I've *ever* seen a production program that uses them).

However, 77's (which have been deprecated in this forum) are *very* heavily
used in the Unisys MCP community.  The '85 standard says "Items and
constants in working storage which bear no hierarchical relationship to one
another need not be grouped into records, provided they do not need to be
further subdivided.  Instead, they are classified and defined as
noncontiguous elementary items.  Each of these items is defined in a
separate data description entry which begins with the special level-number,
77."  (ANSI X3.23-1985 page VI-18, 5.2.1, Noncontiguous Working Storage).
Wording in the 2002 standard is similar.

While it is possible once an 01-level data item has been completely parsed
to determine that it in fact can be treated "noncontiguously", the
implementor is free to treat items that are *by definition* noncontiguous
differently from those that are *by implication* noncontiguous.

In our implementations, for example, 77-level numeric items often don't end
up "in memory" in the ordinary sense at all; they're allocated directly on,
and accessed directly from, the hardware stack.  01-level items, *in
contrast*, are more likely to be allocated in memory.   That raises the
distinct possibility that access to a 77-level item *may* in some
implementations be *more efficient* than access to an otherwise-identical
01-level item.

Since the standard does indeed distinguish between 01-level items
(elementary and otherwise) and 77's in that the latter are explicitly
characterized as "noncontiguous", why deprecate the distinction for all
implementations and suggest the abandonment of 77's on the grounds that
nobody would ever need them?

    -Chuck Stevens
```

- **References:** `<217e491a.0410310949.6a916128@posting.google.com> <10oacqs81h6tk62@corp.supernews.com> <217e491a.0410311752.141b49be@posting.google.com> <10obkpnmsnk32e8@corp.supernews.com> <cm5qrh$11f1$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <r88do0hgoqarvib383eb5mr9i6dciheatv@4ax.com> <cm8pp2$e3$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <qr6go0tqvutkqbtuji7q0919s8j22plnl2@4ax.com> <cmb01c$1gm4$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <tcmio011erm6g9nebq1k8vbea4sghsnohm@4ax.com> <cmdqlb$dl4$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`

```
On Thu, 4 Nov 2004 09:59:07 -0800, "Chuck Stevens"
<charles.stevens@unisys.com> wrote:

>I had intended to comment on this part, but neglected to do so ...
>
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>don't think I've *ever* seen a production program that uses them).

I've never used 66. Reason: my text editor cannot handle indirection,
it can only search on keywords. When I see MOVE A TO SHAMPOO, I search
for all occurances of SHAMPOO. If it's a 66, I have to do another
search for SHAMPOO OR REALPOO. That's annoying.

Why do you need two names for the same thing? If I encountered a 66,
I'd do a global replace and delete the 66.

>However, 77's (which have been deprecated in this forum) are *very* heavily
>used in the Unisys MCP community. ..
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>01-level item.

Efficiency is no longer the programmer's job. I'm happy to let the
optimizer and virtual memory manager worry about it. I put what would
be 77's in a structure which will be in a resident 2K VMM page. 

01  unqualified-variables.
     05  a          
     05  b

>Since the standard does indeed distinguish between 01-level items
>(elementary and otherwise) and 77's in that the latter are explicitly
>characterized as "noncontiguous", why deprecate the distinction for all
>implementations and suggest the abandonment of 77's on the grounds that
>nobody would ever need them?

To abolish the mindset that application programs need to hold the
machine's hand. I thought the objective of standards was
machine-independence.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Infinite Loops and Explicit Exits

_(reply depth: 12)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2004-11-03T10:06:52-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0411031006.6a0a1240@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<clu41g$1t2b$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <10o7eij23spll38@corp.supernews.com> <217e491a.0410302009.c1781e9@posting.google.com> <10o8vebpi1os15d@corp.supernews.com> <217e491a.0410310949.6a916128@posting.google.com> <10oacqs81h6tk62@corp.supernews.com> <217e491a.0410311752.141b49be@posting.google.com> <10obkpnmsnk32e8@corp.supernews.com> <cm5qrh$11f1$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <r88do0hgoqarvib383eb5mr9i6dciheatv@4ax.com> <cm8pp2$e3$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`

```
"Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com> wrote 

> > They are not equivalent. Assuming iteration control is in PERFORM ..
> > THRU,  GO TO <perform-range-exit> = EXIT PERFORM CYCLE = EXIT
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
> event I think they're pretty much equivalent.

What is confusing is that you are saying EXIT <something> and GO TO
<something> are equivalent when they obviously aren't under the same
PERFORM statement.

        PERFORM a THRU a-exit  ||      PERFORM a UNTIL EXIT
        ...                    || 
    a.                         ||   a.   
         IF ... GO TO a-exit.  ||      IF ... EXIT PERFORM.
         ..                    ||      ...
         IF .. GO TO a.        ||      IF .. EXIT PERFORM CYCLE
         ..                    ||      ...
    a-exit.                    ||   b.

are somewhat equivalent, but note it is a completely different
PERFORM, a fact that you hadn't mentioned.

If the left side PERFORM had an UNTIL (or VARYING UNTIL!) then GOTO
a-exit does _not_ do an EXIT PERFORM, but EXIT PERFORM CYCLE. GO TO a
does not trigger the VARYING BY nor would it count as one of the
TIMES.

In fact the left side can be done 'in-line' without the PERFORM at all
if the program is allowing drop throughs.

So, you are correct about which are equivalent, but only when compared
in code which is completely different.  They cannot be substituted
without rewriting.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Infinite Loops and Explicit Exits

- **Message-ID:** `<cmb958$1n6c$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<clu41g$1t2b$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <10o7eij23spll38@corp.supernews.com> <217e491a.0410302009.c1781e9@posting.google.com> <10o8vebpi1os15d@corp.supernews.com> <217e491a.0410310949.6a916128@posting.google.com> <10oacqs81h6tk62@corp.supernews.com> <217e491a.0410311752.141b49be@posting.google.com> <10obkpnmsnk32e8@corp.supernews.com> <cm5qrh$11f1$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <r88do0hgoqarvib383eb5mr9i6dciheatv@4ax.com> <cm8pp2$e3$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <217e491a.0411031006.6a0a1240@posting.google.com>`

```
I *believe* my specification of the rules for EXIT PERFORM as stated in my
proposal to J4 on the subject is correct; I was trying in this thread to
state the issues simply without getting into that rigorous formality
required in the standard.  For example, my document has "When the EXIT
PERFORM statement without the CYCLE phrase occurring outside the context of
an inline PERFORM statement is executed within the range of one or more
out-of-line PERFORM statements, control is passed to the end of the last
PERFORM statement executed."   That's at least closer to reality.

    -Chuck Stevens

"Richard" <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote in message
news:217e491a.0411031006.6a0a1240@posting.google.com...
> "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com> wrote
>
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> > Only partly true.  GO TO <perform-range-exit> is equivalent to EXIT
PERFORM
> > CYCLE only if <perform-range-exit> is a paragraph that contains only the
> > EXIT statement (not a requirement), and EXIT PERFORM CYCLE is equivalent
to
> > EXIT PARAGRAPH only if both occur in the last or only paragraph in the
> > PERFORM range.
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> > containing only EXIT.  I was thinking of the PERFORM FOREVER case, in
which
> > event I think they're pretty much equivalent.
>
…[26 more quoted lines elided]…
> without rewriting.
```

- **References:** `<clu41g$1t2b$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <10o8vebpi1os15d@corp.supernews.com> <217e491a.0410310949.6a916128@posting.google.com> <10oacqs81h6tk62@corp.supernews.com> <217e491a.0410311752.141b49be@posting.google.com> <10obkpnmsnk32e8@corp.supernews.com> <cm5qrh$11f1$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <r88do0hgoqarvib383eb5mr9i6dciheatv@4ax.com> <cm8pp2$e3$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <217e491a.0411031006.6a0a1240@posting.google.com> <cmb958$1n6c$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`

```
"Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com> wrote 

> I *believe* my specification of the rules for EXIT PERFORM as stated in my
> proposal to J4 on the subject is correct; I was trying in this thread to
> state the issues simply without getting into that rigorous formality
> required in the standard.  

But some seemed to be confused by what would be equivalent.

> For example, my document has "When the EXIT
> PERFORM statement without the CYCLE phrase occurring outside the context of
> an inline PERFORM statement is executed within the range of one or more
> out-of-line PERFORM statements, control is passed to the end of the last
> PERFORM statement executed."   That's at least closer to reality.

I do hope you mean the last _active_ PERFORM.

       PERFORM A
       here
       ...
    A.
        PERFORM B
        EXIT PERFORM 
        ...
        .

The 'PERFORM B' is the 'last PERFORM executed', 'here' is where you want to get to.
```

```
"Richard" <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote in message
news:217e491a.0411032201.329878f8@posting.google.com...
> "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com> wrote
>
> > I *believe* my specification of the rules for EXIT PERFORM as stated in
my
> > proposal to J4 on the subject is correct; I was trying in this thread to
> > state the issues simply without getting into that rigorous formality
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> > PERFORM statement without the CYCLE phrase occurring outside the context
of
> > an inline PERFORM statement is executed within the range of one or more
> > out-of-line PERFORM statements, control is passed to the end of the last
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
> The 'PERFORM B' is the 'last PERFORM executed', 'here' is where you want
to get to.

Yes; at this point I'm mostly worried about getting "agreement in principle"
on what it's supposed to do; I have no doubt the wording will be thoroughly
hashed over before it gets into the standard!  ;-)

The *current* such rule for EXIT PERFORM reads "The execution of an EXIT
PERFORM statement without the CYCLE phrase causes control to be passed to an
implicit CONTINUE statement following the END-PERFORM phrase that matches
the most closely preceding, and as yet unterminated, inline PERFORM
statement".  (ISO/IEC 1989:2002 Page 449, 14.8.13.3, EXIT statement, general
rule 9).

Perhaps "... control is passed to an implicit CONTINUE statement following
the most recently executed, and as yet unterminated, out-of-line PERFORM
statement."?

Simply changing "last PERFORM" to "last such PERFORM" might also work; the
EXIT PERFORM does not appear, to me, to be in the perform range of PERFORM
B.

But again, *at this stage* what I'm more concerned about is whether or not
the basic idea would be accepted by J4 (and WG4), not whether we've got all
the serifs right in the rules.  If the whole idea gets shot down, all the
energy and time spent worrying about whether I covered all the cases or not
in the early drafts of the rules is wasted, and I have some rather weightier
issues to consider!

    -Chuck Stevens
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Infinite Loops and Explicit Exits

_(reply depth: 16)_

- **From:** Robert Wagner <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net>
- **Date:** 2004-11-05T05:22:48+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<fuvlo0pav1bh080gh4n4r10c4r1blh0svq@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<217e491a.0410310949.6a916128@posting.google.com> <10oacqs81h6tk62@corp.supernews.com> <217e491a.0410311752.141b49be@posting.google.com> <10obkpnmsnk32e8@corp.supernews.com> <cm5qrh$11f1$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <r88do0hgoqarvib383eb5mr9i6dciheatv@4ax.com> <cm8pp2$e3$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <217e491a.0411031006.6a0a1240@posting.google.com> <cmb958$1n6c$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <217e491a.0411032201.329878f8@posting.google.com> <cmdphl$cq0$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`

```
On Thu, 4 Nov 2004 09:40:05 -0800, "Chuck Stevens"
<charles.stevens@unisys.com> wrote:


>Yes; at this point I'm mostly worried about getting "agreement in principle"
>on what it's supposed to do; I have no doubt the wording will be thoroughly
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>statement."?

I suggest:

The execution of an EXIT PERFORM statement without the CYCLE phrase
causes control to be passed to an implicit CONTINUE statement
following the END-PERFORM or before the implicit end that matches
the most closely preceding, and as yet unterminated,  PERFORM
statement. 

Consider the wording to describe this case:

A.
    PERFORM B VARYING X FROM 1 BY 1 UNTIL X > 3

B. 
    PERFORM VARYING Y FROM 1 BY 1 UNTIL Y > 3
         PERFORM C VARYING Z FROM 1 BY 1 UNTIL Z > 3
         IF  NOT Z > 3
             EXIT PERFORM.
    IF  NOT Y > 3
        EXIT PERFORM.
C. 
    IF  Y = 2 
        EXIT PERFORM.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Infinite Loops and Explicit Exits

_(reply depth: 17)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2004-11-05T09:35:19-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<cmgdkn$260f$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<217e491a.0410310949.6a916128@posting.google.com> <10oacqs81h6tk62@corp.supernews.com> <217e491a.0410311752.141b49be@posting.google.com> <10obkpnmsnk32e8@corp.supernews.com> <cm5qrh$11f1$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <r88do0hgoqarvib383eb5mr9i6dciheatv@4ax.com> <cm8pp2$e3$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <217e491a.0411031006.6a0a1240@posting.google.com> <cmb958$1n6c$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <217e491a.0411032201.329878f8@posting.google.com> <cmdphl$cq0$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <fuvlo0pav1bh080gh4n4r10c4r1blh0svq@4ax.com>`

```
Robert Wagner suggested:

>>
> The execution of an EXIT PERFORM statement without the CYCLE phrase
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>>

Thanks for the suggestion, Robert; unfortunately, I see a couple of problems
with it.

The inline PERFORM context is, I believe, adequately covered already in EXIT
GR's 9 and 10; this looks like a rewrite of GR9 to include both contexts,
which I'm trying to avoid in the (believe it or not) interest of simplicity
and clarity.  Note that END-PERFORM is expressly limited to the context of
*inline* PERFORM, which means your "... END-PERFORM ..." verbiage makes no
sense at all for the out-of-line PERFORM, which is the problem we're trying
to solve here.

And "implicit end" isn't defined.  "Implicit CONTINUE" is used elsewhere and
is more appropriate; one of the tricky parts is in specifying *exactly*
where that implicit CONTINUE lies.

    -Chuck Stevens

"Robert Wagner" <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net> wrote in message
news:fuvlo0pav1bh080gh4n4r10c4r1blh0svq@4ax.com...

thoroughly
> >hashed over before it gets into the standard!  ;-)

an

general

following

> >statement."?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Infinite Loops and Explicit Exits

_(reply depth: 19)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2004-11-05T15:05:03-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<cmh0v0$2j1i$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<217e491a.0410310949.6a916128@posting.google.com> <10oacqs81h6tk62@corp.supernews.com> <217e491a.0410311752.141b49be@posting.google.com> <10obkpnmsnk32e8@corp.supernews.com> <cm5qrh$11f1$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <r88do0hgoqarvib383eb5mr9i6dciheatv@4ax.com> <cm8pp2$e3$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <217e491a.0411031006.6a0a1240@posting.google.com> <cmb958$1n6c$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <217e491a.0411032201.329878f8@posting.google.com> <cmdphl$cq0$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <fuvlo0pav1bh080gh4n4r10c4r1blh0svq@4ax.com> <cmgdkn$260f$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <I8Tid.27860$dj2.1684432@news20.bellglobal.com>`

```

"Donald Tees" <donald_tees@sympatico.ca> wrote in message
news:I8Tid.27860$dj2.1684432@news20.bellglobal.com...
> Chuck Stevens wrote:
> > Robert Wagner suggested:
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> > Thanks for the suggestion, Robert; unfortunately, I see a couple of
problems
> > with it.
> >
> > The inline PERFORM context is, I believe, adequately covered already in
EXIT
> > GR's 9 and 10; this looks like a rewrite of GR9 to include both
contexts,
> > which I'm trying to avoid in the (believe it or not) interest of
simplicity
> > and clarity.  Note that END-PERFORM is expressly limited to the context
of
> > *inline* PERFORM, which means your "... END-PERFORM ..." verbiage makes
no
> > sense at all for the out-of-line PERFORM, which is the problem we're
trying
> > to solve here.
> >
> > And "implicit end" isn't defined.  "Implicit CONTINUE" is used elsewhere
and
> > is more appropriate; one of the tricky parts is in specifying *exactly*
> > where that implicit CONTINUE lies.
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Well, they aren't planning to "replace NEXT SENTENCE" in the next standard;
J4 and WG4 recognize that doing so would Invalidate A Bunch Of Productive
Programs.  It's marked as "Archaic" in the 2002 standard, and it's still
marked as "Archaic" in the draft for the 2008 standard.  As either one puts
it, "There is no plan for the removal of archaic elements; hower, should
their use in program inventories become insignificant, they may be
considered for designation as obsolete by future architects of standard
COBOL."

So, if you're willing to do the research to find out how many existing
production programs worldwide use NEXT SENTENCE, and guarantee out of your
pocket that that number is objectively insignificant enough for you to
indemnify all businesses that might actually have such programs still
running so that they suffer no costs at all in revising those programs,
maybe you can convince J4 (and WG4) to move NEXT SENTENCE into the
"obsolete" category in some future standard after 2008.  Once the standard
*after that* one is in effect, "exit sentence" -- which reintroduces
*almost* the same funtionality using different words -- could be considered
(though I doubt many would do so enthusiastically).

I note that EXIT, EXIT PROGRAM, EXIT METHOD, EXIT FUNCTION, EXIT SECTION and
EXIT PARAGRAPH are all allowed *as sentences* themselves, even as the only
sentence in a paragraph, and I've proposed EXIT PERFORM be allowed in the
same contexts, with some utility.

But what is the run-time utility of the EXIT SENTENCE as a separate
sentence?   This is the significant case in which it differs from NEXT
SENTENCE, which is a phrase of the IF and SEARCH statements, not a sentence
by itself.  Seems to me this proposal adds more confusion than it
eliminates!

    -Chuck Stevens
```

```
<theoretical> EXIT SENTENCE would do).  From my experience it was used - but how 
widely, I can't say.  It is also my VAGUE memory that some other implementations 
have (do?) allow NEXT SENTENCE with some (not all) statements as an extension, 
e.g.

   Read whatever
       At End
           Next Sentence
    end-read
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Infinite Loops and Explicit Exits

_(reply depth: 18)_

- **From:** Robert Wagner <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net>
- **Date:** 2004-11-07T10:59:42+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<mrnro0thin2uvjiij7np21rk7sncin0tb6@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<217e491a.0410311752.141b49be@posting.google.com> <10obkpnmsnk32e8@corp.supernews.com> <cm5qrh$11f1$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <r88do0hgoqarvib383eb5mr9i6dciheatv@4ax.com> <cm8pp2$e3$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <217e491a.0411031006.6a0a1240@posting.google.com> <cmb958$1n6c$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <217e491a.0411032201.329878f8@posting.google.com> <cmdphl$cq0$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <fuvlo0pav1bh080gh4n4r10c4r1blh0svq@4ax.com> <cmgdkn$260f$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`

```
On Fri, 5 Nov 2004 09:35:19 -0800, "Chuck Stevens"
<charles.stevens@unisys.com> wrote:

>Robert Wagner suggested:
>
…[19 more quoted lines elided]…
>And "implicit end" isn't defined.  

I should have said 'implicit scope terminator'.

"Scope terminators serve to delimit the scope of certain procedure
division statements. Scope terminators are of
two types: explicit and implicit.

The explicit scope terminators are the following:
END-ACCEPT END-EVALUATE END-REWRITE ...

The implicit scope terminators are the following:
1) At the end of any sentence, the separator period that terminates
the scope of all previous statements not yet terminated.
2) Within any statement containing another statement, the next phrase
of the containing statement following the
contained statement terminates the scope of any unterminated contained
statement."

Proposed definition v2.0:

The execution of an EXIT PERFORM statement without the CYCLE phrase
causes control to be passed to an implicit CONTINUE statement
following the last executed PERFORM statement. If the PERFORM was
terminated  explicitly, CONTINUE lies after the terminator; if
implicitly, CONTINUE lies before the terminator.

I acknowledge the wording is faulty because it fails to say 'last
_active_ PERFORM statement'. The standard defines 'active' as a
condition of programs, functions and methods, not paragraphs. It
describes a normal PERFORM exit with this language:

"If a paragraph is being executed under control of another COBOL
statement (for example, PERFORM, USE,
SORT, and MERGE) and the paragraph is the last paragraph in the range
of the controlling statement, then an
implied transfer of control occurs from the last statement in the
paragraph to the control mechanism of the last
executed controlling statement."

Consider this case:

  PERFORM A    *> controlling statement #1

A. 
   PERFORM B.   *> controlling statement #2
C.

When paragraph A exits, the "last executed controlling statement" is
#2, not #1. A literal reading of the standard would have paragraph A
return to itself!

If "last executed" is good enough for the '02 standard (shucks, you
know what we meant), it should be good enough for EXIT PERFORM.

>"Implicit CONTINUE" is used elsewhere and
>is more appropriate; one of the tricky parts is in specifying *exactly*
>where that implicit CONTINUE lies.

The second sentence in my proposal covers that. Consider terminators
such as ELSE and WHEN: 

IF (condition)
    PERFORM A VARYING .. UNTIL ..
ELSE
    PERFORM B VARYING .. UNTIL ..
END-IF

An EXIT PERFORM from A obviously sends control before the ELSE, not
after it.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Infinite Loops and Explicit Exits

_(reply depth: 19)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2004-11-08T10:38:18-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<cmoeeq$1k8v$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<217e491a.0410311752.141b49be@posting.google.com> <10obkpnmsnk32e8@corp.supernews.com> <cm5qrh$11f1$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <r88do0hgoqarvib383eb5mr9i6dciheatv@4ax.com> <cm8pp2$e3$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <217e491a.0411031006.6a0a1240@posting.google.com> <cmb958$1n6c$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <217e491a.0411032201.329878f8@posting.google.com> <cmdphl$cq0$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <fuvlo0pav1bh080gh4n4r10c4r1blh0svq@4ax.com> <cmgdkn$260f$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <mrnro0thin2uvjiij7np21rk7sncin0tb6@4ax.com>`

```
"Robert Wagner" <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net> wrote in message
news:mrnro0thin2uvjiij7np21rk7sncin0tb6@4ax.com...
> On Fri, 5 Nov 2004 09:35:19 -0800, "Chuck Stevens"
> <charles.stevens@unisys.com> wrote:
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> >Thanks for the suggestion, Robert; unfortunately, I see a couple of
problems
> >with it.
> >
> >The inline PERFORM context is, I believe, adequately covered already in
EXIT
> >GR's 9 and 10; this looks like a rewrite of GR9 to include both contexts,
> >which I'm trying to avoid in the (believe it or not) interest of
simplicity
> >and clarity.  Note that END-PERFORM is expressly limited to the context
of
> >*inline* PERFORM, which means your "... END-PERFORM ..." verbiage makes
no
> >sense at all for the out-of-line PERFORM, which is the problem we're
trying
> >to solve here.
> >
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
> statement."

I don't see this wording in the discussions on pages 384-385 of Explicit
scope termination and Implicit scope termination.  Could you cite your
source?   I think the published standard's a bit more rigorous in its
definition than what you propose here.

>
> Proposed definition v2.0:
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> following the last executed PERFORM statement.

I think "last" will be rejected on grounds of lack of clarity.  "Most
recently executed and as yet unterminated" is more likely to fly.

> If the PERFORM was
> terminated  explicitly,

Inline PERFORMs and out-of-line PERFORMs are clearly distinguished by those
terms, not by the presence of END-PERFORM.

> CONTINUE lies after the terminator;

It'd have to read "If the PERFORM statement was an inline PERFORM statement,
the implicit CONTINUE statement immediately follows the PERFORM statement."

>if
> implicitly, CONTINUE lies before the terminator.

Nope.  It immediately follows the PERFORM statement and immediately precedes
whatever terminated the PERFORM statement.

> I acknowledge the wording is faulty because it fails to say 'last
> _active_ PERFORM statement'.

> The standard defines 'active' as a
> condition of programs, functions and methods, not paragraphs.

The standard defines "active state" as a condition of programs, functions
and methods.   That is not the same thing as defining "active" as a
condition of programs, functions and methods.

There are four instances of "active PERFORM statement" already in the
standard, two in in the discussion of "explicit and implicit transfers of
control" and two in the "undefined language element list".   Personally, I
think there's enough precedent to use the term.

>It
> describes a normal PERFORM exit with this language:

>
> "If a paragraph is being executed under control of another COBOL
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
> #2, not #1.

Not exactly.  Control is transferred to the *control mechanism of* the last
executed controlling statement; that control mechanism occurs *after* the
last statement performed.

> A literal reading of the standard would have paragraph A
> return to itself!
]
I don't think so.

> If "last executed" is good enough for the '02 standard (shucks, you
> know what we meant), it should be good enough for EXIT PERFORM.

It might well.  I was trying to stick as close as possible to the existing
general rules for exiting an inline PERFORM, modifying them as little as
possible and making the new rules as closely parallel as possible.

> >"Implicit CONTINUE" is used elsewhere and
> >is more appropriate; one of the tricky parts is in specifying *exactly*
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> after it.

No, it sends control to the end of the PERFORM, a significant distinction,
as in
    IF <condition>
         PERFORM A VARYING ... UNTIL ...
         <do-something>
    ELSE
       ....

The "implicit continue statement> is *after* the control mechanism, if any,
associated with the PERFORM, and *before* the ELSE.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Infinite Loops and Explicit Exits

_(reply depth: 20)_

- **From:** Robert Wagner <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net>
- **Date:** 2004-11-10T04:53:43+00:00

- **References:** `<cm5qrh$11f1$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <r88do0hgoqarvib383eb5mr9i6dciheatv@4ax.com> <cm8pp2$e3$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <217e491a.0411031006.6a0a1240@posting.google.com> <cmb958$1n6c$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <217e491a.0411032201.329878f8@posting.google.com> <cmdphl$cq0$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <fuvlo0pav1bh080gh4n4r10c4r1blh0svq@4ax.com> <cmgdkn$260f$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <mrnro0thin2uvjiij7np21rk7sncin0tb6@4ax.com> <cmoeeq$1k8v$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`

```
On Mon, 8 Nov 2004 10:38:18 -0800, "Chuck Stevens"
<charles.stevens@unisys.com> wrote:

>"Robert Wagner" <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net> wrote in message
>news:mrnro0thin2uvjiij7np21rk7sncin0tb6@4ax.com...

>> >And "implicit end" isn't defined.
>>
…[20 more quoted lines elided]…
>definition than what you propose here.

I quoted Committee Draft 1.8, 14.6 Procedural statements and sentences

>> Proposed definition v2.0:
>>
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>recently executed and as yet unterminated" is more likely to fly.

My language matches that of 14.7.2, which says:

"If a paragraph is being executed under control of another COBOL
statement (for example, PERFORM, USE,
SORT, and MERGE) and the paragraph is the last paragraph in the range
of the controlling statement, then an
implied transfer of control occurs from the last statement in the
paragraph to the control mechanism of the LAST 
EXECUTED controlling statement."

>It'd have to read "If the PERFORM statement was an inline PERFORM statement,
>the implicit CONTINUE statement immediately follows the PERFORM statement."
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>whatever terminated the PERFORM statement.

So this:

PERFORM VARYING B FROM 1 BY 1 UNTIL B > 10
    ADD 1 TO COUNTER
    .                         *> implicit terminator

Becomes: 

PERFORM VARYING B FROM 1 BY 1 UNTIL B > 10
    ADD 1 TO COUNTER
    CONTINUE
    .                    *> implicit terminator

>There are four instances of "active PERFORM statement" already in the
>standard, two in in the discussion of "explicit and implicit transfers of
>control" and two in the "undefined language element list".   Personally, I
>think there's enough precedent to use the term.

The first two are in a single sentence dealing with DECLARATIVES. 

>> Consider this case:
>>
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>last statement performed.

For simplicity, my examples did not have a control mechanism except
the default 'next statement'. You did not address the issue of which
controlling statement was the last one executed. It was controlling
statement #2, so why does the program return to the statement after
controlling statement #1?

>> A literal reading of the standard would have paragraph A
>> return to itself!
>]
>I don't think so.

What does the published standard say?

>> >"Implicit CONTINUE" is used elsewhere and
>> >is more appropriate; one of the tricky parts is in specifying *exactly*
…[23 more quoted lines elided]…
>associated with the PERFORM, and *before* the ELSE.

In that case <do-something> is the implicit terminator, not ELSE.  An
EXIT PERFORM from A returns to an implicit continue statement after
PERFORM and before <do-something>, which is what I said.
```

- **References:** `<cm5qrh$11f1$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <r88do0hgoqarvib383eb5mr9i6dciheatv@4ax.com> <cm8pp2$e3$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <217e491a.0411031006.6a0a1240@posting.google.com> <cmb958$1n6c$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <217e491a.0411032201.329878f8@posting.google.com> <cmdphl$cq0$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <fuvlo0pav1bh080gh4n4r10c4r1blh0svq@4ax.com> <cmgdkn$260f$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <mrnro0thin2uvjiij7np21rk7sncin0tb6@4ax.com> <cmoeeq$1k8v$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <ok53p058qe95q5f4lkr5uhoh2rqufsl59g@4ax.com>`

```

"Robert Wagner" <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net> wrote in message 
news:ok53p058qe95q5f4lkr5uhoh2rqufsl59g@4ax.com...
> On Mon, 8 Nov 2004 10:38:18 -0800, "Chuck Stevens"
> <charles.stevens@unisys.com> wrote:
…[29 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Robert,
   Why are you still quoting a VERY old "Committee Draft"?  Time has come to 
actually pay the $18 to download the approved Standard (or don't claim to know 
what the Standard says).

FYI,
   The following is the (MUCH expanded/clarified) defintiion from  "14.4.2.2 
Implicit scope termination" starting on page 384 of the approved Standard.  (any 
"suggestion" you make would need to work with this)

"14.4.2.2 Implicit scope termination

The scope of a statement that is not explicitly terminated is implicitly 
terminated as follows:

1) for a single imperative statement not contained within another statement, by
    a) any element that follows the exhaustion of the statement's syntax, or
    b) the next-encountered statement-name, or
    c) a separator period.

2) for a single imperative statement contained within another statement, by
    a) any element that terminates an imperative statement not contained within 
another statement,
    b) the termination of the scope of any containing statement, or
    c) the next phrase of any containing statement.

3) for a conditional statement not contained within another statement, by a 
separator period.

4) for a conditional statement contained within another statement, by
    a) the termination of any containing statement, or
    b) the next phrase of any containing statement.

Any phrase encountered is the next phrase of the most-recently preceding 
unterminated statement with which that
phrase may be syntactically associated. If all permitted occurrences of a phrase 
have already been specified for a
given statement, a subsequent occurrence of that phrase is not syntactically 
associated with that statement. An
unterminated statement is any statement that has begun but has not yet been 
either explicitly or implicitly
terminated.

A contained statement is also referred to as a nested statement."
```

```
Listen.

Hear that grumbling murmur? That's the members of CODASYL (the people who
sat down in 1959 and ended up giving us COBOL) spinning in their graves.

They put aside their commercial differences and placed the language in the
public domain so it would be free to all.

Up until very recently all COBOL manuals carried a page stating that, and
acknowledging the work and the intentions of the Conference On DAta SYstems
Languages (1959)

Now it costs $18 to see a draft, $200 to buy a final copy, and the people
who do the work either pay for that privelege themselves, or are sponsored
by their companies. And ANSI is a non-profit organisation so they don't even
pay tax on it...(apropos my post in response to Michael about the money
going round...)

But I'm just a trouble maker, so ignore this post...

Pete.
```

- **Date:** 2004-11-12T08:21:35-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<cn2nuf$2gr9$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<cm8pp2$e3$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <217e491a.0411031006.6a0a1240@posting.google.com> <cmb958$1n6c$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <217e491a.0411032201.329878f8@posting.google.com> <cmdphl$cq0$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <fuvlo0pav1bh080gh4n4r10c4r1blh0svq@4ax.com> <cmgdkn$260f$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <mrnro0thin2uvjiij7np21rk7sncin0tb6@4ax.com> <cmoeeq$1k8v$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <ok53p058qe95q5f4lkr5uhoh2rqufsl59g@4ax.com> <2vikd.4789343$6p.784379@news.easynews.com> <26u7p015g4v2brqd5t1h9rd8geiavvderk@4ax.com> <2vjcqjF2n4hvtU1@uni-berlin.de>`

```

"Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote in message
news:2vjcqjF2n4hvtU1@uni-berlin.de...

> Now it costs $18 to see a draft,

Correction.  It costs $18 to download your own .pdf copy of the published
standard from ANSI.   It is not a draft.

> $200 to buy a final copy,

Correction.  I think the price is actually $281, and what that gets you is
the same technical material that ANSI distributes for $18 with different
cover pages.    The ISO version is the original, the ANSI version was taken
from it.

Both are published standards, one published by ISO and one published by
ANSI.  You can get either version from the ANSI standards store.

So far as I know, nobody's distributing a printed version.

The only COBOL standards draft I know of still available on the internet is
the working draft of the *next* standard, to which there's a link on the J4
website.  Basically, the stuff marked obsolete in the 2002 standard has been
deleted, and as a result the page and section numbering might differ, but
aside from that it's pretty much the 2002 standard.  (This might change
after the December J4 meeting, at which producing a new draft to include the
changes thus far approved is up for discussion.  The link to the 2002 FCD on
the J4 website appears, unfortunately, to be a dead end.

> and the people
> who do the work either pay for that privelege themselves, or are sponsored
> by their companies.

True enough.

> And ANSI is a non-profit organisation so they don't even
> pay tax on it...

Which is why ANSI charges $18 to defer their costs in maintaining a
repository for, and distributing copies of, this particular standard.
Personally, I think that's pretty darned inexpensive!

    -Chuck Stevens
```

- **References:** `<cm8pp2$e3$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <217e491a.0411031006.6a0a1240@posting.google.com> <cmb958$1n6c$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <217e491a.0411032201.329878f8@posting.google.com> <cmdphl$cq0$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <fuvlo0pav1bh080gh4n4r10c4r1blh0svq@4ax.com> <cmgdkn$260f$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <mrnro0thin2uvjiij7np21rk7sncin0tb6@4ax.com> <cmoeeq$1k8v$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <ok53p058qe95q5f4lkr5uhoh2rqufsl59g@4ax.com> <2vikd.4789343$6p.784379@news.easynews.com> <26u7p015g4v2brqd5t1h9rd8geiavvderk@4ax.com> <2vjcqjF2n4hvtU1@uni-berlin.de> <cn2nuf$2gr9$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`

```
Correction (Chuck et al),

You may now PURCHASE a printed copy of the FINAL *ANSI* (not ISO) version of the 
approved Standard for $40.  See:

http://www.techstreet.com/cgi-bin/detail?product_id=1095105

FYI,
  To the best of my knowledge, the "free" FDIS" version is no longer available 
on the web.

FYI-2,
  The ANSI (not the ISO) version of  the Standard does include a "permission to 
use" statement.  This is different from the old "Acknowledgement" - but does 
provide medium-free usage permission.

FYI-3,
   Neither the '85 Standard nor the '89 or '91 amendments were available "for 
free" from ANSI (or NIST or anywhere else that I know of).  The only (major) 
difference was that you could "share" copies - but these were HARD-copies, not 
an "electronic" version (except - I think for the final amendment).

Final comment:
   The ISO (and the DIN, BSI, and other national standard bodies) copies of the 
'02 Standard can be purchases as well.  Their prices vary significantly.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Infinite Loops and Explicit Exits

_(reply depth: 10)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2004-11-01T22:47:12-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0411012247.4b7b54f2@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<clu41g$1t2b$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <S7Jgd.3709$K7.2223@news-server.bigpond.net.au> <10o7eij23spll38@corp.supernews.com> <217e491a.0410302009.c1781e9@posting.google.com> <10o8vebpi1os15d@corp.supernews.com> <217e491a.0410310949.6a916128@posting.google.com> <10oacqs81h6tk62@corp.supernews.com> <217e491a.0410311752.141b49be@posting.google.com> <10obkpnmsnk32e8@corp.supernews.com> <cm5qrh$11f1$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`

```
"Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com> wrote 

> One of COBOL's *strengths* is that code that works continues to work.   In a
> PERFORM THRU environment, the purpose of EXIT PERFORM is *clearer* than GO
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> PERFORM would, I believe, be an improvement in clarity and maintainability.

_Neither_ of your examples are equivalent.

         PERFORM A THRU A-Exit
            VARYING .. BY .. FROM .. UNTIL ..

      A.
         ....
           {  GO TO A
           {  EXIT PERFORM CYCLE    *> are _NOT_ equivalent
    
           {  GO TO A-Exit
           {  EXIT PERFORM          *> are _NOT_ equivalent
       
      A-Exit.
          EXIT.
```
