###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Infinite Loops and Explicit Exits

_(reply depth: 7)_

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2004-11-01T17:36:14+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<cm5s6e$h8g$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<clu41g$1t2b$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <S7Jgd.3709$K7.2223@news-server.bigpond.net.au> <10o7eij23spll38@corp.supernews.com> <217e491a.0410302009.c1781e9@posting.google.com> <10o8vebpi1os15d@corp.supernews.com> <217e491a.0410310949.6a916128@posting.google.com> <cm5pat$10a5$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`

```

On  1-Nov-2004, "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com> wrote:

> > You do not need EXIT ~ to eliminate GO TO, you need to get rid of the
> > paragraph labels and THRU/SECTION so GO TO does not fit anywhere and
> > then you will find that EXIT isn't needed either.
>
> And if the paragraph you are constructing gets too unwieldy ...?

When working with standards committees, I have found that people who favor an
exit paragraph for every perform have some power when they show how little
coding changes are needed for a maintenance programmer to add a GO TO
THIS-PARAGRAPH-EXIT upon a changed condition within a paragraph.

This argument will go away if we ever get a compiler that allows them an EXIT
PARAGAPH command.

If all paragraphs are performed, and none are dropped into, then it is a simple
matter to adjust when a paragraph gets too unwieldy.   Just break it into
component paragraphs that are performed.   Adding a paragraph where it can
possibly be dropped into has caused problems.

I've used a standard that had:
 ABORT-SECTION.
       ...
 REST-OF-THE-PROGRAM SECTION.
****    All performed paragraphs are below here.

This standard helped ensure that we wouldn't drop through to performed code.

IMHO, CoBOL would be much better if it never had allowed dropping through from
one paragraph to another - but until the EXIT PARAGRAPH paragraph, that required
a different type of programming mindset.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Infinite Loops and Explicit Exits

_(reply depth: 8)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2004-11-01T11:06:19-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<cm61fc$15ui$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<clu41g$1t2b$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <S7Jgd.3709$K7.2223@news-server.bigpond.net.au> <10o7eij23spll38@corp.supernews.com> <217e491a.0410302009.c1781e9@posting.google.com> <10o8vebpi1os15d@corp.supernews.com> <217e491a.0410310949.6a916128@posting.google.com> <cm5pat$10a5$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <cm5s6e$h8g$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```

"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote in message
news:cm5s6e$h8g$1@peabody.colorado.edu...
>
> On  1-Nov-2004, "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com> wrote:
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> When working with standards committees, I have found that people who favor
an
> exit paragraph for every perform have some power when they show how little
> coding changes are needed for a maintenance programmer to add a GO TO
> THIS-PARAGRAPH-EXIT upon a changed condition within a paragraph.

What I favor is the ability of an implementation of COBOL-<latest-standard>
to compile and execute, *without change*, a program written according to
<current-standard>, even <some-prior-standard>, and provide feature content
that would allow the maintenance programmer to improve the clarity of that
program without requiring its complete restructuring.

I think the addition of inline PERFORM in '85 COBOL, the addition of EXIT
PERFORM and EXIT PERFORM CYCLE in 2002 COBOL, and my suggestion to allow the
latter in the context of out-of-line PERFORMs represent incremental
improvements to the language without in any way invalidating, disparaging or
otherwise archaizing the programs that users have been running for years.

> This argument will go away if we ever get a compiler that allows them an
EXIT
> PARAGAPH command.

Speak to your vendor.  EXIT PARAGRAPH and EXIT SECTION are expressly
required in ISO/IEC 1989:2002.

> If all paragraphs are performed, and none are dropped into, then it is a
simple
> matter to adjust when a paragraph gets too unwieldy.   Just break it into
> component paragraphs that are performed.   Adding a paragraph where it can
> possibly be dropped into has caused problems.

Yes, it can cause problems.  For reasons elaborated slightly below, that's a
common *programmer* error.

, but that's not a characteristic of COBOL that one's likely to change at
this point, as it's been working this way for some 45 years now.

> I've used a standard that had:
>  ABORT-SECTION.
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> This standard helped ensure that we wouldn't drop through to performed
code.

Yes, I've seen that done.

> IMHO, CoBOL would be much better if it never had allowed dropping through
from
> one paragraph to another - but until the EXIT PARAGRAPH paragraph, that
required
> a different type of programming mindset.

Be that as it may, it's been that way since before you or I started working
on COBOL, and it's been that way since before Structured Programming.
Since J4 and WG4 are *very* sensitive about ensuring that COBOL users don't
get their hands slapped on matters of religion or style, they won't likely
give much attention to any proposal along these lines.

Paragraphs in COBOL have as genesis the labels in Fortran and in assembler
languages, not the musings of Dijkstra, and in those languages, you can
"fall into" a label just as you can explicitly transfer execution-time
control to it.  Dijkstra's a johnny-come-lately on this subject.

The standards committees don't want to *drive* people into rewriting their
code, because doing so is as likely to drive them into rewriting their code
in some other language, particularly if the rewrite is extensive.
Archaization of features or capabilities is a subject that is *not* taken
lightly, particularly if the feature is a fundamental one of long standing.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

```
On  1-Nov-2004, "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com> wrote:

> The standards committees don't want to *drive* people into rewriting their
> code, because doing so is as likely to drive them into rewriting their code
> in some other language, particularly if the rewrite is extensive.
> Archaization of features or capabilities is a subject that is *not* taken
> lightly, particularly if the feature is a fundamental one of long standing.

Generally when a company changes its programming standards, it does not mandate
rewriting earlier code.   And I haven't seen people suggest that this should be
done.

Having commands which facilitate changing the standards for new code doesn't
mean it would be a good idea to change working code.   And I haven't seen
anybody suggest that it would be a good idea.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Infinite Loops and Explicit Exits

_(reply depth: 10)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2004-11-01T11:41:12-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<cm63go$1780$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<clu41g$1t2b$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <S7Jgd.3709$K7.2223@news-server.bigpond.net.au> <10o7eij23spll38@corp.supernews.com> <217e491a.0410302009.c1781e9@posting.google.com> <10o8vebpi1os15d@corp.supernews.com> <217e491a.0410310949.6a916128@posting.google.com> <cm5pat$10a5$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <cm5s6e$h8g$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <cm61fc$15ui$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <cm62pq$mi7$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```

"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote in message
news:cm62pq$mi7$1@peabody.colorado.edu...
>
> On  1-Nov-2004, "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com> wrote:
>
> > The standards committees don't want to *drive* people into rewriting
their
> > code, because doing so is as likely to drive them into rewriting their
code
> > in some other language, particularly if the rewrite is extensive.
> > Archaization of features or capabilities is a subject that is *not*
taken
> > lightly, particularly if the feature is a fundamental one of long
standing.
>
> Generally when a company changes its programming standards, it does not
mandate
> rewriting earlier code.   And I haven't seen people suggest that this
should be
> done.
>
> Having commands which facilitate changing the standards for new code
doesn't
> mean it would be a good idea to change working code.   And I haven't seen
> anybody suggest that it would be a good idea.

Those who suggest that GO TO, PERFORM THRU, and the use of SECTIONs in COBOL
should be archaized in, marked obsolete in, or eliminated from the
standard -- and these are the three steps in that process -- are.

To archaize a construct suggests to its users that it'd be a good idea to
fix it because it's old.  To mark a construct obsolete puts its users on
notice that it won't be in the next version of the standard and they'd
better get cracking on fixing it so their programs will continue to compile.

As much as people think PERFORM THRU or GO TO represent the Fundamental
Cause of All Evil in the Modern World Today, there is a large body of
programs out there that have been using exactly those constructs, with no
deleterious effect whatever, for a very long time.   If it works, don't fix
it.  Or, more accurately in this case, if it works, don't deliberately
*break* it.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Infinite Loops and Explicit Exits

_(reply depth: 13)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2004-11-01T23:30:02-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0411012330.5ddc9ec@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<clu41g$1t2b$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <10o7eij23spll38@corp.supernews.com> <217e491a.0410302009.c1781e9@posting.google.com> <10o8vebpi1os15d@corp.supernews.com> <217e491a.0410310949.6a916128@posting.google.com> <cm5pat$10a5$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <cm5s6e$h8g$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <cm61fc$15ui$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <cm62pq$mi7$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <cm63go$1780$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <cm65f1$ofm$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <10od8oaiipd7b7c@corp.supernews.com>`

```
"Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net> wrote 

> > And who are those people who want them marked obsolete in or eliminated
>  from the
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> and GO TO are archaic, the first step toward elimination.

I didn't see anyone _insisting_ on single paragraph PERFORMs, or
indeed no GO TO or fall-through, nor that they should be put into any
categorey at all.

However, if, for example, an EXIT~ does, as had been suggested by
someone else, eliminate the need for GO TO exit-pargarph, then it is
axiomatic that in these cases the exit-pargraph label becomes
redundant and thus the THRU is no longer necessary in those cases.

None of this implies that GO TO or THRU should be eliminated from the
language. Whether the EXIT~s are superior to the GO TO ~exit, or to
other structures, has yet to be seen.

Personally I have difficulty with EXIT PARAGRAPH/SECTION/PERFORM
because, just like GO TO, it only fits within certain ways that code
is constructed.  For example, 'unwielding' code and reusing it the way
I do puts in additional layers of PERFORMs which the EXIT~ won't exit
out of in the same way (whereas there is no issue at all with fully
nested code being unwielded).
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Infinite Loops and Explicit Exits

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2004-11-02T14:53:41+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<cm871l$51j$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<clu41g$1t2b$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <10o7eij23spll38@corp.supernews.com> <217e491a.0410302009.c1781e9@posting.google.com> <10o8vebpi1os15d@corp.supernews.com> <217e491a.0410310949.6a916128@posting.google.com> <cm5pat$10a5$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <cm5s6e$h8g$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <cm61fc$15ui$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <cm62pq$mi7$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <cm63go$1780$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <cm65f1$ofm$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <10od8oaiipd7b7c@corp.supernews.com> <217e491a.0411012330.5ddc9ec@posting.google.com>`

```

On  2-Nov-2004, riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard) wrote:

> Personally I have difficulty with EXIT PARAGRAPH/SECTION/PERFORM
> because, just like GO TO, it only fits within certain ways that code
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> nested code being unwielded).

One big advantage is that it works no matter what style of coding the original
coder did while writing the program.   Let's say a maintenance programmer wants
out of a loop but notices that the original programmer did NOT use PERFORM THRU
EXIT.    No problem, just exit.   If the original programmer DID have exit
paragraphs, still no problem.  Either exit or go to exit.

But without this command, there are big differences in how much that original
programmer needs to touch and modify.

Allowing multiple coding styles with easy maintenance is a *good* thing.
```

- **References:** `<clu41g$1t2b$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <10o7eij23spll38@corp.supernews.com> <217e491a.0410302009.c1781e9@posting.google.com> <10o8vebpi1os15d@corp.supernews.com> <217e491a.0410310949.6a916128@posting.google.com> <cm5pat$10a5$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <cm5s6e$h8g$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <cm61fc$15ui$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <cm62pq$mi7$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <cm63go$1780$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <cm65f1$ofm$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <10od8oaiipd7b7c@corp.supernews.com> <217e491a.0411012330.5ddc9ec@posting.google.com>`

```

"Richard" <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote in message
news:217e491a.0411012330.5ddc9ec@posting.google.com...
> "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net> wrote
>
> > > And who are those people who want them marked obsolete in or
eliminated
> >  from the
> > > standard?
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> categorey at all.

Insisting; to insist, to be emphatic, firm, or resolute on some
matter; from the Latin insistere, (to) stand still on, to take a
stand.

To say "perform single paragraphs only" and "GO TO is not
needed" is to be firm. To say the same unwaveringly is to be
resolute. To say the same repetitively is to be emphatic.

Mr Plinston, you may not see anyone "insisting;" but I have
seen you "insisting" for years and, a quick search of Google
groups, confirms at least ten years of being emphatic, firm,
and resolute on the matters of single paragraph perform and
GO TO.

> However, if, for example, an EXIT~ does, as had been suggested by
> someone else, eliminate the need for GO TO exit-pargarph, then it is
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> other structures, has yet to be seen.

Mr Plinston, I said "implying ... archaic, the first step toward
elimination."

To say "perform single paragraphs only" is to imply that THRU
is not needed. To say "GO TO is not needed" is to say so
directly. To say these things with the knowledge that THRU
and GO TO have been part of the language since the
beginning is to imply "no longer needed" and, therefore, old or
outmoded; thus anitquated and archaic.

The sense of "imply" that I use is: to involve or require as a
necessary condition.
```

```
And here is where I used this particular set of words:

"""The particular set of rules that I use are (there are exceptions of
course):
 
    Only PERFORM single paragraphs (NOT sections, no THRU)
 
    Never Drop Through a label
 
    GO TOs are only used to the top of the current paragraph
        (continue) or out to a common abandon-program
"""

You misrepresent my words as somehow insisting that others do this
when it quite clearly states that it is what _I_ do.

> and "GO TO is not needed" is to be firm.

probably:

  """GO TO is not needed to 'jump over the mess',"""

"""In my view there is actually nothing wrong with GO TOs.  They are
very clear in their intent and easy to follow."""

"""There is actually nothing wrong with a GO TO statement.  It is
perfectly
what its action is and, where the GO TO occurs, there is no confusion
over the logic flow."""

"""In fact there is nothing wrong with a GO TO at all.  It is a very
clear
statement that is unambiguous and always does what is required."""

> groups, confirms at least ten years of being emphatic, firm,
> and resolute on the matters of single paragraph perform and
> GO TO.

or my opinion, or one option among others, or conditional

just one way.

> elimination."

Given that it was Chuck that brought up 'archaic', perhaps you could

shouldn't use it ?

and yet more recently you stated:
```

- **References:** `<clu41g$1t2b$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <10o8vebpi1os15d@corp.supernews.com> <217e491a.0410310949.6a916128@posting.google.com> <cm5pat$10a5$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <cm5s6e$h8g$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <cm61fc$15ui$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <cm62pq$mi7$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <cm63go$1780$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <cm65f1$ofm$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <10od8oaiipd7b7c@corp.supernews.com> <217e491a.0411012330.5ddc9ec@posting.google.com> <10ofruhrt7slp2e@corp.supernews.com> <217e491a.0411022334.5c19f10e@posting.google.com>`

```

"Richard" <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote in message
news:217e491a.0411022334.5c19f10e@posting.google.com...
> "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net> wrote
>
…[102 more quoted lines elided]…
> So find an actual case where I _require_ something.

"The Random House College Dictionary," Basic Manual of
Style, Quotations Marks, rule 10, states, "Use quotation
marks: to emphasize a word or phrase which is itself the
subject of discussion."

The claim I made was, "insisting on ... implying ... archaic."

I supplied a definition of "insist." I then supplied phrases,
properly quoted under rule 10, to establish how such phrases
fit the criteria "insisting on." Later, I showed how the use of
such phrases "imply" that some features of COBOL are
"archaic" and did so by establishing that other necessary
conditions are involved under the definition of "imply" that
I gave.

Mr Plinston, you wrongly assumed that the quoted phrases
were direct quotes, rather than phrases that I had to supply
to discuss the subject of how such phrases "insist." I cannot
address many of the points you raised, because they bear no
resemblance to what I wrote or the context in which I wrote it.

Mr Plinston and Mr Brazee, qualification and opinion makes
no difference.

Poster 1: ... perform thru ...
Plinston: I only perform single paragraphs.
Brazee: I don't like fall-thru paragraphs.
[Three months later]
Poster 2: ... perform thru ...
Plinston: I only perform single paragraphs.
Brazee: I don't like fall-thru paragraphs.
[One year later]
Poster 3: ... [multi-paragraph] sections [that fall-thru] ...
Plinston: I only perform single paragraphs.
Brazee: I don't like fall-thru paragraphs.
[Eight months later]
Poster 4: ... perform thru ...
Plinston: I only perform single paragraphs.
Brazee: I don't like fall-thru paragraphs.

Mr Plinston, it does not take much of this for an observer to
understand that you are "insisting" that others "only perform
single paragraphs" and that Mr Brazee is "insisting" that
execution must not "fall-thru" to the next paragraph.

My point was that "insisting" on a particular style "implies"
that another style, using features long present, should not
be used and, therefore, these features are "no longer needed",
thus "archaic."

The question Mr Brazee asked was, "And who are those
people who want them marked obsolete in or eliminated
from the standard?" My answer (re-phrased) is those who
deprecate a feature by "insisting" on a style that avoids the
feature or advising against the use of the feature. This helps
to push the feature toward the archaic category, which is,
essentially, official deprecation. The next step is to make it
obsolete; followed, later, by automatic removal.

That was, as I understand it, what happened to the ALTER
statement. With the GO TO statement, many "insist" on a
style that avoids GO TO and many advise against using
GO TO. The WG4 vote on making the GO TO statement
archaic was 4-to-5. [This was a few years ago for COBOL
2002.]

A question was asked, answered, explained, justified, and
answered again.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Infinite Loops and Explicit Exits

_(reply depth: 17)_

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2004-11-04T15:56:18+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<cmdjf2$gmi$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<clu41g$1t2b$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <10o8vebpi1os15d@corp.supernews.com> <217e491a.0410310949.6a916128@posting.google.com> <cm5pat$10a5$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <cm5s6e$h8g$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <cm61fc$15ui$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <cm62pq$mi7$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <cm63go$1780$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <cm65f1$ofm$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <10od8oaiipd7b7c@corp.supernews.com> <217e491a.0411012330.5ddc9ec@posting.google.com> <10ofruhrt7slp2e@corp.supernews.com> <217e491a.0411022334.5c19f10e@posting.google.com> <10oj2m0fda3sp6d@corp.supernews.com>`

```

On  3-Nov-2004, "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net> wrote:

> Mr Plinston and Mr Brazee, qualification and opinion makes
> no difference.

They make huge differences.

> Plinston: I only perform single paragraphs.
> Brazee: I don't like fall-thru paragraphs.
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> execution must not "fall-thru" to the next paragraph.

I agree that it doesn't take much of an observer to observe things that aren't
said.   What Mr. Plinston does and what I like are in no way insisting that
others follow our lead.


> My point was that "insisting" on a particular style "implies"
> that another style, using features long present, should not
> be used and, therefore, these features are "no longer needed",
> thus "archaic."

And I have repeatedly disagreed with your point.   Not only is it based upon a
false assumption, the conclusion does not follow.   The whole argument is
entirely in your head.   Your inferences are completely unrelated to what I
actually said.

There's a big difference between what you infer and what I imply.

> The question Mr Brazee asked was, "And who are those
> people who want them marked obsolete in or eliminated
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> obsolete; followed, later, by automatic removal.

I disagree.


> That was, as I understand it, what happened to the ALTER
> statement. With the GO TO statement, many "insist" on a
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> 2002.]

You do have a point here.

> A question was asked, answered, explained, justified, and
> answered again.

Unconvincingly.
```

- **References:** `<clu41g$1t2b$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <10o8vebpi1os15d@corp.supernews.com> <217e491a.0410310949.6a916128@posting.google.com> <cm5pat$10a5$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <cm5s6e$h8g$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <cm61fc$15ui$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <cm62pq$mi7$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <cm63go$1780$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <cm65f1$ofm$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <10od8oaiipd7b7c@corp.supernews.com> <217e491a.0411012330.5ddc9ec@posting.google.com> <10ofruhrt7slp2e@corp.supernews.com> <217e491a.0411022334.5c19f10e@posting.google.com> <10oj2m0fda3sp6d@corp.supernews.com> <cmdjf2$gmi$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```

"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote in message
news:cmdjf2$gmi$1@peabody.colorado.edu...
>
> On  3-Nov-2004, "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net> wrote:
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> They make huge differences.

Not with the repetition shown.

Consider the qualification "in this case." This seems to limit
the consequences of the statement, but when repeated with
different cases (posters), it approaches "in no case." There
is a point at which the qualification must be discarded.

> > Plinston: I only perform single paragraphs.
> > Brazee: I don't like fall-thru paragraphs.
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> I agree that it doesn't take much of an observer to observe things that
aren't
> said.   What Mr. Plinston does and what I like are in no way insisting
that
> others follow our lead.

Mr Brazee, by saying that you have a "lead" that may be
followed, and to express repeatedly the concept that makes
it "lead[ing]," is to insist that others "follow [y]our lead;" else
why say it?

Can you not see that, by using "lead," you are implying that
what others do, different from that "lead," is outmoded, thus
archaic?

>
> > My point was that "insisting" on a particular style "implies"
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> And I have repeatedly disagreed with your point.   Not only is it based
upon a
> false assumption, the conclusion does not follow.   The whole argument is
> entirely in your head.   Your inferences are completely unrelated to what
I
> actually said.
>
> There's a big difference between what you infer and what I imply.

There are different senses for the definition of "imply." Either of
these fits the use I relied on.
( www.m-w.com )
2 : to involve or indicate by inference, association, or necessary
    consequence rather than by direct statement <rights imply
    obligations>
( "The Random House College Dictionary" )
2. to involve or require as a necessary condition.

These are not related to intent of the speaker. For one to say
"At 6 PM, I was stuck in a snow drift outside Chicago." implies
"I was not on a beach at Maui when it was 6 PM in Chicago."
[It seems likely that the importance of this particular implication
would require more information.]
```

- **References:** `<clu41g$1t2b$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <10o8vebpi1os15d@corp.supernews.com> <217e491a.0410310949.6a916128@posting.google.com> <cm5pat$10a5$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <cm5s6e$h8g$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <cm61fc$15ui$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <cm62pq$mi7$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <cm63go$1780$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <cm65f1$ofm$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <10od8oaiipd7b7c@corp.supernews.com> <217e491a.0411012330.5ddc9ec@posting.google.com> <10ofruhrt7slp2e@corp.supernews.com> <217e491a.0411022334.5c19f10e@posting.google.com> <10oj2m0fda3sp6d@corp.supernews.com> <cmdjf2$gmi$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <10ol1ll6f8m6t2f@corp.supernews.com>`

```

On  4-Nov-2004, "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net> wrote:

> > I agree that it doesn't take much of an observer to observe things that
> aren't
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> why say it?

One can recommend without insisting.   One can discuss techniques without
insisting.   I don't believe the world consists only of dictators and sheep, nor
should it.   I infer that you believe differently.

> Can you not see that, by using "lead," you are implying that
> what others do, different from that "lead," is outmoded, thus
> archaic?

No, I cannot.


> These are not related to intent of the speaker. For one to say
> "At 6 PM, I was stuck in a snow drift outside Chicago." implies
> "I was not on a beach at Maui when it was 6 PM in Chicago."
> [It seems likely that the importance of this particular implication
> would require more information.]

However to say "I program this way", or even "I find this way of programming to
be desirable", does not imply "I want other ways of programming to be
proscribed" - even if that is what you infer.
```

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net>

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<10onjhgpd68jge7@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<clu41g$1t2b$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <10o8vebpi1os15d@corp.supernews.com> <217e491a.0410310949.6a916128@posting.google.com> <cm5pat$10a5$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <cm5s6e$h8g$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <cm61fc$15ui$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <cm62pq$mi7$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <cm63go$1780$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <cm65f1$ofm$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <10od8oaiipd7b7c@corp.supernews.com> <217e491a.0411012330.5ddc9ec@posting.google.com> <10ofruhrt7slp2e@corp.supernews.com> <217e491a.0411022334.5c19f10e@posting.google.com> <10oj2m0fda3sp6d@corp.supernews.com> <cmdjf2$gmi$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <10ol1ll6f8m6t2f@corp.supernews.com> <cme29k$prg$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```

"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote in message
news:cme29k$prg$1@peabody.colorado.edu...
>
> On  4-Nov-2004, "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net> wrote:
>
> > > I agree that it doesn't take much of an observer to observe things
that
> > aren't
> > > said.   What Mr. Plinston does and what I like are in no way insisting
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> insisting.

When a comment is specific to a problem, I would suggest
it is a recommendation without insisting. When the comment
does not address the problem, I see it as insisting on something.
For example, a poster provides code to print a report; the code
is written using sections; and the problem is with control breaks.
A recommendation solving the problem does not insist; a
recommendation to use Report Writer does insist; and a
recommendation to use paragraphs, not sections, also insists.

>   I don't believe the world consists only of dictators and sheep, nor
> should it.   I infer that you believe differently.

I think dictators and sheep might have that opinion. I don't.

> > Can you not see that, by using "lead," you are implying that
> > what others do, different from that "lead," is outmoded, thus
> > archaic?
>
> No, I cannot.

Well, I can't argue with that. But if your use of "lead" is not
in the sense of current practice, then different implications
arise.

>
> > These are not related to intent of the speaker. For one to say
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> However to say "I program this way", or even "I find this way of
programming to
> be desirable", does not imply "I want other ways of programming to be
> proscribed" - even if that is what you infer.

Mr Brazee, those statements are ambiguous. I can't tell if, by
"this way," you mean standing on your head, standing on your
feet, sitting in a chair, sitting on the floor, or something else.
To imply, in the sense used, requires a clear statement from
which to draw the necessary consequences or conditions.
The implications arise with the specifics of "this way" and
"this way of programming to be desirable."

Having said that, my dictionary provides a definition of
"desirable" as "advisable; recommendable." It is not a big
leap to conclude that the inadvisable and practice not
recommended is to be proscribed; but it does depend on the
specifics.
```

```

On  5-Nov-2004, "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net> wrote:

> > One can recommend without insisting.   One can discuss techniques without
> > insisting.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> does not address the problem, I see it as insisting on something.

I'm just the opposite.  When someone has a specific fix, I see that fix as close
to being insistent.   When he is talking general styles, I see it as being a
recommendation.

> For example, a poster provides code to print a report; the code
> is written using sections; and the problem is with control breaks.
> A recommendation solving the problem does not insist; a
> recommendation to use Report Writer does insist; and a
> recommendation to use paragraphs, not sections, also insists.

A recommendation solving the problem DOES insist.   A recommendation to use
Report Writer does NOT insist, a recommendation to use paragraphs does NOT
insist.


> >   I don't believe the world consists only of dictators and sheep, nor
> > should it.   I infer that you believe differently.
>
> I think dictators and sheep might have that opinion. I don't.

I inferred differently from the way you segue from "recommend" to "insist".  
What kind of person believes that recommending is the same as insisting?   I
know dictators believe that.   I know sheep do that.     But independent
(bears?), don't.

> Having said that, my dictionary provides a definition of
> "desirable" as "advisable; recommendable." It is not a big
> leap to conclude that the inadvisable and practice not
> recommended is to be proscribed; but it does depend on the
> specifics.

It is a much bigger leap than I am willing to make.   And it is a bigger leap
than I expect others to make.
```

```
Rick Smith wrote:
> "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote in message
> news:cmgl1d$7k7$1@peabody.colorado.edu...
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> do it my way."

This sounds an awfully lot like someone who is insecure.  Debates over 
styles are fine.  Recommendations are fine.  Unless the person is your 
supervisor, though, you can safely ignore any "insisting," since that 
person likely does not have the wherewithal to back up their insistence.

Are you insecure in your programming style?  Do you do things that you 
think other folks would find unpopular?  If so, get a little confidence. 
  I was actually laughed at by my compiler vendor for using the 
"preceding period" style, where paragraph names are done like

. the-next-paragraph.

Did that stop me?  No - I realized the benefits that I realized with 
that style (even once I figured out that the compiler was getting 
confused with the period in column 8 - had to change it to something like

     .
the-next-paragraph.

instead) was worth any good-natured ribbing I might get from the folks 
who support our compiler.  In fact, I recommend it - it's far easier to 
move code around, place things under conditional statements, or remove 
the from conditional statements, without having to worry about whether I 
remembered to remove all the periods.

Do I insist you do the same?  Of course not - I'm just saying what I've 
found works best for me.  :)
```

```
>Did that stop me?  No - I realized the benefits that I realized with 
>that style (even once I figured out that the compiler was getting 
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…

The ANSI Cobol 85 standard removed the distinction between columns 8
and 12. It sounds as though your compiler may be non-compliant.
```

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>

```
Robert,
    Get REAL !!!!

Do you ever check your fact before posting????

The '85 Standard *definitely* kept the A-/B- margin distinction.  (I.e. column 8 
vs column 12).  It is true that the '02 Standard removed this distinction.

If you ever used the FLAGSTD directive with your Micro Focus compiler, you would 
see that it CORRECTLY flags statements starting in column 8 as "non-conforming 
and an extension".
```

_(reply depth: 26)_

- **From:** Robert Wagner <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net>
- **Date:** 2004-11-14T18:31:32+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<t49fp0l3ng8trb6mp4kgkr1of1057ih3p5@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<10ofruhrt7slp2e@corp.supernews.com> <217e491a.0411022334.5c19f10e@posting.google.com> <10oj2m0fda3sp6d@corp.supernews.com> <cmdjf2$gmi$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <10ol1ll6f8m6t2f@corp.supernews.com> <cme29k$prg$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <10onjhgpd68jge7@corp.supernews.com> <cmgl1d$7k7$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <10onr54cu7gg9bc@corp.supernews.com> <y39ld.622$aW.26@fe40.usenetserver.com> <dmqcp0l44477l9oou9h7sp7eir2r7sqi41@4ax.com> <EEBld.4421828$ic1.421132@news.easynews.com>`

```
On Sun, 14 Nov 2004 05:01:24 GMT, "William M. Klein"
<wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> wrote:

>Robert,
>    Get REAL !!!!
>
>Do you ever check your fact before posting????

I checked 1.2.1.3 at 
http://supportline.microfocus.com/supportline/documentation/books/sx22sp1/lrpubb.htm

"Section names and paragraph names begin in area A and are followed by
a period and a space. Level indicators FD, SD, CD and RD begin in area
A and are followed in area B 

[ANS85] or area A 

by the appropriate description. Level numbers 01 and 77 begin in area
A and are followed in area B 

[ANS85] or area A 

by the data description. Level numbers 02 through 49, 66 and 88 begin
in area B. "

I interpret that to mean ANS85 relaxed the columns where statements
continue and, to the point here, where they end. In other words, the
terminating period can be in area A.

>The '85 Standard *definitely* kept the A-/B- margin distinction.  (I.e. column 8 
>vs column 12).  It is true that the '02 Standard removed this distinction.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>and an extension".

What about statements ending in column 8? That was the original
question.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Infinite Loops and Explicit Exits

_(reply depth: 27)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2004-11-15T00:29:25+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ELSld.4484416$ic1.428622@news.easynews.com>`
- **References:** `<10ofruhrt7slp2e@corp.supernews.com> <217e491a.0411022334.5c19f10e@posting.google.com> <10oj2m0fda3sp6d@corp.supernews.com> <cmdjf2$gmi$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <10ol1ll6f8m6t2f@corp.supernews.com> <cme29k$prg$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <10onjhgpd68jge7@corp.supernews.com> <cmgl1d$7k7$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <10onr54cu7gg9bc@corp.supernews.com> <y39ld.622$aW.26@fe40.usenetserver.com> <dmqcp0l44477l9oou9h7sp7eir2r7sqi41@4ax.com> <EEBld.4421828$ic1.421132@news.easynews.com> <t49fp0l3ng8trb6mp4kgkr1of1057ih3p5@4ax.com>`

```
The '02 Standard *does* include the following substantive change (see page 826:

"54) Fixed-form reference format area A and B removed. Areas A and B of the 
previous COBOL standard reference format have been combined into one area called 
the program-text area. Restrictions of area A and area B have been removed. The 
previous COBOL standard reference format is fully upward compatible."

Page IV-42 of the '85 Standard says the following about "continuation" lines:

"7.2.2 Continuation of Lines
Any sentence, entry, phrase, or clause may be continued by starting subsequent 
line(s) in area B. These subsequent lines are called the continuation line(s)."

The text that your quote is a (potentially - but I don't think so - misleading) 
paraphrase of is from pages IV-43 - 44) which states (in part),

"There are two types of such entries: those which begin with a level indicator 
and those which begin with a level-number.

In the Data Division, a level indicator is any of the following: FD, SD, CD, RD.

In those entries that begin with a level indicator, the level indicator begins 
in area A, followed by at least one space, and then followed with the name of 
the subject of entry and appropriate descriptive information.

Those entries that begin with level-numbers are called data description entries.
      ...
In those data description entries that begin with a level-number 01 or 77, the 
level-number begins in area A, followed by at least one space, and then followed 
with its associated record-name or item-name, if ..."

I see no indication (in the Substantive change appendix) that this is any 
different from the '74 Standard, but it might be ...)

   ***

The exact quote (which you faild to include) which I think you ERRONEOUSLY made 
was,

"The ANSI Cobol 85 standard removed the distinction between columns 8 and 12. It 
sounds as though your compiler may be non-compliant."

Again, if you are going to make statements about what the '85 Standard did or 
did not allow or require, then check THAT - not some vendors "re-wording" of it.
```

- **References:** `<10ofruhrt7slp2e@corp.supernews.com> <217e491a.0411022334.5c19f10e@posting.google.com> <10oj2m0fda3sp6d@corp.supernews.com> <cmdjf2$gmi$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <10ol1ll6f8m6t2f@corp.supernews.com> <cme29k$prg$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <10onjhgpd68jge7@corp.supernews.com> <cmgl1d$7k7$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <10onr54cu7gg9bc@corp.supernews.com> <y39ld.622$aW.26@fe40.usenetserver.com> <dmqcp0l44477l9oou9h7sp7eir2r7sqi41@4ax.com> <l6Cld.1226$aW.1151@fe40.usenetserver.com>`

```
On Sat, 13 Nov 2004 23:32:59 -0600, LX-i <lxi0007@netscape.net> wrote:

>Robert Wagner wrote:
>> On Fri, 12 Nov 2004 14:30:19 -0600, LX-i <lxi0007@netscape.net> wrote:
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
>formalized "free-format" text...

I wasn't referring to free-format; I was referring to relaxed rules in
fixed-format. Mr. Klein correctly pointed out that the '85 standard
did not abolish areas A and B (as did the '02 standard). It still
requires statements to START in the same area as before, but it
appears to have relaxed rules on where they END .. at least in the
data division. Not having an official '85 standard, I say that based
on Micro Focus notation in their manual.
```

_(reply depth: 28)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2004-11-15T22:36:25+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Jbamd.3032393$yk.471737@news.easynews.com>`
- **References:** `<10ofruhrt7slp2e@corp.supernews.com> <10oj2m0fda3sp6d@corp.supernews.com> <cmdjf2$gmi$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <10ol1ll6f8m6t2f@corp.supernews.com> <cme29k$prg$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <10onjhgpd68jge7@corp.supernews.com> <cmgl1d$7k7$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <10onr54cu7gg9bc@corp.supernews.com> <y39ld.622$aW.26@fe40.usenetserver.com> <dmqcp0l44477l9oou9h7sp7eir2r7sqi41@4ax.com> <l6Cld.1226$aW.1151@fe40.usenetserver.com> <9obgp09quunk1ga3u76h7t6hhrs1orgrjn@4ax.com> <217e491a.0411151314.626f8b34@posting.google.com>`

```
Richard,
   The quote that Robert included was for fixed format.  The issue is when you 
code:

  01 A.

The "01" has to be in the A-margin, but the '85 Standard (semi-clearly) states 
that the "A" can also be in the A-margin.  What I don't know is whether this was 
or wasn't also true in the '74 Standard.

"more extreme" - and related to the original question of where a period is 
allowed, the question is whether you could (in a conforming '74 compiler) code:

 1 A.

Where "1" is in column 8
"A" is in column
    and
the period (full-stop) is in column 11 (i.e. the A-margin).

     ****

It is worth noting, that in the '02 Standard (but NOT the '85 Standard) even 
when one uses fixed form reference format, the A-/B-margin distinction is 
removed (so simply marking "free format" as an extension or from the '02 
Standard) doesn't address this question.
```

```
<wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> wrote:

  01  A
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Infinite Loops and Explicit Exits

_(reply depth: 30)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2004-11-16T11:51:19+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<XQlmd.4579615$ic1.436287@news.easynews.com>`
- **References:** `<10ol1ll6f8m6t2f@corp.supernews.com> <cme29k$prg$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <10onjhgpd68jge7@corp.supernews.com> <cmgl1d$7k7$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <10onr54cu7gg9bc@corp.supernews.com> <y39ld.622$aW.26@fe40.usenetserver.com> <dmqcp0l44477l9oou9h7sp7eir2r7sqi41@4ax.com> <l6Cld.1226$aW.1151@fe40.usenetserver.com> <9obgp09quunk1ga3u76h7t6hhrs1orgrjn@4ax.com> <217e491a.0411151314.626f8b34@posting.google.com> <Jbamd.3032393$yk.471737@news.easynews.com> <5d3jp0pi57h48g3akbhvk6e1vr71cbsqgp@4ax.com>`

```
The code you present is "clearly" illegal in the '85 Standard - as "continuation 
lines" may ONLY include text in the B-margin.  (See the original quote I posted 
in this thread).

(in the '85 Standard) A separator period (terminating either a statement or a 
data division entry - or anything else)
May:
   A) appear on the same line as the previous portion of the syntactic element
            or
    B) as a continuation line in the B-margin
```

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2004-11-19T06:13:06+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<S9gnd.5335303$6p.864247@news.easynews.com>`
- **References:** `<10onjhgpd68jge7@corp.supernews.com> <cmgl1d$7k7$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <10onr54cu7gg9bc@corp.supernews.com> <y39ld.622$aW.26@fe40.usenetserver.com> <dmqcp0l44477l9oou9h7sp7eir2r7sqi41@4ax.com> <l6Cld.1226$aW.1151@fe40.usenetserver.com> <9obgp09quunk1ga3u76h7t6hhrs1orgrjn@4ax.com> <217e491a.0411151314.626f8b34@posting.google.com> <Jbamd.3032393$yk.471737@news.easynews.com> <5d3jp0pi57h48g3akbhvk6e1vr71cbsqgp@4ax.com> <XQlmd.4579615$ic1.436287@news.easynews.com> <s02rp0tvvjtobf7k1ocp8jler6pcdevd0q@4ax.com>`

```
Putting a period on a line by itself is conforming to the '85 Standard but ONLY 
if you put it in the B-margin.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Infinite Loops and Explicit Exits

_(reply depth: 32)_

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2004-11-19T16:07:15+00:00

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Infinite Loops and Explicit Exits

_(reply depth: 31)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2004-11-16T19:55:30+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<SWsmd.3098150$yk.481776@news.easynews.com>`
- **References:** `<10ol1ll6f8m6t2f@corp.supernews.com> <10onjhgpd68jge7@corp.supernews.com> <cmgl1d$7k7$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <10onr54cu7gg9bc@corp.supernews.com> <y39ld.622$aW.26@fe40.usenetserver.com> <dmqcp0l44477l9oou9h7sp7eir2r7sqi41@4ax.com> <l6Cld.1226$aW.1151@fe40.usenetserver.com> <9obgp09quunk1ga3u76h7t6hhrs1orgrjn@4ax.com> <217e491a.0411151314.626f8b34@posting.google.com> <Jbamd.3032393$yk.471737@news.easynews.com> <5d3jp0pi57h48g3akbhvk6e1vr71cbsqgp@4ax.com> <217e491a.0411161105.475a03e@posting.google.com>`

```
Minor (?) correction.

The period (full stop) when followed by a space is DEFINITELY a "separator".

The following code is valid in the '85 Standard (and the '02 and as far as I 
know every standard)

    MOVE A TO B. ADD 1 TO C.

as well as:

01   FIELD. 05 FIELD-2 PIC X. 05 FIELD-3  PIC 9
        VALUE ZERO. 88
         NON-ZERO VALUE
         1 THRU 9. 05 FIELD-4 PIC A.

It may be UGLY but it is "valid"
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Infinite Loops and Explicit Exits

_(reply depth: 32)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2004-11-16T17:15:47-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0411161715.5393014e@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<10ol1ll6f8m6t2f@corp.supernews.com> <10onr54cu7gg9bc@corp.supernews.com> <y39ld.622$aW.26@fe40.usenetserver.com> <dmqcp0l44477l9oou9h7sp7eir2r7sqi41@4ax.com> <l6Cld.1226$aW.1151@fe40.usenetserver.com> <9obgp09quunk1ga3u76h7t6hhrs1orgrjn@4ax.com> <217e491a.0411151314.626f8b34@posting.google.com> <Jbamd.3032393$yk.471737@news.easynews.com> <5d3jp0pi57h48g3akbhvk6e1vr71cbsqgp@4ax.com> <217e491a.0411161105.475a03e@posting.google.com> <SWsmd.3098150$yk.481776@news.easynews.com>`

```
"William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> wrote 

> The period (full stop) when followed by a space is DEFINITELY a "separator".
> 
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>     MOVE A TO B. ADD 1 TO C.

The full stop is a _statement_ and _sentence_ terminator.  You are
correct that it isn't a _line_ terminator.  It isn't a 'separator' at
all because it is not needed between consecutive statements, as a
separator would be, it is only needed to terminate them.

My reference to it being a terminator and not a separator is that it
should be (IMHO) considered as part of what it is terminating.  The
style that tries to put it in front of the next line is flawed because
that would only be appropriate if it was, in fact, a separator (and
then probably not anyway).

As a comparison the ';' is a terminator in C and a separator in
Pascal.  This does make a difference in blocks of statement, and
especially in if statements.

in Pascal:
        if ( condition ) then
            statement1
        else
            statement2 ;

in C:
        if ( condition )
             statement1 ;
        else
             statement2 ;

in Pascal:
        begin
             statement1 ;
             statement2
        end

in C:
        {
        statement1 ;
        statement2 ;
        }
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Infinite Loops and Explicit Exits

_(reply depth: 33)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2004-11-17T01:38:04+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<0Yxmd.5207684$6p.841919@news.easynews.com>`
- **References:** `<10ol1ll6f8m6t2f@corp.supernews.com> <10onr54cu7gg9bc@corp.supernews.com> <y39ld.622$aW.26@fe40.usenetserver.com> <dmqcp0l44477l9oou9h7sp7eir2r7sqi41@4ax.com> <l6Cld.1226$aW.1151@fe40.usenetserver.com> <9obgp09quunk1ga3u76h7t6hhrs1orgrjn@4ax.com> <217e491a.0411151314.626f8b34@posting.google.com> <Jbamd.3032393$yk.471737@news.easynews.com> <5d3jp0pi57h48g3akbhvk6e1vr71cbsqgp@4ax.com> <217e491a.0411161105.475a03e@posting.google.com> <SWsmd.3098150$yk.481776@news.easynews.com> <217e491a.0411161715.5393014e@posting.google.com>`

```
Are you saying that is the way YOU would design COBOL?  If so, fine - that's 
your opinion.  If you are talking about every COBOL Standard (and all conforming 
implementations) then you are in error.

See (for example) page 82 of the '02 Standard which says,

"3) The COBOL character period, when followed by a space, is a separator. The 
separator period shall be used only
to indicate the end of a sentence, or as shown in formats."
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Infinite Loops and Explicit Exits

_(reply depth: 34)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2004-11-17T09:53:36-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0411170953.5cd32748@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<10ol1ll6f8m6t2f@corp.supernews.com> <l6Cld.1226$aW.1151@fe40.usenetserver.com> <9obgp09quunk1ga3u76h7t6hhrs1orgrjn@4ax.com> <217e491a.0411151314.626f8b34@posting.google.com> <Jbamd.3032393$yk.471737@news.easynews.com> <5d3jp0pi57h48g3akbhvk6e1vr71cbsqgp@4ax.com> <217e491a.0411161105.475a03e@posting.google.com> <SWsmd.3098150$yk.481776@news.easynews.com> <217e491a.0411161715.5393014e@posting.google.com> <0Yxmd.5207684$6p.841919@news.easynews.com>`

```
"William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> wrote 

> "3) The COBOL character period, when followed by a space, is a separator. 
> The separator period 

They may _name_ it as a separator, but its _function_ is a terminator.

> shall be used only to indicate the end of a sentence,

Where 'end' is synonomous with 'terminate' in this context.

> or as shown in formats."

Which is invariably at the end to terminate a part or the whole.

Both separators and terminators separate objects, the difference in
function is at the end of a structure.  A separator is not required, a
terminator is.

     object separator object
     object terminator object terminator

In Cobol the structure must be:

     sentence . sentence .

Thus the 'full stop space' is functionally a terminator.

Yes, the standard is wrong in calling it a separator, not that it
matters much.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Infinite Loops and Explicit Exits

_(reply depth: 35)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2004-11-17T20:06:43+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<nbOmd.5264290$6p.850696@news.easynews.com>`
- **References:** `<10ol1ll6f8m6t2f@corp.supernews.com> <l6Cld.1226$aW.1151@fe40.usenetserver.com> <9obgp09quunk1ga3u76h7t6hhrs1orgrjn@4ax.com> <217e491a.0411151314.626f8b34@posting.google.com> <Jbamd.3032393$yk.471737@news.easynews.com> <5d3jp0pi57h48g3akbhvk6e1vr71cbsqgp@4ax.com> <217e491a.0411161105.475a03e@posting.google.com> <SWsmd.3098150$yk.481776@news.easynews.com> <217e491a.0411161715.5393014e@posting.google.com> <0Yxmd.5207684$6p.841919@news.easynews.com> <217e491a.0411170953.5cd32748@posting.google.com>`

```
The "category" of separator in COBOL includes
   ==
   (
   )
   :

Interestingly enough for purposes of COPY/REPLACING in

    Pic  9(2).99

the "(" and the ")" are separators but the "." is not (because it isn't followed 
by a space.

"separator" (in COBOL terms) does lots of things.  one of them is (for a period) 
to terminate a structure - but that is simply a "subset" of all the things a 
COBOL separator can do/be.
```

```
   I believe all of the '02 Standard is still marked as an "extension" or at 
least a "dialect" in the MF manual - as is (for that matter the '85 Standard).

the LRM is still (believe-it-or-not) based on the '74 Standard with everything 
else marked as one or more dialects.
```

- **References:** `<clu41g$1t2b$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <cm5pat$10a5$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <cm5s6e$h8g$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <cm61fc$15ui$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <cm62pq$mi7$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <cm63go$1780$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <cm65f1$ofm$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <10od8oaiipd7b7c@corp.supernews.com> <217e491a.0411012330.5ddc9ec@posting.google.com> <10ofruhrt7slp2e@corp.supernews.com> <217e491a.0411022334.5c19f10e@posting.google.com> <10oj2m0fda3sp6d@corp.supernews.com>`

```
"Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net> wrote 

> Mr Plinston, you wrongly assumed that the quoted phrases
> were direct quotes, 

They were direct quotes. They were some of my words in quotation
marks. They were, however, truncated of any context, deliberately no
doubt, to misrepresent what they said.

If you do not wish me to interpret quotation marks as indicating a
quotation then either use some other form of mark to indicate
emphasis, as is the usual convention, or indicate, for each quotation
mark, that this quotation mark does not mark a quotation.

> ... rather than phrases that I had to supply
> to discuss the subject of how such phrases "insist."

If they were actually 'phrases that you had to supply', then I suggest
that _you_ take responsibility for what they say.

I have no problem being criticised for what I actually say, but here
you seem to  argue that you made up those phrases.

> Mr Plinston and Mr Brazee, qualification and opinion makes
> no difference.

Obviously these make no difference to you, you see what you want to
see.

You also seem to blindly ignore that I defend the GO TO.

> [Three months later]
> [One year later]
> [Eight months later]

That seems to be over a period(*) of two years, in fact one of the
quotes comes from 2001.  It would hardly seem 'insisting' to a
rational person when it is stated so infrequently.

> My point was that "insisting" on a particular style "implies"
> that another style, using features long present, should not
> be used and, therefore, these features are "no longer needed",
> thus "archaic."

I do understand the point that you attempt to make, I do understand
that you are _insisting_ that it is valid, but it is merely an
unwarranted extrapolation, perhaps of the insecurity you feel your
position has.

> My answer (re-phrased) is those who
> deprecate a feature by "insisting" on a style that avoids the
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> obsolete; followed, later, by automatic removal.

All a complete fantasy.  I don't 'insist' on anything at all, yet
suddenly, in your mind, you have escalated it 3 steps beyond that.

> That was, as I understand it, what happened to the ALTER
> statement. With the GO TO statement, many "insist" on a
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> 2002.]

If you knew anything at all about what my actual 'insistence' was you
would have read what I had submitted to J4 concerning enhancements
which would better cater for use of GO TO by providing mechanisms that
would allow compiler checking to prevent some of the common
programming errors that currently can't be determined by the compile.

On the other hand, Jerrymouse may well have been correct with his:

""Nobody uses SECTIONS and PERFORM THRU... is used only by people with
rabies.""

Are you foaming at the mouth ?
```

```
and others; but when I was deciding what to write, I did not
look at any prior posts.

> > The context was entirely what I supplied when I used the

phrases fits the definition of "insist" that I used and, later, to
show how these phrases might "imply" that certain features
are "archaic." These were words I used in my earlier
response, as "insisting on ... implying ... archaic."

there is, often, a flurry of posts with individuals taking their
usual positions supporting some features and deprecating others.

using perform ... thru and go to, just to see the reaction. After
all, I've been insisting for a while that there is no *the* way.
```

```
"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote 

> I have found that in this medium, alternative ways of *emphasizing* words can be
> useful.

Yes, it does leave the question: if quotation marks (otherwise knowns
as 'quotes') are used merely for emphasis, then what would be used for
indicating a quotation ?

The convention that _I_ tend to use (emphasised so that no one thinks
that I am insisting that others follow this, and that all other
conventions should be made obsolete), are _underlined_, /italics/,
*bold*, and possibly SHOUTING.

Quotes are for quotations, triple quotes for quoted passages.
```

```
Are people who insist on not using Report Writer implying that it is archaic?

Are people who insist on using ADD (and other such commands) implying that

Are people who insist on using 01 levels throughout implying that 77 levels are
archaic?

Are people who insist on not using OO implying that OO is archaic?
```

- **Message-ID:** `<10ofu9fkonoc116@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<clu41g$1t2b$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <S7Jgd.3709$K7.2223@news-server.bigpond.net.au> <10o7eij23spll38@corp.supernews.com> <217e491a.0410302009.c1781e9@posting.google.com> <10o8vebpi1os15d@corp.supernews.com> <217e491a.0410310949.6a916128@posting.google.com> <cm5pat$10a5$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <cm5s6e$h8g$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <cm61fc$15ui$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <cm62pq$mi7$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <cm63go$1780$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <cm65f1$ofm$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <10od8oaiipd7b7c@corp.supernews.com> <cm86ph$4qn$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```

"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote in message
news:cm86ph$4qn$1@peabody.colorado.edu...
>
> On  1-Nov-2004, "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net> wrote:
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> You are entitled to believe what you want about what I am implying.    But
your
> odds of convincing me that this is what I am implying are quite low.

Mr Brazee, how things are said is important to determining
what is implied. Please consider my response to Mr Plinston
and, if you remain unconvined, perhps I can dig up a quote
or two to analyze.

> Are people who insist on not using Report Writer implying that it is
archaic?

It depends. Those who say they use Crystal Reports because
Report Writer cannot handle fonts or graphical images are
implying that Report Writer is outmoded, therefore antiquated
and archaic. Those who say they write their own routines
rather than use Report Writer do not imply that Report Writer
is archaic.

> Are people who insist on using ADD (and other such commands) implying that
> COMPUTE is archaic?

I cannot imagine a context, in which, any statement could imply
that COMPUTE is archaic.

> Are people who insist on using 01 levels throughout implying that 77
levels are
> archaic?

In 1976, CODASYL recommended the removal of level-number
77. COBOL 2002 allows a constant entry to appear only in
level-number 1 or 01. Those who insist on using 01 levels and
reference such detail imply that 77 levels are archaic.

> Are people who insist on not using OO implying that OO is archaic?

If they use such words as "its time has come and gone", then yes.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Infinite Loops and Explicit Exits

_(reply depth: 11)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2004-11-01T23:32:18+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6Izhd.4318728$6p.714113@news.easynews.com>`
- **References:** `<clu41g$1t2b$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <S7Jgd.3709$K7.2223@news-server.bigpond.net.au> <10o7eij23spll38@corp.supernews.com> <217e491a.0410302009.c1781e9@posting.google.com> <10o8vebpi1os15d@corp.supernews.com> <217e491a.0410310949.6a916128@posting.google.com> <cm5pat$10a5$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <cm5s6e$h8g$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <cm61fc$15ui$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <cm62pq$mi7$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <cm63go$1780$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`

```
And it is worth noting that moving "PERFORM ... THRU" (along with GO TO and 
Input/Output Procedures ... THRU) into the ARCHAIC category was voted on - for 
the 2002 Standard (at WG4) and this was rejected.  I don't know if it was 
"re-voted" for 2008.

Personally, I don't remember MUCH "user input" on this before 2002.  If you 
actually do WANT this to happen, you should contact your "national Standards 
body" and NOT just J4 to get the process going.
```

- **References:** `<clu41g$1t2b$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <cm62pq$mi7$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <cm63go$1780$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`

```
.    On  01.11.04
  wrote  charles.stevens@unisys.com (Chuck Stevens)
     on  /COMP/LANG/COBOL
     in  cm63go$1780$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com
  about  Re: Infinite Loops and Explicit Exits


CS> Those who suggest that GO TO, PERFORM THRU, and the use of SECTIONs
CS> in COBOL should be archaized in, marked obsolete in, or eliminated
CS> from the standard -- and these are the three steps in that process --
CS> are.

   GOTO, PERFORM THRU yes, but SECTIONS no. The opposite makes sense.  
The first step to clean up the code is to put a SECTION header on top  
of that paragraph-thru-paragraph sentence and eliminate all the  
paragraph labels inbetween, restructuring that section into proper  
decision and repetition instructions.

   So one can by and by change such a program into something readable  
and maintainable.


Yours,
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Infinite Loops and Explicit Exits

_(reply depth: 12)_

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2004-11-02T17:23:35+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<cm8fqn$aa1$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<clu41g$1t2b$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <cm62pq$mi7$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <cm63go$1780$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <9K6g0sweflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de>`

```

On  2-Nov-2004, l.willms@jpberlin.de (Lueko Willms) wrote:

>    GOTO, PERFORM THRU yes, but SECTIONS no. The opposite makes sense.
> The first step to clean up the code is to put a SECTION header on top
> of that paragraph-thru-paragraph sentence and eliminate all the
> paragraph labels inbetween, restructuring that section into proper
> decision and repetition instructions.

If you eliminate all paragraph labels in between, structurally there is no
difference between paragraphs and sections.   Unless you're discussing memory
management, what is the advantage of putting the word SECTION after a label in
this case?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Infinite Loops and Explicit Exits

_(reply depth: 13)_

- **From:** l.willms@jpberlin.de (Lueko Willms)
- **Date:** 2004-11-02T19:57:00+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9K6kudCeflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de>`
- **References:** `<clu41g$1t2b$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <9K6g0sweflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de> <cm8fqn$aa1$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
.     Am  02.11.04
 schrieb  howard@brazee.net (Howard Brazee)
     bei  /COMP/LANG/COBOL
      in  cm8fqn$aa1$1@peabody.colorado.edu
   ueber  Re: Infinite Loops and Explicit Exits

>>    GOTO, PERFORM THRU yes, but SECTIONS no. The opposite makes sense.
>> The first step to clean up the code is to put a SECTION header on top
>> of that paragraph-thru-paragraph sentence and eliminate all the
>> paragraph labels inbetween, restructuring that section into proper
>> decision and repetition instructions.

HB> If you eliminate all paragraph labels in between, structurally there
HB> is no difference between paragraphs and sections.   Unless you're
HB> discussing memory management, what is the advantage of putting the
HB> word SECTION after a label in this case?

   For one, all four DIVISIONs of a COBOL program (exept the very  
minimal IDENTIFICATION DIVISION) are divided in SECTIONs, that's why  
it should be natural to do that with the PROCEDURE DIVISION, too.

   Then, when the program has event and exception processing in  
DECLARATIVES or a SORT INPUT or OUTPUT PROCEDURE, one needs to have  
sections for "regular" processing, too.

   Third, transforming the sequence of paragraphs performed until some  
THRU-paragraph into a SECTION makes it easy to transform the program  
gradually, in small steps. I.e. one can leave the paragraphs and use  
them as labels for GOTOs, but one can drop the THRU as a first step.  
The next step can be the elimination of the paragraphs and replacing  
them with a wieldy IF .. THEN .. ELSE ... END-IF structure (nested  
ones, if needed). And so on ...

   My style is to only use SECTIONs which all have only one paragraph,  
named ANFANG (German for "BEGIN").


Yours,
Lï¿½ko Willms                                     http://www.mlwerke.de

eine Regierung von Arbeitern, durch Arbeiter und fï¿½r Arbeiter"
```
